sentence: We’ve got to remain energy-independent.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It gives us much more power and freedom than to be worried about what goes on in the Middle East.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We have enough worries over there without having to worry about that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So I have a comprehensive energy policy, but it really does include fighting climate change, because I think that is a serious problem.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I support moving toward more clean, renewable energy as quickly as we can, because I think we can be the 21st century clean energy superpower and create millions of new jobs and businesses.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But I also want to be sure that we don’t leave people behind.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That’s why I’m the only candidate from the very beginning of this campaign who had a plan to help us revitalize coal country, because those coal miners and their fathers and their grandfathers, they dug that coal out.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: A lot of them lost their lives.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: They were injured, but they turned the lights on and they powered our factories.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I don’t want to walk away from them.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we’ve got to do something for them.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But the price of coal is down worldwide.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we have to look at this comprehensively.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And that’s exactly what I have proposed.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I hope you will go to HillaryClinton.com and look at my entire policy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I certainly will, because I think that’s a very fair and important question.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Look, I respect his children.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: His children are incredibly able and devoted, and I think that says a lot about Donald.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I don’t agree with nearly anything else he says or does, but I do respect that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I think that is something that as a mother and a grandmother is very important to me.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So I believe that this election has become in part so—so conflict-oriented, so intense because there’s a lot at stake.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: This is not an ordinary time, and this is not an ordinary election.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We are going to be choosing a president who will set policy for not just four or eight years, but because of some of the important decisions we have to make here at home and around the world, from the Supreme Court to energy and so much else, and so there is a lot at stake.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It’s one of the most consequential elections that we’ve had.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And that’s why I’ve tried to put forth specific policies and plans, trying to get it off of the personal and put it on to what it is I want to do as president.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And that’s why I hope people will check on that for themselves so that they can see that, yes, I’ve spent 30 years, actually maybe a little more, working to help kids and families.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I want to take all that experience to the White House and do that every single day.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, I consider her statement about my children to be a very nice compliment.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I don’t know if it was meant to be a compliment, but it is a great—I’m very proud of my children.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And they’ve done a wonderful job, and they’ve been wonderful, wonderful kids.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So I consider that a compliment.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I will say this about Hillary.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She doesn’t quit.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She doesn’t give up.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I respect that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I tell it like it is.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She’s a fighter.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I disagree with much of what she’s fighting for.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I do disagree with her judgment in many cases.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But she does fight hard, and she doesn’t quit, and she doesn’t give up.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I consider that to be a very good trait.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, thank you for being here tonight, and, Governor Pence, welcome.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is so great to be back at Longwood University in Farmville, Virginia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is a very special place.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Sixty-five years ago, a young, courageous woman, Barbara Johns, led a walkout of her high school, Moton High School.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She made history by protesting school segregation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She believed our nation was stronger together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that walkout led to the Brown v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Board of Education decision that moved us down the path toward equality.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am so proud to be running with another strong, history-making woman, Hillary Clinton, to be president of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m proud because her vision of stronger together, building an economy that works for all, not just those at the top, being safe in the world not only with a strong military, but also strong alliances to battle terrorism and climate change, and also to build a community of respect, just like Barbara Johns tried to do 65 years ago.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s why I’m so proud to be her running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary told me why she asked me to be her running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She said the test of a Clinton administration will not be the signing of a bill or the passage of a bill.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’ll be whether we can make somebody’s life better, whether we can make a classroom better learning environment for schoolkids or teachers, whether we can make a safer — it’s going to be about results.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And she said to me, you’ve been a missionary and a civil rights lawyer.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve been a city councilman and mayor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve been a lieutenant governor and governor and now a U.S.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think you will help me figure out how to govern this nation so that we always keep in mind that the success of the administration is the difference we make in people’s lives.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what I bring to the ticket, that experience having served at all levels of government.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But my primary role is to be Hillary Clinton’s right-hand person and strong supporter as she puts together the most historic administration possible.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I relish that role.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m so proud of her.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll just say this: We trust Hillary Clinton, my wife and I, and we trust her with the most important thing in our life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have a son deployed overseas in the Marine Corps right now.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We trust Hillary Clinton as president and commander-in-chief, but the thought of Donald Trump as commander-in-chief scares us to death.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first off, thank you, Elaine, and thank you to — thank you to Norwood University for their wonderful hospitality and the Commission on Presidential Debates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s deeply humbling for me to be here, to be surrounded by my — my wonderful family.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And, Senator Kaine, it’s an honor to be here with you, as well.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I just — I also want to say — I want to say thanks to everyone that’s looking in tonight, who understands what an enormously important time this is in the life of our nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: For the last seven-and-a-half years, we’ve seen America’s place in the world weakened.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve seen an economy stifled by more taxes, more regulation, a war on coal, and a failing health care reform come to be known as Obamacare, and the American people know that we need to make a change.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And so I want to thank all of you for being — being with us tonight.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I also want to thank Donald Trump for making that call and inviting us to be a part of this ticket.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I have to tell you, I’m a — I’m a small-town boy from a place not too different from Farmville.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I grew up with a cornfield in my backyard.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My grandfather had immigrated to this country when he was about my son’s age.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My mom and dad built a — everything that matters in a small town in Southern Indiana.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They built a family and — and a good name and a business.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And they raised a family.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I dreamed some day of representing my home town in Washington, D.C., but I — honestly, Elaine, I never imagined — never imagined I’d have the opportunity to be governor of the state that I love, let alone be sitting at a table like this in this kind of a position.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So to answer your question, I would say I — I would hope that if — if the responsibility ever fell to me in this role, that I would meet it with the way that I’m going to meet the responsibility should I be elected vice president of the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that’s to bring a lifetime of experience, a lifetime growing up in a small town, a lifetime where I’ve served in the Congress of the United States, where — where I’ve led a state that works in the great state of Indiana, and whatever other responsibilities might follow from this, I — I would hope and, frankly, I would pray to be able to meet that moment with that — that lifetime of experience.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me tell you why I trust Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what people should look at as they look at a public servant.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do they have a passion in their life that showed up before they were in public life?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And have they held onto that passion throughout their life, regardless of whether they were in office or not, succeeding or failing?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has that passion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: From a time as a kid in a Methodist youth group in the suburbs of Chicago, she has been focused on serving others with a special focus on empowering families and kids.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: As a civil rights lawyer in the South, with the Children’s Defense Fund, first lady of Arkansas and this country, senator, secretary of state, it’s always been about putting others first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s a sharp contrast with Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump always puts himself first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He built a business career, in the words of one of his own campaign staffers, “off the backs of the little guy.” And as a candidate, he started his campaign with a speech where he called Mexicans rapists and criminals, and he has pursued the discredited and really outrageous lie that President Obama wasn’t born in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is so painful to suggest that we go back to think about these days where an African-American could not be a citizen of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I can’t imagine how Governor Pence can defend the insult-driven selfish “me first” style of Donald Trump.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let me — let me say first and foremost that, Senator, you and Hillary Clinton would know a lot about an insult-driven campaign.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It really is remarkable.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: At a time when literally, in the wake of Hillary Clinton’s tenure as secretary of state, where she was the architect of the Obama administration’s foreign policy, we see entire portions of the world, particularly the wider Middle East, literally spinning out of control.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the situation we’re watching hour by hour in Syria today is the result of the failed foreign policy and the weak foreign policy that Hillary Clinton helped lead in this administration and create.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The newly emboldened — the aggression of Russia, whether it was in Ukraine or now their heavy-handed approach…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You guys love Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You both have said…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … their heavy-handed approach.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You both have said — you both have said Vladimir Putin is a better leader than the president.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But in the midst — Elaine, thank you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Thank you, Senator, I’ll…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys have praised Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How can that…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, Senator, I must have hit a…[crosstalk]…I must have hit a nerve here.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because at a time of great challenge in the life of this nation, where we’ve weakened America’s place in the world, stifled America’s economy, the campaign of Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine has been an avalanche of insults.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, to get to your question about trustworthiness, Donald Trump has built a business through hard times and through good times.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s brought an extraordinary business acumen.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s employed tens of thousands of people in this country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And paid few taxes and lost a billion a year.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But there’s a — there’s a reason why people question the trustworthiness of Hillary Clinton.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that’s because they’re paying attention.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the reality is, when she was secretary of state, Senator, come on.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She had a Clinton Foundation accepting contributions from foreign governments.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You are Donald Trump’s apprentice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me talk about this…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, I think I’m still on my time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I think — isn’t this a discussion?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, let’s talk about the state of…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let me interrupt — let me interrupt you and finish my sentence, if I can.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Finish your sentence.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation accepted foreign contributions from foreign governments and foreign donors while she was secretary of state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: OK, now I can weigh in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She had a private server…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, I get to weigh in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, let me just say this…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … that was discovered…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … keep that pay to play process out of the reach of the public.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence — Governor Pence doesn’t think the world’s going so well and he, you know, is going to say it’s everybody’s fault.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me tell you this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Hillary Clinton became secretary of state, Governor Pence, do you know that Osama bin Laden was alive?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you know that we had 175,000 troops deployed in the battlefield in Iraq and Afghanistan?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you know that Iran was racing toward a nuclear weapon and Russia was expanding its stockpile?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Under Secretary Clinton’s leadership, she was part of the national team, public safety team that went after and revived the dormant hunt against bin Laden and wiped him off the face of the Earth.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked to deal with the Russians to reduce their chemical weapons stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked a tough negotiation with nations around the world to eliminate the Iranian nuclear weapons program without firing a shot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Eliminate the Iranian nuclear weapons program?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Absolutely, without firing a shot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And instead of 175,000 American troops deployed overseas, we now have 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These are very, very good things.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Iraq has been overrun by ISIS, because Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, if you want to put more American troops in Iraq, you can propose that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton — Hillary Clinton — Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate a status of forces agreement…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, that is incorrect.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s incorrect.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And so we removed — we removed all of our…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … troops from Iraq, and ISIS was able to be conjured up in that vacuum.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I’d like to correct…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and overrun vast areas of Iraq.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor, President Bush said we would leave Iraq at the end of 2011.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, Elaine, Iraq didn’t want our troops to stay, and they wouldn’t give us the protection for our troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And guess what?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If a nation where our troops are serving does not want us to stay, we’re not going to stay without their protection.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was a failure of the secretary of state…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If Governor Pence wants to put more troops back in Iraq, that’s…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I think the fact that — that under this past administration of which Hillary Clinton was a part, we’ve almost doubled the national debt is atrocious.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I’m very proud of the fact that — I come from a state that works.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The state of Indiana has balanced budgets.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We cut taxes, we’ve made record investments in education and in infrastructure, and I still finish my term with $2 billion in the bank.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s a little bit different than when Senator Kaine was governor here in Virginia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He actually — he actually tried to raise taxes by about $4 billion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He left his state about $2 billion in the hole.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: In the state of Indiana, we’ve cut unemployment in half; unemployment doubled when he was governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I think he’s a very fitting running mate for Hillary Clinton, because in the wake of a season where American families are struggling in this economy under the weight of higher taxes and Obamacare and the war on coal and the stifling avalanche of regulation coming out of this administration, Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want more of the same.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It really is remarkable that they actually are advocating a trillion dollars in tax increases, which I get that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You tried to raise taxes here in Virginia and were unsuccessful.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But a trillion dollars in tax increases, more regulation, more of the same war on coal, and more of Obamacare that now even former President Bill Clinton calls Obamacare a crazy plan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to build on Obamacare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They want to expand it into a single-payer program.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And for all the world, Hillary Clinton just thinks Obamacare is a good start.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, Donald Trump and I have a plan to get this economy moving again just the way that it worked in the 1980s, just the way it worked in the 1960s, and that is by lowering taxes across the board for working families, small businesses and family farms, ending the war on coal that is hurting jobs and hurting this economy even here in Virginia, repealing Obamacare lock, stock, and barrel, and repealing all of the executive orders that Barack Obama has signed that are stifling economic growth in this economy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can get America moving again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Put on top of that the kind of trade deals that’ll put the American worker first, and you’ve got a prescription for real growth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when you get the economy growing, Elaine, that’s when you can deal with the national debt.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When we get back to 3.5 percent to 4 percent growth with Donald Trump’s plan will do, then we’re going to have the resources to meet our nation’s needs at home and abroad, and we’re going to have the ability to bring down the national debt.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, on the economy, there’s a fundamental choice for the American electorate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you want a “you’re hired” president in Hillary Clinton or do you want a “you’re fired” president in Donald Trump?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think that’s not such a hard choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I have a plan that’s on the table that’s a “you’re hired” plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Five components.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First thing we do is we invest in manufacturing, infrastructure, and research in the clean energy jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second thing is we invest in our workforce, from pre-K education to great teachers to debt-free college and tuition-free college for families that make less than $125,000 a year.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Third, we promote fairness by raising the minimum wage, so you can’t work full-time and be under the poverty level, and by paying women equal pay for equal work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fourth, we promote small business growth, just as we’ve done in Virginia, to make it easier to start and grow small businesses.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I each grew up in small-business families.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My dad, who ran an iron working and welding shop, is here tonight.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, fifth, we have a tax plan that targets tax relief to middle- class individuals and small businesses and asks those at the very top who’ve benefited as we’ve come out of recession to pay more.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump plan is a different plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s a “you’re fired” plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And there’s two key elements to it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, Donald Trump said wages are too high.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And both Donald Trump and Mike Pence think we ought to eliminate the federal minimum wage.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Mike Pence, when he was in Congress, voted against raising the minimum wage above $5.15.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he has been a one-man bulwark against minimum wage increases in Indiana.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The second component of the plan is massive tax breaks for the very top, trillions of dollars of tax breaks for people just like Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The problem with this, Elaine, is that’s exactly what we did 10 years ago and it put the economy into the deepest recession — the deepest recession since the 1930s.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Independent analysts say the Clinton plan would grow the economy by 10.5 million jobs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump plan would cost 3.5 million jobs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Donald Trump — why would he do this?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Because his tax plan basically helps him.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if he ever met his promise and he gave his tax returns to the American public like he said he would, we would see just how much his economic plan is really a Trump-first plan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first, let me say, I appreciated the “you’re hired,” “you’re fired” thing, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You use that a whole lot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I think your running mate used a lot of pre-done lines.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, what — what you all just heard out there is more taxes, $2 trillion in more spending, more deficits, more debt, more government.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if you think that’s all working, then you look at the other side of the table.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the truth of the matter is, the policies of this administration, which Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine want to continue, have run this economy into a ditch.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re in the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fifteen million new jobs?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … slowest economic recovery since the Great Depression.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fifteen million new jobs?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are millions more people living in poverty today than the day that Barack Obama with Hillary Clinton at his side…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the poverty level and the median income…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … stepped into the Oval Office.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … improved dramatically between 2014 and 2015.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You — honestly, Senator, you can roll out the numbers and the sunny side, but I got to tell you, people in Scranton know different.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: People in Fort Wayne, Indiana, know different.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, this economy is struggling.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The answer to this economy is not more taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But it’s not the giveaway tax relief to the folks at the top.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s not more spending…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am interested to hear whether he’ll defend his running mate’s not releasing taxes and not paying taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Absolutely I will.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, this is probably the difference between Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And, I mean, Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine — God bless you for it, career public servants, that’s great — Donald Trump is a businessman, not a career politician.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He actually built a business.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Those tax returns that were — that came out publicly this week show that he faced some pretty tough times 20 years ago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But like virtually every other business, including the New York Times not too long ago, he used what’s called net operating loss.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have a tax code, Senator, that actually is designed to encourage entrepreneurship in this country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But why won’t he release his tax returns?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, we’re answering the question about — about a business thing, is he…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I do want to come back to that, but…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: His tax returns — his tax returns showed he went through a very difficult time, but he used the tax code just the way it’s supposed to be used.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he did it brilliantly.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How do you know that?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You haven’t seen his tax returns.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He created a runway — because he’s created a business that’s worth billions of dollars today.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How do you know that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with regard to paying taxes, this whole riff about not paying taxes and people saying he didn’t pay taxes for years, Donald Trump has created tens of thousands of jobs.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he’s paid payroll taxes, sales taxes, property taxes…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me talk about something.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The only issue on taxes — Hillary Clinton is going to raise taxes, and Donald Trump and I are going to cut them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump started this campaign in 2014 and he said, “If I run for president, I will absolutely release my taxes.” He’s broken his first…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he will.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s broken his first promise.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second, he stood on the stage…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He hasn’t broken his promise.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He said he’s…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He stood on the stage last week and when Hillary said, you haven’t been paying taxes, he said, “That makes me smart.” So it’s smart not to pay for our military?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s smart not to pay for veterans?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s smart not to pay for teachers?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I guess all of us who do pay for those things, I guess we’re stupid.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the last thing I’ll say is this…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, do you take all the deductions that you’re entitled to?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The last thing — the last thing I want to ask Governor Pence is…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence had to give Donald Trump his tax returns to show he was qualified to be vice president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump must give the American public his tax returns to show that he’s qualified to be president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he’s breaking his promise.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, I have to respond to this.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’ll be — I’ll be very respectful.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, Donald Trump has filed over 100 pages of financial disclosure, which is what the law requires.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he said he would release his tax returns.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The American people can review that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he’s going — Senator, he’s going to release his tax returns when the audit is over…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Richard Nixon released tax returns when he was under audit.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They’re going to raise your taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to cut your taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you can’t meet Nixon’s standard…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’re having fun up here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, we’re going to protect Social Security, which is one of the greatest programs that the American government has ever done.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It happened at a time when you would work your whole life, your whole life, raising your kids, working, being a Little League coach or a Sunday school teacher, and then you would retire into poverty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Social Security has enabled people to retire with dignity and overwhelmingly not be in poverty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have to keep it solvent.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we will keep it solvent.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ll look for strategies like adjusting the payroll tax cap upward in order to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what Hillary and I will not do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I want to make this very plain.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will never, ever engage in a risky scheme to privatize Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump wrote a book and he said Social Security is a Ponzi scheme and privatization would be good for all of us.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when Congressman Pence was in Congress, he was the chief cheerleader for the privatization of Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Even after President Bush stopped pushing for it, Congressman Pence kept pushing for it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’re going to stand up against efforts to privatize Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ll look for ways to keep it solvent going forward, focusing primarily on the payroll tax cap.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thanks, Elaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There they go again.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go read — go read the book.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: All Donald Trump — all Donald Trump and I have said about Social Security is we’re going to meet our obligations to our seniors.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go read the book.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve said we’re going to meet the obligations of Medicare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s what this campaign is really about, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I get, this is — this is the old scare tactic that they roll out…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But — but you have a voting record, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I get all of that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I just, look…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There’s a question that you asked a little bit earlier that I want to go back to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I can’t believe that you won’t defend your own voting record.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I have to go back to.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, I — you’re running with Hillary Clinton, who wants to raise taxes by $1 trillion, increase spending by $2 trillion, and you say you’re going to keep the promises of Social Security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I are going to cut taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to — we’re going to — we’re going to…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’re not going to cut taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’re going to raise taxes on the middle class.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … reform government programs so we can meet the obligations of Social Security and Medicare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Stay on the path that your party has us on, we’re going to be in a — in a mountain range of debt.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’re going to face hard choices and…[crosstalk] Gentleman, I want to move on now.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You did ask this question about debt, and the debt explosion on the Trump plan is much, much bigger than anything on the Clinton side.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Three hundred and five economists said your plan is bad for the economy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, I think that’s a very fair comment.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think we put a lot on police shoulders.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And this is something I got a lot of scar tissue and experience on.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was a city councilman and mayor in Richmond.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when I came in, we had one of the highest homicide rates in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We fought very, very hard over the course of my time in local office with our police department, and we reduced our homicide rate nearly in half.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And then when I was governor of Virginia, we worked hard, too.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we did something we had really wanted to do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: For the first time ever, we cracked the top 10, 10 safest states, because we worked together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what I learned as a mayor and a governor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The way you make communities safer and the way you make police safer is through community policing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You build the bonds between the community and the police force, build bonds of understanding, and then when people feel comfortable in their communities, that gap between the police and the communities they serve narrows.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when that gap narrows, it’s safer for the communities and it’s safer for the police.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That model still works across our country, but there are some other models that don’t work, an overly aggressive, more militarized model.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump recently said we need to do more stop-and-frisk around the country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That would be a big mistake because it polarizes the relationship between the police and the community.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So here’s what we’ll do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’ll focus on community policing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will focus on — and Hillary Clinton has rolled out a really comprehensive mental health reform package that she worked on with law enforcement professionals, and we will also fight the scourge of gun violence in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m a gun-owner.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m a strong Second Amendment supporter.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I’ve got a lot of scar tissue, because when I was governor of Virginia, there was a horrible shooting at Virginia Tech, and we learned that through that painful situation that gaps in the background record check system should have been closed and it could have prevented that crime, and so we’re going to work to do things like close background record checks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if we do, we won’t have the tragedies that we did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: One of those killed at Virginia Tech was a guy named Liviu Librescu.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He was a 70-plus-year-old Romanian Holocaust survivor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He had survived the Holocaust.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Then he survived the Soviet Union takeover of his country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But then he was a visiting professor at Virginia Tech, and he couldn’t survive the scourge of gun violence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We can support the Second Amendment and do things like background record checks and make us safer, and that will make police safer, too.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You know, my uncle was a cop, a career cop, on the beat in downtown Chicago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He was my hero when I was growing up.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’d go up to visit my dad’s family in Chicago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My three brothers and I would marvel at my uncle when he would come out in his uniform, sidearm at his side.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Police officers are the best of us.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the men and women, white, African-American, Asian, Latino, Hispanic, they put their lives on the line every single day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me say, at the risk of agreeing with you, community policing is a great idea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s worked in the Hoosier state.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we fully support that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I are going to make sure that law enforcement have the resources and the tools to be able to really restore law and order to the cities and communities in this nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s probably — probably why the 330,000 members of the Fraternal Order of Police endorsed Donald Trump as the next president of the United States of America, because they see his commitment to them.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They see his commitment to law and order.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But they also — they also hear the bad mouthing, the bad mouthing that comes from people that seize upon tragedy in the wake of police action shootings as — as a reason to — to use a broad brush to accuse law enforcement of — of implicit bias or institutional racism.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that really has got to stop.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, when an African-American police officer in Charlotte named Brentley Vinson, an all-star football player who went to Liberty University here in the state, came home, followed his dad into law enforcement, joined the force in Charlotte, joined the force in Charlotte in 2014, was involved in a police action shooting that claimed the life of Keith — Keith Lamont Scott, it was a tragedy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I — we — we mourn with those who mourn.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We — we grieve with those who grieve.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’re saddened at the loss of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton actually referred to that moment as an example of implicit bias in the police force, where — where she used — when she was asked in the debate a week ago whether there was implicit bias in law enforcement, her only answer was that there’s implicit bias in everyone in the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I just think…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can I — can I explain…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … I just think what we ought to do is we ought to stop seizing on these moments of tragedy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We ought to assure the public that we’ll have a full and complete and transparent investigation whenever there’s a loss of life because of police action.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, Senator, please, you know, enough of this seeking every opportunity to demean law enforcement broadly by making the accusation of implicit bias every time tragedy occurs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine — Elaine, people shouldn’t be afraid to bring up issues of bias in law enforcement.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you’re afraid to have…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m not afraid to bring that up.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if — if you’re afraid to have the discussion, you’ll never solve it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And so here’s — here’s an example, heartbreaking.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We would agree this is a heartbreaking example.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The guy, Philando Castile, who was killed in St.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Paul, he was a worker, a valued worker in a local school.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he was killed for no apparent reason in an incident that will be discussed and will be investigated.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But when folks went and explored this situation, what they found is that Philando Castile, who was a — they called him Mr.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Rogers with Dreadlocks in the school that he worked.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The kids loved him.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he had been stopped by police 40 or 50 times before that fatal incident.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you look at sentencing in this country, African-Americans and Latinos get sentenced for the same crimes at very different rates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We need criminal justice reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, we do.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Indiana has passed criminal justice reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I just want to say, those who say that we should not…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But that’s not what you’re talking about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … we should not be able to bring up and talk about bias in the system, we’ll never solve the problem…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, when African-American police officers involved in a police action shooting involving an African-American, why would Hillary Clinton accuse that African-American police officer of implicit bias?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I guess I can’t believe you are defending the position that there is no bias and it’s a topic we don’t even…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I did not make that statement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I have the deepest respect for Senator Scott, and he’s a close friend.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And what I would say is that we — we need to adopt criminal justice reform nationally.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I — I signed criminal justice reform in the state of Indiana, Senator, and we’re very proud of it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I worked when I was in Congress on the second chance act.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have got to do a better job recognizing and correcting the errors in the system that do reflect on institutional bias in criminal justice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what — what — what Donald Trump and I are saying is let’s not have the reflex of assuming the worst of men and women in law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We truly do believe that law enforcement is not a force for racism or division in our country…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, can I…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Law enforcement in this country is a force for good.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They are the — they truly are people that put their lives on the line every single day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I would — I would suggest to you, what we need to do is assert a stronger leadership at the national level to support law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just heard Senator Kaine reject stop-and-frisk.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I would suggest to you that the families that live in our inner cities that are besieged by crime…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me — let me…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, if I could — if I could jump in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ve heard Senator Scott make that eloquent plea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And look, criminal justice is about respecting the law and being respected by the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So there is a fundamental respect issue here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just want to talk about the tone that’s set from the top.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump during his campaign has called Mexicans rapists and criminals.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s called women slobs, pigs, dogs, disgusting.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I don’t like saying that in front of my wife and my mother.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He attacked an Indiana-born federal judge and said he was unqualified to hear a federal lawsuit because his parents were Mexican.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He went after John McCain, a POW, and said he wasn’t a hero because he’d been captured.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said African-Americans are living in Hell.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he perpetrated this outrageous and bigoted lie that President Obama is not a U.S.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you want to have a society where people are respected and respect laws, you can’t have somebody at the top who demeans every group that he talks about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just — again, I cannot believe that Governor Pence will defend the insult-driven campaign that Donald Trump has run.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump’s laid out a plan to end illegal immigration once and for all in this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve been talking it to death for 20 years.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to continue the policies of open borders, amnesty, catch and release, sanctuary cities, all the things that are driving — that are driving wages down in this country, Senator, and also too often with criminal aliens in the country, it’s bringing heartbreak.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I — Donald Trump has a plan that he laid out in Arizona, that will deal systemically with illegal immigration, beginning with border security, internal enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s probably why for the first time in the history of Immigration and Customs Enforcement their union actually endorsed Donald Trump as the next president of the United States, because they know they need help to enforce the laws of this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Donald Trump has laid out a priority to remove criminal aliens, remove people that have overstayed their visas.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And — and once we have accomplished all of that, which will — which will strengthen our economy, strengthen the rule of law in the country and make our communities safer once the criminal aliens are out, then we’ll deal with those that remain.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I have to tell you, I just — I was listening to the avalanche of insults coming out of Senator Kaine a minute ago.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These were Donald’s — hold on a second, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s my time, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I apologize.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s your two minutes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I apologize.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I forgive you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He says ours is an insult-driven campaign.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Did you all just hear that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ours is an insult-driven campaign?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, to be honest with you, if Donald Trump had said all of the things that you’ve said he said in the way you said he said them, he still wouldn’t have a fraction of the insults that Hillary Clinton leveled when she said that half of our supporters were a basket of deplorables.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s — she said they were irredeemable, they were not American.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, it’s extraordinary.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then she labeled one after another “ism” on millions of Americans who believe that we can have a stronger America at home and abroad, who believe we can get this economy moving again, who believe that we can end illegal immigration once and for all.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So, Senator, this — this insult-driven campaign, I mean…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s small potatoes compared to Hillary Clinton…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: calling half of Donald Trump’s supporters a basket of deplorables.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton said something on the campaign trail, and the very next day, she said, you know what, I shouldn’t have said that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She said she shouldn’t have said half.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, that’s right, so now we’re even.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Look for Donald Trump apologizing to John McCain for saying he wasn’t a hero…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … to Donald Trump apologizing for calling women slobs, pigs, dogs, disgusting.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She apologized for saying “half.”

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Did Donald Trump apologize for taking after somebody in a Twitter war and making fun of her weight?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Did he apologize for saying African-Americans are living in Hell?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Did he apologize for saying President Obama was not even a citizen of the United States?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You will look in vain to see Donald Trump ever taking responsibility for anybody and apologizing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Immigration.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There’s two plans on the table.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I believe in comprehensive immigration reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump believes in deportation nation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to pick your choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I want a bipartisan reform that will put keeping families together as the top goal, second, that will help focus enforcement efforts on those who are violent, third, that will do more border control, and, fourth, that will provide a path to citizenship for those who work hard, pay taxes, play by the rules, and take criminal background record checks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s our proposal.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump proposes to deport 16 million people, 11 million who are here without documents.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And both Donald Trump and Mike Pence want to get rid of birthright citizenship.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So if you’re born here, but your parents don’t have documents, they want to eliminate that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s another 4.5 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys — and Donald Trump has said it — deportation force.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They want to go house to house, school to school, business to business, and kick out 16 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I cannot believe…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s nonsense.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I cannot believe that Governor Pence would sit here and defend his running mate’s claim that we should create a deportation force to — so that they’ll all be gone.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, we have a deportation force.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s called Immigrations and Customs Enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the union for Immigrations and Customs Enforcement for the first time in their history endorsed Donald Trump to be the next president of the United States of America.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So you like the 16 million deportations?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, that’s — that’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, what you just heard is they have a plan for open borders, amnesty.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Our plan is like Ronald Reagan’s plan from 1986.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They call it comprehensive immigration reform — they call it comprehensive immigration reform on Capitol Hill.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We all know the routine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s amnesty.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And you heard one of the last things he mentioned was border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s how Washington always plays it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They always say we’re going to do this, we’re going to do that, we’ll eventually get the border…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … border security three years ago, and Governor Pence was against it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ronald Reagan said a nation without borders is not a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump is committed to restoring the borders of this nation and securing our nation, enforcing our laws.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I think Donald Trump laid out a series of priorities that doesn’t ends with border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It begins with border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And after we secure the border, not only build a wall, but beneath the ground and in the air, we do internal enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But he said the focus has to be on criminal aliens.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just — we just had a conversation about law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just had a conversation about the — the violence that’s besetting our cities.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The reality is that there’s heartbreak and tragedy that has struck American families because people that came into this country illegally are now involved in criminal enterprise and activity.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we don’t have the resources or the will to deport them systematically.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump has said we’re going to move those people out, people who’ve overstayed their visas.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to enforce the law of this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to strengthen Immigrations and Customs Enforcements with more resources and more personnel to be able to do that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then Donald Trump has made it clear, once we’ve done all of those things, that we’re going to reform the immigration system that we have…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I just have to correct Governor Pence….

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … where people can come into this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s the order that you should do it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Border security, removing criminal aliens, upholding with law, and then — but then, Senator, I’ll work you when you go back to the Senate, I promise you, we’ll work with you to reform the immigration system.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I look forward to working together in whatever capacities we serve in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I just want to make it very, very clear that he’s trying to fuzz up what Donald Trump has said.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump spoke in Phoenix, he looked the audience in the eye and he said, no, we’re building a wall, and we’re deporting everybody.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said, quote, “They will all be gone.” “They will all be gone.” And this is one of these ones where you can just go to the tape on it and see what Donald Trump has said.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s talking about criminal aliens.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And to add to it, and to add to it, and to add to it, we are a nation of immigrants.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Mike Pence and I both are descended from immigrant families.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Some things, you know, maybe weren’t said so great about the Irish when they came, but we’ve done well by absorbing immigrants, and it’s made our nation stronger.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump says Mexicans are rapists and criminals, Mexican immigrants, when Donald Trump says about your judge, a Hoosier judge, he said that Judge Curiel was unqualified to hear a case because his parents were Mexican, I can’t imagine how you could defend that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat has decreased in some ways, because bin Laden is dead.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat has decreased in some ways because an Iranian nuclear weapons program has been stopped.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat to United States troops has been decreased in some ways because there’s not 175,000 in a dangerous part of the world.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There’s only 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But there are other parts of the world that are challenging.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me tell you this: To beat terrorism, there’s only one candidate who can do it, and it’s Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Remember, Hillary Clinton was the senator from New York on 9/11.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was there at the World Trade Center when they were still searching for victims and survivors.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s seared onto her, the need to beat terrorism.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And she’s got a plan to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was part of the national security team that wiped out bin Laden.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s her plan to defeat ISIL.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, we’ve got to keep taking out their leaders on the battlefield.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was part of the team that got bin Laden, and she’ll lead the team that will get Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, the head of ISIS.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second, we’ve got to disrupt financing networks, third, disrupt their ability to recruit on the Internet, in their safe havens.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, fourth, we also have to work with allies to share and surge intelligence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s the Hillary Clinton plan; she’s got the experience to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump can’t start a Twitter war with Miss Universe without shooting himself in the foot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump doesn’t have a plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said, “I have a secret plan,” and then he said, “Um, I know more than all the generals about ISIL.” And then he said, “I’m going to call the generals to help me figure out a plan.” And finally he said, “I’m going to fire all the generals.” He doesn’t have a plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he does have dangerous ideas.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s four.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He trash talks the military.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The military is a disaster, John McCain’s no hero, the generals need all to be fired, and I know more than them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wants to tear up alliances.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: NATO is obsolete, and we’ll only work together with Israel if they pay “big league.” Third, he loves dictators.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s got kind of a personal Mount Rushmore, Vladimir Putin, Kim Jong-un, Moammar Gadhafi…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and Saddam Hussein.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And last and most dangerously, Donald Trump believes — Donald Trump believes that the world will be safer if more nations have nuclear weapons.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s said Saudi Arabia should get them, Japan should get them, Korea should get them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when he was confronted with this, and told, wait a minute, terrorists could get those, proliferation could lead to nuclear war, here’s what Donald Trump said, and I quote: “Go ahead, folks, enjoy yourselves.” I’d love to hear Governor Pence tell me what’s so enjoyable or comical about nuclear war.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Did you work on that one a long time?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because that had a lot of really creative lines in it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I’m going to see if you can defend any of it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, I can defend — I — I — I can — I can make very clear to the American people, after traveling millions of miles as our secretary of state, after being the architect of the foreign policy of this administration, America is less safe today than it was the day that Barack Obama became president of the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s absolutely inarguable.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve weakened America’s place in the world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s been a combination of factors, but mostly it’s been a lack of leadership.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I will give you — and I was in Washington, D.C., on 9/11.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I saw the clouds of smoke rise from the Pentagon.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was in Virginia where the Pentagon’s…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I know you were.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We all lived through that day as a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was heartbreaking.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I want to give this president credit for bringing Osama bin Laden to justice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the truth is, Osama bin Laden led Al Qaida.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Our primary threat today is ISIS.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And because Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate a status of forces agreement that would have allowed some American combat troops to remain in Iraq and secure the hard fought gains the American soldier had won by 2009, ISIS was able to be literally conjured up out of the desert, and it’s overrun vast areas that the American soldier had won in Operation Iraqi Freedom.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My heart breaks for the likes of Lance Cpl.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Scott Zubowski.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He fell in Fallujah in 2005.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He fought hard through some of the most difficult days in Operation Iraqi Freedom, and he paid the ultimate sacrifice to defend our freedom and secure that nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that nation was secured in 2009.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But because Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama failed to provide a status of forces agreement and leave sufficient troops in there, we are back at war.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The president just ordered more troops on the ground.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We are back at war in Iraq.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Scott Zubowski, whose mom would always come to Memorial Day events in Newcastle, Indiana, to see me, and I’d always give her a hug and tell her we’re never going to forget her son and we never will, Scott Zubowski and the sacrifices the American soldier made were squandered in Iraq because this administration created a vacuum in which ISIS was able to grow.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And a reference to the Iranian deal, the Iranian deal that Hillary Clinton initiated, $150 billion to the radical mullahs in Iran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Stopping a nuclear weapons program without firing a shot?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You didn’t stop the nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yes, we did.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You essentially…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Even the Israeli military says it stopped.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … guaranteed that Iran will someday become a nuclear power, because there’s no limitations once the period of time of the treaty comes off.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I think it’s — I think it’s a great question, Elaine, but it really does begin with us reforming our immigration system and putting the interests, particularly the safety and security of the American people, first.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, Donald Trump has called for extreme vetting for people coming into this country so that we don’t bring people into the United States who are hostile to our Bill of Rights freedoms, who are hostile to the American way life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But also, Donald Trump and I are committed to suspending the Syrian refugee program and programs and immigration from areas of the world that have been compromised by terrorism.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to increase the Syrian refugee program by 500…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, I want to…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Yeah, and so — but first, you know, let’s make sure we’re putting the safety and security of the American people first instead of Hillary Clinton expanding the Syrian refugee program…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Or instead of you violating the Constitution by blocking people based on their national origin rather than whether they’re dangerous.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not — that’s absolutely false.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what the Seventh Circuit decided just — here’s the difference, Elaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Seventh Circuit…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have different views on — on refugee issues and on immigration.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I want to do enforcement based on, are people dangerous?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys say all Mexicans are bad.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s absolutely false.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And with respect to refugees, we want to keep people out if they’re dangerous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump said keep them out if they’re Muslim.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … put a program in place to keep them out if they’re from Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yesterday an appellate court with three Republican judges struck down the Pence plan…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and said it was discriminatory…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And those judges — those judges said…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We should focus upon danger, not upon discrimination.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, to your point, those judges said it was because there wasn’t any evidence yet that — that ISIS had infiltrated the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, Germany just arrested three Syrian refugees that were connected to ISIS.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But they told you there’s a right way and a wrong way to do it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, look, if you’re going to be critical of me on that, that’s fair game.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I will tell you, after two Syrian refugees were involved in the attack in Paris that is called Paris’ 9/11, as governor of the state of Indiana, I have no higher priority than the safety and security of the people of my state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor Pence…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So you bet I suspended that program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor Pence, I just…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I stand by that decision.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if I’m vice president of the United States or Donald Trump is president, we’re going to put the safety and security of the American people first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can we just be clear — Hillary and I will do immigration enforcement and we’ll vet refugees based on whether they’re dangerous or not.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We won’t do it based on discriminating against you from the country you come from or the religion that you practice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the problem with that…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That is completely antithetical to the Jeffersonian values of…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, the director of the FBI, our homeland security, said we can’t know for certain who these people are coming from Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yes, we can, and when we don’t let them know, we don’t let them in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So — the FBI…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When we don’t know who they are, we don’t let them in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The FBI and homeland security said we can’t know for certain.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You’ve got to err on the side of the safety and security of the American people, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: By trashing all Syrians or trashing all Muslims?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: wants us to expand the Syrian refugee program…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to put the safety and security of the American people first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Intelligence surge is two things, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s two things.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s, first, dramatically expanding our intelligence capacities by hiring great professionals, but also we’ve got some of the best intel and cyber employees in the world right here in the United States working for many of our private sector companies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So it involves increasing our own workforce, but striking great partnerships with some of our cyber and intel experts in the private sector so that we can, consistent with constitutional principles, gather more intelligence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But the second piece of this is really, really important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It also means creating stronger alliances, because you gather intelligence and then you share your intelligence back and forth with allies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s how you find out who may be trying to recruit, who may be trying to come from one country to the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Alliances are critical.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s why Donald Trump’s claim that he wants to — that NATO is obsolete and that we need to get rid of NATO is so dangerous.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not his plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, he said NATO is obsolete.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, look, if you put aside — push aside your alliances, who you’re going to share intelligence with?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton is the secretary of state who knows how to build alliances.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She built the sanctions regime around the world that stopped the Iranian nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what an intelligence surge means.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Better skill and capacity, but also better alliances.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Can I speak about the cybersecurity surge at all?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: First, Donald Trump just spoke about this issue this week.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have got to bring together the best resources of this country to understand that cyber warfare is the new warfare of the asymmetrical enemies that we face in this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I look forward if I’m privileged to be in this role of working with you in the Senate to make sure that we resource that effort.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will work together in whatever roles we inhabit.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have an intelligence, sir.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I will also tell you that it’s important in this moment to remember that Hillary Clinton had a private server in her home that had classified information on it…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … about drone strikes, e-mails from the president of the United States of America were on there.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Her private server was subject to being hacked by foreign…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We could put cybersecurity first if we just make sure the next secretary of state doesn’t have a private server.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A full investigation concluded that not one reasonable prosecutor would take any additional step.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You don’t get to decide the rights and wrongs of this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have a justice system that does that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And a Republican FBI director did an investigation and concluded that…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: If your son or my son handled classified information the way Hillary Clinton did…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … they’d be court martialed.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That is absolutely false and you know that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Absolutely true.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And you know that, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s absolutely true.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Because the FBI did an investigation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And they concluded that there was no reasonable prosecutor who would take it further.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The United States of America needs to begin to exercise strong leadership to protect the vulnerable citizens and over 100,000 children in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton’s top priority when she became secretary of state was the Russian reset, the Russian reset.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: After the Russian reset, the Russians invaded Ukraine and took over Crimea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the small and bullying leader of Russia is now dictating terms to the United States to the point where all the United States of America — the greatest nation on Earth — just withdraws from talks about a cease-fire while Vladimir Putin puts a missile defense system in Syria while he marshals the forces and begins — look, we have got to begin to lean into this with strong, broad-shouldered American leadership.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It begins by rebuilding our military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the Russians and the Chinese have been making enormous investments in the military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have the smallest Navy since 1916.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have the lowest number of troops since the end of the Second World War.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve got to work with the Congress, and Donald Trump will, to rebuild our military and project American strength in the world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But about Aleppo and about Syria, I truly do believe that what America ought to do right now is immediately establish safe zones, so that families and vulnerable families with children can move out of those areas, work with our Arab partners, real time, right now, to make that happen.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And secondly, I just have to tell you that the provocations by Russia need to be met with American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if Russia chooses to be involved and continue, I should say, to be involved in this barbaric attack on civilians in Aleppo, the United States of America should be prepared to use military force to strike military targets of the Assad regime to prevent them from this humanitarian crisis that is taking place in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There’s a broad range of other things that we ought to do, as well.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We ought to deploy a missile defense shield to the Czech Republic and Poland which Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama pulled back on out of not wanting to offend the Russians back in 2009.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve just got to have American strength on the world stage.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When Donald Trump becomes president of the United States, the Russians and other countries in the world will know they’re dealing with a strong American president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I also agree that the establishment of humanitarian zones in northern Syria with the provision of international human aid, consistent with the U.N.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Security Council resolution that was passed in February 2014, would be a very, very good idea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary also has the ability to stand up to Russia in a way that this ticket does not.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump, again and again, has praised Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And it’s clear that he has business dealings with Russian oligarchs who are very connected to Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump campaign management team had to be fired a month or so ago because of those shadowy connections with pro-Putin forces.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence made the odd claim, he said inarguably Vladimir Putin is a better leader than President Obama.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin has run his economy into the ground.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He persecutes LGBT folks and journalists.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you don’t know the difference between dictatorship and leadership, then you got to go back to a fifth-grade civics class.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll tell you what offends me…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, that offended me.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence just said — Governor Pence just said that Donald Trump will rebuild the military.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, he won’t.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump is avoiding paying taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The New York Times story — and we need to get this — but the New York Times suggested that he probably didn’t pay taxes for about 18 years starting in 1995.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Those years included the years of 9/11.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So get this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: On 9/11, Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump’s hometown was attacked by the worst terrorist attack in the history of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Young men and women — young men and women signed up to serve in the military to fight terrorism.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton went to Washington to get funds to rebuild her city and protect first responders, but Donald Trump was fighting a very different fight.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was a fight to avoid paying taxes so that he wouldn’t support the fight against terror.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wouldn’t support troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wouldn’t — he wouldn’t support — this is important, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When a guy running for president will not support the troops, not support veterans, not support teachers, that’s really important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I said about Aleppo, we do agree the notion is we have to create a humanitarian zone in northern Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s very important.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first and foremost, Donald Trump supports our troops.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump supports our veterans.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He won’t pay taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump has paid all the taxes that he’s — do you not take deductions?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: How does that work?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Honestly, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Honestly, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is about our troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is about our troops.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand why you want to change — I understand why you want to change the subject.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How can you support the troops if you won’t pay taxes?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand why you want to change the subject.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me be very clear on this Russian thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The larger question here…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you think Donald Trump is smart to not pay taxes?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: What we’re dealing with is the — you know, there’s an old proverb that says the Russian bear never dies, it just hibernates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the truth of the matter is, the weak and feckless foreign policy of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama has awakened an aggression in Russia that first appeared a few years ago with their move in Georgia, now their move into Crimea, now their move into the wider Middle East.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And all the while, all we do is fold our arms and say we’re not having talks anymore.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: To answer your question, we just need American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We need to — we need to marshal the resources of our allies in the region, and in the immediate, we need to act and act now to get people out of harm’s way.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The — the safe zones would have to be — as the senator said, there’s already a framework for this that’s been recognized by the international community.

speaker: PENCE
