sentence: The United States of America needs to be prepared to work with our allies in the region to create a route for safe passage and then to protect people in those areas, including with a no-fly zone.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, look, this is very tough stuff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I served on the Foreign Affairs Committee for a decade.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I traveled in and out of that region for 10 years.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I saw what the American soldier won in Operation Iraqi Freedom.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And to see the weak and feckless leadership that Hillary Clinton was the architect of and the foreign policy of the Obama administration…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, let me — let me come back…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … is deeply troubling to me.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That will all change the day Donald Trump becomes president of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and talk about — let me talk about the things that Governor Pence doesn’t want to acknowledge, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge that we stopped the Iranian nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … that Hillary was part of the team that got bin Laden.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … that it’s a good thing, not a bad thing, that it’s a good thing — not a bad thing — that we’re down from 175,000 troops deployed overseas to 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But let me tell you what will really make the Middle East dangerous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump’s idea that more nations should get nuclear weapons, Saudi Arabia, Japan, South Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Ronald Reagan said something really interesting about nuclear proliferation back in the 1980s.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said the problem with nuclear proliferation is that some fool or maniac could trigger a catastrophic event.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I think that’s who Governor Pence’s running mate is, exactly who Governor Reagan warned us about.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And come on.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, that was even beneath you and Hillary Clinton.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that — that’s pretty low.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But do you — do you think — do you think we should have — more nuclear weapons in the world will make us safer?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what Donald Trump thinks.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ronald Reagan also said nuclear war should never be fought because it can never be won.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the United States of America needs to make investments in modernizing our nuclear force for both deterrence…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But can you defend Donald Trump’s claim that more nations should get nuclear weapons?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and assurance to our allies.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But let me go back to this Iran thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, he keeps saying that they prevented — that Hillary Clinton started the deal with the Iranians prevented Iran from getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what the Israeli joint chiefs of staff is saying right now.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, that’s not what — that’s not what Israel thinks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Gadi Eizenkot, you can go check it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You wouldn’t necessarily know that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go to the tape.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I know you boycotted Prime Minister Netanyahu’s speech when he came before the Congress.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, I visited him in his office.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I visited him in his office.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You boycotted the speech.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The point is, what this Iran — so-called Iran deal did was essentially guarantee — I mean, when I was in Congress, I fought hard on a bipartisan basis with Republican and Democrat members to move forward the toughest sanctions, it — literally in the history of the United States, against Iran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And then Hillary used them to get a deal.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We were bringing them to heel, but the goal was always that we would only lift the sanctions if Iran permanently renounced their nuclear ambitions.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me just mention one thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They have not — Elaine, let me finish a sentence.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They have not renounced their nuclear ambitions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when the deal’s period runs out, there’s no limitation on them obtaining weapons.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and the fact that they got $1.7 billion in a ransom payment…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … is astonishing to the American people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Six times tonight, I have said to Governor Pence I can’t imagine how you can defend your running mate’s position on one issue after the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And in all six cases, he’s refused to defend his running mate.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let’s — no, no, don’t put words in my mouth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s going…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yet he is asking everybody to vote for somebody that he cannot defend.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just think that should be underlined.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, I’m — look…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m very, very happy to defend Donald Trump.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: If he wants to take these one at a time, I’ll take them one at a time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: More nations should get nuclear weapons.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Try to defend that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Don’t put words in my mouth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, he never said that, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He absolutely said it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Saudi Arabia, South Korea, Japan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Most of the stuff you’ve said, he’s never said.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to be tough on Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So let’s start with not praising Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump and Mike Pence have said he’s a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Donald Trump has business…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, we haven’t.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … has business dealings — has business dealings with Russia that he refuses to disclose.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has gone toe-to-toe with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went toe-to-toe with Russia as secretary of state to do the New START Agreement to reduce Russia’s nuclear stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She’s had the experience doing it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went toe-to-toe with Russia and lodged protests when they went into Georgia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ve done the same thing about Ukraine, but more than launching protests, we’ve put punishing economic sanctions on Russia that we need to continue.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump, on the other hand, didn’t know that Russia had invaded the Crimea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Oh, that’s nonsense.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He was on a TV show a couple months back, and he said, “I’ll guarantee you this, Russia’s not going into the Ukraine.” And he had to be reminded that they had gone into the Crimea two years before.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He knew that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has gone toe-to-toe with Russia to work out a deal on New START.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She got them engaged in a meaningful way to cap Iran’s nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yet she stood up to them on issues such as Syria and their invasion of Georgia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to have the ability to do that, and Hillary does.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: On the other hand, in Donald Trump, you have somebody who praises Vladimir Putin all the time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: America should really wonder about a President Trump, who had a campaign manager with ties to Putin, pro-Putin elements in the Ukraine, who had to be fired for that reason.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They should wonder — when Donald Trump is sitting down with Vladimir Putin, is it going to be America’s bottom line or is it going to be Donald Trump’s bottom line that he’s going to be worried about with all of his business dealings?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, this could be solved if Donald Trump would be willing to release his tax returns, as he told the American public that he would do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I know he’s laughing at this, but every president…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what’s it got to do with Russia?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Every president since Richard Nixon has done it, and Donald Trump has said I’m doing business with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The only way the American public will see whether he has a conflict of interest…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, he hasn’t said that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He has, actually.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thanks.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m just trying to keep up with the insult-driven campaign on the other side of the table.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You know, I’m just saying facts about your running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I know you can’t defend.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m happy to defend him, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Don’t put words in my mouth that I’m not defending him.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m happy to defend him.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Most of what you said is completely false, and the American people know that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll run through the list of things where you won’t defend…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: This isn’t the old days where you can just say stuff and people believe it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, this is the alternative universe of Washington, D.C., versus reality.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton said her number-one priority was a reset with Russia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That reset resulted in the invasion of Ukraine, after they’d infiltrated with what are called little green men, Russian soldiers that were dressing up like Ukrainian dissidents, and then they moved all the way into Crimea, took over the Crimean Peninsula.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump knew that happened.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He basically was saying it’s not going to happen again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The truth of the matter is that what you have in the rise of aggressive Russia, which has had — increased its influence in Iran, that’s now — now because of this deal is on a pathway in the future to obtain a nuclear — the leading state sponsor of terror in the world in Iran now has a closer working relationship with Russia because of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama’s foreign policy and $150 billion and sanctions all being lifted.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then, of course, Syria, I mean, it really is extraordinary that — Syria is imploding.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just asked a very thoughtful question about the disaster in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: ISIS is headquartered in Raqqa.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It is — ISIS from Raqqa has overrun vast areas that at great sacrifice the American soldier won in Operation Iraqi Freedom, and yet Senator Kaine still sits here, loyal soldier — I get all that — in saying that the foreign policy of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama somehow made the world more secure.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, it really is astonishing that on the day…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We wiped out the leader of Al Qaida.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … on the day that Iran released four American hostages…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We stopped Iran from getting nuclear weapons.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … we delivered $400 million in cash as a ransom payment for Americans held by the radical mullahs in Tehran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we stopped a nuclear weapons program without a shot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Plain and simple.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Business dealings.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump — that’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump is a strong leader…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump’s son says that the Trump organization…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … who is going to lead with American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to rebuild our military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me — let me — this whole Putin thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, America is stronger than Russia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Our economy is 16 times larger than the Russian economy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: America’s political system is superior to the crony, corrupt capitalist system in Russia in every way.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When Donald Trump and I observe that, as I’ve said in Syria, in Iran, in Ukraine, that the small and bullying leader of Russia has been stronger on the world stage than this administration, that’s stating painful facts.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not an endorsement of Vladimir Putin.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s an indictment of the weak and feckless leadership…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, this is one where we can just kind of go to the tape on it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But Governor Pence said, inarguably, Vladimir Putin is a better leader than President Obama.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That is absolutely inaccurate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And — and — and I just think a guy who praises…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He said he’s stronger — he’s been stronger on the world stage.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, he said leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if — and I’ll just say this, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just said better.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you mistake leadership for dictatorship, and you can’t tell the difference, a country that’s running its economy into the ground…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Yeah, here we go.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: This is the grade school thing again?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … persecuting journalists…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Right, this is grade school.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … if you can’t tell the difference, you shouldn’t be commander-in-chief.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And with Donald Trump — Donald Trump’s sons say that they have all these business dealings with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Those could be disclosed with tax returns, but they refuse to do them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Americans need to worry about whether Donald Trump will be watching out for America’s bottom line or his own bottom line.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin is a dictator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin is a dictator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s not a leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Anybody who thinks otherwise doesn’t know Russian history and they don’t know Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton knows exactly who this guy is.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: John McCain said, I look in his eyes and I see KGB.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary kind of has that same feeling.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So how do deal with him?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to — we do have to deal with Russia in a lot of different ways.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There are areas where we can cooperate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So it was Hillary Clinton who worked with Russia on the New START Treaty to reduce their nuclear weapons stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was Hillary Clinton that worked with Russia to get them engaged in a community of nations to stop the Iranian nuclear weapons program without firing a shot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She’s not going around praising Vladimir Putin as a great guy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But she knows how to sit down at a table and negotiate tough deals.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is a very challenging part of the world, and we ought to have a commander-in-chief who is prepared and done it, rather than somebody who goes around praising Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first, we need to — we need to make a commitment to rebuild our military, including modernizing our nuclear forces.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we also need — we also need an effective American diplomacy that will marshal the resources of nations in the Asian Pacific Rim to put pressure on North Korea, on Kim Jong-un, to abandon his nuclear ambitions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It has to remain the policy of the United States of America the denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula, plain and simple.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when Donald Trump is president of the United States, we’re — we’re not going to have the — the kind of posture in the world that has Russia invading Crimea and Ukraine, that has the Chinese building new islands in the South China Sea, that has literally the world, including North Korea, flouting American power.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to — we’re going to go back to the days of peace through strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I have to tell you that — that all this talk about tax returns — and I get it, you know, you want to keep bringing that up.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It must have — must have…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Until he…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … done well in some focus group.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But here — Hillary Clinton and her husband set up a private foundation called the Clinton Foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: While she was secretary of state, the Clinton Foundation accepted tens of millions of dollars from foreign governments and foreign donors.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Now, you all need to know out there, this is basic stuff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Foreign donors, and certainly foreign governments, cannot participate in the American political process.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They cannot make financial contributions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the Clintons figured out a way to create a foundation where foreign governments and foreign donors could donate millions of dollars.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then we found, thanks to the good work of the Associated Press, that more than half of her private meetings when she was secretary of state were given to major donors of the Clinton Foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When you talk about all these — all these baseless rumors about Russia and the rest, Hillary Clinton — you asked the trustworthy question at the very beginning — the reason…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … the reason the American people don’t trust Hillary Clinton is because they are looking at the pay to play politics that she operated with the Clinton Foundation through a private server…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … while she’s secretary of state.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And they’re saying enough is enough.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m going to talk about the foundation, and then I’ll talk about North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So, on the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am glad to talk about the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation is one of the highest- rated charities in the world.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It provides AIDS drugs to about 11.5 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It helps Americans deal with opioid overdoses.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It gets higher rankings for its charity than the American Red Cross does.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation does an awful lot of good work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton as secretary of state took no action to benefit the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The State Department did an investigation, and they concluded that everything Hillary Clinton did as secretary of state was completely in the interest of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So the foundation does good work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary Clinton as secretary of state acted in the interests of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But let’s compare this now with the Trump organization and the Trump Foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump organization is an octopus-like organization with tentacles all over the world whose conflict of interests could only be known if Donald Trump would release his tax returns.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s refused to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: His sons have said that the organization has a lot of business dealings in Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And remember, the Trump organization is not a non-profit.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s putting money into Donald Trump’s pockets and into the pockets of his children, whereas the Clinton Foundation is a non-profit and no Clinton family member draws any salary.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Trump Foundation is a non-profit.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: In addition, Donald Trump has a foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The foundation was just fined for illegally contributing foundation dollars to a political campaign of a Florida attorney general.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They made an illegal contribution, and then they tried to hide it by disguising it to somebody else.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the person they donated to was somebody whose office was charged with investigating Trump University.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is the difference between a foundation that does good work and a secretary of state who acted in accord with American interest and somebody who is conflicted and doing work around the world and won’t share with the American public what he’s doing and what those conflicts are.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thank you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Trump Foundation is a private family foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They give virtually every cent in the Trump Foundation to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Political contributions?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Less than ten cents on the dollar in the Clinton Foundation has gone to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A $20,000 portrait of Donald Trump?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Less than 10 cents on the dollar of the Clinton Foundation has gone to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Ninety percent.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It has been a platform for the Clintons to travel the world, to have staff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But honestly, Senator, we would know a lot more about it if Hillary Clinton would just turn over the 33,000 e-mails…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … that she refused to turn over in her private server…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and we’d have a much better picture of what the Clinton Foundation was about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If we — look, a president should take action to defend the United States against imminent threat.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You have to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A president has to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now exactly what action, you would have to determine what your intelligence was, how certain you were of that intelligence, but you would have to take action.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You asked the question about how do we deal with a North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m on the Foreign Relations Committee.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We just did an extensive sanctions package against North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And interestingly enough, Elaine, the U.N.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: followed and did this — virtually the same package.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Often China will use their veto in the Security Council to veto a package like that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They’re starting to get worried about North Korea, too.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So they actually supported the sanctions package, even though many of the sanctions are against Chinese firms, Chinese financial institutions.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So we’re working together with China, and we need to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: China’s another one of those relationships where it’s competitive, it’s also challenging, and in times like North Korea, we have to be able to cooperate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary understands that very well.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went once famously to China and stood up at a human rights meeting and looked them in the eye and said, “Women’s rights are human rights.” They didn’t want her to say that, but she did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But she’s also worked on a lot of diplomatic and important diplomatic deals with China.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what it’s going to take.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The thing I would worry a little bit about is that Donald Trump owes about $650 million to banks, including the Bank of China.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m not sure he could stand up so tough to the people who have loaned him money.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, that’s an easy one for me, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s an easy one.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m really fortunate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I grew up in a wonderful household with great Irish Catholic parents.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My mom and dad are sitting right here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was educated by Jesuits at Rockhurst High School in Kansas City.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My 40th reunion is in 10 days.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I worked with Jesuit missionaries in Honduras, now nearly 35 years ago, and they were the heroes of my life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I try to practice my religion in a very devout way and follow the teachings of my church in my own personal life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I don’t believe in this nation, a First Amendment nation, where we don’t raise any religion over the other, and we allow people to worship as they please, that the doctrines of any one religion should be mandated for everyone.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: For me, the hardest struggle in my faith life was the Catholic Church is against the death penalty and so am I.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I was governor of a state, and the state law said that there was a death penalty for crimes if the jury determined them to be heinous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And so I had to grapple with that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When I was running for governor, I was attacked pretty strongly because of my position on the death penalty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I looked the voters of Virginia in the eye and said, look, this is my religion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m not going to change my religious practice to get one vote, but I know how to take an oath and uphold the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you elect me, I will uphold the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I was elected, and I did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was very, very difficult to allow executions to go forward, but in circumstances where I didn’t feel like there was a case for clemency, I told Virginia voters I would uphold the law, and I did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That was a real struggle.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I think it is really, really important that those of us who have deep faith lives don’t feel like we could just substitute our own views for everybody else in society, regardless of their views.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, it’s a wonderful question.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And my Christian faith is at the very heart of who I am.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I was also raised in a wonderful family of faith.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was a church on Sunday morning and grace before dinner.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But my Christian faith became real for me when I made a personal decision for Christ when I was a freshman in college.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I’ve tried to live that out however imperfectly every day of my life since.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with my wife at my side, we’ve followed a calling into public service, where we’ve — we’ve tried to — we’ve tried to keep faith with the values that we cherish.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with regard to when I struggle, I appreciate, and — and — and — I have a great deal of respect for Senator Kaine’s sincere faith.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s shared.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But for me, I would tell you that for me the sanctity of life proceeds out of the belief that — that ancient principle that — where God says before you were formed in the womb, I knew you, and so for my first time in public life, I sought to stand with great compassion for the sanctity of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The state of Indiana has also sought to make sure that we expand alternatives in health care counseling for women, non-abortion alternatives.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m also very pleased at the fact we’re well on our way in Indiana to becoming the most pro-adoption state in America.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I think if you’re going to be pro-life, you should — you should be pro-adoption.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what I can’t understand is with Hillary Clinton and now Senator Kaine at her side is to support a practice like partial-birth abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, to hold to the view — and I know Senator Kaine, you hold pro-life views personally — but the very idea that a child that is almost born into the world could still have their life taken from them is just anathema to me.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I cannot — I can’t conscience about — about a party that supports that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Or that — I know you’ve historically opposed taxpayer funding of abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton wants to — wants to repeal the longstanding provision in the law where we said we wouldn’t use taxpayer dollars to fund abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So for me, my faith informs my life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I try and spend a little time on my knees every day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But it all for me begins with cherishing the dignity, the worth, the value of every human life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, this is a fundamental question, a fundamental question.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I are both people out of religious backgrounds, from Methodist church experience, which was really formative for her as a public servant.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But we really feel like you should live fully and with enthusiasm the commands of your faith.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But it is not the role of the public servant to mandate that for everybody else.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So let’s talk about abortion and choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let’s talk about them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We support Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We support the constitutional right of American women to consult their own conscience, their own supportive partner, their own minister, but then make their own decision about pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s something we trust American women to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we don’t think that women should be punished, as Donald Trump said they should, for making the decision to have an abortion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence wants to repeal Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said he wants to put it on the ash heap of history.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we have some young people in the audience who weren’t even born when Roe was decided.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is pretty important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Before Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Wade, states could pass criminal laws to do just that, to punish women if they made the choice to terminate a pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think you should live your moral values.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But the last thing, the very last thing that government should do is have laws that would punish women who make reproductive choices.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that is the fundamental difference between a Clinton-Kaine ticket and a Trump-Pence ticket that wants to punish women who make that choice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, it’s really not.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I would never support legislation that punished women who made the heartbreaking choice to end a pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Then why did Donald Trump say that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just never would.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Why did he say that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, it’s — look, he’s not a polished politician like you and Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I would admit that’s not a polished…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You know, things don’t always come out exactly the way he means them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, can I say…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I’m telling you what the policy of our administration would be.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Great line from the — great line from the gospel of Matthew.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: From the fullness of the heart, the mouth speaks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump says women should be punished or Mexicans are rapists and criminals…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m telling you…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … or John McCain is not a hero, he is showing you who he is.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, you’ve whipped out that Mexican thing again.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can you defend it?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are criminal aliens in this country, Tim, who have come into this country illegally who are perpetrating violence and taking American lives.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You want to — you want to use a big broad brush against Mexicans on that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He also said and many of them are good people.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You keep leaving that out of your quote.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if you want me to go there, I’ll go there.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But here’s — there is a choice, and it is a choice on life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I couldn’t be more proud to be standing with Donald Trump, who’s standing for the right to life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s a principle that — Senator Kaine — and I’m very gentle about this, because I really do respect you — it’s a principle that you embrace.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I have appreciated the fact that you’ve supported the Hyde amendment, which bans the use of taxpayer funding for abortion, in the past, but that’s not Hillary Clinton’s view.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: People need to understand, we can come together as a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can create a culture of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: More and more young people today are embracing life because we know we are — we’re better for it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can — like Mother Teresa said at that famous national prayer breakfast…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is important —

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … bring the — let’s welcome the children into our world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are so many families around the country who can’t have children.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We could improve adoption…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … so that families that can’t have children can adopt more readily those children from crisis pregnancies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor, why don’t you trust women to make this choice for themselves?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We can encourage people to support life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Of course we can.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But why don’t you trust women?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Why doesn’t Donald Trump trust women to make this choice for themselves?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what we ought to be doing in public life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Living our lives of faith or motivation with enthusiasm and excitement, convincing each other, dialoguing with each other about important moral issues of the day…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because there are…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … but on fundamental issues of morality, we should let women make their own decisions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because there is — a society can be judged by how it deals with its most vulnerable, the aged, the infirm, the disabled, and the unborn.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I believe it with all my heart.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I couldn’t be more proud to be standing with a pro-life candidate in Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s a really important one.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That may be the $64,000 question, because it has been a divisive campaign.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And again, Hillary is running a campaign about stronger together, and Donald Trump — and this is — this is not directed at this man, except to the extent that he can’t defend Donald Trump — Donald Trump has run a campaign that’s been about one insult after the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But we do have to bring the country together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So here’s what we’ll do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton was first lady, then senator for eight years and secretary of state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I served in the Senate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I’m really amazed, Elaine, as I talk to Republican senators, how well they regard and respect Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was on the Armed Services Committee.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was on other committees.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked across the aisle when she was first lady to get the CHIP program passed so that 8 million low-income kids have health insurance in this country, including 150,000 in Indiana.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked across the aisle after 9/11 to get health benefits for the first responders who bravely went into the towers and into the Pentagon.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked to get benefits for — TRICARE benefits for National Guard members, including Hoosiers and Virginians in the National Guard.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She has a track record of working across the aisle to make things happen.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, you know, Elaine, I have the same track record.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was a governor of Virginia with two Republican houses.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And in the Senate, I have good working relationships across the aisle.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Because I think it’s fine to be a Democrat or Republican or independent, but after Election Day, the goal is work together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary Clinton has a track record of accomplishment across the aisle that will enable her to do just that when we work with the new Congress in January.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thank you, Elaine, and thanks for a great discussion…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Thank you, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: This is a very challenging time in the life of our nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Weakened America’s place in the world after the leadership of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama on the world stage has been followed by an economy that is truly struggling, stifled by an avalanche of more taxes, more regulation, Obamacare, the war on coal, and the kind of trade deals that have put American workers in the back seat.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I think the best way that we can bring people together is through change in Washington, D.C.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You know, I served in Washington, D.C., for 12 years in the Congress of the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I served with many Republicans and Democrats, men and women of goodwill.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The potential is there to really change the direction of this country, but it’s going to take leadership to do it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The American people want to see our nation standing tall on the world stage again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They want to see us supporting our military, rebuilding our military, commanding the respect of the world, and they want to see the American economy off to the races again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They want to see an American comeback.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Donald Trump’s entire career has been about building.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s been about — it’s going through hardship just like a businessperson does and finding a way through smarts and ingenuity and resilience to fight forward and — when Donald Trump becomes president of the United States, we’re going to have a stronger America.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When you hear him say he wants to make America great again, when we do that, I truly do believe the American people are going to be standing taller.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They’re going to see that real change can happen after decades of just talking about it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when that happens, the American people are going to stand tall, stand together, and we’ll have the kind of unity that’s been missing for way too long.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: How are you, Donald?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, thank you, Lester, and thanks to Hofstra for hosting us.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: The central question in this election is really what kind of country we want to be and what kind of future we’ll build together.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Today is my granddaughter’s second birthday, so I think about this a lot.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: First, we have to build an economy that works for everyone, not just those at the top.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That means we need new jobs, good jobs, with rising incomes.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I want us to invest in you.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I want us to invest in your future.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That means jobs in infrastructure, in advanced manufacturing, innovation and technology, clean, renewable energy, and small business, because most of the new jobs will come from small business.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We also have to make the economy fairer.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That starts with raising the national minimum wage and also guarantee, finally, equal pay for women’s work.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I also want to see more companies do profit-sharing.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: If you help create the profits, you should be able to share in them, not just the executives at the top.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I want us to do more to support people who are struggling to balance family and work.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I’ve heard from so many of you about the difficult choices you face and the stresses that you’re under.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So let’s have paid family leave, earned sick days.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Let’s be sure we have affordable child care and debt-free college.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: How are we going to do it?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We’re going to do it by having the wealthy pay their fair share and close the corporate loopholes.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Finally, we tonight are on the stage together, Donald Trump and I.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Donald, it’s good to be with you.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We’re going to have a debate where we are talking about the important issues facing our country.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You have to judge us, who can shoulder the immense, awesome responsibilities of the presidency, who can put into action the plans that will make your life better.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I hope that I will be able to earn your vote on November 8th.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Thank you, Lester.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our jobs are fleeing the country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re going to Mexico.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re going to many other countries.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You look at what China is doing to our country in terms of making our product.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re devaluing their currency, and there’s nobody in our government to fight them.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And we have a very good fight.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And we have a winning fight.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Because they’re using our country as a piggy bank to rebuild China, and many other countries are doing the same thing.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So we’re losing our good jobs, so many of them.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When you look at what’s happening in Mexico, a friend of mine who builds plants said it’s the eighth wonder of the world.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re building some of the biggest plants anywhere in the world, some of the most sophisticated, some of the best plants.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: With the United States, as he said, not so much.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So Ford is leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You see that, their small car division leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Thousands of jobs leaving Michigan, leaving Ohio.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re all leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And we can’t allow it to happen anymore.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: As far as child care is concerned and so many other things, I think Hillary and I agree on that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We probably disagree a little bit as to numbers and amounts and what we’re going to do, but perhaps we’ll be talking about that later.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But we have to stop our jobs from being stolen from us.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have to stop our companies from leaving the United States and, with it, firing all of their people.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: All you have to do is take a look at Carrier air conditioning in Indianapolis.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They left—fired 1,400 people.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re going to Mexico.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So many hundreds and hundreds of companies are doing this.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We cannot let it happen.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Under my plan, I’ll be reducing taxes tremendously, from 35 percent to 15 percent for companies, small and big businesses.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That’s going to be a job creator like we haven’t seen since Ronald Reagan.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s going to be a beautiful thing to watch.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Companies will come.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They will build.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They will expand.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: New companies will start.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I look very, very much forward to doing it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have to renegotiate our trade deals, and we have to stop these countries from stealing our companies and our jobs.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I think that trade is an important issue.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Of course, we are 5 percent of the world’s population; we have to trade with the other 95 percent.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And we need to have smart, fair trade deals.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We also, though, need to have a tax system that rewards work and not just financial transactions.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And the kind of plan that Donald has put forth would be trickle-down economics all over again.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: In fact, it would be the most extreme version, the biggest tax cuts for the top percent of the people in this country than we’ve ever had.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I call it trumped-up trickle-down, because that’s exactly what it would be.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That is not how we grow the economy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We just have a different view about what’s best for growing the economy, how we make investments that will actually produce jobs and rising incomes.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think we come at it from somewhat different perspectives.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I understand that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You know, Donald was very fortunate in his life, and that’s all to his benefit.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He started his business with $14 million, borrowed from his father, and he really believes that the more you help wealthy people, the better off we’ll be and that everything will work out from there.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I don’t buy that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I have a different experience.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: My father was a small-businessman.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He worked really hard.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He printed drapery fabrics on long tables, where he pulled out those fabrics and he went down with a silkscreen and dumped the paint in and took the squeegee and kept going.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And so what I believe is the more we can do for the middle class, the more we can invest in you, your education, your skills, your future, the better we will be off and the better we’ll grow.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That’s the kind of economy I want us to see again.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, for one thing—and before we start on that—my father gave me a very small loan in 1975, and I built it into a company that’s worth many, many billions of dollars, with some of the greatest assets in the world, and I say that only because that’s the kind of thinking that our country needs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our country’s in deep trouble.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We don’t know what we’re doing when it comes to devaluations and all of these countries all over the world, especially China.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re the best, the best ever at it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: What they’re doing to us is a very, very sad thing.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So we have to do that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have to renegotiate our trade deals.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And, Lester, they’re taking our jobs, they’re giving incentives, they’re doing things that, frankly, we don’t do.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Let me give you the example of Mexico.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They have a VAT tax.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We’re on a different system.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When we sell into Mexico, there’s a tax.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When they sell in—automatic, 16 percent, approximately.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When they sell into us, there’s no tax.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s a defective agreement.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s been defective for a long time, many years, but the politicians haven’t done anything about it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Now, in all fairness to Secretary Clinton—yes, is that OK?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I want you to be very happy.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s very important to me.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But in all fairness to Secretary Clinton, when she started talking about this, it was really very recently.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She’s been doing this for 30 years.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And why hasn’t she made the agreements better?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: The NAFTA agreement is defective.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Just because of the tax and many other reasons, but just because of the fact…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Secretary Clinton and others, politicians, should have been doing this for years, not right now, because of the fact that we’ve created a movement.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They should have been doing this for years.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: What’s happened to our jobs and our country and our economy generally is—look, we owe $20 trillion.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We cannot do it any longer, Lester.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, the first thing you do is don’t let the jobs leave.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: The companies are leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I could name, I mean, there are thousands of them.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re leaving, and they’re leaving in bigger numbers than ever.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And what you do is you say, fine, you want to go to Mexico or some other country, good luck.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We wish you a lot of luck.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But if you think you’re going to make your air conditioners or your cars or your cookies or whatever you make and bring them into our country without a tax, you’re wrong.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And once you say you’re going to have to tax them coming in, and our politicians never do this, because they have special interests and the special interests want those companies to leave, because in many cases, they own the companies.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So what I’m saying is, we can stop them from leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have to stop them from leaving.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And that’s a big, big factor.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, let’s stop for a second and remember where we were eight years ago.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We had the worst financial crisis, the Great Recession, the worst since the 1930s.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That was in large part because of tax policies that slashed taxes on the wealthy, failed to invest in the middle class, took their eyes off of Wall Street, and created a perfect storm.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: In fact, Donald was one of the people who rooted for the housing crisis.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He said, back in 2006, “Gee, I hope it does collapse, because then I can go in and buy some and make some money.” Well, it did collapse.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That’s called business, by the way.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Nine million people—nine million people lost their jobs.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Five million people lost their homes.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And $13 trillion in family wealth was wiped out.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Now, we have come back from that abyss.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And it has not been easy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we’re now on the precipice of having a potentially much better economy, but the last thing we need to do is to go back to the policies that failed us in the first place.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Independent experts have looked at what I’ve proposed and looked at what Donald’s proposed, and basically they’ve said this, that if his tax plan, which would blow up the debt by over $5 trillion and would in some instances disadvantage middle-class families compared to the wealthy, were to go into effect, we would lose 3.5 million jobs and maybe have another recession.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: They’ve looked at my plans and they’ve said, OK, if we can do this, and I intend to get it done, we will have 10 million more new jobs, because we will be making investments where we can grow the economy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Take clean energy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Some country is going to be the clean- energy superpower of the 21st century.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Donald thinks that climate change is a hoax perpetrated by the Chinese.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think it’s real.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I do not say that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think science is real.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I do not say that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I think it’s important that we grip this and deal with it, both at home and abroad.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And here’s what we can do.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We can deploy a half a billion more solar panels.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We can have enough clean energy to power every home.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We can build a new modern electric grid.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That’s a lot of jobs; that’s a lot of new economic activity.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So I’ve tried to be very specific about what we can and should do, and I am determined that we’re going to get the economy really moving again, building on the progress we’ve made over the last eight years, but never going back to what got us in trouble in the first place.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She talks about solar panels.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We invested in a solar company, our country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That was a disaster.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They lost plenty of money on that one.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Now, look, I’m a great believer in all forms of energy, but we’re putting a lot of people out of work.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our energy policies are a disaster.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our country is losing so much in terms of energy, in terms of paying off our debt.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You can’t do what you’re looking to do with $20 trillion in debt.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: The Obama administration, from the time they’ve come in, is over 230 years’ worth of debt, and he’s topped it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: He’s doubled it in a course of almost eight years, seven-and-a-half years, to be semi- exact.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So I will tell you this.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have to do a much better job at keeping our jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And we have to do a much better job at giving companies incentives to build new companies or to expand, because they’re not doing it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And all you have to do is look at Michigan and look at Ohio and look at all of these places where so many of their jobs and their companies are just leaving, they’re gone.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And, Hillary, I’d just ask you this.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You’ve been doing this for 30 years.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Why are you just thinking about these solutions right now?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: For 30 years, you’ve been doing it, and now you’re just starting to think of solutions.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, actually…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I will bring—excuse me.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I will bring back jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You can’t bring back jobs.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, actually, I have thought about this quite a bit.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Yeah, for 30 years.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I have—well, not quite that long.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think my husband did a pretty good job in the 1990s.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think a lot about what worked and how we can make it work again…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, he approved NAFTA… [crosstalk]

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … million new jobs, a balanced budget…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: He approved NAFTA, which is the single worst trade deal ever approved in this country.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Incomes went up for everybody.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Manufacturing jobs went up also in the 1990s, if we’re actually going to look at the facts.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: When I was in the Senate, I had a number of trade deals that came before me, and I held them all to the same test.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Will they create jobs in America?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Will they raise incomes in America?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And are they good for our national security?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Some of them I voted for.

sentence: The biggest one, a multinational one known as CAFTA, I voted against.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And because I hold the same standards as I look at all of these trade deals.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But let’s not assume that trade is the only challenge we have in the economy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think it is a part of it, and I’ve said what I’m going to do.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I’m going to have a special prosecutor.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We’re going to enforce the trade deals we have, and we’re going to hold people accountable.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: When I was secretary of state, we actually increased American exports globally 30 percent.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We increased them to China 50 percent.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So I know how to really work to get new jobs and to get exports that helped to create more new jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But you haven’t done it in 30 years or 26 years or any number you want to…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I’ve been a senator, Donald…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You haven’t done it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You haven’t done it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I have been a secretary of state…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I have done a lot…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Your husband signed NAFTA, which was one of the worst things that ever happened to the manufacturing industry.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, that’s your opinion.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That is your opinion.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You go to New England, you go to Ohio, Pennsylvania, you go anywhere you want, Secretary Clinton, and you will see devastation where manufacture is down 30, 40, sometimes 50 percent.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: NAFTA is the worst trade deal maybe ever signed anywhere, but certainly ever signed in this country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And now you want to approve Trans-Pacific Partnership.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You were totally in favor of it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Then you heard what I was saying, how bad it is, and you said, I can’t win that debate.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But you know that if you did win, you would approve that, and that will be almost as bad as NAFTA.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Nothing will ever top NAFTA.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, that is just not accurate.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I was against it once it was finally negotiated and the terms were laid out.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I wrote about that in…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You called it the gold standard.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You called it the gold standard of trade deals.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You said it’s the finest deal you’ve ever seen.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And then you heard what I said about it, and all of a sudden you were against it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, Donald, I know you live in your own reality, but that is not the facts.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: The facts are—I did say I hoped it would be a good deal, but when it was negotiated…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … which I was not responsible for, I concluded it wasn’t.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I wrote about that in my book…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So is it President Obama’s fault?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … before you even announced.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Is it President Obama’s fault?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Look, there are differences…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Secretary, is it President Obama’s fault?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Because he’s pushing it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There are different views about what’s good for our country, our economy, and our leadership in the world.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I think it’s important to look at what we need to do to get the economy going again.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That’s why I said new jobs with rising incomes, investments, not in more tax cuts that would add $5 trillion to the debt.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But you have no plan.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But in—oh, but I do.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Secretary, you have no plan.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: In fact, I have written a book about it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It’s called “Stronger Together.” You can pick it up tomorrow at a bookstore…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That’s about all you’ve… [crosstalk]

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … or at an airport near you.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But it’s because I see this—we need to have strong growth, fair growth, sustained growth.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We also have to look at how we help families balance the responsibilities at home and the responsibilities at business.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we have a very robust set of plans.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And people have looked at both of our plans, have concluded that mine would create 10 million jobs and yours would lose us 3.5 million jobs, and explode the debt which would have a recession.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You are going to approve one of the biggest tax cuts in history.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You are going to approve one of the biggest tax increases in history.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You are going to drive business out.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Your regulations are a disaster, and you’re going to increase regulations all over the place.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And by the way, my tax cut is the biggest since Ronald Reagan.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’m very proud of it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It will create tremendous numbers of new jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But regulations, you are going to regulate these businesses out of existence.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When I go around—Lester, I tell you this, I’ve been all over.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And when I go around, despite the tax cut, the thing—the things that business as in people like the most is the fact that I’m cutting regulation.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You have regulations on top of regulations, and new companies cannot form and old companies are going out of business.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And you want to increase the regulations and make them even worse.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’m going to cut regulations.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’m going to cut taxes big league, and you’re going to raise taxes big league, end of story.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That can’t—that can’t be left to stand.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I kind of assumed that there would be a lot of these charges and claims, and so…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we have taken the home page of my website, HillaryClinton.com, and we’ve turned it into a fact-checker.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So if you want to see in real-time what the facts are, please go and take a look.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Because what I have proposed…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And take a look at mine, also, and you’ll see.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … would not add a penny to the debt, and your plans would add $5 trillion to the debt.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: What I have proposed would cut regulations and streamline them for small businesses.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: What I have proposed would be paid for by raising taxes on the wealthy, because they have made all the gains in the economy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I think it’s time that the wealthy and corporations paid their fair share to support this country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, could I just finish—I think I… [crosstalk]

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I think I should—you go to her website, and you take a look at her website.

sentence: She’s going to raise taxes $1.3 trillion.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And look at her website.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You know what?

sentence: It’s no difference than this.

sentence: She’s telling us how to fight ISIS.

sentence: Just go to her website.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She tells you how to fight ISIS on her website.

sentence: I don’t think General Douglas MacArthur would like that too much.

sentence: You need better relationships between the communities and the police, because in some cases, it’s not good.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But you look at Dallas, where the relationships were really studied, the relationships were really a beautiful thing, and then five police officers were killed one night very violently.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So there’s some bad things going on.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Some really bad things.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But we need—Lester, we need law and order.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And we need law and order in the inner cities, because the people that are most affected by what’s happening are African-American and Hispanic people.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And it’s very unfair to them what our politicians are allowing to happen.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I’ve heard—I’ve heard Donald say this at his rallies, and it’s really unfortunate that he paints such a dire negative picture of black communities in our country.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You know, the vibrancy of the black church, the black businesses that employ so many people, the opportunities that so many families are working to provide for their kids.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There’s a lot that we should be proud of and we should be supporting and lifting up.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But we do always have to make sure we keep people safe.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There are the right ways of doing it, and then there are ways that are ineffective.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Stop-and-frisk was found to be unconstitutional and, in part, because it was ineffective.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It did not do what it needed to do.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Now, I believe in community policing.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And, in fact, violent crime is one-half of what it was in 1991.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Property crime is down 40 percent.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We just don’t want to see it creep back up.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We’ve had 25 years of very good cooperation.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But there were some problems, some unintended consequences.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Too many young African-American and Latino men ended up in jail for nonviolent offenses.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And it’s just a fact that if you’re a young African-American man and you do the same thing as a young white man, you are more likely to be arrested, charged, convicted, and incarcerated.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So we’ve got to address the systemic racism in our criminal justice system.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We cannot just say law and order.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We have to say—we have to come forward with a plan that is going to divert people from the criminal justice system, deal with mandatory minimum sentences, which have put too many people away for too long for doing too little.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We need to have more second chance programs.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I’m glad that we’re ending private prisons in the federal system; I want to see them ended in the state system.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You shouldn’t have a profit motivation to fill prison cells with young Americans.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So there are some positive ways we can work on this.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I believe strongly that commonsense gun safety measures would assist us.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Right now—and this is something Donald has supported, along with the gun lobby—right now, we’ve got too many military-style weapons on the streets.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: In a lot of places, our police are outgunned.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We need comprehensive background checks, and we need to keep guns out of the hands of those who will do harm.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And we finally need to pass a prohibition on anyone who’s on the terrorist watch list from being able to buy a gun in our country.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: If you’re too dangerous to fly, you are too dangerous to buy a gun.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So there are things we can do, and we ought to do it in a bipartisan way.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Lester, I think implicit bias is a problem for everyone, not just police.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think, unfortunately, too many of us in our great country jump to conclusions about each other.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And therefore, I think we need all of us to be asking hard questions about, you know, why am I feeling this way?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But when it comes to policing, since it can have literally fatal consequences, I have said, in my first budget, we would put money into that budget to help us deal with implicit bias by retraining a lot of our police officers.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I’ve met with a group of very distinguished, experienced police chiefs a few weeks ago.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: They admit it’s an issue.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: They’ve got a lot of concerns.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Mental health is one of the biggest concerns, because now police are having to handle a lot of really difficult mental health problems on the street.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: They want support, they want more training, they want more assistance.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I think the federal government could be in a position where we would offer and provide that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’d like to respond to that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: First of all, I agree, and a lot of people even within my own party want to give certain rights to people on watch lists and no-fly lists.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I agree with you.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: When a person is on a watch list or a no-fly list, and I have the endorsement of the NRA, which I’m very proud of.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: These are very, very good people, and they’re protecting the Second Amendment.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But I think we have to look very strongly at no-fly lists and watch lists.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And when people are on there, even if they shouldn’t be on there, we’ll help them, we’ll help them legally, we’ll help them get off.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But I tend to agree with that quite strongly.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I do want to bring up the fact that you were the one that brought up the words super-predator about young black youth.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And that’s a term that I think was a—it’s—it’s been horribly met, as you know.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I think you’ve apologized for it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But I think it was a terrible thing to say.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And when it comes to stop-and-frisk, you know, you’re talking about taking guns away.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, I’m talking about taking guns away from gangs and people that use them.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I don’t think—I really don’t think you disagree with me on this, if you want to know the truth.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I think maybe there’s a political reason why you can’t say it, but I really don’t believe—in New York City, stop-and-frisk, we had 2,200 murders, and stop-and-frisk brought it down to 500 murders.

sentence: Five hundred murders is a lot of murders.

sentence: It’s hard to believe, 500 is like supposed to be good?

sentence: But we went from 2,200 to 500.

sentence: And it was continued on by Mayor Bloomberg.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And it was terminated by current mayor.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But stop-and-frisk had a tremendous impact on the safety of New York City.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Tremendous beyond belief.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So when you say it has no impact, it really did.

sentence: It had a very, very big impact.

sentence: Well, it’s also fair to say, if we’re going to talk about mayors, that under the current mayor, crime has continued to drop, including murders.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So there is…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You’re wrong.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: No, I’m not.

sentence: Murders are up.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You check it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: New York—New York has done an excellent job.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I give credit—I give credit across the board going back two mayors, two police chiefs, because it has worked.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And other communities need to come together to do what will work, as well.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Look, one murder is too many.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But it is important that we learn about what has been effective.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And not go to things that sound good that really did not have the kind of impact that we would want.

sentence: Who disagrees with keeping neighborhoods safe?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But let’s also add, no one should disagree about respecting the rights of young men who live in those neighborhoods.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And so we need to do a better job of working, again, with the communities, faith communities, business communities, as well as the police to try to deal with this problem.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’d like to just respond, if I might.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’d just like to respond.

sentence: Look, the African-American community has been let down by our politicians.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They talk good around election time, like right now, and after the election, they said, see ya later, I’ll see you in four years.

sentence: They’ve been abused and used in order to get votes by Democrat politicians, because that’s what it is.

sentence: Well, I—I do think…

sentence: And I will tell you, you look at the inner cities—and I just left Detroit, and I just left Philadelphia, and I just—you know, you’ve seen me, I’ve been all over the place.

sentence: And I’ve met some of the greatest people I’ll ever meet within these communities.

sentence: I think—I think—I think Donald just criticized me for preparing for this debate.

sentence: And, yes, I did.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And you know what else I prepared for?

sentence: Sidney Blumenthal works for the campaign and close—very close friend of Secretary Clinton.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And her campaign manager, Patti Doyle, went to—during the campaign, her campaign against President Obama, fought very hard.

sentence: And you can go look it up, and you can check it out.

sentence: And if you look at CNN this past week, Patti Solis Doyle was on Wolf Blitzer saying that this happened.

sentence: Blumenthal sent McClatchy, highly respected reporter at McClatchy, to Kenya to find out about it.

sentence: And I’ll tell you why I’m satisfied with it.

sentence: Because I want to get on to defeating ISIS, because I want to get on to creating jobs, because I want to get on to having a strong border, because I want to get on to things that are very important to me and that are very important to the country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Well, nobody was pressing it, nobody was caring much about it.

sentence: I figured you’d ask the question tonight, of course.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But nobody was caring much about it.

sentence: And I think I did a good job.

sentence: Secretary Clinton also fought it.

sentence: I mean, you know—now, everybody in mainstream is going to say, oh, that’s not true.

sentence: Well, it was very—I say nothing.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I say nothing.
