sentence: And I think I did a great job and a great service not only for the country, but even for the president, in getting him to produce his birth certificate.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, just listen to what you heard.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: [laughter] And clearly, as Donald just admitted, he knew he was going to stand on this debate stage, and Lester Holt was going to be asking us questions, so he tried to put the whole racist birther lie to bed.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But it can’t be dismissed that easily.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He has really started his political activity based on this racist lie that our first black president was not an American citizen.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There was absolutely no evidence for it, but he persisted, he persisted year after year, because some of his supporters, people that he was trying to bring into his fold, apparently believed it or wanted to believe it.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But, remember, Donald started his career back in 1973 being sued by the Justice Department for racial discrimination because he would not rent apartments in one of his developments to African-Americans, and he made sure that the people who worked for him understood that was the policy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He actually was sued twice by the Justice Department.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So he has a long record of engaging in racist behavior.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And the birther lie was a very hurtful one.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You know, Barack Obama is a man of great dignity.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I could tell how much it bothered him and annoyed him that this was being touted and used against him.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But I like to remember what Michelle Obama said in her amazing speech at our Democratic National Convention: When they go low, we go high.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And Barack Obama went high, despite Donald Trump’s best efforts to bring him down.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I would love to respond.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: First of all, I got to watch in preparing for this some of your debates against Barack Obama.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You treated him with terrible disrespect.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I watched the way you talk now about how lovely everything is and how wonderful you are.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It doesn’t work that way.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You were after him, you were trying to—you even sent out or your campaign sent out pictures of him in a certain garb, very famous pictures.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I don’t think you can deny that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But just last week, your campaign manager said it was true.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So when you tried to act holier than thou, it really doesn’t work.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It really doesn’t.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Now, as far as the lawsuit, yes, when I was very young, I went into my father’s company, had a real estate company in Brooklyn and Queens, and we, along with many, many other companies throughout the country—it was a federal lawsuit—were sued.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We settled the suit with zero—with no admission of guilt.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It was very easy to do.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But they sued many people.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I notice you bring that up a lot.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And, you know, I also notice the very nasty commercials that you do on me in so many different ways, which I don’t do on you.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Maybe I’m trying to save the money.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But, frankly, I look—I look at that, and I say, isn’t that amazing?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Because I settled that lawsuit with no admission of guilt, but that was a lawsuit brought against many real estate firms, and it’s just one of those things.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’ll go one step further.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: In Palm Beach, Florida, tough community, a brilliant community, a wealthy community, probably the wealthiest community there is in the world, I opened a club, and really got great credit for it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: No discrimination against African-Americans, against Muslims, against anybody.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And it’s a tremendously successful club.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I’m so glad I did it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I have been given great credit for what I did.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I’m very, very proud of it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And that’s the way I feel.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That is the true way I feel.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I think cyber security, cyber warfare will be one of the biggest challenges facing the next president, because clearly we’re facing at this point two different kinds of adversaries.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There are the independent hacking groups that do it mostly for commercial reasons to try to steal information that they then can use to make money.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But increasingly, we are seeing cyber attacks coming from states, organs of states.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: The most recent and troubling of these has been Russia.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: There’s no doubt now that Russia has used cyber attacks against all kinds of organizations in our country, and I am deeply concerned about this.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I know Donald’s very praiseworthy of Vladimir Putin, but Putin is playing a really… [crosstalk] …tough, long game here.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And one of the things he’s done is to let loose cyber attackers to hack into government files, to hack into personal files, hack into the Democratic National Committee.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And we recently have learned that, you know, that this is one of their preferred methods of trying to wreak havoc and collect information.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We need to make it very clear—whether it’s Russia, China, Iran or anybody else—the United States has much greater capacity.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And we are not going to sit idly by and permit state actors to go after our information, our private-sector information or our public-sector information.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And we’re going to have to make it clear that we don’t want to use the kinds of tools that we have.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: We don’t want to engage in a different kind of warfare.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But we will defend the citizens of this country.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And the Russians need to understand that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think they’ve been treating it as almost a probing, how far would we go, how much would we do.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And that’s why I was so—I was so shocked when Donald publicly invited Putin to hack into Americans.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That is just unacceptable.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It’s one of the reasons why 50 national security officials who served in Republican information—in administrations…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … have said that Donald is unfit to be the commander- in-chief.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It’s comments like that that really worry people who understand the threats that we face.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I do want to say that I was just endorsed—and more are coming next week—it will be over 200 admirals, many of them here—admirals and generals endorsed me to lead this country.

sentence: And I’m very proud of it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I was just endorsed by ICE.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So when Secretary Clinton talks about this, I mean, I’ll take the admirals and I’ll take the generals any day over the political hacks that I see that have led our country so brilliantly over the last 10 years with their knowledge.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Because look at the mess that we’re in.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Look at the mess that we’re in.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: As far as the cyber, I agree to parts of what Secretary Clinton said.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We should be better than anybody else, and perhaps we’re not.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I don’t think anybody knows it was Russia that broke into the DNC.

sentence: She’s saying Russia, Russia, Russia, but I don’t—maybe it was.

sentence: I mean, it could be Russia, but it could also be China.

sentence: We learned that Bernie Sanders was taken advantage of by your people, by Debbie Wasserman Schultz.

sentence: Look what happened to her.

sentence: But Bernie Sanders was taken advantage of.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That’s what we learned.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Now, whether that was Russia, whether that was China, whether it was another country, we don’t know, because the truth is, under President Obama we’ve lost control of things that we used to have control over.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We came in with the Internet, we came up with the Internet, and I think Secretary Clinton and myself would agree very much, when you look at what ISIS is doing with the Internet, they’re beating us at our own game.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: So we have to get very, very tough on cyber and cyber warfare.

sentence: It is—it is a huge problem.

sentence: I have a son.

sentence: He’s 10 years old.

sentence: But that’s true throughout our whole governmental society.

sentence: We have so many things that we have to do better, Lester, and certainly cyber is one of them.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I think there are a number of issues that we should be addressing.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I have put forth a plan to defeat ISIS.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It does involve going after them online.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: I think we need to do much more with our tech companies to prevent ISIS and their operatives from being able to use the Internet to radicalize, even direct people in our country and Europe and elsewhere.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But we also have to intensify our air strikes against ISIS and eventually support our Arab and Kurdish partners to be able to actually take out ISIS in Raqqa, end their claim of being a Caliphate.

sentence: We’re making progress.

sentence: And we’re hoping that within the year we’ll be able to push ISIS out of Iraq and then, you know, really squeeze them in Syria.

sentence: But we have to be cognizant of the fact that they’ve had foreign fighters coming to volunteer for them, foreign money, foreign weapons, so we have to make this the top priority.

sentence: And I would also do everything possible to take out their leadership.

sentence: I was involved in a number of efforts to take out Al Qaida leadership when I was secretary of state, including, of course, taking out bin Laden.

sentence: And I think we need to go after Baghdadi, as well, make that one of our organizing principles.

sentence: Because we’ve got to defeat ISIS, and we’ve got to do everything we can to disrupt their propaganda efforts online.

sentence: And now they have the oil all over the place, including the oil—a lot of the oil in Libya, which was another one of her disasters.

sentence: He actually advocated for the actions we took in Libya and urged that Gadhafi be taken out, after actually doing some business with him one time.

sentence: But the larger point—and he says this constantly—is George W.

sentence: Bush made the agreement about when American troops would leave Iraq, not Barack Obama.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And the only way that American troops could have stayed in Iraq is to get an agreement from the then-Iraqi government that would have protected our troops, and the Iraqi government would not give that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But let’s talk about the question you asked, Lester.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: The question you asked is, what do we do here in the United States?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: That’s the most important part of this.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: How do we prevent attacks?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: How do we protect our people?

sentence: And I think we’ve got to have an intelligence surge, where we are looking for every scrap of information.

sentence: I was so proud of law enforcement in New York, in Minnesota, in New Jersey.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You know, they responded so quickly, so professionally to the attacks that occurred by Rahami.

sentence: And they brought him down.

sentence: Thank you, Bob.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And thank you for agreeing to moderate this debate this evening.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you to Lynn University for welcoming us here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, it’s good to be with you again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We were together at a humorous event a little earlier, and it’s nice to maybe funny this time, not on purpose.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll see what happens.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is obviously an area of great concern to the entire world, and to America in particular, which is to see a — a complete change in the — the structure and the — the environment in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With the Arab Spring, came a great deal of hope that there would be a change towards more moderation, and opportunity for greater participation on the part of women in public life, and in economic life in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But instead, we’ve seen in nation after nation, a number of disturbing events.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course we see in Syria, 30,000 civilians having been killed by the military there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We see in — in Libya, an attack apparently by, I think we know now, by terrorists of some kind against — against our people there, four people dead.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our hearts and — and minds go out to them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Mali has been taken over, the northern part of Mali by al-Qaeda type individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have in — in Egypt, a Muslim Brotherhood president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so what we’re seeing is a pretty dramatic reversal in the kind of hopes we had for that region.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course the greatest threat of all is Iran, four years closer to a nuclear weapon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and we’re going to have to recognize that we have to do as the president has done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I congratulate him on — on taking out Osama bin Laden and going after the leadership in al-Qaeda.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we can’t kill our way out of this mess.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to have to put in place a very comprehensive and robust strategy to help the — the world of Islam and other parts of the world, reject this radical violent extremism, which is — it’s certainly not on the run.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s certainly not hiding.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a group that is now involved in 10 or 12 countries, and it presents an enormous threat to our friends, to the world, to America, long term, and we must have a comprehensive strategy to help reject this kind of extremism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, my first job as commander in chief, Bob, is to keep the American people safe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what we’ve done over the last four years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We ended the war in Iraq, refocused our attention on those who actually killed us on 9/11.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, Al Qaeda’s core leadership has been decimated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In addition, we’re now able to transition out of Afghanistan in a responsible way, making sure that Afghans take responsibility for their own security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that allows us also to rebuild alliances and make friends around the world to combat future threats.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now with respect to Libya, as I indicated in the last debate, when we received that phone call, I immediately made sure that, number one, that we did everything we could to secure those Americans who were still in harm’s way; number two, that we would investigate exactly what happened, and number three, most importantly, that we would go after those who killed Americans and we would bring them to justice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly what we’re going to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think it’s important to step back and think about what happened in Libya.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Keep in mind that I and Americans took leadership in organizing an international coalition that made sure that we were able to, without putting troops on the ground at the cost of less than what we spent in two weeks in Iraq, liberate a country that had been under the yoke of dictatorship for 40 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Got rid of a despot who had killed Americans and as a consequence, despite this tragedy, you had tens of thousands of Libyans after the events in Benghazi marching and saying America is our friend.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We stand with them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now that represents the opportunity we have to take advantage of.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, Governor Romney, I’m glad that you agree that we have been successful in going after Al Qaida, but I have to tell you that, you know, your strategy previously has been one that has been all over the map and is not designed to keep Americans safe or to build on the opportunities that exist in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, my strategy is pretty straightforward, which is to go after the bad guys, to make sure we do our very best to interrupt them, to — to kill them, to take them out of the picture.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But my strategy is broader than that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s — that’s important, of course.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the key that we’re going to have to pursue is a — is a pathway to get the Muslim world to be able to reject extremism on its own.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t want another Iraq, we don’t want another Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right course for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The right course for us is to make sure that we go after the — the people who are leaders of these various anti-American groups and these — these jihadists, but also help the Muslim world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And how do we do that?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A group of Arab scholars came together, organized by the U.N., to look at how we can help the — the world reject these — these terrorists.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer they came up with was this: One, more economic development.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should key our foreign aid, our direct foreign investment, and that of our friends, we should coordinate it to make sure that we — we push back and give them more economic development.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, better education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, gender equality.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number four, the rule of law.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to help these nations create civil societies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But what’s been happening over the last couple of years is, as we’ve watched this tumult in the Middle East, this rising tide of chaos occur, you see Al Qaida rushing in, you see other jihadist groups rushing in.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and they’re throughout many nations in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s wonderful that Libya seems to be making some progress, despite this terrible tragedy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But next door, of course, we have Egypt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Libya’s 6 million population; Egypt, 80 million population.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want — we want to make sure that we’re seeing progress throughout the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With Mali now having North Mali taken over by Al Qaida; with Syria having Assad continuing to — to kill, to murder his own people, this is a region in tumult.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, of course, Iran on the path to a nuclear weapon, we’ve got real (inaudible).

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, I’m glad that you recognize that Al Qaida is a threat, because a few months ago when you were asked what’s the biggest geopolitical threat facing America, you said Russia, not Al Qaida; you said Russia, in the 1980s, they’re now calling to ask for their foreign policy back because, you know, the Cold War’s been over for 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But Governor, when it comes to our foreign policy, you seem to want to import the foreign policies of the 1980s, just like the social policies of the 1950s and the economic policies of the 1920s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You say that you’re not interested in duplicating what happened in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But just a few weeks ago, you said you think we should have more troops in Iraq right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the — the challenge we have — I know you haven’t been in a position to actually execute foreign policy — but every time you’ve offered an opinion, you’ve been wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said we should have gone into Iraq, despite that fact that there were no weapons of mass destruction.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that we should still have troops in Iraq to this day.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You indicated that we shouldn’t be passing nuclear treaties with Russia despite the fact that 71 senators, Democrats and Republicans, voted for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that, first, we should not have a timeline in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Then you said we should.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now you say maybe or it depends, which means not only were you wrong, but you were also confusing in sending mixed messages both to our troops and our allies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, what — what we need to do with respect to the Middle East is strong, steady leadership, not wrong and reckless leadership that is all over the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And unfortunately, that’s the kind of opinions that you’ve offered throughout this campaign, and it is not a recipe for American strength, or keeping America safe over the long haul.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, of course I don’t concur with what the president said about my own record and the things that I’ve said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t happen to be accurate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But — but I can say this, that we’re talking about the Middle East and how to help the Middle East reject the kind of terrorism we’re seeing, and the rising tide of tumult and — and confusion.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and attacking me is not an agenda.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Attacking me is not talking about how we’re going to deal with the challenges that exist in the Middle East, and take advantage of the opportunity there, and stem the tide of this violence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’ll respond to a couple of things that you mentioned.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, Russia I indicated is a geopolitical foe.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a geopolitical foe, and I said in the same — in the same paragraph I said, and Iran is the greatest national security threat we face.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Russia does continue to battle us in the U.N.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: time and time again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have clear eyes on this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not going to wear rose-colored glasses when it comes to Russia, or Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’m certainly not going to say to him, I’ll give you more flexibility after the election.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: After the election, he’ll get more backbone.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, with regards to Iraq, you and I agreed I believe that there should be a status of forces agreement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Oh you didn’t?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You didn’t want a status of…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I would not have had done was left 10,000 troops in Iraq that would tie us down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that certainly would not help us in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sorry, you actually — there was a — there was an effort on the part of the president to have a status of forces agreement, and I concurred in that, and said that we should have some number of troops that stayed on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was something I concurred with… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …that your posture.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was my posture as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You thought it should have been 5,000 troops… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … I thought there should have been more troops, but you know what?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The answer was we got… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … no troops through whatsoever.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This was just a few weeks ago that you indicated that we should still have troops in Iraq.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, I… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I’m sorry that’s a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …that’s a — I indicated… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …major speech.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I indicated that you failed to put in place a status… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …of forces agreement at the end of the conflict that existed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor — here — here’s — here’s one thing… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …here’s one thing I’ve learned as commander in chief.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve got to be clear, both to our allies and our enemies, about where you stand and what you mean.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You just gave a speech a few weeks ago in which you said we should still have troops in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not a recipe for making sure that we are taking advantage of the opportunities and meeting the challenges of the Middle East.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it is absolutely true that we cannot just meet these challenges militarily.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what I’ve done throughout my presidency and will continue to do is, number one, make sure that these countries are supporting our counterterrorism efforts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, make sure that they are standing by our interests in Israel’s security, because it is a true friend and our greatest ally in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number three, we do have to make sure that we’re protecting religious minorities and women because these countries can’t develop unless all the population, not just half of it, is developing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number four, we do have to develop their economic — their economic capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But number five, the other thing that we have to do is recognize that we can’t continue to do nation building in these regions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of American leadership is making sure that we’re doing nation building here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will help us maintain the kind of American leadership that we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we’ve done is organize the international community, saying Assad has to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve mobilized sanctions against that government.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have made sure that they are isolated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have provided humanitarian assistance and we are helping the opposition organize, and we’re particularly interested in making sure that we’re mobilizing the moderate forces inside of Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But ultimately, Syrians are going to have to determine their own future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so everything we’re doing, we’re doing in consultation with our partners in the region, including Israel which obviously has a huge interest in seeing what happens in Syria; coordinating with Turkey and other countries in the region that have a great interest in this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This — what we’re seeing taking place in Syria is heartbreaking, and that’s why we are going to do everything we can to make sure that we are helping the opposition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we also have to recognize that, you know, for us to get more entangled militarily in Syria is a serious step, and we have to do so making absolutely certain that we know who we are helping; that we’re not putting arms in the hands of folks who eventually could turn them against us or allies in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I am confident that Assad’s days are numbered.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what we can’t do is to simply suggest that, as Governor Romney at times has suggested, that giving heavy weapons, for example, to the Syrian opposition is a simple proposition that would lead us to be safer over the long term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s step back and talk about what’s happening in Syria and how important it is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, 30,000 people being killed by their government is a humanitarian disaster.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Secondly, Syria is an opportunity for us because Syria plays an important role in the Middle East, particularly right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Syria is Iran’s only ally in the Arab world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s their route to the sea.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s the route for them to arm Hezbollah in Lebanon, which threatens, of course, our ally, Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so seeing Syria remove Assad is a very high priority for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, seeing a — a replacement government being responsible people is critical for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, we don’t want to have military involvement there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t want to get drawn into a military conflict.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so the right course for us, is working through our partners and with our own resources, to identify responsible parties within Syria, organize them, bring them together in a — in a form of — if not government, a form of — of — of council that can take the lead in Syria.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then make sure they have the arms necessary to defend themselves.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We do need to make sure that they don’t have arms that get into the — the wrong hands.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those arms could be used to hurt us down the road.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to make sure as well that we coordinate this effort with our allies, and particularly with — with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the Saudi’s and the Qatari, and — and the Turks are all very concerned about this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re willing to work with us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have a very effective leadership effort in Syria, making sure that the — the insurgent there are armed and that the insurgents that become armed, are people who will be the responsible parties.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Recognize — I believe that Assad must go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe he will go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I believe — we want to make sure that we have the relationships of friendship with the people that take his place, steps that in the years to come we see Syria as a — as a friend, and Syria as a responsible party in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — this is a critical opportunity for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what I’m afraid of is we’ve watched over the past year or so, first the president saying, well we’ll let the U.N.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: deal with it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And Assad — excuse me, Kofi Annan came in and said we’re going to try to have a ceasefire.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That didn’t work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then it went to the Russians and said, let’s see if you can do something.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should be playing the leadership role there, not on the ground with military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …by the leadership role.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are playing the leadership role.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We organized the Friends of Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are mobilizing humanitarian support, and support for the opposition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are making sure that those we help are those who will be friends of ours in the long term and friends of our allies in the region over the long term.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But going back to Libya — because this is an example of how we make choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When we went in to Libya, and we were able to immediately stop the massacre there, because of the unique circumstances and the coalition that we had helped to organize.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We also had to make sure that Moammar Gadhafi didn’t stay there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And to the governor’s credit, you supported us going into Libya and the coalition that we organized.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it came time to making sure that Gadhafi did not stay in power, that he was captured, Governor, your suggestion was that this was mission creep, that this was mission muddle.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Imagine if we had pulled out at that point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, Moammar Gadhafi had more American blood on his hands than any individual other than Osama bin Laden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we were going to make sure that we finished the job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of the reason why the Libyans stand with us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we did so in a careful, thoughtful way, making certain that we knew who we were dealing with, that those forces of moderation on the ground were ones that we could work with, and we have to take the same kind of steady, thoughtful leadership when it comes to Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what we’re doing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to have our military involved in Syria.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think there is a necessity to put our military in Syria at this stage.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t anticipate that in the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As I indicated, our objectives are to replace Assad and to have in place a new government which is friendly to us, a responsible government, if possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I want to make sure they get armed and they have the arms necessary to defend themselves, but also to remove — to remove Assad.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I do not want to see a military involvement on the part of our — of our troops.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And this isn’t — this isn’t going to be necessary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we have, with our partners in the region, we have sufficient resources to support those groups.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But look, this has been going on for a year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a time — this should have been a time for American leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should have taken a leading role, not militarily, but a leading role organizationally, governmentally to bring together the parties; to find responsible parties.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As you hear from intelligence sources even today, the — the insurgents are highly disparate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They haven’t come together.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They haven’t formed a unity group, a council of some kind.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That needs to happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America can help that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we need to make sure they have the arms they need to carry out the very important role which is getting rid of Assad.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I’ll — I’ll be very quick.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What you just heard Governor Romney said is he doesn’t have different ideas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s because we’re doing exactly what we should be doing to try to promote a moderate Syrian leadership and a — an effective transition so that we get Assad out.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ve shown.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ll continue to show.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I don’t, because I think that America has to stand with democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The notion that we would have tanks run over those young people who were in Tahrir Square, that is not the kind of American leadership that John F.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Kennedy talked about 50 years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is that now that you have a democratically elected government in Egypt, that they have to make sure that they take responsibility for protecting religious minorities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have put significant pressure on them to make sure they’re doing that; to recognize the rights of women, which is critical throughout the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These countries can’t develop if young women are not given the kind of education that they need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They have to abide by their treaty with Israel.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is a red line for us, because not only is Israel’s security at stake, but our security is at stake if that unravels.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They have to make sure that they’re cooperating with us when it comes to counterterrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we will help them with respect to developing their own economy, because ultimately what’s going to make the Egyptian revolution successful for the people of Egypt, but also for the world, is if those young people who gathered there are seeing opportunities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their aspirations are similar to young people’s here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They want jobs, they want to be able to make sure their kids are going to a good school.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They want to make sure that they have a roof over their heads and that they have the prospects of a better life in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so one of the things that we’ve been doing is, is, for example, organizing entrepreneurship conferences with these Egyptians to give them a sense of how they can start rebuilding their economy in a way that’s noncorrupt, that’s transparent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what is also important for us to understand is, is that for America to be successful in this region there’s some things that we’re going to have to do here at home as well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, one of the challenges over the last decade is we’ve done experiments in nation building in places like Iraq and Afghanistan and we’ve neglected, for example, developing our own economy, our own energy sectors, our own education system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s very hard for us to project leadership around the world when we’re not doing what we need to do…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe, as the president indicated, and said at the time that I supported his — his action there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I felt that — I wish we’d have had a better vision of the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I wish that, looking back at the beginning of the president’s term and even further back than that, that we’d have recognized that there was a growing energy and passion for freedom in that part of the world, and that we would have worked more aggressively with our friend and with other friends in the region to have them make the transition towards a more representative form of government, such that it didn’t explode in the way that it did.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But once it exploded, I felt the same as the president did, which is these freedom voices and the streets of Egypt, where the people who were speaking of our principles and the President Mubarak had done things which were unimaginable and the idea of him crushing his people was not something that we could possibly support.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me step back and talk about what I think our mission has to be in the Middle East and even more broadly, because our purpose is to make sure the world is more — is peaceful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want a peaceful planet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want people to be able to enjoy their lives and know they’re going to have a bright and prosperous future, not be at war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s our purpose.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the mantle of leadership for the — promoting the principles of peace has fallen to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t ask for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s an honor that we have it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But for us to be able to promote those principles of peace requires us to be strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that begins with a strong economy here at home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Unfortunately, the economy is not stronger.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When the — when the president of Iraq — excuse me, of Iran, Ahmadinejad, says that our debt makes us not a great country, that’s a frightening thing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Former chief of the — Joint Chiefs of Staff said that — Admiral Mullen said that our debt is the biggest national security threat we face.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — we have weakened our economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need a strong economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have as well a strong military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our military is second to none in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re blessed with terrific soldiers, and extraordinary technology and intelligence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the idea of a trillion dollar in cuts through sequestration and budget cuts to the military would change that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have strong allies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our association and connection with our allies is essential to America’s strength.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re the great nation that has allies, 42 allies and friends around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, finally, we have to stand by our principles.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we’re strong in each of those things, American influence will grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But unfortunately, in nowhere in the world is America’s influence will grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But unfortunately, in — nowhere in the world is America’s influence greater today than it was four years ago.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s because we’ve become weaker in each of those four… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well I — I absolutely believe that America has a — a responsibility, and the privilege of helping defend freedom and promote the principles that — that make the world more peaceful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And those principles include human rights, human dignity, free enterprise, freedom of expression, elections.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because when there are elections, people tend to vote for peace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t vote for war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So we want to promote those principles around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We recognize that there are places of conflict in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want to end those conflicts to the extent humanly possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But in order to be able to fulfill our role in the world, America must be strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America must lead.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And for that to happen, we have to strengthen our economy here at home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have 23 million people struggling to get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have an economy that over the last three years keeps slowing down its growth rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have kids coming out of college, half of them can’t find a job today, or a job that’s commensurate with their college degree.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to get our economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And our military, we’ve got to strengthen our military long-term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t know what the world is going to throw at us down the road.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we make decisions today in the military that — that will confront challenges we can’t imagine.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the 2000 debates, there was no mention of terrorism, for instance.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And a year later, 9/11 happened.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So, we have to make decisions based upon uncertainty, and that means a strong military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not cut our military budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to also stand by our allies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I think the tension that existed between Israel and the United States was very unfortunate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think also that pulling our missile defense program out of Poland in the way we did was also unfortunate in terms of, if you will, disrupting the relationship in some ways that existed between us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then, of course, with regards to standing for our principles, when — when the students took to the streets in Tehran and the people there protested, the Green Revolution occurred, for the president to be silent I thought was an enormous mistake.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to stand for our principles, stand for our allies, stand for a strong military and stand for a stronger economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: America remains the one indispensable nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the world needs a strong America, and it is stronger now than when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because we ended the war in Iraq, we were able to refocus our attention on not only the terrorist threat, but also beginning a transition process in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It also allowed us to refocus on alliances and relationships that had been neglected for a decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney, our alliances have never been stronger, in Asia, in Europe, in Africa, with Israel, where we have unprecedented military and intelligence cooperation, including dealing with the Iranian threat.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what we also have been able to do is position ourselves so we can start rebuilding America, and that’s what my plan does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that we’re bringing manufacturing back to our shores so that we’re creating jobs here, as we’ve done with the auto industry, not rewarding companies that are shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that we’ve got the best education system in the world, including retraining our workers for the jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Doing everything we can to control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve cut our oil imports to the lowest level in two decades because we’ve developed oil and natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we also have to develop clean energy technologies that will allow us to cut our exports in half by 2020.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership that we need to show.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got to make sure that we reduce our deficit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Unfortunately, Governor Romney’s plan doesn’t do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to do it in a responsible way by cutting out spending we don’t need, but also asking the wealthiest to pay a little bit more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That way we can invest in the research and technology that’s always kept us at the cutting edge.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has taken a different approach throughout this campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Both at home and abroad, he has proposed wrong and reckless policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He’s praised George Bush as a good economic steward and Dick Cheney as somebody who’s — who shows great wisdom and judgment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And taking us back to those kinds of strategies that got us into this mess are not the way that we are going to maintain leadership in the 21st century.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve got a policy for the future and agenda for the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And when it comes to our economy here at home, I know what it takes to create 12 million new jobs and rising take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what we’ve seen over the last four years is something I don’t want to see over the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president said by now we’d be a 5.4 percent unemployment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re 9 million jobs short of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will get America working again and see rising take-home pay again, and I’ll do it with five simple steps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number one, we are going to have North American energy independence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to do it by taking full advantage of oil, coal, gas, nuclear and our renewables.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, we’re going to increase our trade.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Trade grows about 12 percent year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It doubles about every — every five or so years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can do better than that, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The opportunities for us in Latin America we have just not taken advantage of fully.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, Latin America’s economy is almost as big as the economy of China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re all focused on China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Latin America is a huge opportunity for us — time zone, language opportunities.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, we’re going to have to have training programs that work for our workers and schools that finally put the parents and the teachers and the kids first, and the teachers’ unions going to have to go behind.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then we’re going to have to get to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can’t expect entrepreneurs and businesses large and small to take their life savings or their company’s money and invest in America if they think we’re headed to the road to Greece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s where we’re going right now unless we finally get off this spending and borrowing binge.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ll get us on track to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, number five, we’ve got to champion small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Small business is where jobs come from.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Two-thirds of our jobs come from small businesses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: New business formation is down to the lowest level in 30 years under this administration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to bring it back and get back good jobs and rising take-home pay.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, let’s talk about what we need to compete.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, Governor Romney talks about small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, Governor, when you were in Massachusetts, small businesses development ranked about 48th, I think out of 50 states in Massachusetts, because the policies that you are promoting actually don’t help small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the way you define small businesses includes folks at the very top.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they include you and me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not the kind of small business promotion we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But let’s take an example that we know is going to make a difference in the 21st century and that’s our education policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We didn’t have a lot of chance to talk about this in the last debate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, under my leadership, what we’ve done is reformed education, working with governors, 46 states.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve seen progress and gains in schools that were having a terrible time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they’re starting to finally make progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I now want to do is to hire more teachers, especially in math and science, because we know that we’ve fallen behind when it comes to math and science.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And those teachers can make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney, when you were asked by teachers whether or not this would help the economy grow, you said this isn’t going to help the economy grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When you were asked about reduced class sizes, you said class sizes don’t make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I tell you, if you talk to teachers, they will tell you it does make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’ve got math teachers who are able to provide the kind of support that they need for our kids, that’s what’s going to determine whether or not the new businesses are created here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Companies are going to locate here depending on whether we’ve got the most highly skilled workforce.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the kinds of budget proposals that you’ve put forward, when we don’t ask either you or me to pay a dime more in terms of reducing the deficit, but instead we slash support for education, that’s undermining our long-term competitiveness.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not good for America’s position in the world, and the world notices.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well — well, I need to speak a moment…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … if you’ll let me, Bob, just about education…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … because I’m — I’m so proud of the state that I had the chance to be governor of.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have every two years tests that look at how well our kids are doing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fourth graders and eighth graders are tested in English and math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: While I was governor, I was proud that our fourth graders came out number one of all 50 states in English, and then also in math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And our eighth graders number one in English and also in math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First time one state had been number one in all four measures.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How did we do that?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, Republicans and Democrats came together on a bipartisan basis to put in place education principles that focused on having great teachers in the classroom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ten years earlier…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that was — that was — that was what allowed us to become the number one state in the nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But that was 10 years before you took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then you cut education spending when you came into office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The first — the first — the first — and we kept our schools number one in the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re still number one today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the principles that we put in place, we also gave kids not just a graduation exam that determined whether they were up to the skills needed to — to be able compete, but also if they graduated the quarter of their class, they got a four-year tuition- free ride at any Massachusetts public institution of higher learning.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That happened before you came into office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was actually mine, actually, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You got that fact wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s come back and talk about the military, but all the way — all the way through.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, I’m going through from the very beginning — we’re going to cut about 5 percent of the discretionary budget, excluding military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He should have answered the first question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, Governor Romney’s called for $5 trillion of tax cuts that he says he’s going to pay for by closing deductions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the math doesn’t work, but he continues to claim that he’s going to do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He then wants to spend another $2 trillion on military spending that our military is not asking for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, keep in mind that our military spending has gone up every single year that I’ve been in office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We spend more on our military than the next 10 countries combined; China, Russia, France, the United Kingdom, you name it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The next 10.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I did was work with our joint chiefs of staff to think about, what are we going to need in the future to make sure that we are safe?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the budget that we’ve put forward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, what you can’t do is spend $2 trillion in additional military spending that the military is not asking for, $5 trillion on tax cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You say that you’re going to pay for it by closing loopholes and deductions, without naming what those loopholes and deductions are.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then somehow you’re also going to deal with the deficit that we’ve already got.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The math simply doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it comes to our military, what we have to think about is not, you know just budgets, we’ve got to think about capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to be thinking about cyber security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to be talking about space.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what our budget does, but it’s driven by strategy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not driven by politics.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not driven by members of Congress, and what they would like to see.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s driven by, what are we going to need to keep the American people safe?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what our budget does, and it also then allows us to reduce our deficit, which is a significant national security concern.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because we’ve got to make sure that our economy is strong at home so that we can project military power overseas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m pleased that I’ve balanced budgets.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was on the world of business for 25 years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you didn’t balance your budget, you went out of business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I went into the Olympics that was out of balance, and we got it on balance, and made a success there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I had the chance to be governor of a state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Four years in a row, Democrats and Republicans came together to balance the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We cut taxes 19 times and balanced our budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president hasn’t balanced a budget yet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I expect to have the opportunity to do so myself.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to be able to balance the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s talk about military spending, and that’s this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our Navy is old — excuse me, our Navy is smaller now than at any time since 1917.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The Navy said they needed 313 ships to carry out their mission.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re now at under 285.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re headed down to the low 200s if we go through a sequestration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s unacceptable to me.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure that we have the ships that are required by our Navy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our Air Force is older and smaller than at any time since it was founded in 1947.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve changed for the first time since FDR — since FDR we had the — we’ve always had the strategy of saying we could fight in two conflicts at once.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now we’re changing to one conflict.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, this, in my view, is the highest responsibility of the President of the United States, which is to maintain the safety of the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not cut our military budget by a trillion dollars, which is a combination of the budget cuts the president has, as well as the sequestration cuts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That, in my view, is making — is making our future less certain and less secure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, I just need to comment on this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, the sequester is not something that I’ve proposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is something that Congress has proposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It will not happen.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The budget that we are talking about is not reducing our military spending.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is maintaining it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think Governor Romney maybe hasn’t spent enough time looking at how our military works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You mentioned the Navy, for example, and that we have fewer ships than we did in 1916.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Governor, we also have fewer horses and bayonets, because the nature of our military’s changed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have these things called aircraft carriers, where planes land on them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have these ships that go underwater, nuclear submarines.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question is not a game of Battleship, where we’re counting slips.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s what are our capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so when I sit down with the Secretary of the Navy and the Joint Chiefs of Staff, we determine how are we going to be best able to meet all of our defense needs in a way that also keeps faith with our troops, that also makes sure that our veterans have the kind of support that they need when they come home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is not reflected in the kind of budget that you’re putting forward because it just doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, we visited the website quite a bit and it still doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, Israel is a true friend.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is our greatest ally in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if Israel is attacked, America will stand with Israel.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve made that clear throughout my presidency.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will stand with Israel if they are attacked.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is the reason why, working with Israel, we have created the strongest military and intelligence cooperation between our two countries in history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, this week we’ll be carrying out the largest military exercise with Israel in history, this very week.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But to the issue of Iran, as long as I’m president of the United States Iran will not get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I made that clear when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We then organized the strongest coalition and the strongest sanctions against Iran in history, and it is crippling their economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their currency has dropped 80 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their oil production has plunged to the lowest level since they were fighting a war with Iraq 20 years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So their economy is in a shambles.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason we did this is because a nuclear Iran is a threat to our national security, and it is a threat to Israel’s national security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We cannot afford to have a nuclear arms race in the most volatile region of the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran is a state sponsor of terrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And for them to be able to provide nuclear technology to non-state actors, that’s unacceptable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they have said that they want to see Israel wiped off the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So the work that we’ve done with respect to sanctions now offers Iran a choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They can take the diplomatic route and end their nuclear program or they will have to face a united world and a United States president, me, who said we’re not going to take any options off the table.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The disagreement I have with Governor Romney is that, during the course of this campaign, he’s often talked as if we should take premature military action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that would be a mistake, because when I’ve sent young men and women into harm’s way, I always understand that that is the last resort, not the first resort.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, I want to underscore the same point the president mad,e which is that if I’m President of the United States, when I’m President of the United States, we will stand with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if Israel is attacked, we have their back, not just diplomatically, not just culturally, but militarily.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, with regards to Iran and the threat of Iran, there’s no question but that a nuclear Iran, a nuclear-capable Iran is unacceptable to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It presents a threat not only to our friends but ultimately a threat to us to have Iran have nuclear material, nuclear weapons that could be used against us or used to be threatening to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It is also essential for us to understand what our mission is in Iran, and that is to dissuade Iran from having a nuclear weapon through peaceful and diplomatic means.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And crippling sanctions are something I called for five years ago, when I was in Israel, speaking at the Herzliya Conference.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I laid out seven steps, crippling sanctions were number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they do work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re seeing it right now in the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely the right thing to do, to have crippling sanctions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would have put them in place earlier.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s good that we have them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, something I would add today is I would tighten those sanctions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would say that ships that carry Iranian oil, can’t come into our ports.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I imagine the E.U.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: would agree with us as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not only ships couldn’t, but I’d say companies that are moving their oil can’t, people who are trading in their oil can’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would tighten those sanctions further.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Secondly, I’d take on diplomatic isolation efforts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d make sure that Ahmadinejad is indicted under the Genocide Convention.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: His words amount to genocide incitation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would indict him for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would also make sure that their diplomats are treated like the pariah they are around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The same way we treated the apartheid diplomats of South Africa.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to increase pressure time, and time again on Iran because anything other than a — a — a solution to this, which says — which stops this — this nuclear folly of theirs, is unacceptable to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And of course, a military action is the last resort.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It is something one would only – only consider if all of the other avenues had been — had been tried to their full extent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all those are reports in the newspaper.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But our goal is to get Iran to recognize it needs to give up its nuclear program and abide by the U.N.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: resolutions that have been in place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because they have the opportunity to reenter the community of nations, and we would welcome that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There — there are people in Iran who have the same aspirations as people all around the world for a better life.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we hope that their leadership takes the right decision, but the deal we’ll accept is they end their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s very straightforward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m glad that Governor Romney agrees with the steps that we’re taking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, there have been times, Governor, frankly, during the course of this campaign, where it sounded like you thought that you’d do the same things we did, but you’d say them louder and somehow that — that would make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it turns out that the work involved in setting up these crippling sanctions is painstaking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s meticulous.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We started from the day we got into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason is was so important — and this is a testament to how we’ve restored American credibility and strength around the world — is we had to make sure that all the countries participated, even countries like Russia and China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because if it’s just us that are imposing sanctions — we’ve had sanctions in place a long time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s because we got everybody to agree that Iran is seeing so much pressure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got to maintain that pressure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There is a deal to be had, and that is that they abide by the rules that have already been established.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They convince the international community they are not pursuing a nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are inspections that are very intrusive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But over time, what they can do is regain credibility.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the meantime, though, we’re not going to let up the pressure until we have clear evidence that that takes place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one last thing — just — just to make this point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The clock is ticking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re not going to allow Iran to perpetually engage in negotiations that lead nowhere.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve been very clear to them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, because of the intelligence coordination that we do with a range of countries, including Israel, we have a sense of when they would get breakout capacity, which means that we would not be able to intervene in time to stop their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that clock is ticking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re going to make sure that if they do not meet the demands of the international community, then we are going to take all options necessary to make sure they don’t have a nuclear weapon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think from the very beginning, one of the challenges we’ve had with Iran is that they have looked at this administration, and felt that the administration was not as strong as it needed to be.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they saw weakness where they had expected to find American strength.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I say that because from the very beginning, the president in his campaign four years ago, said he would meet with all the world’s worst actors in his first year, he’d sit down with Chavez and Kim Jong-il, with Castro and President Ahmadinejad of Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think they looked and thought, well, that’s an unusual honor to receive from the President of the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then the president began what I have called an apology tour, of going to various nations in the Middle East and criticizing America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they looked at that and saw weakness.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then when there were dissidents in the streets of Tehran, a Green Revolution, holding signs saying, is America with us, the president was silent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they noticed that as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think that when the president said he was going to create daylight between ourselves and Israel, that they noticed that as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: All of these things suggested, I think, to the Iranian mullahs that, hey, you know, we can keep on pushing along here, we can keep talks going on, we’re just going to keep on spinning centrifuges.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now there are some 10,000 centrifuges spinning uranium, preparing to create a nuclear threat to the United States and to the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s unacceptable for us, and it’s essential for a president to show strength from the very beginning, to make it very clear what is acceptable and not acceptable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And an Iranian nuclear program is not acceptable to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They must not develop nuclear capability.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the way to make sure they understand that is by having, from the very beginning, the tightest sanctions possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They need to be tightened.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our diplomatic isolation needs to be tougher.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to indict Ahmadinejad.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to put the pressure on them as hard as we possibly can, because if we do that, we won’t have to take the military action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, let me just respond.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nothing Governor Romney just said is true, starting with this notion of me apologizing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This has been probably the biggest whopper that’s been told during the course of this campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And every fact checker and every reporter who’s looked at it, Governor, has said this is not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to tightening sanctions, look, as I said before, we’ve put in the toughest, most crippling sanctions ever.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact is, while we were coordinating an international coalition to make sure these sanctions were effective, you were still invested in a Chinese state oil company that was doing business with the Iranian oil sector.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So I’ll let the American people decide, judge, who’s going to be more effective and more credible when it comes to imposing crippling sanctions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And with respect to our attitude about the Iranian revolution, I was very clear about the murderous activities that had taken place and that was contrary to international law and everything that civilized people stand for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and so the strength that we have shown in Iran is shown by the fact that we’ve been able to mobilize the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I came into office, the world was divided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran was resurgent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran is at its weakest point, economically, strategically, militarily, then since — then in many years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are going to continue to keep the pressure on to make sure that they do not get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s in America’s national interest and that will be the case so long as I’m president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re four years closer to a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re four years closer to a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and — we should not have wasted these four years to the extent they — they continue to be able to spin these centrifuges and get that much closer.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, the reason I call it an apology tour is because you went to the Middle East and you flew to Egypt and to Saudi Arabia and to Turkey and Iraq.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, you skipped Israel, our closest friend in the region, but you went to the other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, they noticed that you skipped Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then in those nations, and on Arabic TV, you said that America had been dismissive and derisive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said that on occasion America had dictated to other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, America has not dictated to other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have freed other nations from dictators.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, let me — let me respond.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we’re going to talk about trips that we’ve taken — when I was a candidate for office, first trip I took was to visit our troops.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when I went to Israel as a candidate, I didn’t take donors.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I didn’t attend fundraisers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I went to Yad Beshef (ph), the Holocaust museum there, to remind myself the nature of evil and why our bond with Israel will be unbreakable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then I went down to the border towns of Storok (ph), which had experienced missiles raining dowm from Hamas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I saw families there who showed me there where missiles had come down near their children’s bedrooms.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I was reminded of what that would mean if those were my kids.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Which is why as president, we funded an Iron Dome program to stop those missiles.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So that’s how I’ve used my travels, when I travel to Israel and when I travel to the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the — the central question at this point is going to be: Who is going to be credible to all parties involved?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they can look at my track record, whether it’s Iran sanctions, whether it’s dealing with counterterrorism, whether it’s supporting democracy, whether it’s supporting women’s rights, whether it’s supporting religious minorities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they can say that the President of the United States and the United States of America has stood on the right side of history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that kind of credibility is precisely why we’ve been able to show leadership on a wide range of issues facing the world right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Bob, let’s not go into hypotheticals of that nature.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our relationship with Israel, my relationship with the prime minister of Israel is such that we would not get a call saying our bombers are on the way, or their fighters are on the way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the kind of thing that would have been discussed and thoroughly evaluated well before that kind of — (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me — let me come back — we can come back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s come back to what the president was speaking about, which is what’s happening in the world and the president’s statement that things are going so well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I look at what’s happening around the world, and i see Iran four years closer to a bomb.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see the Middle East with a rising tide of violence, chaos, tumult.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see jihadists continuing to spread, whether they’re rising or just about the same level, hard to precisely measure, but it’s clear they’re there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re very strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
