sentence: I see Syria with 30,000 civilians dead, Assad still in power.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see our trade deficit with China, larger than it’s — growing larger every year, as a matter of fact.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I look around the world and I don’t feel that you see North Korea, continuing to export their nuclear technology, Russia said they’re not going to follow Nunn-Lugar any more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re back away from a nuclear proliferation treaty that we had with them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I look around the world, I don’t see our influence growing around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see our influence receding, in part because of the failure of the president to deal with our economic challenges at home; in part because of our withdrawal from our commitment to our military in the way I think it ought to be; in part because of the — the — the turmoil with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean, the president received a letter from 38 Democrat senators saying the tensions with Israel were a real problem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They asked him, please repair the tension — Democrat senators — please repair the tension…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … the damage in his — in his own party.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, the problem is, is that on a whole range of issues, whether it’s the Middle East, whether it’s Afghanistan, whether it’s Iraq, whether it’s now Iran, you’ve been all over the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, I’m — I’m pleased that you now are endorsing our policy of applying diplomatic pressure and potentially having bilateral discussions with the Iranians to end their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But just a few years ago you said that’s something you’d never do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the same way that you initially opposed a timetable in Afghanistan, now you’re for it, although it depends.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the same way that you say you would have ended the war in Iraq, but recently gave a speech saying that we should have 20,000 more folks in there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The same way that you said that it was mission creep to go after Gadhafi.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to going after Osama bin Laden, you said, well, any president would make that call.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when you were a candidate in 2008, as I was, and I said if I got bin Laden in our sights I would take that shot, you said we shouldn’t move heaven and earth to get one man.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you said we should ask Pakistan for permission.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we had asked Pakistan permission, we would not have gotten him.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it was worth moving heaven and earth to get him.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, after we killed bin Laden I was at ground zero for a memorial and talked to a young women who was four years old when 9/11 happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the last conversation she had with her father was him calling from the twin towers, saying “Peyton (ph), I love you and I will always watch over you.” And for the next decade, she was haunted by that conversation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she said to me, “You know, by finally getting bin Laden, that brought some closure to me.” And when we do things like that — when we bring those who have harmed us to justice, that sends a message to the world and it tells Peyton (ph) that we did not forget her father.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I make that point because that’s the kind of clarity of leadership, and those decisions are not always popular.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those decisions generally — generally are not poll-tested.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And even some in my own party, including my current vice president, had the same critique as you did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what the American people understand is that I look at what we need to get done to keep the American people safe and to move our interests forward, and I make those decisions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t — but you can’t have the president just lay out a whole series of items without giving me a chance to respond.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, that’s probably true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, we’re going to be finished by 2014, and when I’m president, we’ll make sure we bring our troops out by the end of 2014.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The commanders and the generals there are on track to do so.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve seen progress over the past several years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The surge has been successful and the training program is proceeding apace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are now a large number of Afghan Security Forces, 350,000 that are ready to step in to provide security and we’re going to be able to make that transition by the end of 2014.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So our troops will come home at that point.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can tell you at the same time, that we will make sure that we look at what’s happening in Pakistan, and recognize that what’s happening in Pakistan is going to have a major impact on the success in Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I say that because I know a lot of people that feel like we should just brush our hands and walk away.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I don’t mean you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, but some people in the — in our nation feel that Pakistan is being nice to us, and that we should walk away fro mthem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But Pakistan is important to the region, to the world and to us, because Pakistan has 100 nuclear warheads and they’re rushing to build a lot more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ll have more than Great Britain sometime in the — in the relatively near future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They also have the Haqqani Network and the Taliban existent within their country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so a Pakistan that falls apart, becomes a failed state, would be of extraordinary danger to Afghanistan and to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we’re going to have to remain helpful in encouraging Pakistan to move towards a more stable government and rebuild the relationship with us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that means that our aid that we provide to Pakistan is going to have to be conditioned upon certain benchmarks being met.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So for me, I look at this as both a need to help move Pakistan in the right direction, and also to get Afghanistan to be ready, and they will be ready by the end of 2014.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I came into office, we were still bogged down in Iraq and Afghanistan had been drifting for a decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We ended the war in Iraq, refocused our attention on Afghanistan, and we did deliver a surge of troops.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That was facilitated in part because we had ended the war in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are now in a position where we have met many of the objectives that got us there in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of what had happened is we’d forgotten why we had gone.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We went because there were people who were responsible for 3,000 American deaths.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we decimated Al Qaida’s core leadership in the border regions between Afghanistan and Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We then started to build up Afghan forces.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re now in a position where we can transition out, because there’s no reason why Americans should die when Afghans are perfectly capable of defending their own country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, that transition has to take place in a responsible fashion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve been there a long time, and we’ve got to make sure that we and our coalition partners are pulling out responsibly and giving Afghans the capabilities that they need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I think the American people recognize is after a decade of war it’s time to do some nation building here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we can now do is free up some resources, to, for example, put Americans back to work, especially our veterans, rebuilding our roads, our bridges, our schools, making sure that, you know, our veterans are getting the care that they need when it comes to post-traumatic stress disorder and traumatic brain injury, making sure that the certifications that they need for good jobs of the future are in place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, I was having lunch with some — a veteran in Minnesota who had been a medic dealing with the most extreme circumstances.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When he came home and he wanted to become a nurse, he had to start from scratch.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we’ve said is let’s change those certifications.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The first lady has done great work with an organization called Joining Forces putting our veterans back to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, veterans’ unemployment is actually now lower than general population.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It was higher when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So those are the kinds of things that we can now do because we’re making that transition in Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, it’s not time to divorce a nation on Earth that has 100 nuclear weapons and is on the way to double that at some point, a nation that has serious threats from terrorist groups within its nation, as I indicated before, the Taliban, Haqqani Network.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a nation that’s not like — like others and it does not have a civilian leadership that is calling the shots there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You have the ISI, their intelligence organization, is probably the most powerful of the — of three branches there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then you have the military and then you have the civilian government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a nation, which, if it falls apart, if it — if it becomes a failed state, there are nuclear weapons there and you’ve got — you’ve got terrorists there who could grab their — their hands onto those nuclear weapons.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is — this is an important part of the world for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Pakistan is — is technically an ally, and they’re not acting very much like an ally right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we have some work to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — I don’t blame the administration for the fact that the relationship with Pakistan is strained.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we had to go into Pakistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We had to go in there to get Osama bin Laden.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was the right thing to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and that upset them, but obviously there was a great deal of anger even before that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we’re going to have to work with the — with the people in Pakistan to try and help them move to a more responsible course than the one that they’re on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s important for them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s important for the nuclear weapons.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s important for the success of Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because inside Pakistan, you have a — a large group of Pashtun that are — that are Taliban.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re going to come rushing back in to Afghanistan when we go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s one of the reasons the Afghan Security Forces have so much work to do to be able to fight against that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s important for us to recognize that we can’t just walk away from Pakistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we do need to make sure that as we — as we send support for them, that this is tied to them making progress on — on matters that would lead them to becoming a civil society.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well I believe we should use any and all means necessary to take out people who pose a threat to us and our friends around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s widely reported that drones are being used in drone strikes, and I support that and entirely, and feel the president was right to up the usage of that technology, and believe that we should continue to use it, to continue to go after the people that represent a threat to this nation and to our friends.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me also note that as I said earlier, we’re going to have to do more than just going after leaders and — and killing bad guys, important as that is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re also going to have to have a farm more effective and comprehensive strategy to help move the world away from terror and Islamic extremism.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We haven’t done that yet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We talk a lot about these things, but you look at the — the record, you look at the record.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You look at the record of the last four years and say is Iran closer to a bomb?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is the Middle East in tumult?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is — is al-Qaida on the run, on its heels?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is — are Israel and the Palestinians closer to reaching a peace agreement?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, they haven’t had talks in two years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have not seen the progress we need to have, and I’m convinced that with strong leadership and an effort to build a strategy based upon helping these nations reject extremism, we can see the kind of peace and prosperity the world demands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, keep in mind our strategy wasn’t just going after bin Laden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created partnerships throughout the region to deal with extremism in Somalia, in Yemen, in Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we’ve also done is engaged these governments in the kind of reforms that are actually going to make a difference in people’s lives day to day, to make sure that their governments aren’t corrupt, to make sure that they’re treating women with the kind of respect and dignity that every nation that succeeds has shown and to make sure that they’ve got a free market system that works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So across the board, we are engaging them in building capacity in these countries.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have stood on the side of democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One thing I think Americans should be proud of, when Tunisians began to protest, this nation — me, my administration — stood with them earlier than just about any country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In Egypt we stood on the side of democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In Libya we stood on the side of the people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, there’s no doubt that attitudes about Americans have changed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there are always going to be elements in these countries that potentially threaten the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we want to shrink those groups and those networks and we can do that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’re always also going to have to maintain vigilance when it comes to terrorist activities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The truth, though, is that Al Qaeda is much weaker than it was when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they don’t have the same capacities to attack the U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: homeland and our allies as they did four years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it will continue to be terrorist networks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have to remain vigilant, as I just said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But with respect to China, China is both an adversary, but also a potential partner in the international community if it’s following the rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So my attitude coming into office was that we are going to insist that China plays by the same rules as everybody else.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I know Americans had seen jobs being shipped overseas; businesses and workers not getting a level playing field when it came to trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the reason why I set up a trade task force to go after cheaters when it came to international trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the reason why we have brought more cases against China for violating trade rules than the other — the previous administration had done in two terms.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve won just about every case that we’ve filed, that has been decided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, just recently steelworkers in Ohio and throughout the Midwest — Pennsylvania — are in a position now to sell steel to China because we won that case.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We had a tire case in which they were flooding us with cheap domestic tires — or — or cheap Chinese tires.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we put a stop to it and as a consequence saved jobs throughout America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I have to say that Governor Romney criticized me for being too tough in that tire case; said this wouldn’t be good for American workers and that it would be protectionist.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I tell you, those workers don’t feel that way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They feel as if they had finally an administration who was going to take this issue seriously.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the long term, in order for us to compete with China, we’ve also got to make sure, though, that we’re taking — taking care of business here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we don’t have the best education system in the world, if we don’t continue to put money into research and technology that will allow us to create great businesses here in the United States, that’s how we lose the competition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, unfortunately, Governor Romney’s budget and his proposals would not allow us to make those investments.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, it’s not government that makes business successful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not government investments that makes businesses grow and hire people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me also note that the greatest threat that the world faces, the greatest national security threat is a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s talk about China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China has an interest that’s very much like ours in one respect, and that is they want a stable world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want to see protectionism.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want to see the world break out into — into various forms of chaos, because they have to — they have to manufacture goods and put people to work and they have about 20,000 — 20 million, rather, people coming out of the farms every year coming into the cities, needing jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So they want the economy to work and the world to be free and open.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we can be a partner with China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to be an adversary in any way, shape or form.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can work with them, we can collaborate with them, if they’re willing to be responsible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, they look at us and say, Is it a good idea to be with America?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How strong are we going to be?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How strong is our economy?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at the fact that we owe ’em a trillion dollars and owe other people $16 trillion in total, including that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at our — our decision to — to cut back on our military capabilities.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A trillion dollars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The secretary of defense called these trillion dollars of cuts to our military devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not my term, it’s the president’s own secretary of defense called these trillion dollars of cuts to our military devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not my term, it’s the president’s own Secretary of Defense, called them devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at America’s commitments around the world and they see what’s happening, and they say, well, OK.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is America going to be strong?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer is, yes, if I’m president, America will be very strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll also make sure that we have trade relations with China that work for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve watched year in and year out as companies have shut down and people have lost their jobs because China has not played by the same rules, in part by holding down artificially the value of their currency.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It holds down the prices of their goods.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It means our goods aren’t as competitive and we lose jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s got to end.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re making some progress; they need to make more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why on day one, i will label them a currency manipulator, which allows us to apply tariffs where they’re taking jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re stealing our intellectual property, our patents, our designs, our technology, hacking into our computers, counterfeiting our goods.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They have to understand we want to trade with them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want a world that’s stable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We like free enterprise, but you got to play by the rules.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, they sell us about this much stuff every year, and we sell them about this much stuff every year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So it’s pretty clear who doesn’t want a trade war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there’s one going on right now, which we don’t know about it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a silent one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they’re winning.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have enormous trade imbalance with China, and it’s worse this year than last year, and it’s worse last year than the year before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we have to understand that we can’t just surrender and lose jobs year in and year out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to say to our friend in China, look, you guys are playing aggressively.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We understand it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But this can’t keep on going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t keep on holding down the value of your currency, stealing our intellectual property, counterfeiting our products, selling them around the world, even to the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was with one company that makes valves and — and process industries and they said, look, we were — we were having some valves coming in that — that were broken and we had to repair them under warranty and we looked them and — and they had our serial number on them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then we noticed that there was more than one with that same serial number.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They were counterfeit products being made overseas with the same serial number as a U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: company, the same packaging, these were being sold into our market and around the world as if they were made by the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This can’t go on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want a great relationship with China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China can be our partner, but — but that doesn’t mean they can just roll all over us and steal our jobs on an unfair basis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Governor Romney’s right, you are familiar with jobs being shipped overseas because you invested in companies that were shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, that’s — you’re right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean that’s how our free market works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I’ve made a different bet on American workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we had taken your advice Governor Romney about our auto industry, we’d be buying cars from China instead of selling cars to China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we take your advice with respect to how we change our tax codes so that companies that earn profits overseas don’t pay U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: taxes compared to companies here that are paying taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now that’s estimated to create 800,000 jobs, the problem is they won’t be here, they’ll be in places like China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’re not making investments in education and basic research, which is not something that the private sector is doing at a sufficient pace right now and has never done, then we will lose the (inaudible) in things like clean energy technology.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now with respect to what we’ve done with China already, U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: exports have doubled since I came into office, to China and actually currencies are at their most advantageous point for U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: exporters since 1993.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We absolutely have to make more progress and that’s why we’re going to keep on pressing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to our military and Chinese security, part of the reason that we were able to pivot to the Asia-Pacific region after having ended the war in Iraq and transitioning out of Afghanistan, is precisely because this is going to be a massive growth area in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we believe China can be a partner, but we’re also sending a very clear signal that America is a Pacific power; that we are going to have a presence there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are working with countries in the region to make sure, for example, that ships can pass through; that commerce continues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re organizing trade relations with countries other than China so that China starts feeling more pressure about meeting basic international standards.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ve shown in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership that we’ll continue to show.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I just want to take one of those points, again, attacking me as not talking about an agenda for — for getting more trade and opening up more jobs in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the president mentioned the auto industry and that somehow I would be in favor of jobs being elsewhere.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Nothing could be further from the truth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m a son of Detroit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was born in Detroit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My dad was head of a car company.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I like American cars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I would do nothing to hurt the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: auto industry.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan to get the industry on its feet when it was in real trouble was not to start writing checks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was President Bush that wrote the first checks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I disagree with that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I said they need — these companies need to go through a managed bankruptcy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And in that process, they can get government help and government guarantees, but they need to go through bankruptcy to get rid of excess cost and the debt burden that they’d — they’d built up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And fortunately… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, that’s not what you said… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, you did not…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can take a look at the op-ed… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You did not say that you would provide government help.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I said that we would provide guarantees, and — and that was what was able to allow these companies to go through bankruptcy, to come out of bankruptcy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Under no circumstances would I do anything other than to help this industry get on its feet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the idea that has been suggested that I would liquidate the industry, of course not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course not.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s check the record.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the height of silliness… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let — let — let’s… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have never said I would liquidate… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I would liquidate the industry.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, the people in Detroit don’t forget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …and — and that’s why I have the kind of commitment to ensure that our industries in this country can compete and be successful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We in this country can — can compete successfully with anyone in the world, and we’re going to.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to have to have a president, however, that doesn’t think that somehow the government investing in — in car companies like Tesla and — and Fisker, making electric battery cars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is not research, Mr President, these are the government investing in companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Investing in Solyndra.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a company, this isn’t basic research.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I want to invest in research.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Research is great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Providing funding to universities and think tanks is great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But investing in companies?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Absolutely not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the wrong way to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The fact of the matter is… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m still speaking.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I want to make sure that we make — we make America more competitive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that we do those things that make America the most attractive place in the world for entrepreneurs, innovators, businesses to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you’re investing in companies doesn’t do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In fact it makes it less likely for them to come here…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …because the private sector’s not going to invest in a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m — I’m — I’m happy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …company… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …to respond to you… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …if — if you’re… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …you’ve had the floor for a while.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …get someone else’s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — look, I think anybody out there can check the record.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, you keep on trying to, you know airbrush history here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You were very clear that you would not provide, government assistance to the U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: auto companies, even if they went through bankruptcy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that they could get it in the private marketplace.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That wasn’t true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They would have gone through a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re wrong… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …they would have gone through a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …you’re wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I am not wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I am not wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People can look it up, you’re right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People will look it up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But more importantly it is true that in order for us to be competitive, we’re going to have to make some smart choices right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Cutting our education budget, that’s not a smart choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will not help us compete with China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Cutting our investments in research and technology, that’s not a smart choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will not help us compete with China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bringing down our deficit by adding $7 trillion of tax cuts and military spending that our military is not asking for, before we even get to the debt that we currently have, that is not going to make us more competitive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those are the kinds of choices that the American people face right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Having a tax code that rewards companies that are shipping jobs overseas instead of companies that are investing here in the United States, that will not make us more competitive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the one thing that I’m absolutely clear about is that after a decade in which we saw drift, jobs being shipped overseas, nobody championing American workers and American businesses, we’ve now begun to make some real progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we can’t do is go back to the same policies that got us into such difficulty in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we have to move forward and not go back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I couldn’t agree more about going forward, but I certainly don’t want to go back to the policies of the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The policies of the last four years have seen incomes in America decline every year for middle income families, now down $4,300 during your term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Twenty-three million Americans still struggling to find a good job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you came to office 32 million people on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Today, 47 million people on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you came to office, just over $10 trillion in debt, now $16 trillion in debt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It hasn’t worked.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said by now we’d be at 5.4 percent unemployment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re 9 million jobs short of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve met some of those people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve met them in Appleton, Wisconsin.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I met a young woman in — in Philadelphia who’s coming out of — out of college, can’t find work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve been — Ann was with someone just the other day that was just weeping about not being able to get work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s just a tragedy in a nation so prosperous as ours, that the last four years have been so hard.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s why it’s so critical, that we make America once again the most attractive place in the world to start businesses, to build jobs, to grow the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s not going to happen by just hiring teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I love to — I love teachers, and I’m happy to have states and communities that want to hire teachers do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: By the way, I don’t like to have the federal government start pushing its weight deeper and deeper into our schools.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let the states and localities do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was a governor.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The federal government didn’t hire our teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: UNIDENTIFIED

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I love teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I want to get our private sector growing and I know how to do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, thank you very much, Bob, Governor Romney, and to Lynn University.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve now heard three debates, months of campaigning and way too many TV commercials.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now you’ve got a choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last four years we’ve made real progress digging our way out of policies that gave us two prolonged wars, record deficits and the worst economic crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney wants to take us back to those policies, a foreign policy that’s wrong and reckless, economic policies that won’t create jobs, won’t reduce our deficit, but will make sure that folks at the very top don’t have to play by the same rules that you do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve got a different vision for America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to build on our strengths.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve put forward a plan to make sure that we’re bringing manufacturing jobs back to our shores by rewarding companies and small businesses that are investing here, not overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to make sure we’ve got the best education system in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re retaining our workers for the jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to control our own energy by developing oil and natural gas but also the energy sources of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Yes, I want to reduce our deficit by cutting spending that we don’t need but also by asking the wealthy to do a little bit more so that we can invest in things like research and technology that are the key to a 21st century economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: As Commander in Chief, I will maintain the strongest military in the world, keep faith with our troops and go after those who would do us harm.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: but after a decade of war, I think we all recognize we’ve got to do some nation building here at home, rebuilding our roads, our bridges and especially caring for our Veterans who sacrificed so much for our freedom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve been through tough times but we always bounce back because of our character, because we pull together and if I have the privilege of being your president for another four years, I promise you I will always listen to your voices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will fight for your families and I will work every single day to make sure that America continues to be the greatest nation on earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, folks at Lynn University, good to be with you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m optimistic about the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m excited about our prospects as a nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to see peace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to see growing peace in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s our objective.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have an opportunity to have real leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America’s going to have that kind of leadership and continue to promote principles of peace to make a world a safer place and make people in this country more confident that their future is secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I also want to make sure that we get this economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there are two very different paths the country can take.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One is a path represented by the president, which at the end of four years would mean we’d have $20 trillion in debt heading towards Greece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get us on track to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path will mean continuing declining in take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure our take-home pay turns around and starts to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path will mean continuing declining in take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure take-home pay turns around and starts to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path means 20 million people out of work struggling for a good job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get people back to work with 12 million new jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure that we get people off of food stamps, not by cutting the program, but by getting them good jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America’s going to come back, and for that to happen, we’re going to have to have a president who can work across the aisle.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in a state where my legislature was 87 percent Democrat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I learned how to get along on the other side of the aisle.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got to do that in Washington.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Washington is broken.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to get this country back, and will work with good Democrats and good Republicans to do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This nation is the hope of the earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve been blessed by having a nation that’s free and prosperous thanks to the contributions of the greatest generation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ve held a torch for the world to see — the torch of freedom and hope and opportunity.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, it’s our turn to take that torch.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m convinced we’ll do it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need strong leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to be that leader with your support.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll work with you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll lead you in an open and honest way, and I ask for your vote.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to be the next president of the United States to support and help this great nation and to make sure that we all together remain America as the hope of the earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you so much.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jeremy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate your — your question, and thank you for being here this evening and to all of those from Nassau County that have come, thank you for your time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you to Hofstra University and to Candy Crowley for organizing and leading this — this event.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, also for being part of this — this debate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Your question — your question is one that’s being asked by college kids all over this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in Pennsylvania with someone who had just graduated — this was in Philadelphia — and she said, “I’ve got my degree.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can’t find a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve got three part- time jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re just barely enough to pay for my food and pay for an apartment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can’t begin to pay back my student loans.” So what we have to do is two things.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to make sure that we make it easier for kids to afford college.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And also make sure that when they get out of college, there’s a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When I was governor of Massachusetts, to get a high school degree, you had to pass an exam.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you graduated in the top quarter of your airlines, we gave you a John and Abigail Adams scholarship, four years tuition free in the college of your choice in Massachusetts, it’s a public institution.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure we keep our Pell grant program growing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re also going to have our loan program, so that people are able to afford school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the key thing is to make sure you can get a job when you get out of school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what’s happened over the last four years has been very, very hard for America’s young people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want you to be able to get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to get this economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With half of college kids graduating this year without a college — excuse me, without a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And without a college level job, that’s just unacceptable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And likewise you’ve got more and more debt on your back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So more debt and less jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to change that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to create good jobs again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to make sure that you have the kind of opportunity you deserve.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And kids across this country are going to recognize, we’re bringing back an economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not going to be like the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The middle-class has been crushed over the last four years, and jobs have been too scarce.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to bring them back, and I’m going to do that, and make sure that when you graduate — when do you graduate?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you come out in 2014, I presume I’m going to be president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure you get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thanks Jeremy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Yeah, you bet.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Jeremy, first of all, your future is bright.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact that you’re making an investment in higher education is critical.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not just to you, but to the entire nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the most important thing we can do is to make sure that we are creating jobs in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But not just jobs, good paying jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ones that can support a family.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I want to do, is build on the five million jobs that we’ve created over the last 30 months in the private sector alone.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And there are a bunch of things we can do to make sure your future is bright.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, I want to build manufacturing jobs in this country again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now when Governor Romney said we should let Detroit go bankrupt.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said we’re going to bet on American workers and the American auto industry and it’s come surging back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to do that in industries, not just in Detroit, but all across the country and that means we change our tax code so we’re giving incentives to companies that are investing here in the United States and creating jobs here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It also means we’re helping them and small businesses to export all around the world to new markets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, we’ve got to make sure that we have the best education system in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact that you’re going to college is great, but I want everybody to get a great education and we’ve worked hard to make sure that student loans are available for folks like you, but I also want to make sure that community colleges are offering slots for workers to get retrained for the jobs that are out there right now and the jobs of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number three, we’ve got to control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, not only oil and natural gas, which we’ve been investing in; but also, we’ve got to make sure we’re building the energy source of the future, not just thinking about next year, but ten years from now, 20 years from now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we’ve invested in solar and wind and biofuels, energy efficient cars.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to reduce our deficit, but we’ve got to do it in a balanced way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Asking the wealthy to pay a little bit more along with cuts so that we can invest in education like yours.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And let’s take the money that we’ve been spending on war over the last decade to rebuild America, roads, bridges schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We do those things, not only is your future going to be bright but America’s future is going to bright as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well what you’re seeing in this country is 23 million people struggling to find a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And a lot of them, as you say, Candy, have been out of work for a long, long, long time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s policies have been exercised over the last four years and they haven’t put Americans back to work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have fewer people working today than we had when the president took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the — the unemployment rate was 7.8 percent when he took office, it’s 7.8 percent now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But if you calculated that unemployment rate, taking back the people who dropped out of the workforce, it would be 10.7 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have not made the progress we need to make to put people back to work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why I put out a five-point plan that gets America 12 million new jobs in four years and rising take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help Jeremy get a job when he comes out of school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help people across the country that are unemployed right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And one thing that the president said, which I want to make sure that we understand, he said that I said we should take Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan was to have the company go through bankruptcy like 7-Eleven did and Macy’s and Condell (ph) Airlines and come out stronger.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I know he keeps saying, you want to take Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, the president took Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You took General Motors bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You took Chrysler bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So when you say that I wanted to take the auto industry bankrupt, you actually did.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think it’s important to know that that was a process that was necessary to get those companies back on their feet, so they could start hiring more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was precisely what I recommended and ultimately what happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, what Governor Romney said just isn’t true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He wanted to take them into bankruptcy without providing them any way to stay open.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we would have lost a million jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that — don’t take my word for it, take the executives at GM and Chrysler, some of whom are Republicans, may even support Governor Romney.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But they’ll tell you his prescription wasn’t going to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney’s says he’s got a five-point plan?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney doesn’t have a five-point plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He has a one-point plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that plan is to make sure that folks at the top play by a different set of rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s been his philosophy in the private sector, that’s been his philosophy as governor, that’s been his philosophy as a presidential candidate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can make a lot of money and pay lower tax rates than somebody who makes a lot less.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can ship jobs overseas and get tax breaks for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can invest in a company, bankrupt it, lay off the workers, strip away their pensions, and you still make money.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly the philosophy that we’ve seen in place for the last decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what’s been squeezing middle class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have fought back for four years to get out of that mess.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The last thing we need to do is to go back to the very same policies that got us there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That — that Detroit — that Detroit answer…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … that Detroit answer…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … and the rest of the answer, way off the mark.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The most important thing we can do is to make sure we control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what I’ve done since I’ve been president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have increased oil production to the highest levels in 16 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Natural gas production is the highest it’s been in decades.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have seen increases in coal production and coal employment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is we can’t just produce traditional source of energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve also got to look to the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we doubled fuel efficiency standards on cars.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That means that in the middle of the next decade, any car you buy, you’re going to end up going twice as far on a gallon of gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we doubled clean — clean energy production like wind and solar and biofuels.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And all these things have contributed to us lowering our oil imports to the lowest levels in 16 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I want to build on that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means, yes, we still continue to open up new areas for drilling.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We continue to make it a priority for us to go after natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got potentially 600,000 jobs and 100 years worth of energy right beneath our feet with natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we can do it in an environmentally sound way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’ve also got to continue to figure out how we have efficiency energy, because ultimately that’s how we’re going to reduce demand and that’s what’s going to keep gas prices lower.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney will say he’s got an all-of-the-above plan, but basically his plan is to let the oil companies write the energy policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So he’s got the oil and gas part, but he doesn’t have the clean energy part.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we are only thinking about tomorrow or the next day and not thinking about 10 years from now, we’re not going to control our own economic future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because China, Germany, they’re making these investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m not going to cede those jobs of the future to those countries.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I expect those new energy sources to be built right here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s going to help Jeremy get a job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s also going to make sure that you’re not paying as much for gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s look at the president’s policies, all right, as opposed to the rhetoric, because we’ve had four years of policies being played out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the president’s right in terms of the additional oil production, but none of it came on federal land.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, oil production is down 14 percent this year on federal land, and gas production was down 9 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because the president cut in half the number of licenses and permits for drilling on federal lands, and in federal waters.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So where’d the increase come from?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well a lot of it came from the Bakken Range in North Dakota.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What was his participation there?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The administration brought a criminal action against the people drilling up there for oil, this massive new resource we have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what was the cost?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: 20 or 25 birds were killed and brought out a migratory bird act to go after them on a criminal basis.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I want to make sure we use our oil, our coal, our gas, our nuclear, our renewables.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe very much in our renewable capabilities; ethanol, wind, solar will be an important part of our energy mix.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But what we don’t need is to have the president keeping us from taking advantage of oil, coal and gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This has not been Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Talk to the people that are working in those industries.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in coal country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People grabbed my arms and said, “Please save my job.” The head of the EPA said, “You can’t build a coal plant.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll virtually — it’s virtually impossible given our regulations.” When the president ran for office, he said if you build a coal plant, you can go ahead, but you’ll go bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right course for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s take advantage of the energy resources we have, as well as the energy sources for the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we do that, if we do what I’m planning on doing, which is getting us energy independent, North America energy independence within eight years, you’re going to see manufacturing jobs come back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because our energy is low cost, that are already beginning to come back because of our abundant energy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get America and North America energy independent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll do it by more drilling, more permits and licenses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to bring that pipeline in from Canada.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How in the world the president said no to that pipeline?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will never know.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is about bringing good jobs back for the middle class of America, and that’s what I’m going to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, there’s no doubt that world demand’s gone up, but our production is going up, and we’re using oil more efficiently.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And very little of what Governor Romney just said is true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve opened up public lands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re actually drilling more on public lands than in the previous administration and my — the previous president was an oil man.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And natural gas isn’t just appearing magically.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re encouraging it and working with the industry.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when I hear Governor Romney say he’s a big coal guy, I mean, keep in mind, when — Governor, when you were governor of Massachusetts, you stood in front of a coal plant and pointed at it and said, “This plant kills,” and took great pride in shutting it down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now suddenly you’re a big champion of coal.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I’ve tried to do is be consistent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: With respect to something like coal, we made the largest investment in clean coal technology, to make sure that even as we’re producing more coal, we’re producing it cleaner and smarter.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Same thing with oil, same thing with natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the proof is our oil imports are down to the lowest levels in 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Oil production is up, natural gas production is up, and, most importantly, we’re also starting to build cars that are more efficient.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s creating jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That means those cars can be exported, ’cause that’s the demand around the world, and it also means that it’ll save money in your pocketbook.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the strategy you need, an all-of-the-above strategy, and that’s what we’re going to do in the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But that’s not what you’ve done in the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the problem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the last four years, you cut permits and licenses on federal land and federal waters in half.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not true, Governor Romney.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So how much did you cut (inaudible)?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut them by, then?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, we have actually produced more oil —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut licenses and permits on federal land and federal waters?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, here’s what we did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There were a whole bunch of oil companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, no, I had a question and the question was how much did you cut them by?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You want me to answer a question —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut them by?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m happy to answer the question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Here’s what happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You had a whole bunch of oil companies who had leases on public lands that they weren’t using.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what we said was you can’t just sit on this for 10, 20, 30 years, decide when you want to drill, when you want to produce, when it’s most profitable for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are public lands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if you want to drill on public lands, you use it or you lose it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: OK, (inaudible) —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what we did was take away those leases.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are now reletting them so that we can actually make a profit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And production on private — on government land —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Production is up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, it isn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Production on government land of oil is down 14 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And production on gas — (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s just not true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, there’s no question but the people recognize that we have not produced more (inaudible) on federal lands and in federal waters.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And coal, coal production is not up; coal jobs are not up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was just at a coal facility, where some 1,200 people lost their jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The right course for America is to have a true all-of-the-above policy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think anyone really believes that you’re a person who’s going to be pushing for oil and gas and coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll get your chance in a moment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m still speaking.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer is I don’t believe people think that’s the case —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: — (inaudible).

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That wasn’t the question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was a statement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think the American people believe that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will fight for oil, coal and natural gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the proof, the proof of whether a strategy is working or not is what the price is that you’re paying at the pump.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you’re paying less than you paid a year or two ago, why, then, the strategy is working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you’re paying more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When the president took office, the price of gasoline here in Nassau County was about $1.86 a gallon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, it’s $4.00 a gallon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The price of electricity is up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the president’s energy policies are working, you’re going to see the cost of energy come down.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will fight to create more energy in this country, to get America energy secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And part of that is bringing in a pipeline of oil from Canada, taking advantage of the oil and coal we have here, drilling offshore in Alaska, drilling offshore in Virginia where the people want it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those things will get us the energy we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, think about what the governor — think about what the governor just said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said when I took office, the price of gasoline was $1.80, $1.86.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Why is that?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because the economy was on the verge of collapse, because we were about to go through the worst recession since the Great Depression, as a consequence of some of the same policies that Governor Romney’s now promoting.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, it’s conceivable that Governor Romney could bring down gas prices because with his policies, we might be back in that same mess.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I want to do is to create an economy that is strong, and at the same time produce energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And with respect to this pipeline that Governor Romney keeps on talking about, we’ve — we’ve built enough pipeline to wrap around the entire earth once.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, I’m all for pipelines.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m all for oil production.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I’m not for is us ignoring the other half of the equation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, for example, on wind energy, when Governor Romney says “these are imaginary jobs.” When you’ve got thousands of people right now in Iowa, right now in Colorado, who are working, creating wind power with good-paying manufacturing jobs, and the Republican senator in that — in Iowa is all for it, providing tax breaks (ph) to help this work and Governor Romney says I’m opposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’d get rid of it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not an energy strategy for the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we need to win that future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I intend to win it as President of the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He gets the first —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He actually got —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He actually got the first question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I get the last question — last answer —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Candy, Candy —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Candy, I don’t have a policy of stopping wind jobs in Iowa and that — they’re not phantom jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re real jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate wind jobs in Iowa and across our country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate the jobs in coal and oil and gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: — we’re taking advantage of our energy resources.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll bring back manufacturing to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to get through a very aggressive energy policy, 31/2 million more jobs in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s critical to our future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, it’s not going to —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Used to being interrupted.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re doing great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you very much.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And let me tell you, you’re absolutely right about part of that, which is I want to bring the rates down, I want to simplify the tax code, and I want to get middle- income taxpayers to have lower taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the reason I want middle-income taxpayers to have lower taxes is because middle-income taxpayers have been buried over the past four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ve seen, as middle-income people in this country, incomes go down $4,300 a family, even as gasoline prices have gone up $2,000.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Health insurance premiums, up $2,500.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Food prices up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Utility prices up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The middle-income families in America have been crushed over the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I want to get some relief to middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s part — that’s part one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, how about deductions?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: ‘Cause I’m going to bring rates down across the board for everybody, but I’m going to limit deductions and exemptions and credits, particularly for people at the high end, because I am not going to have people at the high end pay less than they’re paying now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The top 5 percent of taxpayers will continue to pay 60 percent of the income tax the nation collects.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So that’ll stay the same.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle-income people are going to get a tax break.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so, in terms of bringing down deductions, one way of doing that would be say everybody gets — I’ll pick a number — $25,000 of deductions and credits, and you can decide which ones to use.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Your home mortgage interest deduction, charity, child tax credit, and so forth, you can use those as part of filling that bucket, if you will, of deductions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But your rate comes down and the burden also comes down on you for one more reason, and that is every middle-income taxpayer no longer will pay any tax on interest, dividends or capital gains.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No tax on your savings.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That makes life a lot easier.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you’re getting interest from a bank, if you’re getting a statement from a mutual fund or any other kind of investment you have, you don’t have to worry about filing taxes on that, because there’ll be no taxes for anybody making $200,000.00 per year and less, on your interest, dividends and capital gains.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Why am I lowering taxes on the middle-class?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because under the last four years, they’ve been buried.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I want to help people in the middle-class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not — I will not under any circumstances, reduce the share that’s being paid by the highest income taxpayers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not, under any circumstances increase taxes on the middle-class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s spending, the president’s borrowing will cost this nation to have to raise taxes on the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not just at the high end.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A recent study has shown the people in the middle-class will see $4,000.00 per year in higher taxes as a result of the spending and borrowing of this administration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not let that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get us on track to a balanced budget, and I’m going to reduce the tax burden on middle income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what’s that going to do?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help those families, and it’s going to create incentives to start growing jobs again in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My philosophy on taxes has been simple.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is, I want to give middle-class families and folks who are striving to get into the middle-class some relief.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because they have been hit hard over the last decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last 15, over the last 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So four years ago I stood on a stage just like this one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Actually it was a town hall, and I said I would cut taxes for middle- class families, and that’s what I’ve done, by $3,600.00.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said I would cut taxes for small businesses, who are the drivers and engines of growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve cut them 18 times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to continue those tax cuts for middle-class families, and for small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is, if we’re serious about reducing the deficit, if this is genuinely a moral obligation to the next generation, then in addition to some tough spending cuts, we’ve also got to make sure that the wealthy do a little bit more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I’ve said is, your first $250,000.00 worth of income, no change.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means 98 percent of American families, 97 percent of small businesses, they will not see a tax increase.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m ready to sign that bill right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The only reason it’s not happening is because Governor Romney’s allies in Congress have held the 98 percent hostage because they want tax breaks for the top 2 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also says is for above $250,000, we can go back to the tax rates we had when Bill Clinton was president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created 23 million new jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of what took us from deficits to surplus.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It will be good for our economy and it will be good for job creation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has a different philosophy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He was on 60 Minutes just two weeks ago and he was asked: Is it fair for somebody like you, making $20 million a year, to pay a lower tax rate than a nurse or a bus driver, somebody making $50,000 year?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he said, “Yes, I think that’s fair.” Not only that, he said, “I think that’s what grows the economy.” Well, I fundamentally disagree with that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think what grows the economy is when you get that tax credit that we put in place for your kids going to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that grows the economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think what grows the economy is when we make sure small businesses are getting a tax credit for hiring veterans who fought for our country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That grows our economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we just have a different theory.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when Governor Romney stands here, after a year of campaigning, when during a Republican primary he stood on stage and said “I’m going to give tax cuts” — he didn’t say tax rate cuts, he said “tax cuts to everybody,” including the top 1 percent, you should believe him because that’s been his history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly the kind of top-down economics that is not going to work if we want a strong middle class and an economy that’s striving for everybody.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re absolutely right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You heard what I said about my tax plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The top 5 percent will continue to pay 60 percent, as they do today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not looking to cut taxes for wealthy people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I am looking to cut taxes for middle-income people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And why do I want to bring rates down, and at the same time lower exemptions and deductions, particularly for people at the high end?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because if you bring rates down, it makes it easier for small business to keep more of their capital and hire people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And for me, this is about jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get America’s economy going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fifty-four percent of America’s workers work in businesses that are taxed as individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So when you bring those rates down, those small businesses are able to keep more money and hire more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: For me, I look at what’s happened in the last four years and say this has been a disappointment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can do better than this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for, how many months, 43 months with unemployment above 8 percent, 23 million Americans struggling to find a good job right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are 3.5 million more women living in poverty today than when the president took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to live like this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can get this economy going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My five-point plan does it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Energy independence for North America in five years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Opening up more trade, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Cracking down on China when they cheat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Getting us to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fixing our training programs for our workers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, championing small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make small businesses grow and thrive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know how to make that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I spent my life in the private sector.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know why jobs come and why they go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they’re going now because of the policies of this administration.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, it’s not settled.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, the cost of lowering rates for everybody across the board, 20 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Along with what he also wants to do in terms of eliminating the estate tax, along what he wants to do in terms of corporates, changes in the tax code, it costs about $5 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney then also wants to spend $2 trillion on additional military programs even though the military’s not asking for them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s $7 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He also wants to continue the Bush tax cuts for the wealthiest Americans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s another trillion dollars — that’s $8 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, what he says is he’s going to make sure that this doesn’t add to the deficit and he’s going to cut middleclass taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when he’s asked, how are you going to do it, which deductions, which loopholes are you going to close?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He can’t tell you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — the fact that he only has to pay 14 percent on his taxes when a lot of you are paying much higher.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He’s already taken that off the board, capital gains are going to continue to be at a low rate so we — we’re not going to get money that way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We haven’t heard from the governor any specifics beyond Big Bird and eliminating funding for Planned Parenthood in terms of how he pays for that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney was a very successful investor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If somebody came to you, Governor, with a plan that said, here, I want to spend $7 or $8 trillion, and then we’re going to pay for it, but we can’t tell you until maybe after the election how we’re going to do it, you wouldn’t take such a sketchy deal and neither should you, the American people, because the math doesn’t add up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and what’s at stake here is one of two things, either Candy — this blows up the deficit because keep in mind, this is just to pay for the additional spending that he’s talking about, $7 trillion – $8 trillion before we even get to the deficit we already have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Or, alternatively, it’s got to be paid for, not only by closing deductions for wealthy individuals, that — that will pay for about 4 percent reduction in tax rates.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re going to be paying for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re going to lose some deductions, and you can’t buy the sales pitch.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nobody who’s looked at it that’s serious, actually believes it adds up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well of course they add up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I was — I was someone who ran businesses for 25 years, and balanced the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I ran the Olympics and balanced the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
