sentence: Number two, Governor Romney, before he knew the facts, before he even knew that our ambassador was killed, he was out making a political statement which was panned by the media around the world.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And this talk about this — this weakness.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — I don’t understand what my friend’s talking about here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We — this is a president who’s gone out and done everything he has said he was going to do.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is a guy who’s repaired our alliances so the rest of the world follows us again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is the guy who brought the entire world, including Russia and China, to bring about the most devastating — most devastating — the most devastating efforts on Iran to make sure that they in fact stop (inaudible).

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, I — I just — I mean, these guys bet against America all the time.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because that was exactly what we were told by the intelligence community.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The intelligence community told us that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: As they learned more facts about exactly what happened, they changed their assessment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why there’s also an investigation headed by Tom Pickering, a leading diplomat from the Reagan years, who is doing an investigation as to whether or not there are any lapses, what the lapses were, so that they will never happen again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, we weren’t told they wanted more security there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We did not know they wanted more security again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And by the way, at the time we were told exactly — we said exactly what the intelligence community told us that they knew.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was the assessment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And as the intelligence community changed their view, we made it clear they changed their view.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why I said we will get to the bottom of this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You know, usually when there’s a crisis, we pull together.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We pull together as a nation.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But as I said, even before we knew what happened to the ambassador, the governor was holding a press conference — was holding a press conference.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s not presidential leadership.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Oh, gosh, yes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Urinating on Taliban corpses?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not apologize for…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What — what we should not be apologizing for are standing up for our values.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not be doing is saying to the Egyptian people, while Mubarak is cracking down on them, that he’s a good guy and, in the next week, say he ought to go.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not be doing is rejecting claims for — for calls for more security in our barracks, in our Marine — we need Marines in Benghazi when the commander on the ground says we need more forces for security.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: There were requests for extra security; those requests were not honored.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, this was the anniversary of 9/11.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It was Libya, a country we knew we had Al Qaida cells there, as we know Al Qaida and its affiliates are on the rise in Northern Africa.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we did not give our ambassador in Benghazi a Marine detachment?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of course there’s an investigation, so we can make sure that this never happens again, but when it comes to speaking up for our values, we should not apologize for those.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Here’s the problem.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at all the various issues out there, and it’s unraveling before our eyes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president talks about sanctions on Iran.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They got — we’ve had four…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We cannot allow Iran to gain a nuclear weapons capability.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, let’s take a look at where we’ve gone — come from.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When Barack Obama was elected, they had enough fissile material — nuclear material to make one bomb.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now they have enough for five.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re racing toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re four years closer toward a nuclear weapons capability.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve had four different sanctions, the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: on Iran, three from the Bush administration, one here.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And the only reason we got it is because Russia watered it down and prevented the — the sanctions from hitting the central bank.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney proposed these sanctions in 2007.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In Congress, I’ve been fighting for these sanctions since 2009.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The administration was blocking us every step of the way.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Only because we had strong bipartisan support for these tough sanctions were we able to overrule their objections and put them in spite of the administration.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Imagine what would have happened if we had these sanctions in place earlier.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You think Iran’s not brazen?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at what they’re doing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re stepping up their terrorist attacks.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They tried a terrorist attack in the United States last year when they tried to blow up the Saudi ambassador at a restaurant in Washington, D.C.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And talk about credibility?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When this administration says that all options are on the table, they send out senior administration officials that send all these mixed signals.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so, in order to solve this peacefully — which is everybody’s goal — you have to have the ayatollahs change their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at where they are.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re moving faster toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s because this administration has no credibility on this issue.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s because this administration watered down sanctions, delayed sanctions, tried to stop us for putting the tough sanctions in place.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now we have them in place because of Congress.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They say the military option’s on the table, but it’s not being viewed as credible.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And the key is to do this peacefully, is to make sure that we have credibility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Under a Romney administration, we will have credibility on this issue.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s incredible.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, imagine had we let the Republican Congress work out the sanctions.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You think there’s any possibility the entire world would have joined us, Russia and China, all of our allies?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: These are the most crippling sanctions in the history of sanctions, period.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When Governor Romney’s asked about it, he said, “We gotta keep these sanctions.” When he said, “Well, you’re talking about doing more,” what are you — you’re going to go to war?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Is that what you want to do?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want to prevent war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the interesting thing is, how are they going to prevent war?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: How are they going to prevent war if they say there’s nothing more that we — that they say we should do than what we’ve already done, number one.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And number two, with regard to the ability of the United States to take action militarily, it is — it is not in my purview to talk about classified information.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But we feel quite confident we could deal a serious blow to the Iranians.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But number two, the Iranians are — the Israelis and the United States, our military and intelligence communities are absolutely the same exact place in terms of how close — how close the Iranians are to getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are a good way away.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There is no difference between our view and theirs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When my friend talks about fissile material, they have to take this highly enriched uranium, get it from 20 percent up, then they have to be able to have something to put it in.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There is no weapon that the Iranians have at this point.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Both the Israelis and we know — we’ll know if they start the process of building a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So all this bluster I keep hearing, all this loose talk, what are they talking about?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Are you talking about, to be more credible — what more can the president do, stand before the United Nations, tell the whole world, directly communicate to the ayatollah, we will not let them acquire a nuclear weapon, period, unless he’s talking about going to war.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let’s look at this from the view of the ayatollahs.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What do they see?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see this administration trying to water down sanctions in Congress for over two years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re moving faster toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re spinning the centrifuges faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see us saying when we come into the administration, when they’re sworn in, we need more space with our ally, Israel.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see President Obama in New York City the same day Bibi Netanyahu is and he, instead of meeting with him, goes on a — on a daily talk show.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see, when we say that these options are on the table, the secretary of defense walked them back.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They are not changing their mind.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we have to do, is change their mind so they stop pursuing nuclear weapons, and they’re going faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can debate a time line.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can debate the time line, whether there’s — it’s that short a time or longer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I agree that it’s probably longer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Number two, it’s all about… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I don’t want to go into classified stuff.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we both agree that to do this peacefully you’ve got to get them to change their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re not changing their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And look at what this administration…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me tell you what the ayatollah sees.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You have to have credibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The ayatollah sees his economy being crippled.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The ayatollah sees that there are 50 percent fewer exports of oil.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He sees the currency going into the tank.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He sees the economy going into freefall.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And he sees the world for the first time totally united in opposition to him getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now, with regard to Bibi, who’s been my friend 39 years, the president has met with Bibi a dozen times.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s spoken to Bibi Netanyahu as much as he’s spoken to anybody.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The idea that we’re not — I was in a, just before he went to the U.N., I was in a conference call with the — with the president, with him talking to Bibi for well over an hour, in — in — in stark relief and detail of what was going on.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is a bunch of stuff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, here’s the deal.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, it means it’s simply inaccurate.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: (LAUGHTER) We Irish call it malarkey.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We Irish call it malarkey.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But last thing.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The secretary of defense has made it absolutely clear, we didn’t walk anything back.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We will not allow the Iranians to get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What Bibi held up there was when they get to the point where they can enrich uranium enough to put into a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They don’t have a weapon to put it into.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let’s all calm down a little bit here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Iran is more isolated today than when we took office.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It was on the ascendancy when we took office.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is totally isolated.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I don’t know what world this guy’s living in.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Thank heavens we had these sanctions in place.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s in spite of their opposition.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’ve given 20 waivers to this sanction.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And all I have to point to are the results.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re four years closer toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I think that case speaks for itself.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: By the way, they… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … they are not four years closer to a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of course they are.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re — they’re closer to being able to get enough fissile material to put in a weapon if they had a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Oh, I didn’t say — no, I’m not saying that.

speaker: BIDEN

sentence: You’re out of Medicare.” You can buy back in if you want with this voucher, which will not keep pace — will not keep pace with health care costs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because if it did keep pace with health care costs, there would be no savings.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why they go the voucher.

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: RYAN

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Barack Obama four years ago running for president said if you don’t have any fresh ideas, use stale tactics to scare voters.

sentence: We see no justification in these economic times for those, and they’re patriotic Americans.

sentence: They’re not suggesting it, but my friends are insisting on it; 120,000 families by continuing that tax cut will get an additional $500 billion in tax relief in the next 10 years and their income is an average of $8 million.

sentence: We want to extend permanently the middle-class tax cut for — permanently, from the Bush middle-class tax cut.

sentence: And on top of that, they’ve got another tax cut coming that’s $5 trillion that all of the studies point out will in fact give another $250 million — yeah, $250,000 a year to those 120,000 families and raise taxes for people who are middle income with a child by $2,000 a year.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The last people who need help are 120,000 families for another — another $500 billion tax cut over the next 10 years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our entire premise of these tax reform plans is to grow the economy and create jobs.

sentence: It’s a plan that’s estimated to create 7 million jobs.

sentence: Now, we think that government taking 28 percent of a family and business’s income is enough.

sentence: Look, if you taxed every person and successful business making over $250,000 at 100 percent, it would only run the government for 98 days.

sentence: If everybody who paid income taxes last year, including successful small businesses, doubled their income taxes this year, we’d still have a $300 billion deficit.

sentence: The average tax rate on businesses in the industrialized world is 25 percent, and the president wants the top effective tax rate on successful small businesses to go above 40 percent.

sentence: This one tax would actually tax about 53 percent of small-business income.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we are saying is, lower tax rates across the board and close loopholes, primarily to the higher-income people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We have three bottom lines: Don’t raise the deficit, don’t raise taxes on the middle class, and don’t lower the share of income that is borne by the high-income earners.

sentence: He’ll keep saying this $5 trillion plan, I suppose.

speaker: RYAN

sentence: We want to work with Congress — we want to work with the Congress on how best to achieve this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That means successful.

sentence: Mitt — what we’re saying is, lower tax rates 20 percent, start with the wealthy, work with Congress to do it…

sentence: And I might add, this so-called — I know we don’t want to use the fancy word “sequester,” this automatic cut — that was part of a debt deal that they asked for.

sentence: And let me tell you what my friend said at a press conference announcing his support of the deal.

sentence: Look, when I think about Afghanistan, I think about the incredible job that our troops have done.

sentence: When I went to the Ahgandah (ph) Valley in Kandahar before the surge, I sat down with a young private in the 82nd from the Monamanee (ph) Indian reservation who would tell me what he did every day, and I was in awe.

sentence: And to see what they had in front of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And then to go back there in December, to go throughout Helmand with the Marines, to see what they had accomplished, it’s nothing short of amazing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we don’t want to do is lose the gains we’ve gotten.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, we’ve disagreed from time to time on a few issues.

sentence: We would have more likely taken into accounts the recommendations from our commanders, General Petraeus, Admiral Mullen, on troop levels throughout this year’s fighting season.

sentence: We’ve been skeptical about negotiations with the Taliban, especially while they’re shooting at us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we want to see the 2014 transition be successful, and that means we want to make sure our commanders have what they need to make sure that it is successful so that this does not once again become a launching pad for terrorists.

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My friend and the governor say it’s based on conditions, which means it depends.

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: BIDEN

sentence: We want — look, one of my best friends in Janesville, a reservist, is at a forward-operating base in eastern Afghanistan right now.

sentence: I want — I want him and all of our troops to come home as soon and safely as possible.

sentence: You know, if it was just this, I’d feel like we would — we would be able to call this a success, but it’s not.

sentence: What we are witnessing as we turn on our television screens these days is the absolute unraveling of the Obama foreign policy.

sentence: Which is — which is… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, the Taliban — what we’ve found out, and we — you saw it in Iraq, Martha, unless you set a timeline, Baghdad, in the case of Iraq, and — and Kabul, in the case of Afghanistan will not step up.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re happy to let us continue to do the job; international security forces to do the job.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The only way they step up is to say, “Fellas, we’re leaving; we’ve trained you; step up, step up.”

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s the only way it works.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The military reason…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … was bringing — by the way, when the president announced the surge, you’ll remember, Martha, he said the surge will be out by the end of the summer.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The military said the surge will be out.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Nothing political about this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Before the surge occurred — so you be a little straight with me here, too — before the surge occurred, we said they’ll be out by the end of the summer.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s what the military said.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The reason for that is…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There are people that are concerned, but not the Joint Chiefs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was their recommendation in the Oval Office to the president of the United States of America.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I sat there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’m sure you’ll find someone who disagrees with the Pentagon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’m positive you’ll find that within the military.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But that’s not the case here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, secondly, the reason why the military said that is, you cannot wait and have a cliff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It takes — you know — months and months and months to draw down forces.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let me… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let me try and illustrate the issue here, because I think this — it can get a little confusing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve all met with General Allen and General Scaparrotti in Afghanistan to talk about fighting seasons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Here’s the way it works.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The mountain passes fill in with snow.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The Taliban and the terrorists and the Haqqani and the Quetta Shura come over from Pakistan to fight our men and women.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When it fills in with snow, they can’t do it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we call fighting seasons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In the warm months, fighting gets really high.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In the winter, it goes down.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so when Admiral Mullen and General Petraeus came to Congress and said, if you pull these people out before the fighting season is end, it puts people more at risk.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the problem.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Yes, we drew 22,000 troops down last month, but the remaining troops that are there, who still have the same mission to prosecute counterinsurgency, are doing it with fewer people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That makes them less safe.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Fighting season…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re sending fewer people out in all of these hotspots to do the same job that they were supposed to do a month ago.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because we turned it over…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we took 22,000 people out…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … we turned it over to the Afghan troops we trained.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: No one got pulled out that didn’t get filled in by trained Afghan personnel.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And he’s — he’s conflating two issues.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The fighting season that Petraeus was talking about and former — and Admiral Mullen was the fighting season this spring.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s what he was talking about.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We did not — we did not pull them out.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The calendar works the same every year.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It does work the same every year.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But we’re not staying there…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Spring, summer, fall.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s warm, or it’s not.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still fighting us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still coming over the passes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still coming into Zabul, to Kunar, to all of these areas, but we are sending fewer people to the front to fight them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And that’s… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s right, because that’s the Afghan responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’ve trained them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not in the east.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Not in the east?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: East is the most dangerous place in the world.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s right.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why we don’t want to send fewer people to the…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s — that’s why we should send Americans in to do the job, instead of the — you’d rather Americans be going in doing the job instead of the trainees?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We are already sending Americans to do the job, but fewer of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the whole problem.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s right.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’re sending in more Afghans to do the job, Afghans to do the job.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Different country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s a different country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is five times as large geographically, it has one-fifth the population, that is Libya, one-fifth the population, five times as large geographically.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s in a part of the world where they’re not going to see whatever would come from that war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It seep into a regional war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You’re in a country that is heavily populated in the midst of the most dangerous area in the world.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, in fact, if in fact it blows up and the wrong people gain control, it’s going to have impact on the entire region causing potentially regional wars.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are working hand and glove with the Turks, with the Jordanians, with the Saudis, and with all the people in the region attempting to identify the people who deserve the help so that when Assad goes — and he will go — there will be a legitimate government that follows on, not an Al Qaida-sponsored government that follows on.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And all this loose talk of my friend, Governor Romney, and the congressman, about how we’re going to do, we could do so much more in there, what more would they do other than put American boots on the ground?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The last thing America needs is to get in another ground war in the Middle East, requiring tens of thousands, if not well over 100,000 American forces.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That — they are the facts.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are the facts.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now, every time the governor is asked about this, he doesn’t say anything.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He — he goes up with a whole lot of verbiage, but when he gets pressed he says, no, he would not do anything different than we are doing now.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Are they proposing putting American troops on the ground?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Putting American aircraft in the airspace?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Is that what they’re proposing?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: If they do, they should speak up and say so, but that’s not what they’re saying.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are doing it exactly like we need to do to identify those forces who, in fact, will provide for a stable government and not cause a regional Sunni-Shia war when Bassad (sic) — when Bashar Assad falls.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Nobody is proposing to send troops to Syria.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: American troops.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, let me say it this way.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: How would we do things differently?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We wouldn’t refer to Bashar Assad as a reformer when he’s killing his own civilians with his Russian-provided weapons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We wouldn’t be outsourcing our foreign policy to the United Nations giving Vladimir Putin veto power over our efforts to try and deal with this issue.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’s vetoed three of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Hillary Clinton went to Russia to try and convince them not to do so.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They thwarted her efforts.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: She said they were on the wrong side of history.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: She was right about that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is just one more example of how the Russia reset’s not working.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so where are we?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: After international pressure mounted, the President Obama said Bashar Assad should go.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s been over a year.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The man has slaughtered tens of thousands of his own people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And more foreign fighters are spilling into this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: So the longer this has gone on, the more people, groups like Al Qaida are going in.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We could have more easily identified the free Syrian army, the freedom fighters, working with our allies, the Turks, the Qataris, the Saudis, had we had a better plan in place to begin with working through our allies.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But, no, we waited for Kofi Annan to try and come up with an agreement through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That bought Bashar Assad time.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We gave Russia veto power over our efforts through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And meanwhile about 30,000 Syrians are dead.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What would my friend do differently?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: If you notice, he never answers the question.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: No, I would — I — we would not be going through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: in all of these things.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me — you don’t go through the U.N.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are in the process now — and have been for months — in making sure that help, humanitarian aid, as well as other aid and training is getting to those forces that we believe, the Turks believe, the Jordanians believe, the Saudis believe are the free forces inside of Syria.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is underway.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Our allies were all on the same page, NATO, as well as our Arab allies, in terms of trying to get a settlement.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was their idea.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’re the ones that said, “Enough.” With regard to the reset not working, the fact of the matter is that Russia has a different interest in Syria than we do, and that’s not in our interest.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Then Iran keeps their greatest ally in the region.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’s a sponsor of terrorism.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’ll probably continue slaughtering his people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We and the world community will lose our credibility on this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, he mentioned the reset…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Well, we agree with the same red line, actually, they do on chemical weapons, but not putting American troops in, other than to secure those chemical weapons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re right about that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But what we should have done earlier is work with those freedom fighters, those dissidents in Syria.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We should not have called Bashar Assad a reformer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … we should not have — we should not have waited to Russia…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … should not have waited for Russia to give us the green light at the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: to do something about it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re — they’re still arming the man.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Iran is flying flights over Iraq…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the opposition is being armed.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … to help Bashar Assad.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And, by the way, if we had the status-of-forces agreement that the vice president said he would bet his vice presidency on in Iraq, we probably would have been able to prevent that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But he failed to achieve that, as well, again.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What is in the national interests of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What is in the national security of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s got to be in the strategic national interests of our country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Each situation will — will come up with its own set of circumstances, but putting American troops on the ground?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s got to be within the national security interests of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That means like embargoes and sanctions and overflights, those are things that don’t put American troops on the ground.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But if you’re talking about putting American troops on the ground, only in our national security interests.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I don’t see how a person can separate their public life from their private life or from their faith.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our faith informs us in everything we do.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: My faith informs me about how to take care of the vulnerable, of how to make sure that people have a chance in life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, you want to ask basically why I’m pro-life?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s not simply because of my Catholic faith.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s a factor, of course.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But it’s also because of reason and science.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You know, I think about 10 1/2 years ago, my wife Janna and I went to Mercy Hospital in Janesville where I was born, for our seven week ultrasound for our firstborn child, and we saw that heartbeat.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: A little baby was in the shape of a bean.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And to this day, we have nicknamed our firstborn child Liza, “Bean.” Now I believe that life begins at conception.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why — those are the reasons why I’m pro-life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now I understand this is a difficult issue, and I respect people who don’t agree with me on this, but the policy of a Romney administration will be to oppose abortions with the exceptions for rape, incest and life of the mother.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What troubles me more is how this administration has handled all of these issues.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at what they’re doing through Obamacare with respect to assaulting the religious liberties of this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re infringing upon our first freedom, the freedom of religion, by infringing on Catholic charities, Catholic churches, Catholic hospitals.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our church should not have to sue our federal government to maintain their religious liberties.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And with respect to abortion, the Democratic Party used to say they wanted it to be safe, legal and rare.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now they support it without restriction and with taxpayer funding.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Taxpayer funding in Obamacare, taxpayer funding with foreign aid.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president himself went to China and said that he sympathized and wouldn’t second guess their one child policy of forced abortions and sterilizations.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That to me is pretty extreme.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My religion defines who I am, and I’ve been a practicing Catholic my whole life.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And has particularly informed my social doctrine.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The Catholic social doctrine talks about taking care of those who — who can’t take care of themselves, people who need help.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With regard to — with regard to abortion, I accept my church’s position on abortion as a — what we call a (inaudible) doctrine.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Life begins at conception in the church’s judgment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I accept it in my personal life.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But I refuse to impose it on equally devout Christians and Muslims and Jews, and I just refuse to impose that on others, unlike my friend here, the — the congressman.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — I do not believe that we have a right to tell other people that — women they can’t control their body.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s a decision between them and their doctor.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: In my view and the Supreme Court, I’m not going to interfere with that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With regard to the assault on the Catholic church, let me make it absolutely clear, no religious institution, Catholic or otherwise, including Catholic Social Services, Georgetown Hospital, Mercy Hospital, any hospital, none has to either refer contraception, none has to pay for contraception, none has to be a vehicle to get contraception in any insurance policy they provide.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is a fact.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is a fact.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now with regard to the way in which the — we differ, my friend says that he — well I guess he accepts Governor Romney’s position now, because in the past he has argued that there was — there’s rape and forcible rape.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s argued that in the case of rape or incest, it was still — it would be a crime to engage in having an abortion.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I just fundamentally disagree with my friend.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: All I’m saying is, if you believe that life begins at conception, that, therefore, doesn’t change the definition of life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s a principle.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The policy of a Romney administration is to oppose abortion with exceptions for rape, incest and life of the mother.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, I’ve got to take issue with the Catholic church and religious liberty.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You have on the issue… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … why would they keep — why would they keep suing you?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s a distinction without a difference.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t think that unelected judges should make this decision; that people through their elected representatives in reaching a consensus in society through the democratic process should make this determination.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The court — the next president will get one or two Supreme Court nominees.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s how close Roe v.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Just ask yourself, with Robert Bork being the chief adviser on the court for — for Mr.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Romney, who do you think he’s likely to appoint?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Do you think he’s likely to appoint someone like Scalia or someone else on the court far right that would outlaw (inaudible) — outlaw abortion?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I suspect that would happen.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I guarantee you, that will not happen.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We picked two people.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We pick people who are open-minded.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’ve been good justices.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So keep an eye on the Supreme Court…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Was there a litmus test on them?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There was no litmus test.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We picked people who had an open mind; did not come with an agenda.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would say to him the same thing I say to my son who did serve a year in Iraq, that we only have one truly sacred obligation as a government.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s to equip those we send into harm’s way and care for those who come home.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s the only sacred obligation we have.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Everything else falls behind that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would also tell him that the fact that he, this decorated soldier you talked about, fought for his country, that that should be honored.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He should not be thrown into a category of a 47 percent who don’t pay their taxes while he was out there fighting and not having to pay taxes, and somehow not taking responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would also tell him that there are things that have occurred in this campaign and occur in every campaign that I’m sure both of us regret anyone having said, particularly in these — these special new groups that can go out there, raise all the money they want, not have to identify themselves, who say the most scurrilous things about the other candidate.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s — it’s an abomination.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But the bottom line here is I’d ask that hero you referenced to take a look at whether or not Governor Romney or President Obama has the conviction to help lift up the middle class, restore them to where they were before this great recession hit and they got wiped out.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Or whether or not he’s going to continue to focus on taking care of only the very wealthy, not asking them to make — pay any part of the deal to bring — bring back the middle class and the economy of this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’d ask him to take a look at whether the president of the United States has acted wisely in the use of force and whether or not the slipshod comments being made by my — my — or by Governor Romney serve — serve our interests very well.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But there are things that have been said in campaigns that I — I find not very appealing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: First of all, I’d thank him to his service to our country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Second of all, I’d say we are not going to impose these devastating cuts on our military which compromises their mission and their safety.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And then I would say, you have a president who ran for president four years ago promising hope and change, who has now turned his campaign into attack, blame and defame.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You see, if you don’t have a good record to run on, then you paint your opponent as someone to run from.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That was what President Obama said in 2008.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s what he’s doing right now.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at all the string of broken promises.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: If you like your health care plan, you can keep it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Try telling that to the 20 million people who are projected to lose their health insurance if Obamacare goes through or the 7-point million — 7.4 million seniors who are going to lose it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said this: I guarantee if you make less than $250,000, your taxes won’t go up.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of the 21 tax increases in Obamacare, 12 of them hit the middle class.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said health insurance premiums will go down $2,500 per family, per year?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’ve gone up $3,000, and they’re expected to go up another $2,400.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said, “I promise by the end of my first term I’ll cut the deficit in half in four years”?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve had four budgets, four trillion-dollar deficits.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: A debt crisis is coming.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can’t keep spending and borrowing like this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can’t keep spending money we don’t have.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Leaders run to problem to fix problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: President Obama has not even put a credible plan on the table in any of his four years to deal with this debt crisis.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I passed two budgets to deal with this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney’s put ideas on the table.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve got to tackle this debt crisis before it tackles us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The president likes to say he has a plan.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He gave a speech.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We asked his budget office, “Can we see the plan?” They sent us to the press secretary.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He gave us a copy of the speech.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We asked the Congressional Budget Office, “Tell us what President Obama’s plan is to prevent a debt crisis.” They said, “It’s a speech, we can’t estimate speeches.” You see, that’s what we get in this administration — speeches — but we’re not getting leadership.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney is uniquely qualified to fix these problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: His lifetime of experience, his proven track record of bipartisanship.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And what do we have from the president?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He broke his big promise to bring people together to solve the country’s biggest problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And what I would tell him is we don’t have to settle for this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can do better than this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I hope I’ll get equal time.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The two budgets the congressman introduced have eviscerated all the things that the middle class cares about.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is (inaudible) he will knock 19 million people off of Medicare.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It will kick 200,000 children off of early education.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It will eliminate the tax credit people have to be able to send their children to college.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It cuts education by $450 billion.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It does — it does virtually nothing except continue to increase the tax cuts for the very wealthy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, you know, we’ve had enough of this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The idea that he’s so concerned about these deficits, I’ve pointed out he voted to put two wars on a credit card.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not raising taxes is not cutting taxes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And by the way, our budget…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have not raised… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … by 3 percent a year instead of 4.5 percent like they propose.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not spending more money as much as they say is not a spending cut.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Honesty, no one else could?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: There are plenty of fine people who could lead this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But what you need are people who, when they say they’re going to do something, they go do it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What you need are, when people see problems, they offer solutions to fix those problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re not getting that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, we can grow this economy faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what our five- point plan for a stronger middle class is all about.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s about getting 12 million jobs, higher take-home pay, getting people out of poverty into the middle class.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That means going with proven, pro- growth policies that we know works to get people back to work.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Putting ideas on the table, working with Democrats — that actually works sometimes — and then… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, he gets 40, I get 15, that’s OK.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s all right.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me tell you.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — my — my record stands for itself.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I never say anything I don’t mean.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Everybody knows, whatever I say, I do.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And my whole life has been devoted to leveling the playing field for middle-class people, giving them an even break, treating Main Street and Wall Street the same, hold them to the same responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look at my record.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s been all about the middle class.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re the people who grow this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We think you grow this country from the middle out, not from the top down.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, let — let me say at the outset that I want to thank you, Martha, for doing this, and Centre College.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The fact is that we’re in a situation where we inherited a god-awful circumstance.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: People are in real trouble.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We acted to move to bring relief to the people who need the most help now.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And — and in the process, we — in case you haven’t noticed, we have strong disagreements, but I — you probably detected my frustration with their attitude about the American people.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My friend says that 30 percent of the American people are takers.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Romney points out 47 percent of the people won’t take responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s talking about my mother and father.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s talking about the places I grew up in, my neighbors in Scranton and Claymont, and he’s talking about — he’s talking about the people that have built this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: All they’re looking for, Martha, all they’re looking for is an even shot.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Whenever you give them the shot, they’ve done it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’ve done it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Whenever you’ve leveled the playing field, they’ve been able to move.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And they want a little bit of peace of mind.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the president and I are not going to rest until that playing field is leveled, they, in fact, have a clear shot, and they have peace of mind, until they can turn to their kid and say with a degree of confidence, “Honey, it’s going to be OK.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s going to be OK.” That’s what this is all about.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I want to thank you, as well, Martha, Danville, Kentucky, Centre College, and I want to thank you, Joe.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s been an honor to engage in this critical debate.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We face a very big choice.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What kind of country are we going to be?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What kind of country are we going to give our kids?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: President Obama, he had his chance.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He made his choices.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: His economic agenda, more spending, more borrowing, higher taxes, a government takeover of health care.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s not working.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s failed to create the jobs we need.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Twenty-three million Americans are struggling for work today.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Fifteen percent of Americans are in poverty.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is not what a real recovery looks like.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You deserve better.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney and I want to earn your support.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re offering real reforms for a real recovery for every American.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney — his experience, his ideas, his solutions — is uniquely qualified to get this job done.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: At a time when we have a jobs crisis in America, wouldn’t it be nice to have a job-creator in the White House?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The choice is clear: a stagnant economy that promotes more government dependency or a dynamic, growing economy that promotes opportunity and jobs.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney and I will not duck the tough issues, and we will not blame others for the next four years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We will take responsibility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we will not try to replace our founding principles.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We will reapply our founding principles.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The choice is clear, and the choice rests with you.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we ask you for your vote.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, thank you very much, Jim, for this opportunity.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to thank Governor Romney and the University of Denver for your hospitality.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are a lot of points I want to make tonight, but the most important one is that 20 years ago I became the luckiest man on Earth because Michelle Obama agreed to marry me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so I just want to wish, Sweetie, you happy anniversary and let you know that a year from now we will not be celebrating it in front of 40 million people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: (LAUGHTER) You know, four years ago we went through the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Millions of jobs were lost, the auto industry was on the brink of collapse.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The financial system had frozen up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And because of the resilience and the determination of the American people, we’ve begun to fight our way back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last 30 months, we’ve seen 5 million jobs in the private sector created.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The auto industry has come roaring back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And housing has begun to rise.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we all know that we’ve still got a lot of work to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question here tonight is not where we’ve been, but where we’re going.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney has a perspective that says if we cut taxes, skewed towards the wealthy, and roll back regulations, that we’ll be better off.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve got a different view.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think we’ve got to invest in education and training.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s important for us to develop new sources of energy here in America, that we change our tax code to make sure that we’re helping small businesses and companies that are investing here in the United States, that we take some of the money that we’re saving as we wind down two wars to rebuild America and that we reduce our deficit in a balanced way that allows us to make these critical investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it ultimately is going to be up to the voters — to you — which path we should take.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Are we going to double on top-down economic policies that helped to get us into this mess or do we embrace a new economic patriotism that says America does best when the middle class does best?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m looking forward to having that debate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jim.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s an honor to be here with you, and I appreciate the chance to be with the president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m pleased to be at the University of Denver, appreciate their welcome, and also the Presidential Commission on these debates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And congratulations to you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, on your anniversary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sure this was the most romantic place you could imagine, here — here with me.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I… (LAUGHTER) Congratulations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is obviously a very tender topic.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve had the occasion over the last couple of years of meeting people across the country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in Dayton, Ohio, and a woman grabbed my arm and she said, “I’ve been out of work since May.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Can you help me?” Ann yesterday was at a rally in Denver and a woman came up to her with a baby in her arms and said, “Ann, my husband has had four jobs in three years, part-time jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’s lost his most recent job and we’ve now just lost our home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Can you help us?” And the answer is, yes, we can help, but it’s going to take a different path.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not the one we’ve been on, not the one the president describes as a top-down, cut taxes for the rich.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not what I’m going to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan has five basic parts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One, get us energy independent, North American energy independent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That creates about 4 million jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, open up more trade, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Crack down on China, if and when they cheat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, make sure our people have the skills they need to succeed and the best schools in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re far away from that now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number four, get to us a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number five, champion small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s small business that creates the jobs in America, and over the last four years, small business people have decided that America may not be the place to open a new business because new business startups are down to a 30-year low.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, I’m concerned that the path that we’re on has just been unsuccessful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president has a view very similar to the view he had when he ran four years, that a bigger government, spending more, taxing more, regulating more — if you will, trickle-down government — would work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right answer for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll restore the vitality that gets America working again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, let me talk specifically about what I think we need to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First, we’ve got to improve our education system and we’ve made enormous progress drawing on ideas both from Democrats and Republicans that are already starting to show gains in some of the toughest to deal with schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got a program called Race to the Top that has prompted reforms in 46 states around the country, raising standards, improving how we train teachers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So now I want to hire another 100,000 new math and science teachers, and create 2 million more slots in our community colleges so that people can get trained for the jobs that are out there right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to make sure that we keep tuition low for our young people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to our tax code, Governor Romney and I both agree that our corporate tax rate is too high, so I want to lower it, particularly for manufacturing, taking it down to 25 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also want to close those loopholes that are giving incentives for companies that are shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to provide tax breaks for companies that are investing here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: On energy, Governor Romney and I, we both agree that we’ve got to boost American energy production, and oil and natural gas production are higher than they’ve been in years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that we’ve got to look at the energy sources of the future, like wind and solar and biofuels, and make those investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So all of this is possible.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, in order for us to do it, we do have to close our deficit, and one of the things I’m sure we’ll be discussing tonight is, how do we deal with our tax code?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And how do we make sure that we are reducing spending in a responsible way, but also, how do we have enough revenue to make those investments?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is where there’s a difference, because Governor Romney’s central economic plan calls for a $5 trillion tax cut — on top of the extension of the Bush tax cuts — that’s another trillion dollars — and $2 trillion in additional military spending that the military hasn’t asked for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s $8 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: How we pay for that, reduce the deficit, and make the investments that we need to make, without dumping those costs onto middle-class Americans, I think is one of the central questions of this campaign.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to clear up the record and go through it piece by piece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, I don’t have a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t have a tax cut of a scale that you’re talking about.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My view is that we ought to provide tax relief to people in the middle class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’m not going to reduce the share of taxes paid by high-income people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: High-income people are doing just fine in this economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ll do fine whether you’re president or I am.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The people who are having the hard time right now are middle- income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Under the president’s policies, middle-income Americans have been buried.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re just being crushed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle- income Americans have seen their income come down by $4,300.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a — this is a tax in and of itself.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll call it the economy tax.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s been crushing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: At the same time, gasoline prices have doubled under the president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Electric rates are up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Food prices are up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Health care costs have gone up by $2,500 a family.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle-income families are being crushed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so the question is how to get them going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ve described it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s energy and trade, the right kind of training programs, balancing our budget and helping small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those are the — the cornerstones of my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the president mentioned a couple of other ideas I’ll just note.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First, education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I agree: Education is key, particularly the future of our economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But our training programs right now, we’ve got 47 of them, housed in the federal government, reporting to eight different agencies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Overhead is overwhelming.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got to get those dollars back to the states and go to the workers so they can create their own pathways to get in the training they need for jobs that will really help them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The second area, taxation, we agree, we ought to bring the tax rates down.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I do, both for corporations and for individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But in order for us not to lose revenue, have the government run out of money, I also lower deductions and credits and exemptions, so that we keep taking in the same money when you also account for growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The third area, energy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Energy is critical, and the president pointed out correctly that production of oil and gas in the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But not due to his policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In spite of his policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, all of the increase in natural gas and oil has happened on private land, not on government land.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: On government land, your administration has cut the number of permits and licenses in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I’m president, I’ll double them, and also get the — the oil from offshore and Alaska.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ll bring that pipeline in from Canada.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, by the way, I like coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure we can continue to burn clean coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People in the coal industry feel like it’s getting crushed by your policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get America and North America energy independent so we can create those jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, with regards to that tax cut, look, I’m not looking to cut massive taxes and to reduce the — the revenues going to the government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My — my number-one principal is, there will be no tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to underline that: no tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I do want to reduce the burden being paid by middle-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — and to do that, that also means I cannot reduce the burden paid by high-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So any — any language to the contrary is simply not accurate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think — let’s talk about taxes, because I think it’s instructive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, four years ago, when I stood on this stage, I said that I would cut taxes for middle-class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly what I did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We cut taxes for middle-class families by about $3,600.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason is, because I believe that we do best when the middle class is doing well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And by giving them those tax cuts, they had a little more money in their pocket, and so maybe they can buy a new car.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are certainly in a better position to weather the extraordinary recession that we went through.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They can buy a computer for their kid who’s going off to college, which means they’re spending more money, businesses have more customers, businesses make more profits, and then hire more workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney’s proposal that he has been promoting for 18 months calls for a $5 trillion tax cut, on top of $2 trillion of additional spending for our military.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he is saying that he is going to pay for it by closing loopholes and deductions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The problem is that he’s been asked over 100 times how you would close those deductions and loopholes, and he hasn’t been able to identify them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I’m going to make an important point here, Jim.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When you add up all the loopholes and deductions that upper-income individuals can — are currently taking advantage of, you take those all away, you don’t come close to paying for $5 trillion in tax cuts and $2 trillion in additional military spending.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why independent studies looking at this said the only way to meet Governor Romney’s pledge of not reducing the deficit or — or — or not adding to the deficit is by burdening middle-class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The average middle-class family with children would pay about $2,000 more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, that’s not my analysis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the analysis of economists who have looked at this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and that kind of top — top-down economics, where folks at the top are doing well, so the average person making $3 million is getting a $250,000 tax break, while middle-class families are burdened further, that’s not what I believe is a recipe for economic growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, but — but virtually — virtually everything he just said about my tax plan is inaccurate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So if the tax plan he described were a tax plan I was asked to support, I’d say absolutely not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not looking for a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I’ve said is I won’t put in place a tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s part one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So there’s no economist that can say Mitt Romney’s tax plan adds $5 trillion if I say I will not add to the deficit with my tax plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, I will not reduce the share paid by high-income individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know that you and your running mate keep saying that and I know it’s a popular thing to say with a lot of people, but it’s just not the case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I’ve got five boys.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m used to people saying something that’s not always true, but just keep on repeating it and ultimately hoping I’ll believe it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But that — that is not the case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not reduce the taxes paid by high-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And number three, I will not under any circumstances raise taxes on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will lower taxes on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, you cite a study.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are six other studies that looked at the study you describe and say it’s completely wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I saw a study that came out today that said you’re going to raise taxes by $3,000 to $4,000 on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are all these studies out there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let’s get at the bottom line.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That is, I want to bring down rates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to bring the rates down, at the same time lower deductions and exemptions and credits and so forth, so we keep getting the revenue we need.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you’d think, well, then why lower the rates?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the reason is because small business pays that individual rate; 54 percent of America’s workers work in businesses that are taxed not at the corporate tax rate, but at the individual tax rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we lower that rate, they will be able to hire more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: For me, this is about jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is about getting jobs for the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, for 18 months he’s been running on this tax plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now, five weeks before the election, he’s saying that his big, bold idea is, “Never mind.” And the fact is that if you are lowering the rates the way you described, Governor, then it is not possible to come up with enough deductions and loopholes that only affect high-income individuals to avoid either raising the deficit or burdening the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s — it’s math.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s arithmetic.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney and I do share a deep interest in encouraging small-business growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So at the same time that my tax plan has already lowered taxes for 98 percent of families, I also lowered taxes for small businesses 18 times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I want to do is continue the tax rates — the tax cuts that we put into place for small businesses and families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I have said that for incomes over $250,000 a year, that we should go back to the rates that we had when Bill Clinton was president, when we created 23 million new jobs, went from deficit to surplus, and created a whole lot of millionaires to boot.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason this is important is because by doing that, we cannot only reduce the deficit, we cannot only encourage job growth through small businesses, but we’re also able to make the investments that are necessary in education or in energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we do have a difference, though, when it comes to definitions of small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Under — under my plan, 97 percent of small businesses would not see their income taxes go up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney says, well, those top 3 percent, they’re the job creators, they’d be burdened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But under Governor Romney’s definition, there are a whole bunch of millionaires and billionaires who are small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Donald Trump is a small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I know Donald Trump doesn’t like to think of himself as small anything, but — but that’s how you define small businesses if you’re getting business income.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that kind of approach, I believe, will not grow our economy, because the only way to pay for it without either burdening the middle class or blowing up our deficit is to make drastic cuts in things like education, making sure that we are continuing to invest in basic science and research, all the things that are helping America grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that would be a mistake.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, let me just come back on that — on that point, which is these…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … the small businesses we’re talking about…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s fun, isn’t it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, President, you’re — Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, you’re absolutely right, which is that, with regards to 97 percent of the businesses are not — not taxed at the 35 percent tax rate, they’re taxed at a lower rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But those businesses that are in the last 3 percent of businesses happen to employ half — half of all the people who work in small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those are the businesses that employ one-quarter of all the workers in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And your plan is to take their tax rate from 35 percent to 40 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, and — and I’ve talked to a guy who has a very small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’s in the electronics business in — in St.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He has four employees.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said he and his son calculated how much they pay in taxes, federal income tax, federal payroll tax, state income tax, state sales tax, state property tax, gasoline tax.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It added up to well over 50 percent of what they earned.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And your plan is to take the tax rate on successful small businesses from 35 percent to 40 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The National Federation of Independent Businesses has said that will cost 700,000 jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to cost jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so what I do is I bring down the tax rates, lower deductions and exemptions, the same idea behind Bowles-Simpson, by the way, get the rates down, lower deductions and exemptions, to create more jobs, because there’s nothing better for getting us to a balanced budget than having more people working, earning more money, paying more taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s by far the most effective and efficient way to get this budget balanced.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Jim, I — you may want to move onto another topic, but I — I would just say this to the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you believe that we can cut taxes by $5 trillion and add $2 trillion in additional spending that the military is not asking for, $7 trillion — just to give you a sense, over 10 years, that’s more than our entire defense budget — and you think that by closing loopholes and deductions for the well-to-do, somehow you will not end up picking up the tab, then Governor Romney’s plan may work for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think math, common sense, and our history shows us that’s not a recipe for job growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, we’ve tried this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve tried both approaches.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The approach that Governor Romney’s talking about is the same sales pitch that was made in 2001 and 2003, and we ended up with the slowest job growth in 50 years, we ended up moving from surplus to deficits, and it all culminated in the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bill Clinton tried the approach that I’m talking about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created 23 million new jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We went from deficit to surplus.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And businesses did very well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, in some ways, we’ve got some data on which approach is more likely to create jobs and opportunity for Americans and I believe that the economy works best when middle-class families are getting tax breaks so that they’ve got some money in their pockets, and those of us who have done extraordinarily well because of this magnificent country that we live in, that we can afford to do a little bit more to make sure we’re not blowing up the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, the president began this segment, so I think I get the last word.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, but he gets the first word of that segment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I get the last word (inaudible) I hope.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me just make this comment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think first of all, let me — let me repeat — let me repeat what I said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not in favor of a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is not to put in place any tax cut that will add to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s point one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So you may keep referring to it as a $5 trillion tax cut, but that’s not my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, let’s look at history.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is not like anything that’s been tried before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is to bring down rates, but also bring down deductions and exemptions and credits at the same time so the revenue stays in, but that we bring down rates to get more people working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is putting people back to work in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re suffering in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we talk about evidence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look at the evidence of the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely extraordinary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got 23 million people out of work or stopped looking for work in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s just — it’s — we’ve got — when the president took office, 32 million people on food stamps; 47 million on food stamps today; economic growth this year slower than last year, and last year slower than the year before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Going forward with the status quo is not going to cut it for the American people who are struggling today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m glad you raised that, and it’s a — it’s a critical issue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think it’s not just an economic issue, I think it’s a moral issue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think it’s, frankly, not moral for my generation to keep spending massively more than we take in, knowing those burdens are going to be passed on to the next generation and they’re going to be paying the interest and the principal all their lives.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the amount of debt we’re adding, at a trillion a year, is simply not moral.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So how do we deal with it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, mathematically, there are three ways that you can cut a deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One, of course, is to raise taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two is to cut spending.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And number is to grow the economy, because if more people work in a growing economy, they’re paying taxes, and you can get the job done that way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The presidents would — president would prefer raising taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The problem with raising taxes is that it slows down the rate of growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you could never quite get the job done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to lower spending and encourage economic growth at the same time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What things would I cut from spending?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, I will eliminate all programs by this test, if they don’t pass it: Is the program so critical it’s worth borrowing money from China to pay for it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if not, I’ll get rid of it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Obamacare’s on my list.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I apologize, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I use that term with all respect, by the way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I’ll get rid of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sorry, Jim, I’m going to stop the subsidy to PBS.

speaker: ROMNEY
