sentence: This is because younger people might be able to get health insurance for $5,000, young and healthy folks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Older folks, less healthy folks, what’s going to end up happening is that you’re going to be the only ones left in your employer-based system, your employers won’t be able to afford it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And once you’re out on your own with this $5,000 credit, Senator McCain, for the first time, is going to be taxing the health care benefits that you have from your employer.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is your plan, John.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For the first time in history, you will be taxing people’s health care benefits.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: By the way, the average policy costs about $12,000.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if you’ve got $5,000 and it’s going to cost you $12,000, that’s a loss for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Last point about Senator McCain’s plan is that insurers right now, the main restrictions on what they do is primarily state law and, under Senator McCain’s plan, those rules would be stripped away and you would start seeing a lot more insurance companies cherry-picking and excluding people from coverage.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That, I think, is a mistake and I think that this is a fundamental difference in our campaign and how we would approach health care.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Hey, Joe, you’re rich, congratulations, because what Joe wanted to do was buy the business that he’s been working for 10-12 hours a day, seven days a week, and you said that you wanted to spread the wealth, but — in other words, take Joe’s money and then you decide what to do with it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, Joe, you’re rich, congratulations, and you will then fall into the category where you’ll have to pay a fine if you don’t provide health insurance that Senator Obama mandates, not the kind that you think is best for your family, your children, your employees, but the kind that he mandates for you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s big government at its best.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, 95 percent of the people in America will receive more money under my plan because they will receive not only their present benefits, which may be taxed, which will be taxed, but then you add $5,000 onto it, except for those people who have the gold-plated Cadillac insurance policies that have to do with cosmetic surgery and transplants and all of those kinds of things.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the good thing about this is they’ll be able to go across America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The average cost of a health care insurance plan in America today is $5,800.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m going to give them $5,000 to take with them wherever they want to go, and this will give them affordability.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This will give them availability.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This will give them a chance to choose their own futures, not have Senator Obama and government decide that for them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This really gets down to the fundamental difference in our philosophies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you notice that in all of this proposal, Senator — government wants — Senator Obama wants government to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama wants government to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want, Joe, you to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to leave money in your pocket.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want you to be able to choose the health care for you and your family.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what I’m all about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ve got too much government and too much spending and the government is — the size of government has grown by 40 percent in the last eight years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can’t afford that in the next eight years and Senator Obama, with the Democrats in charge of Congress, things have gotten worse.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Have you noticed, they’ve been in charge the last two years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Very briefly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You all just heard my plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’ve got an employer-based health care plan, you keep it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, under Senator McCain’s plan there is a strong risk that people would lose their employer-based health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the choice you’ll have is having your employer no longer provide you health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And don’t take my word for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Chamber of Commerce, which generally doesn’t support a lot of Democrats, said that this plan could lead to the unraveling of the employer-based health care system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All I want to do, if you’ve already got health care, is lower your costs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That includes you, Joe.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would never and have never in all the years I’ve been there imposed a litmus test on any nominee to the court.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s not appropriate to do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I thought it was a bad decision.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think there were a lot of decisions that were bad.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think that decisions should rest in the hands of the states.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m a federalist.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I believe strongly that we should have nominees to the United States Supreme Court based on their qualifications rather than any litmus test.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, let me say that there was a time a few years ago when the United States Senate was about to blow up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Republicans wanted to have just a majority vote to confirm a judge and the Democrats were blocking in an unprecedented fashion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We got together seven Republicans, seven Democrats.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You were offered a chance to join.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You chose not to because you were afraid of the appointment of, quote, “conservative judges.” I voted for Justice Breyer and Justice Ginsburg.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Not because I agreed with their ideology, but because I thought they were qualified and that elections have consequences when presidents are nominated.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This is a very important issue we’re talking about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama voted against Justice Breyer and Justice Roberts on the grounds that they didn’t meet his ideological standards.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s not the way we should judge these nominees.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Elections have consequences.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They should be judged on their qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And so that’s what I will do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I will find the best people in the world — in the United States of America who have a history of strict adherence to the Constitution.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And not legislating from the bench.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would consider anyone in their qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I do not believe that someone who has supported Roe v.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Wade that would be part of those qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I certainly would not impose any litmus test.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it’s true that we shouldn’t apply a strict litmus test and the most important thing in any judge is their capacity to provide fairness and justice to the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it is true that this is going to be, I think, one of the most consequential decisions of the next president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is very likely that one of us will be making at least one and probably more than one appointments and Roe versus Wade probably hangs in the balance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now I would not provide a litmus test.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I am somebody who believes that Roe versus Wade was rightly decided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that abortion is a very difficult issue and it is a moral issue and one that I think good people on both sides can disagree on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what ultimately I believe is that women in consultation with their families, their doctors, their religious advisers, are in the best position to make this decision.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that the Constitution has a right to privacy in it that shouldn’t be subject to state referendum, any more than our First Amendment rights are subject to state referendum, any more than many of the other rights that we have should be subject to popular vote.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So this is going to be an important issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will look for those judges who have an outstanding judicial record, who have the intellect, and who hopefully have a sense of what real-world folks are going through.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ll just give you one quick example.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain and I disagreed recently when the Supreme Court made it more difficult for a woman named Lilly Ledbetter to press her claim for pay discrimination.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For years, she had been getting paid less than a man had been paid for doing the exact same job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when she brought a suit, saying equal pay for equal work, the judges said, well, you know, it’s taken you too long to bring this lawsuit, even though she didn’t know about it until fairly recently.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We tried to overturn it in the Senate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I supported that effort to provide better guidance to the courts; John McCain opposed it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that it’s important for judges to understand that if a woman is out there trying to raise a family, trying to support her family, and is being treated unfairly, then the court has to stand up, if nobody else will.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the kind of judge that I want.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obviously, that law waived the statute of limitations, which you could have gone back 20 or 30 years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It was a trial lawyer’s dream.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let me talk to you about an important aspect of this issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to change the culture of America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Those of us who are proudly pro-life understand that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s got to be courage and compassion that we show to a young woman who’s facing this terribly difficult decision.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama, as a member of the Illinois State Senate, voted in the Judiciary Committee against a law that would provide immediate medical attention to a child born of a failed abortion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted against that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And then, on the floor of the State Senate, as he did 130 times as a state senator, he voted present.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Then there was another bill before the Senate Judiciary Committee in the state of Illinois not that long ago, where he voted against a ban on partial-birth abortion, one of the late-term abortion, a really — one of the bad procedures, a terrible.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And then, on the floor of the Illinois State Senate, he voted present.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t know how you vote “present” on some of that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t know how you align yourself with the extreme aspect of the pro- abortion movement in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s his record, and that’s a matter of his record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And he’ll say it has something to do with Roe v.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Wade, about the Illinois State Senate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It was clear-cut votes that Senator Obama voted, I think, in direct contradiction to the feelings and views of mainstream America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Yes, let me respond to this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If it sounds incredible that I would vote to withhold lifesaving treatment from an infant, that’s because it’s not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — here are the facts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There was a bill that was put forward before the Illinois Senate that said you have to provide lifesaving treatment and that would have helped to undermine Roe v.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The fact is that there was already a law on the books in Illinois that required providing lifesaving treatment, which is why not only myself but pro-choice Republicans and Democrats voted against it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the Illinois Medical Society, the organization of doctors in Illinois, voted against it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their Hippocratic Oath would have required them to provide care, and there was already a law in the books.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: With respect to partial-birth abortion, I am completely supportive of a ban on late-term abortions, partial-birth or otherwise, as long as there’s an exception for the mother’s health and life, and this did not contain that exception.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I attempted, as many have in the past, of including that so that it is constitutional.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that was rejected, and that’s why I voted present, because I’m willing to support a ban on late-term abortions as long as we have that exception.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The last point I want to make on the issue of abortion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is an issue that — look, it divides us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And in some ways, it may be difficult to — to reconcile the two views.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there surely is some common ground when both those who believe in choice and those who are opposed to abortion can come together and say, “We should try to prevent unintended pregnancies by providing appropriate education to our youth, communicating that sexuality is sacred and that they should not be engaged in cavalier activity, and providing options for adoption, and helping single mothers if they want to choose to keep the baby.” Those are all things that we put in the Democratic platform for the first time this year, and I think that’s where we can find some common ground, because nobody’s pro-abortion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s always a tragic situation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We should try to reduce these circumstances.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Just again, the example of the eloquence of Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He’s health for the mother.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, that’s been stretched by the pro-abortion movement in America to mean almost anything.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s the extreme pro-abortion position, quote, “health.” But, look, Cindy and I are adoptive parents.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We know what a treasure and joy it is to have an adopted child in our lives.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ll do everything we can to improve adoption in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But that does not mean that we will cease to protect the rights of the unborn.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we have to come together.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we have to work together, and, of course, it’s vital that we do so and help these young women who are facing such a difficult decision, with a compassion, that we’ll help them with the adoptive services, with the courage to bring that child into this world and we’ll help take care of it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This probably has more to do with our economic future than anything and that means it also has a national security implication, because there’s never been a nation on earth that saw its economy decline and continued to maintain its primacy as a military power.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’ve got to get our education system right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, typically, what’s happened is that there’s been a debate between more money or reform, and I think we need both.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In some cases, we are going to have to invest.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Early childhood education, which closes the achievement gap, so that every child is prepared for school, every dollar we invest in that, we end up getting huge benefits with improved reading scores, reduced dropout rates, reduced delinquency rates.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s going to be critically important for us to recruit a generation of new teachers, an army of new teachers, especially in math and science, give them higher pay, give them more professional development and support in exchange for higher standards and accountability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think it’s important for us to make college affordable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now, I meet young people all across the country who either have decided not to go to college or if they’re going to college, they are taking on $20,000, $30,000, $50,000, $60,000 worth of debt, and it’s very difficult for them to go into some fields, like basic research in science, for example, thinking to themselves that they’re going to have a mortgage before they even buy a house.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I’ve proposed a $4,000 tuition credit, every student, every year, in exchange for some form of community service, whether it’s military service, whether it’s Peace Corps, whether it’s working in a community.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we do those things, then I believe that we can create a better school system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there’s one last ingredient that I just want to mention, and that’s parents.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can’t do it just in the schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Parents are going to have to show more responsibility.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’ve got to turn off the TV set, put away the video games, and, finally, start instilling that thirst for knowledge that our students need.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, it’s the civil rights issue of the 21st century.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There’s no doubt that we have achieved equal access to schools in America after a long and difficult and terrible struggle.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But what is the advantage in a low income area of sending a child to a failed school and that being your only choice?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So choice and competition amongst schools is one of the key elements that’s already been proven in places in like New Orleans and New York City and other places, where we have charter schools, where we take good teachers and we reward them and promote them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we find bad teachers another line of work.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we have to be able to give parents the same choice, frankly, that Senator Obama and Mrs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obama had and Cindy and I had to send our kids to the school — their kids to the school of their choice.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Charter schools aren’t the only answer, but they’re providing competition.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They are providing the kind of competitions that have upgraded both schools — types of schools.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, throwing money at the problem is not the answer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You will find that some of the worst school systems in America get the most money per student.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I believe that we need to reward these good teachers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to encourage programs such as Teach for America and Troops to Teachers where people, after having served in the military, can go right to teaching and not have to take these examinations which — or have the certification that some are required in some states.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, we must improve education in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: As far as college education is concerned, we need to make those student loans available.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to give them a repayment schedule that they can meet.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have full student loan program for in-state tuition.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we certainly need to adjust the certain loan eligibility to inflation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, we have a tradition of local control of the schools and that’s a tradition that has served us well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I do think that it is important for the federal government to step up and help local school districts do some of the things they need to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now we tried to do this under President Bush.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He put forward No Child Left Behind.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Unfortunately, they left the money behind for No Child Left Behind.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And local school districts end up having more of a burden, a bunch of unfunded mandates, the same kind of thing that happened with special education where we did the right thing by saying every school should provide education to kids with special needs, but we never followed through on the promise of funding, and that left local school districts very cash-strapped.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I want to do is focus on early childhood education, providing teachers higher salaries in exchange for more support.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain and I actually agree on two things that he just mentioned.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Charter schools, I doubled the number of charter schools in Illinois despite some reservations from teachers unions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s important to foster competition inside the public schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we also agree on the need for making sure that if we have bad teachers that they are swiftly — after given an opportunity to prove themselves, if they can’t hack it, then we need to move on because our kids have to have their best future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Where we disagree is on the idea that we can somehow give out vouchers — give vouchers as a way of securing the problems in our education system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I also have to disagree on Senator McCain’s record when it comes to college accessibility and affordability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Recently his key economic adviser was asked about why he didn’t seem to have some specific programs to help young people go to college and the response was, well, you know, we can’t give money to every interest group that comes along.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I don’t think America’s youth are interest groups, I think they’re our future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is an example of where we are going to have to prioritize.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can’t say we’re going to do things and then not explain in concrete terms how we’re going to pay for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’re going to do some of the things you mentioned, like lowering loan rates or what have you, somebody has got to pay for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not going to happen on its own.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m sure you’re aware, Senator Obama, of the program in the Washington, D.C., school system where vouchers are provided and there’s a certain number, I think it’s a thousand and some and some 9,000 parents asked to be eligible for that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because they wanted to have the same choice that you and I and Cindy and your wife have had.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that is because they wanted to choose the school that they thought was best for their children.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we all know the state of the Washington, D.C., school system.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was vouchers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was vouchers, Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’m frankly surprised you didn’t pay more attention to that example.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now as far as the No Child Left Behind is concerned, it was a great first beginning in my view.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It had its flaws, it had its problems, the first time we had looked at the issue of education in America from a nationwide perspective.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we need to fix a lot of the problems.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to sit down and reauthorize it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But, again, spending more money isn’t always the answer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think the Head Start program is a great program.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: A lot of people, including me, said, look, it’s not doing what it should do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: By the third grade many times children who were in the Head Start program aren’t any better off than the others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s reform it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s reform it and fund it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was, of course, out-of-bounds by the Democrats.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to reform these programs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have transparency.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have rewards.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s a system that cries out for accountability and transparency and the adequate funding.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I just said to you earlier, town hall meeting after town hall meeting, parents come with kids, children — precious children who have autism.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Sarah Palin knows about that better than most.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ll find and we’ll spend the money, research, to find the cause of autism.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ll care for these young children.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And all Americans will open their wallets and their hearts to do so.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But to have a situation, as you mentioned in our earlier comments, that the most expensive education in the world is in the United States of America also means that it cries out for reform, as well.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I will support those reforms, and I will fund the ones that are reformed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I’m not going to continue to throw money at a problem.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve got to tell you that vouchers, where they are requested and where they are agreed to, are a good and workable system.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s been proven.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ll just make a quick comment about vouchers in D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain’s absolutely right: The D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: school system is in terrible shape, and it has been for a very long time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got a wonderful new superintendent there who’s working very hard with the young mayor there to try…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Who supports vouchers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … who initiated — actually, supports charters.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She supports vouchers, also.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But the — but here’s the thing, is that, even if Senator McCain were to say that vouchers were the way to go — I disagree with him on this, because the data doesn’t show that it actually solves the problem — the centerpiece of Senator McCain’s education policy is to increase the voucher program in D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: by 2,000 slots.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That leaves all of you who live in the other 50 states without an education reform policy from Senator McCain.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if we are going to be serious about this issue, we’ve got to have a president who is going to tackle it head-on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what I intend to do as president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because there’s not enough vouchers; therefore, we shouldn’t do it, even though it’s working.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you again, Bob.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thanks to Hofstra.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s great to be with you again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think we’ve had a very healthy discussion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, as I said in my opening remarks, these are very difficult times and challenges for America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they were graphically demonstrated again today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: America needs a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We cannot be satisfied with what we’ve been doing for the last eight years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have a record of reform, and taking on my party, the other party, the special interests, whether it be an HMO Patients’ Bill of Rights, or trying to clean up the campaign finance system in — in this country, or whether it be establishment of a 9/11 Commission, I have a long record of it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve been a careful steward of your tax dollars.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to make health care affordable and available.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to make quality education there for all of our citizens, not just the privileged few.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to stop the spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to stop the spending, which has mortgaged your children’s futures.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: All of these things and all the promises and commitments that Senator Obama and I made (inaudible) made to you tonight will base — will be based on whether you can trust us or not to be careful stewards of your tax dollar, to make sure America is safe and secure and prosperous, to make sure we reform the institutions of government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s why I’ve asked you not only to examine my record, but my proposals for the future of this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve spent my entire life in the service of this nation and putting my country first.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thereâ€™s a long line of McCains that have served our country for a long time in war and in peace, it’s been the great honor of my life, and I’ve been proud to serve.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I hope you’ll give me an opportunity to serve again.

speaker: CLINTON

sentence: Well, thank you, Lester, and thanks to Hofstra for hosting us.

sentence: First, we have to build an economy that works for everyone, not just those at the top.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: She’s got experience, but it’s bad experience.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And this country can’t afford to have another four years of that kind of experience.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, one thing.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: One thing, Lester.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: You know, he tried to switch from looks to stamina.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But this is a man who has called women pigs, slobs and dogs, and someone who has said pregnancy is an inconvenience to employers, who has said…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I never said that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: women don’t deserve equal pay unless they do as good a job as men.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I didn’t say that.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And one of the worst things he said was about a woman in a beauty contest.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: He loves beauty contests, supporting them and hanging around them.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And he called this woman “Miss Piggy.” Then he called her “Miss Housekeeping,” because she was Latina.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Donald, she has a name.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Where did you find this?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Where did you find this?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Her name is Alicia Machado.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Where did you find this?

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And she has become a U.S.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: citizen, and you can bet…

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: … she’s going to vote this November.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Let me just tell you… [applause]

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You know, Hillary is hitting me with tremendous commercials.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Some of it’s said in entertainment.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Some of it’s said—somebody who’s been very vicious to me, Rosie O’Donnell, I said very tough things to her, and I think everybody would agree that she deserves it and nobody feels sorry for her.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But you want to know the truth?

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I was going to say something…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: … extremely rough to Hillary, to her family, and I said to myself, “I can’t do it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I just can’t do it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s inappropriate.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s not nice.” But she spent hundreds of millions of dollars on negative ads on me, many of which are absolutely untrue.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They’re untrue.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And they’re misrepresentations.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I will tell you this, Lester: It’s not nice.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And I don’t deserve that.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But it’s certainly not a nice thing that she’s done.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s hundreds of millions of ads.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And the only gratifying thing is, I saw the polls come in today, and with all of that money…

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: … $200 million is spent, and I’m either winning or tied, and I’ve spent practically nothing.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: Well, I support our democracy.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And sometimes you win, sometimes you lose.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: But I certainly will support the outcome of this election.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: And I know Donald’s trying very hard to plant doubts about it, but I hope the people out there understand: This election’s really up to you.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: It’s not about us so much as it is about you and your families and the kind of country and future you want.

speaker: CLINTON
sentence: So I sure hope you will get out and vote as though your future depended on it, because I think it does.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I want to make America great again.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We are a nation that is seriously troubled.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We’re losing our jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: People are pouring into our country.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: The other day, we were deporting 800 people.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And perhaps they passed the wrong button, they pressed the wrong button, or perhaps worse than that, it was corruption, but these people that we were going to deport for good reason ended up becoming citizens.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Ended up becoming citizens.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And it was 800.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: And now it turns out it might be 1,800, and they don’t even know.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Look, here’s the story.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I want to make America great again.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I’m going to be able to do it.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I don’t believe Hillary will.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: The answer is, if she wins, I will absolutely support her.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, thank you for being here tonight, and, Governor Pence, welcome.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is so great to be back at Longwood University in Farmville, Virginia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is a very special place.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Sixty-five years ago, a young, courageous woman, Barbara Johns, led a walkout of her high school, Moton High School.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She made history by protesting school segregation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She believed our nation was stronger together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that walkout led to the Brown v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Board of Education decision that moved us down the path toward equality.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am so proud to be running with another strong, history-making woman, Hillary Clinton, to be president of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m proud because her vision of stronger together, building an economy that works for all, not just those at the top, being safe in the world not only with a strong military, but also strong alliances to battle terrorism and climate change, and also to build a community of respect, just like Barbara Johns tried to do 65 years ago.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s why I’m so proud to be her running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary told me why she asked me to be her running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She said the test of a Clinton administration will not be the signing of a bill or the passage of a bill.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’ll be whether we can make somebody’s life better, whether we can make a classroom better learning environment for schoolkids or teachers, whether we can make a safer — it’s going to be about results.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And she said to me, you’ve been a missionary and a civil rights lawyer.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve been a city councilman and mayor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve been a lieutenant governor and governor and now a U.S.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think you will help me figure out how to govern this nation so that we always keep in mind that the success of the administration is the difference we make in people’s lives.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what I bring to the ticket, that experience having served at all levels of government.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But my primary role is to be Hillary Clinton’s right-hand person and strong supporter as she puts together the most historic administration possible.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I relish that role.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m so proud of her.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll just say this: We trust Hillary Clinton, my wife and I, and we trust her with the most important thing in our life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have a son deployed overseas in the Marine Corps right now.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We trust Hillary Clinton as president and commander-in-chief, but the thought of Donald Trump as commander-in-chief scares us to death.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first off, thank you, Elaine, and thank you to — thank you to Norwood University for their wonderful hospitality and the Commission on Presidential Debates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s deeply humbling for me to be here, to be surrounded by my — my wonderful family.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And, Senator Kaine, it’s an honor to be here with you, as well.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I just — I also want to say — I want to say thanks to everyone that’s looking in tonight, who understands what an enormously important time this is in the life of our nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: For the last seven-and-a-half years, we’ve seen America’s place in the world weakened.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve seen an economy stifled by more taxes, more regulation, a war on coal, and a failing health care reform come to be known as Obamacare, and the American people know that we need to make a change.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And so I want to thank all of you for being — being with us tonight.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I also want to thank Donald Trump for making that call and inviting us to be a part of this ticket.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I have to tell you, I’m a — I’m a small-town boy from a place not too different from Farmville.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I grew up with a cornfield in my backyard.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My grandfather had immigrated to this country when he was about my son’s age.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My mom and dad built a — everything that matters in a small town in Southern Indiana.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They built a family and — and a good name and a business.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And they raised a family.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I dreamed some day of representing my home town in Washington, D.C., but I — honestly, Elaine, I never imagined — never imagined I’d have the opportunity to be governor of the state that I love, let alone be sitting at a table like this in this kind of a position.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So to answer your question, I would say I — I would hope that if — if the responsibility ever fell to me in this role, that I would meet it with the way that I’m going to meet the responsibility should I be elected vice president of the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that’s to bring a lifetime of experience, a lifetime growing up in a small town, a lifetime where I’ve served in the Congress of the United States, where — where I’ve led a state that works in the great state of Indiana, and whatever other responsibilities might follow from this, I — I would hope and, frankly, I would pray to be able to meet that moment with that — that lifetime of experience.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me tell you why I trust Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what people should look at as they look at a public servant.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do they have a passion in their life that showed up before they were in public life?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And have they held onto that passion throughout their life, regardless of whether they were in office or not, succeeding or failing?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has that passion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: From a time as a kid in a Methodist youth group in the suburbs of Chicago, she has been focused on serving others with a special focus on empowering families and kids.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: As a civil rights lawyer in the South, with the Children’s Defense Fund, first lady of Arkansas and this country, senator, secretary of state, it’s always been about putting others first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s a sharp contrast with Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump always puts himself first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He built a business career, in the words of one of his own campaign staffers, “off the backs of the little guy.” And as a candidate, he started his campaign with a speech where he called Mexicans rapists and criminals, and he has pursued the discredited and really outrageous lie that President Obama wasn’t born in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is so painful to suggest that we go back to think about these days where an African-American could not be a citizen of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I can’t imagine how Governor Pence can defend the insult-driven selfish “me first” style of Donald Trump.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let me — let me say first and foremost that, Senator, you and Hillary Clinton would know a lot about an insult-driven campaign.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It really is remarkable.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: At a time when literally, in the wake of Hillary Clinton’s tenure as secretary of state, where she was the architect of the Obama administration’s foreign policy, we see entire portions of the world, particularly the wider Middle East, literally spinning out of control.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the situation we’re watching hour by hour in Syria today is the result of the failed foreign policy and the weak foreign policy that Hillary Clinton helped lead in this administration and create.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The newly emboldened — the aggression of Russia, whether it was in Ukraine or now their heavy-handed approach…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You guys love Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You both have said…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … their heavy-handed approach.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You both have said — you both have said Vladimir Putin is a better leader than the president.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But in the midst — Elaine, thank you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Thank you, Senator, I’ll…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys have praised Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How can that…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, Senator, I must have hit a…[crosstalk]…I must have hit a nerve here.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because at a time of great challenge in the life of this nation, where we’ve weakened America’s place in the world, stifled America’s economy, the campaign of Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine has been an avalanche of insults.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, to get to your question about trustworthiness, Donald Trump has built a business through hard times and through good times.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s brought an extraordinary business acumen.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s employed tens of thousands of people in this country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And paid few taxes and lost a billion a year.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But there’s a — there’s a reason why people question the trustworthiness of Hillary Clinton.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that’s because they’re paying attention.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the reality is, when she was secretary of state, Senator, come on.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She had a Clinton Foundation accepting contributions from foreign governments.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You are Donald Trump’s apprentice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me talk about this…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, I think I’m still on my time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I think — isn’t this a discussion?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, let’s talk about the state of…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let me interrupt — let me interrupt you and finish my sentence, if I can.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Finish your sentence.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation accepted foreign contributions from foreign governments and foreign donors while she was secretary of state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: OK, now I can weigh in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She had a private server…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, I get to weigh in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, let me just say this…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … that was discovered…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … keep that pay to play process out of the reach of the public.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence — Governor Pence doesn’t think the world’s going so well and he, you know, is going to say it’s everybody’s fault.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me tell you this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Hillary Clinton became secretary of state, Governor Pence, do you know that Osama bin Laden was alive?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you know that we had 175,000 troops deployed in the battlefield in Iraq and Afghanistan?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you know that Iran was racing toward a nuclear weapon and Russia was expanding its stockpile?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Under Secretary Clinton’s leadership, she was part of the national team, public safety team that went after and revived the dormant hunt against bin Laden and wiped him off the face of the Earth.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked to deal with the Russians to reduce their chemical weapons stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked a tough negotiation with nations around the world to eliminate the Iranian nuclear weapons program without firing a shot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Eliminate the Iranian nuclear weapons program?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Absolutely, without firing a shot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And instead of 175,000 American troops deployed overseas, we now have 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These are very, very good things.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Iraq has been overrun by ISIS, because Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, if you want to put more American troops in Iraq, you can propose that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton — Hillary Clinton — Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate a status of forces agreement…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, that is incorrect.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s incorrect.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And so we removed — we removed all of our…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … troops from Iraq, and ISIS was able to be conjured up in that vacuum.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I’d like to correct…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and overrun vast areas of Iraq.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor, President Bush said we would leave Iraq at the end of 2011.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, Elaine, Iraq didn’t want our troops to stay, and they wouldn’t give us the protection for our troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And guess what?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If a nation where our troops are serving does not want us to stay, we’re not going to stay without their protection.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was a failure of the secretary of state…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If Governor Pence wants to put more troops back in Iraq, that’s…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I think the fact that — that under this past administration of which Hillary Clinton was a part, we’ve almost doubled the national debt is atrocious.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I’m very proud of the fact that — I come from a state that works.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The state of Indiana has balanced budgets.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We cut taxes, we’ve made record investments in education and in infrastructure, and I still finish my term with $2 billion in the bank.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s a little bit different than when Senator Kaine was governor here in Virginia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He actually — he actually tried to raise taxes by about $4 billion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He left his state about $2 billion in the hole.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: In the state of Indiana, we’ve cut unemployment in half; unemployment doubled when he was governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I think he’s a very fitting running mate for Hillary Clinton, because in the wake of a season where American families are struggling in this economy under the weight of higher taxes and Obamacare and the war on coal and the stifling avalanche of regulation coming out of this administration, Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want more of the same.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It really is remarkable that they actually are advocating a trillion dollars in tax increases, which I get that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You tried to raise taxes here in Virginia and were unsuccessful.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But a trillion dollars in tax increases, more regulation, more of the same war on coal, and more of Obamacare that now even former President Bill Clinton calls Obamacare a crazy plan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to build on Obamacare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They want to expand it into a single-payer program.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And for all the world, Hillary Clinton just thinks Obamacare is a good start.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, Donald Trump and I have a plan to get this economy moving again just the way that it worked in the 1980s, just the way it worked in the 1960s, and that is by lowering taxes across the board for working families, small businesses and family farms, ending the war on coal that is hurting jobs and hurting this economy even here in Virginia, repealing Obamacare lock, stock, and barrel, and repealing all of the executive orders that Barack Obama has signed that are stifling economic growth in this economy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can get America moving again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Put on top of that the kind of trade deals that’ll put the American worker first, and you’ve got a prescription for real growth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when you get the economy growing, Elaine, that’s when you can deal with the national debt.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When we get back to 3.5 percent to 4 percent growth with Donald Trump’s plan will do, then we’re going to have the resources to meet our nation’s needs at home and abroad, and we’re going to have the ability to bring down the national debt.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, on the economy, there’s a fundamental choice for the American electorate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you want a “you’re hired” president in Hillary Clinton or do you want a “you’re fired” president in Donald Trump?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think that’s not such a hard choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I have a plan that’s on the table that’s a “you’re hired” plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Five components.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First thing we do is we invest in manufacturing, infrastructure, and research in the clean energy jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second thing is we invest in our workforce, from pre-K education to great teachers to debt-free college and tuition-free college for families that make less than $125,000 a year.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Third, we promote fairness by raising the minimum wage, so you can’t work full-time and be under the poverty level, and by paying women equal pay for equal work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fourth, we promote small business growth, just as we’ve done in Virginia, to make it easier to start and grow small businesses.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I each grew up in small-business families.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My dad, who ran an iron working and welding shop, is here tonight.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, fifth, we have a tax plan that targets tax relief to middle- class individuals and small businesses and asks those at the very top who’ve benefited as we’ve come out of recession to pay more.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump plan is a different plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s a “you’re fired” plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And there’s two key elements to it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, Donald Trump said wages are too high.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And both Donald Trump and Mike Pence think we ought to eliminate the federal minimum wage.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Mike Pence, when he was in Congress, voted against raising the minimum wage above $5.15.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he has been a one-man bulwark against minimum wage increases in Indiana.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The second component of the plan is massive tax breaks for the very top, trillions of dollars of tax breaks for people just like Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The problem with this, Elaine, is that’s exactly what we did 10 years ago and it put the economy into the deepest recession — the deepest recession since the 1930s.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Independent analysts say the Clinton plan would grow the economy by 10.5 million jobs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump plan would cost 3.5 million jobs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Donald Trump — why would he do this?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Because his tax plan basically helps him.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if he ever met his promise and he gave his tax returns to the American public like he said he would, we would see just how much his economic plan is really a Trump-first plan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first, let me say, I appreciated the “you’re hired,” “you’re fired” thing, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You use that a whole lot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I think your running mate used a lot of pre-done lines.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, what — what you all just heard out there is more taxes, $2 trillion in more spending, more deficits, more debt, more government.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if you think that’s all working, then you look at the other side of the table.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, the truth of the matter is, the policies of this administration, which Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine want to continue, have run this economy into a ditch.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re in the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fifteen million new jobs?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … slowest economic recovery since the Great Depression.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Fifteen million new jobs?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are millions more people living in poverty today than the day that Barack Obama with Hillary Clinton at his side…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the poverty level and the median income…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … stepped into the Oval Office.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … improved dramatically between 2014 and 2015.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You — honestly, Senator, you can roll out the numbers and the sunny side, but I got to tell you, people in Scranton know different.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: People in Fort Wayne, Indiana, know different.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, this economy is struggling.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The answer to this economy is not more taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But it’s not the giveaway tax relief to the folks at the top.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s not more spending…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am interested to hear whether he’ll defend his running mate’s not releasing taxes and not paying taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Absolutely I will.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, this is probably the difference between Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And, I mean, Hillary Clinton and Senator Kaine — God bless you for it, career public servants, that’s great — Donald Trump is a businessman, not a career politician.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He actually built a business.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Those tax returns that were — that came out publicly this week show that he faced some pretty tough times 20 years ago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But like virtually every other business, including the New York Times not too long ago, he used what’s called net operating loss.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have a tax code, Senator, that actually is designed to encourage entrepreneurship in this country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But why won’t he release his tax returns?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, we’re answering the question about — about a business thing, is he…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I do want to come back to that, but…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: His tax returns — his tax returns showed he went through a very difficult time, but he used the tax code just the way it’s supposed to be used.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he did it brilliantly.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How do you know that?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You haven’t seen his tax returns.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He created a runway — because he’s created a business that’s worth billions of dollars today.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How do you know that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with regard to paying taxes, this whole riff about not paying taxes and people saying he didn’t pay taxes for years, Donald Trump has created tens of thousands of jobs.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he’s paid payroll taxes, sales taxes, property taxes…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me talk about something.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The only issue on taxes — Hillary Clinton is going to raise taxes, and Donald Trump and I are going to cut them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump started this campaign in 2014 and he said, “If I run for president, I will absolutely release my taxes.” He’s broken his first…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he will.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s broken his first promise.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second, he stood on the stage…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He hasn’t broken his promise.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He said he’s…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He stood on the stage last week and when Hillary said, you haven’t been paying taxes, he said, “That makes me smart.” So it’s smart not to pay for our military?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s smart not to pay for veterans?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s smart not to pay for teachers?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I guess all of us who do pay for those things, I guess we’re stupid.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the last thing I’ll say is this…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, do you take all the deductions that you’re entitled to?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The last thing — the last thing I want to ask Governor Pence is…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence had to give Donald Trump his tax returns to show he was qualified to be vice president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump must give the American public his tax returns to show that he’s qualified to be president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he’s breaking his promise.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, I have to respond to this.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’ll be — I’ll be very respectful.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, Donald Trump has filed over 100 pages of financial disclosure, which is what the law requires.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he said he would release his tax returns.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The American people can review that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And he’s going — Senator, he’s going to release his tax returns when the audit is over…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Richard Nixon released tax returns when he was under audit.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They’re going to raise your taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to cut your taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you can’t meet Nixon’s standard…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’re having fun up here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, we’re going to protect Social Security, which is one of the greatest programs that the American government has ever done.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It happened at a time when you would work your whole life, your whole life, raising your kids, working, being a Little League coach or a Sunday school teacher, and then you would retire into poverty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Social Security has enabled people to retire with dignity and overwhelmingly not be in poverty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have to keep it solvent.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we will keep it solvent.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ll look for strategies like adjusting the payroll tax cap upward in order to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what Hillary and I will not do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I want to make this very plain.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will never, ever engage in a risky scheme to privatize Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump wrote a book and he said Social Security is a Ponzi scheme and privatization would be good for all of us.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when Congressman Pence was in Congress, he was the chief cheerleader for the privatization of Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Even after President Bush stopped pushing for it, Congressman Pence kept pushing for it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’re going to stand up against efforts to privatize Social Security.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ll look for ways to keep it solvent going forward, focusing primarily on the payroll tax cap.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thanks, Elaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There they go again.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go read — go read the book.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: All Donald Trump — all Donald Trump and I have said about Social Security is we’re going to meet our obligations to our seniors.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go read the book.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve said we’re going to meet the obligations of Medicare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s what this campaign is really about, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I get, this is — this is the old scare tactic that they roll out…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But — but you have a voting record, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I get all of that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I just, look…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There’s a question that you asked a little bit earlier that I want to go back to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I can’t believe that you won’t defend your own voting record.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I have to go back to.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, I — you’re running with Hillary Clinton, who wants to raise taxes by $1 trillion, increase spending by $2 trillion, and you say you’re going to keep the promises of Social Security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I are going to cut taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to — we’re going to — we’re going to…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’re not going to cut taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’re going to raise taxes on the middle class.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … reform government programs so we can meet the obligations of Social Security and Medicare.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Stay on the path that your party has us on, we’re going to be in a — in a mountain range of debt.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’re going to face hard choices and…[crosstalk] Gentleman, I want to move on now.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You did ask this question about debt, and the debt explosion on the Trump plan is much, much bigger than anything on the Clinton side.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Three hundred and five economists said your plan is bad for the economy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, I think that’s a very fair comment.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think we put a lot on police shoulders.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And this is something I got a lot of scar tissue and experience on.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was a city councilman and mayor in Richmond.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when I came in, we had one of the highest homicide rates in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We fought very, very hard over the course of my time in local office with our police department, and we reduced our homicide rate nearly in half.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And then when I was governor of Virginia, we worked hard, too.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we did something we had really wanted to do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: For the first time ever, we cracked the top 10, 10 safest states, because we worked together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s what I learned as a mayor and a governor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The way you make communities safer and the way you make police safer is through community policing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You build the bonds between the community and the police force, build bonds of understanding, and then when people feel comfortable in their communities, that gap between the police and the communities they serve narrows.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when that gap narrows, it’s safer for the communities and it’s safer for the police.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That model still works across our country, but there are some other models that don’t work, an overly aggressive, more militarized model.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump recently said we need to do more stop-and-frisk around the country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That would be a big mistake because it polarizes the relationship between the police and the community.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So here’s what we’ll do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We’ll focus on community policing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will focus on — and Hillary Clinton has rolled out a really comprehensive mental health reform package that she worked on with law enforcement professionals, and we will also fight the scourge of gun violence in the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m a gun-owner.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m a strong Second Amendment supporter.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I’ve got a lot of scar tissue, because when I was governor of Virginia, there was a horrible shooting at Virginia Tech, and we learned that through that painful situation that gaps in the background record check system should have been closed and it could have prevented that crime, and so we’re going to work to do things like close background record checks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if we do, we won’t have the tragedies that we did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: One of those killed at Virginia Tech was a guy named Liviu Librescu.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He was a 70-plus-year-old Romanian Holocaust survivor.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He had survived the Holocaust.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Then he survived the Soviet Union takeover of his country.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But then he was a visiting professor at Virginia Tech, and he couldn’t survive the scourge of gun violence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We can support the Second Amendment and do things like background record checks and make us safer, and that will make police safer, too.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You know, my uncle was a cop, a career cop, on the beat in downtown Chicago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He was my hero when I was growing up.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’d go up to visit my dad’s family in Chicago.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My three brothers and I would marvel at my uncle when he would come out in his uniform, sidearm at his side.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Police officers are the best of us.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the men and women, white, African-American, Asian, Latino, Hispanic, they put their lives on the line every single day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me say, at the risk of agreeing with you, community policing is a great idea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s worked in the Hoosier state.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we fully support that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I are going to make sure that law enforcement have the resources and the tools to be able to really restore law and order to the cities and communities in this nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s probably — probably why the 330,000 members of the Fraternal Order of Police endorsed Donald Trump as the next president of the United States of America, because they see his commitment to them.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They see his commitment to law and order.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But they also — they also hear the bad mouthing, the bad mouthing that comes from people that seize upon tragedy in the wake of police action shootings as — as a reason to — to use a broad brush to accuse law enforcement of — of implicit bias or institutional racism.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that really has got to stop.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, when an African-American police officer in Charlotte named Brentley Vinson, an all-star football player who went to Liberty University here in the state, came home, followed his dad into law enforcement, joined the force in Charlotte, joined the force in Charlotte in 2014, was involved in a police action shooting that claimed the life of Keith — Keith Lamont Scott, it was a tragedy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I — we — we mourn with those who mourn.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We — we grieve with those who grieve.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we’re saddened at the loss of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton actually referred to that moment as an example of implicit bias in the police force, where — where she used — when she was asked in the debate a week ago whether there was implicit bias in law enforcement, her only answer was that there’s implicit bias in everyone in the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I just think…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can I — can I explain…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … I just think what we ought to do is we ought to stop seizing on these moments of tragedy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We ought to assure the public that we’ll have a full and complete and transparent investigation whenever there’s a loss of life because of police action.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, Senator, please, you know, enough of this seeking every opportunity to demean law enforcement broadly by making the accusation of implicit bias every time tragedy occurs.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine — Elaine, people shouldn’t be afraid to bring up issues of bias in law enforcement.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you’re afraid to have…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m not afraid to bring that up.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if — if you’re afraid to have the discussion, you’ll never solve it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And so here’s — here’s an example, heartbreaking.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We would agree this is a heartbreaking example.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The guy, Philando Castile, who was killed in St.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Paul, he was a worker, a valued worker in a local school.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he was killed for no apparent reason in an incident that will be discussed and will be investigated.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But when folks went and explored this situation, what they found is that Philando Castile, who was a — they called him Mr.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Rogers with Dreadlocks in the school that he worked.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The kids loved him.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he had been stopped by police 40 or 50 times before that fatal incident.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you look at sentencing in this country, African-Americans and Latinos get sentenced for the same crimes at very different rates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We need criminal justice reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, we do.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Indiana has passed criminal justice reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I just want to say, those who say that we should not…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But that’s not what you’re talking about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … we should not be able to bring up and talk about bias in the system, we’ll never solve the problem…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, when African-American police officers involved in a police action shooting involving an African-American, why would Hillary Clinton accuse that African-American police officer of implicit bias?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I guess I can’t believe you are defending the position that there is no bias and it’s a topic we don’t even…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I did not make that statement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I have the deepest respect for Senator Scott, and he’s a close friend.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And what I would say is that we — we need to adopt criminal justice reform nationally.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I — I signed criminal justice reform in the state of Indiana, Senator, and we’re very proud of it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I worked when I was in Congress on the second chance act.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have got to do a better job recognizing and correcting the errors in the system that do reflect on institutional bias in criminal justice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what — what — what Donald Trump and I are saying is let’s not have the reflex of assuming the worst of men and women in law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We truly do believe that law enforcement is not a force for racism or division in our country…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, can I…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Law enforcement in this country is a force for good.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They are the — they truly are people that put their lives on the line every single day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I would — I would suggest to you, what we need to do is assert a stronger leadership at the national level to support law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just heard Senator Kaine reject stop-and-frisk.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I would suggest to you that the families that live in our inner cities that are besieged by crime…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me — let me…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, if I could — if I could jump in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ve heard Senator Scott make that eloquent plea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And look, criminal justice is about respecting the law and being respected by the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So there is a fundamental respect issue here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just want to talk about the tone that’s set from the top.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump during his campaign has called Mexicans rapists and criminals.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s called women slobs, pigs, dogs, disgusting.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I don’t like saying that in front of my wife and my mother.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He attacked an Indiana-born federal judge and said he was unqualified to hear a federal lawsuit because his parents were Mexican.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He went after John McCain, a POW, and said he wasn’t a hero because he’d been captured.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said African-Americans are living in Hell.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And he perpetrated this outrageous and bigoted lie that President Obama is not a U.S.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you want to have a society where people are respected and respect laws, you can’t have somebody at the top who demeans every group that he talks about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just — again, I cannot believe that Governor Pence will defend the insult-driven campaign that Donald Trump has run.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump’s laid out a plan to end illegal immigration once and for all in this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve been talking it to death for 20 years.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to continue the policies of open borders, amnesty, catch and release, sanctuary cities, all the things that are driving — that are driving wages down in this country, Senator, and also too often with criminal aliens in the country, it’s bringing heartbreak.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I — Donald Trump has a plan that he laid out in Arizona, that will deal systemically with illegal immigration, beginning with border security, internal enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s probably why for the first time in the history of Immigration and Customs Enforcement their union actually endorsed Donald Trump as the next president of the United States, because they know they need help to enforce the laws of this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Donald Trump has laid out a priority to remove criminal aliens, remove people that have overstayed their visas.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And — and once we have accomplished all of that, which will — which will strengthen our economy, strengthen the rule of law in the country and make our communities safer once the criminal aliens are out, then we’ll deal with those that remain.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I have to tell you, I just — I was listening to the avalanche of insults coming out of Senator Kaine a minute ago.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These were Donald’s — hold on a second, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s my time, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I apologize.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s your two minutes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I apologize.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I forgive you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He says ours is an insult-driven campaign.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Did you all just hear that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ours is an insult-driven campaign?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, to be honest with you, if Donald Trump had said all of the things that you’ve said he said in the way you said he said them, he still wouldn’t have a fraction of the insults that Hillary Clinton leveled when she said that half of our supporters were a basket of deplorables.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s — she said they were irredeemable, they were not American.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, it’s extraordinary.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then she labeled one after another “ism” on millions of Americans who believe that we can have a stronger America at home and abroad, who believe we can get this economy moving again, who believe that we can end illegal immigration once and for all.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So, Senator, this — this insult-driven campaign, I mean…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s small potatoes compared to Hillary Clinton…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: calling half of Donald Trump’s supporters a basket of deplorables.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton said something on the campaign trail, and the very next day, she said, you know what, I shouldn’t have said that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She said she shouldn’t have said half.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, that’s right, so now we’re even.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Look for Donald Trump apologizing to John McCain for saying he wasn’t a hero…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … to Donald Trump apologizing for calling women slobs, pigs, dogs, disgusting.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: She apologized for saying “half.”

speaker: KAINE
