sentence: Did Donald Trump apologize for taking after somebody in a Twitter war and making fun of her weight?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Did he apologize for saying African-Americans are living in Hell?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Did he apologize for saying President Obama was not even a citizen of the United States?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You will look in vain to see Donald Trump ever taking responsibility for anybody and apologizing.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Immigration.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There’s two plans on the table.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I believe in comprehensive immigration reform.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump believes in deportation nation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to pick your choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I want a bipartisan reform that will put keeping families together as the top goal, second, that will help focus enforcement efforts on those who are violent, third, that will do more border control, and, fourth, that will provide a path to citizenship for those who work hard, pay taxes, play by the rules, and take criminal background record checks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s our proposal.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump proposes to deport 16 million people, 11 million who are here without documents.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And both Donald Trump and Mike Pence want to get rid of birthright citizenship.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So if you’re born here, but your parents don’t have documents, they want to eliminate that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s another 4.5 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys — and Donald Trump has said it — deportation force.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They want to go house to house, school to school, business to business, and kick out 16 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I cannot believe…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s nonsense.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I cannot believe that Governor Pence would sit here and defend his running mate’s claim that we should create a deportation force to — so that they’ll all be gone.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, we have a deportation force.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s called Immigrations and Customs Enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the union for Immigrations and Customs Enforcement for the first time in their history endorsed Donald Trump to be the next president of the United States of America.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So you like the 16 million deportations?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, that’s — that’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, what you just heard is they have a plan for open borders, amnesty.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Our plan is like Ronald Reagan’s plan from 1986.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They call it comprehensive immigration reform — they call it comprehensive immigration reform on Capitol Hill.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We all know the routine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s amnesty.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And you heard one of the last things he mentioned was border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s how Washington always plays it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They always say we’re going to do this, we’re going to do that, we’ll eventually get the border…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … border security three years ago, and Governor Pence was against it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ronald Reagan said a nation without borders is not a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump is committed to restoring the borders of this nation and securing our nation, enforcing our laws.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I think Donald Trump laid out a series of priorities that doesn’t ends with border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It begins with border security.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And after we secure the border, not only build a wall, but beneath the ground and in the air, we do internal enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But he said the focus has to be on criminal aliens.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just — we just had a conversation about law enforcement.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just had a conversation about the — the violence that’s besetting our cities.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The reality is that there’s heartbreak and tragedy that has struck American families because people that came into this country illegally are now involved in criminal enterprise and activity.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we don’t have the resources or the will to deport them systematically.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump has said we’re going to move those people out, people who’ve overstayed their visas.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to enforce the law of this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to strengthen Immigrations and Customs Enforcements with more resources and more personnel to be able to do that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then Donald Trump has made it clear, once we’ve done all of those things, that we’re going to reform the immigration system that we have…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I just have to correct Governor Pence….

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … where people can come into this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s the order that you should do it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Border security, removing criminal aliens, upholding with law, and then — but then, Senator, I’ll work you when you go back to the Senate, I promise you, we’ll work with you to reform the immigration system.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I look forward to working together in whatever capacities we serve in.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I just want to make it very, very clear that he’s trying to fuzz up what Donald Trump has said.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump spoke in Phoenix, he looked the audience in the eye and he said, no, we’re building a wall, and we’re deporting everybody.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said, quote, “They will all be gone.” “They will all be gone.” And this is one of these ones where you can just go to the tape on it and see what Donald Trump has said.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s talking about criminal aliens.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And to add to it, and to add to it, and to add to it, we are a nation of immigrants.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Mike Pence and I both are descended from immigrant families.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Some things, you know, maybe weren’t said so great about the Irish when they came, but we’ve done well by absorbing immigrants, and it’s made our nation stronger.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump says Mexicans are rapists and criminals, Mexican immigrants, when Donald Trump says about your judge, a Hoosier judge, he said that Judge Curiel was unqualified to hear a case because his parents were Mexican, I can’t imagine how you could defend that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat has decreased in some ways, because bin Laden is dead.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat has decreased in some ways because an Iranian nuclear weapons program has been stopped.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The terrorist threat to United States troops has been decreased in some ways because there’s not 175,000 in a dangerous part of the world.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There’s only 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But there are other parts of the world that are challenging.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let me tell you this: To beat terrorism, there’s only one candidate who can do it, and it’s Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Remember, Hillary Clinton was the senator from New York on 9/11.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was there at the World Trade Center when they were still searching for victims and survivors.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s seared onto her, the need to beat terrorism.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And she’s got a plan to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was part of the national security team that wiped out bin Laden.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s her plan to defeat ISIL.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: First, we’ve got to keep taking out their leaders on the battlefield.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was part of the team that got bin Laden, and she’ll lead the team that will get Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, the head of ISIS.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Second, we’ve got to disrupt financing networks, third, disrupt their ability to recruit on the Internet, in their safe havens.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, fourth, we also have to work with allies to share and surge intelligence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s the Hillary Clinton plan; she’s got the experience to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump can’t start a Twitter war with Miss Universe without shooting himself in the foot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump doesn’t have a plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said, “I have a secret plan,” and then he said, “Um, I know more than all the generals about ISIL.” And then he said, “I’m going to call the generals to help me figure out a plan.” And finally he said, “I’m going to fire all the generals.” He doesn’t have a plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But he does have dangerous ideas.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Here’s four.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He trash talks the military.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The military is a disaster, John McCain’s no hero, the generals need all to be fired, and I know more than them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wants to tear up alliances.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: NATO is obsolete, and we’ll only work together with Israel if they pay “big league.” Third, he loves dictators.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s got kind of a personal Mount Rushmore, Vladimir Putin, Kim Jong-un, Moammar Gadhafi…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and Saddam Hussein.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And last and most dangerously, Donald Trump believes — Donald Trump believes that the world will be safer if more nations have nuclear weapons.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s said Saudi Arabia should get them, Japan should get them, Korea should get them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And when he was confronted with this, and told, wait a minute, terrorists could get those, proliferation could lead to nuclear war, here’s what Donald Trump said, and I quote: “Go ahead, folks, enjoy yourselves.” I’d love to hear Governor Pence tell me what’s so enjoyable or comical about nuclear war.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Did you work on that one a long time?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because that had a lot of really creative lines in it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I’m going to see if you can defend any of it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, I can defend — I — I — I can — I can make very clear to the American people, after traveling millions of miles as our secretary of state, after being the architect of the foreign policy of this administration, America is less safe today than it was the day that Barack Obama became president of the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s absolutely inarguable.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve weakened America’s place in the world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s been a combination of factors, but mostly it’s been a lack of leadership.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, I will give you — and I was in Washington, D.C., on 9/11.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I saw the clouds of smoke rise from the Pentagon.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was in Virginia where the Pentagon’s…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I know you were.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We all lived through that day as a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was heartbreaking.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I want to give this president credit for bringing Osama bin Laden to justice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the truth is, Osama bin Laden led Al Qaida.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Our primary threat today is ISIS.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And because Hillary Clinton failed to renegotiate a status of forces agreement that would have allowed some American combat troops to remain in Iraq and secure the hard fought gains the American soldier had won by 2009, ISIS was able to be literally conjured up out of the desert, and it’s overrun vast areas that the American soldier had won in Operation Iraqi Freedom.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: My heart breaks for the likes of Lance Cpl.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Scott Zubowski.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He fell in Fallujah in 2005.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He fought hard through some of the most difficult days in Operation Iraqi Freedom, and he paid the ultimate sacrifice to defend our freedom and secure that nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that nation was secured in 2009.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But because Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama failed to provide a status of forces agreement and leave sufficient troops in there, we are back at war.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The president just ordered more troops on the ground.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We are back at war in Iraq.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And Scott Zubowski, whose mom would always come to Memorial Day events in Newcastle, Indiana, to see me, and I’d always give her a hug and tell her we’re never going to forget her son and we never will, Scott Zubowski and the sacrifices the American soldier made were squandered in Iraq because this administration created a vacuum in which ISIS was able to grow.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And a reference to the Iranian deal, the Iranian deal that Hillary Clinton initiated, $150 billion to the radical mullahs in Iran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Stopping a nuclear weapons program without firing a shot?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You didn’t stop the nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yes, we did.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You essentially…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Even the Israeli military says it stopped.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … guaranteed that Iran will someday become a nuclear power, because there’s no limitations once the period of time of the treaty comes off.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, I think it’s — I think it’s a great question, Elaine, but it really does begin with us reforming our immigration system and putting the interests, particularly the safety and security of the American people, first.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, Donald Trump has called for extreme vetting for people coming into this country so that we don’t bring people into the United States who are hostile to our Bill of Rights freedoms, who are hostile to the American way life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But also, Donald Trump and I are committed to suspending the Syrian refugee program and programs and immigration from areas of the world that have been compromised by terrorism.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine want to increase the Syrian refugee program by 500…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, I want to…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Yeah, and so — but first, you know, let’s make sure we’re putting the safety and security of the American people first instead of Hillary Clinton expanding the Syrian refugee program…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Or instead of you violating the Constitution by blocking people based on their national origin rather than whether they’re dangerous.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not — that’s absolutely false.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what the Seventh Circuit decided just — here’s the difference, Elaine.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Seventh Circuit…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have different views on — on refugee issues and on immigration.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I want to do enforcement based on, are people dangerous?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: These guys say all Mexicans are bad.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s absolutely false.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And with respect to refugees, we want to keep people out if they’re dangerous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump said keep them out if they’re Muslim.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … put a program in place to keep them out if they’re from Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yesterday an appellate court with three Republican judges struck down the Pence plan…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and said it was discriminatory…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And those judges — those judges said…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We should focus upon danger, not upon discrimination.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, to your point, those judges said it was because there wasn’t any evidence yet that — that ISIS had infiltrated the United States.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, Germany just arrested three Syrian refugees that were connected to ISIS.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But they told you there’s a right way and a wrong way to do it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, look, if you’re going to be critical of me on that, that’s fair game.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I will tell you, after two Syrian refugees were involved in the attack in Paris that is called Paris’ 9/11, as governor of the state of Indiana, I have no higher priority than the safety and security of the people of my state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor Pence…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So you bet I suspended that program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor Pence, I just…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I stand by that decision.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if I’m vice president of the United States or Donald Trump is president, we’re going to put the safety and security of the American people first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can we just be clear — Hillary and I will do immigration enforcement and we’ll vet refugees based on whether they’re dangerous or not.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We won’t do it based on discriminating against you from the country you come from or the religion that you practice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the problem with that…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That is completely antithetical to the Jeffersonian values of…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Elaine, the director of the FBI, our homeland security, said we can’t know for certain who these people are coming from Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yes, we can, and when we don’t let them know, we don’t let them in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So — the FBI…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When we don’t know who they are, we don’t let them in.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The FBI and homeland security said we can’t know for certain.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You’ve got to err on the side of the safety and security of the American people, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: By trashing all Syrians or trashing all Muslims?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: wants us to expand the Syrian refugee program…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to put the safety and security of the American people first.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Intelligence surge is two things, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s two things.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s, first, dramatically expanding our intelligence capacities by hiring great professionals, but also we’ve got some of the best intel and cyber employees in the world right here in the United States working for many of our private sector companies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So it involves increasing our own workforce, but striking great partnerships with some of our cyber and intel experts in the private sector so that we can, consistent with constitutional principles, gather more intelligence.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But the second piece of this is really, really important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It also means creating stronger alliances, because you gather intelligence and then you share your intelligence back and forth with allies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s how you find out who may be trying to recruit, who may be trying to come from one country to the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Alliances are critical.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s why Donald Trump’s claim that he wants to — that NATO is obsolete and that we need to get rid of NATO is so dangerous.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not his plan.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, he said NATO is obsolete.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And, look, if you put aside — push aside your alliances, who you’re going to share intelligence with?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton is the secretary of state who knows how to build alliances.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She built the sanctions regime around the world that stopped the Iranian nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what an intelligence surge means.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Better skill and capacity, but also better alliances.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Can I speak about the cybersecurity surge at all?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: First, Donald Trump just spoke about this issue this week.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have got to bring together the best resources of this country to understand that cyber warfare is the new warfare of the asymmetrical enemies that we face in this country.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I look forward if I’m privileged to be in this role of working with you in the Senate to make sure that we resource that effort.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We will work together in whatever roles we inhabit.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have an intelligence, sir.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I will also tell you that it’s important in this moment to remember that Hillary Clinton had a private server in her home that had classified information on it…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … about drone strikes, e-mails from the president of the United States of America were on there.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Her private server was subject to being hacked by foreign…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We could put cybersecurity first if we just make sure the next secretary of state doesn’t have a private server.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A full investigation concluded that not one reasonable prosecutor would take any additional step.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You don’t get to decide the rights and wrongs of this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We have a justice system that does that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And a Republican FBI director did an investigation and concluded that…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: If your son or my son handled classified information the way Hillary Clinton did…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … they’d be court martialed.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That is absolutely false and you know that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Absolutely true.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And you know that, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s absolutely true.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Because the FBI did an investigation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And they concluded that there was no reasonable prosecutor who would take it further.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The United States of America needs to begin to exercise strong leadership to protect the vulnerable citizens and over 100,000 children in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton’s top priority when she became secretary of state was the Russian reset, the Russian reset.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: After the Russian reset, the Russians invaded Ukraine and took over Crimea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the small and bullying leader of Russia is now dictating terms to the United States to the point where all the United States of America — the greatest nation on Earth — just withdraws from talks about a cease-fire while Vladimir Putin puts a missile defense system in Syria while he marshals the forces and begins — look, we have got to begin to lean into this with strong, broad-shouldered American leadership.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It begins by rebuilding our military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the Russians and the Chinese have been making enormous investments in the military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have the smallest Navy since 1916.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We have the lowest number of troops since the end of the Second World War.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve got to work with the Congress, and Donald Trump will, to rebuild our military and project American strength in the world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But about Aleppo and about Syria, I truly do believe that what America ought to do right now is immediately establish safe zones, so that families and vulnerable families with children can move out of those areas, work with our Arab partners, real time, right now, to make that happen.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And secondly, I just have to tell you that the provocations by Russia need to be met with American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if Russia chooses to be involved and continue, I should say, to be involved in this barbaric attack on civilians in Aleppo, the United States of America should be prepared to use military force to strike military targets of the Assad regime to prevent them from this humanitarian crisis that is taking place in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There’s a broad range of other things that we ought to do, as well.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We ought to deploy a missile defense shield to the Czech Republic and Poland which Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama pulled back on out of not wanting to offend the Russians back in 2009.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’ve just got to have American strength on the world stage.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When Donald Trump becomes president of the United States, the Russians and other countries in the world will know they’re dealing with a strong American president.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I also agree that the establishment of humanitarian zones in northern Syria with the provision of international human aid, consistent with the U.N.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Security Council resolution that was passed in February 2014, would be a very, very good idea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary also has the ability to stand up to Russia in a way that this ticket does not.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump, again and again, has praised Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And it’s clear that he has business dealings with Russian oligarchs who are very connected to Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump campaign management team had to be fired a month or so ago because of those shadowy connections with pro-Putin forces.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence made the odd claim, he said inarguably Vladimir Putin is a better leader than President Obama.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin has run his economy into the ground.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He persecutes LGBT folks and journalists.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you don’t know the difference between dictatorship and leadership, then you got to go back to a fifth-grade civics class.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll tell you what offends me…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, that offended me.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence just said — Governor Pence just said that Donald Trump will rebuild the military.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, he won’t.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump is avoiding paying taxes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The New York Times story — and we need to get this — but the New York Times suggested that he probably didn’t pay taxes for about 18 years starting in 1995.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Those years included the years of 9/11.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So get this.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: On 9/11, Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump’s hometown was attacked by the worst terrorist attack in the history of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Young men and women — young men and women signed up to serve in the military to fight terrorism.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton went to Washington to get funds to rebuild her city and protect first responders, but Donald Trump was fighting a very different fight.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was a fight to avoid paying taxes so that he wouldn’t support the fight against terror.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wouldn’t support troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He wouldn’t — he wouldn’t support — this is important, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When a guy running for president will not support the troops, not support veterans, not support teachers, that’s really important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I said about Aleppo, we do agree the notion is we have to create a humanitarian zone in northern Syria.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s very important.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first and foremost, Donald Trump supports our troops.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump supports our veterans.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He won’t pay taxes.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump has paid all the taxes that he’s — do you not take deductions?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: How does that work?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Honestly, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Honestly, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is about our troops.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It is about our troops.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand why you want to change — I understand why you want to change the subject.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: How can you support the troops if you won’t pay taxes?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I understand why you want to change the subject.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me be very clear on this Russian thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The larger question here…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Do you think Donald Trump is smart to not pay taxes?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: What we’re dealing with is the — you know, there’s an old proverb that says the Russian bear never dies, it just hibernates.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the truth of the matter is, the weak and feckless foreign policy of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama has awakened an aggression in Russia that first appeared a few years ago with their move in Georgia, now their move into Crimea, now their move into the wider Middle East.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And all the while, all we do is fold our arms and say we’re not having talks anymore.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: To answer your question, we just need American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We need to — we need to marshal the resources of our allies in the region, and in the immediate, we need to act and act now to get people out of harm’s way.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The — the safe zones would have to be — as the senator said, there’s already a framework for this that’s been recognized by the international community.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The United States of America needs to be prepared to work with our allies in the region to create a route for safe passage and then to protect people in those areas, including with a no-fly zone.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But, look, this is very tough stuff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I served on the Foreign Affairs Committee for a decade.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I traveled in and out of that region for 10 years.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I saw what the American soldier won in Operation Iraqi Freedom.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And to see the weak and feckless leadership that Hillary Clinton was the architect of and the foreign policy of the Obama administration…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, let me — let me come back…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … is deeply troubling to me.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That will all change the day Donald Trump becomes president of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … and talk about — let me talk about the things that Governor Pence doesn’t want to acknowledge, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge that we stopped the Iranian nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … that Hillary was part of the team that got bin Laden.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He doesn’t want to acknowledge…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … that it’s a good thing, not a bad thing, that it’s a good thing — not a bad thing — that we’re down from 175,000 troops deployed overseas to 15,000.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But let me tell you what will really make the Middle East dangerous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump’s idea that more nations should get nuclear weapons, Saudi Arabia, Japan, South Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Ronald Reagan said something really interesting about nuclear proliferation back in the 1980s.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said the problem with nuclear proliferation is that some fool or maniac could trigger a catastrophic event.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I think that’s who Governor Pence’s running mate is, exactly who Governor Reagan warned us about.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And come on.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, that was even beneath you and Hillary Clinton.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And that — that’s pretty low.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But do you — do you think — do you think we should have — more nuclear weapons in the world will make us safer?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, the…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what Donald Trump thinks.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Ronald Reagan also said nuclear war should never be fought because it can never be won.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And the United States of America needs to make investments in modernizing our nuclear force for both deterrence…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But can you defend Donald Trump’s claim that more nations should get nuclear weapons?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and assurance to our allies.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But let me go back to this Iran thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, he keeps saying that they prevented — that Hillary Clinton started the deal with the Iranians prevented Iran from getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what the Israeli joint chiefs of staff is saying right now.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, that’s not what — that’s not what Israel thinks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Gadi Eizenkot, you can go check it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You wouldn’t necessarily know that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Go to the tape.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I know you boycotted Prime Minister Netanyahu’s speech when he came before the Congress.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, I visited him in his office.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I visited him in his office.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You boycotted the speech.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The point is, what this Iran — so-called Iran deal did was essentially guarantee — I mean, when I was in Congress, I fought hard on a bipartisan basis with Republican and Democrat members to move forward the toughest sanctions, it — literally in the history of the United States, against Iran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And then Hillary used them to get a deal.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We were bringing them to heel, but the goal was always that we would only lift the sanctions if Iran permanently renounced their nuclear ambitions.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, let me just mention one thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They have not — Elaine, let me finish a sentence.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They have not renounced their nuclear ambitions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when the deal’s period runs out, there’s no limitation on them obtaining weapons.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and the fact that they got $1.7 billion in a ransom payment…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … is astonishing to the American people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Six times tonight, I have said to Governor Pence I can’t imagine how you can defend your running mate’s position on one issue after the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And in all six cases, he’s refused to defend his running mate.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, let’s — no, no, don’t put words in my mouth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He’s going…[crosstalk]

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yet he is asking everybody to vote for somebody that he cannot defend.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I just think that should be underlined.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, I’m — look…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m very, very happy to defend Donald Trump.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: If he wants to take these one at a time, I’ll take them one at a time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: More nations should get nuclear weapons.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Try to defend that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Don’t put words in my mouth.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, he never said that, Senator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He absolutely said it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Saudi Arabia, South Korea, Japan.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Most of the stuff you’ve said, he’s never said.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to be tough on Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So let’s start with not praising Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump and Mike Pence have said he’s a great leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Donald Trump has business…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, we haven’t.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … has business dealings — has business dealings with Russia that he refuses to disclose.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has gone toe-to-toe with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went toe-to-toe with Russia as secretary of state to do the New START Agreement to reduce Russia’s nuclear stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She’s had the experience doing it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went toe-to-toe with Russia and lodged protests when they went into Georgia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we’ve done the same thing about Ukraine, but more than launching protests, we’ve put punishing economic sanctions on Russia that we need to continue.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump, on the other hand, didn’t know that Russia had invaded the Crimea.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Oh, that’s nonsense.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He was on a TV show a couple months back, and he said, “I’ll guarantee you this, Russia’s not going into the Ukraine.” And he had to be reminded that they had gone into the Crimea two years before.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He knew that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton has gone toe-to-toe with Russia to work out a deal on New START.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She got them engaged in a meaningful way to cap Iran’s nuclear weapons program.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And yet she stood up to them on issues such as Syria and their invasion of Georgia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to have the ability to do that, and Hillary does.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: On the other hand, in Donald Trump, you have somebody who praises Vladimir Putin all the time.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: America should really wonder about a President Trump, who had a campaign manager with ties to Putin, pro-Putin elements in the Ukraine, who had to be fired for that reason.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They should wonder — when Donald Trump is sitting down with Vladimir Putin, is it going to be America’s bottom line or is it going to be Donald Trump’s bottom line that he’s going to be worried about with all of his business dealings?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now, this could be solved if Donald Trump would be willing to release his tax returns, as he told the American public that he would do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I know he’s laughing at this, but every president…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what’s it got to do with Russia?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Every president since Richard Nixon has done it, and Donald Trump has said I’m doing business with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The only way the American public will see whether he has a conflict of interest…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, he hasn’t said that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He has, actually.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thanks.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m just trying to keep up with the insult-driven campaign on the other side of the table.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You know, I’m just saying facts about your running mate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I know you can’t defend.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m happy to defend him, Senator.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Don’t put words in my mouth that I’m not defending him.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m happy to defend him.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Most of what you said is completely false, and the American people know that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’ll run through the list of things where you won’t defend…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: This isn’t the old days where you can just say stuff and people believe it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, this is the alternative universe of Washington, D.C., versus reality.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Hillary Clinton said her number-one priority was a reset with Russia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That reset resulted in the invasion of Ukraine, after they’d infiltrated with what are called little green men, Russian soldiers that were dressing up like Ukrainian dissidents, and then they moved all the way into Crimea, took over the Crimean Peninsula.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump knew that happened.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He basically was saying it’s not going to happen again.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The truth of the matter is that what you have in the rise of aggressive Russia, which has had — increased its influence in Iran, that’s now — now because of this deal is on a pathway in the future to obtain a nuclear — the leading state sponsor of terror in the world in Iran now has a closer working relationship with Russia because of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama’s foreign policy and $150 billion and sanctions all being lifted.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then, of course, Syria, I mean, it really is extraordinary that — Syria is imploding.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just asked a very thoughtful question about the disaster in Aleppo.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: ISIS is headquartered in Raqqa.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It is — ISIS from Raqqa has overrun vast areas that at great sacrifice the American soldier won in Operation Iraqi Freedom, and yet Senator Kaine still sits here, loyal soldier — I get all that — in saying that the foreign policy of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama somehow made the world more secure.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, it really is astonishing that on the day…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We wiped out the leader of Al Qaida.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … on the day that Iran released four American hostages…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We stopped Iran from getting nuclear weapons.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … we delivered $400 million in cash as a ransom payment for Americans held by the radical mullahs in Tehran.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we stopped a nuclear weapons program without a shot.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Plain and simple.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Business dealings.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump — that’s nonsense.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump is a strong leader…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Donald Trump’s son says that the Trump organization…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … who is going to lead with American strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to rebuild our military.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And let me — let me — this whole Putin thing.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Look, America is stronger than Russia.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Our economy is 16 times larger than the Russian economy.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: America’s political system is superior to the crony, corrupt capitalist system in Russia in every way.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When Donald Trump and I observe that, as I’ve said in Syria, in Iran, in Ukraine, that the small and bullying leader of Russia has been stronger on the world stage than this administration, that’s stating painful facts.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s not an endorsement of Vladimir Putin.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That’s an indictment of the weak and feckless leadership…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … of Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, this is one where we can just kind of go to the tape on it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But Governor Pence said, inarguably, Vladimir Putin is a better leader than President Obama.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: That is absolutely inaccurate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And — and — and I just think a guy who praises…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He said he’s stronger — he’s been stronger on the world stage.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: No, he said leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if — and I’ll just say this, Governor.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You just said better.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If you mistake leadership for dictatorship, and you can’t tell the difference, a country that’s running its economy into the ground…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Yeah, here we go.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: This is the grade school thing again?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … persecuting journalists…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Right, this is grade school.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … if you can’t tell the difference, you shouldn’t be commander-in-chief.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And with Donald Trump — Donald Trump’s sons say that they have all these business dealings with Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Those could be disclosed with tax returns, but they refuse to do them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Americans need to worry about whether Donald Trump will be watching out for America’s bottom line or his own bottom line.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin is a dictator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Vladimir Putin is a dictator.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s not a leader.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Anybody who thinks otherwise doesn’t know Russian history and they don’t know Vladimir Putin.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton knows exactly who this guy is.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: John McCain said, I look in his eyes and I see KGB.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary kind of has that same feeling.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So how do deal with him?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You’ve got to — we do have to deal with Russia in a lot of different ways.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: There are areas where we can cooperate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So it was Hillary Clinton who worked with Russia on the New START Treaty to reduce their nuclear weapons stockpile.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was Hillary Clinton that worked with Russia to get them engaged in a community of nations to stop the Iranian nuclear weapons program without firing a shot.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She’s not going around praising Vladimir Putin as a great guy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But she knows how to sit down at a table and negotiate tough deals.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is a very challenging part of the world, and we ought to have a commander-in-chief who is prepared and done it, rather than somebody who goes around praising Vladimir Putin as a great leader.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, first, we need to — we need to make a commitment to rebuild our military, including modernizing our nuclear forces.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And we also need — we also need an effective American diplomacy that will marshal the resources of nations in the Asian Pacific Rim to put pressure on North Korea, on Kim Jong-un, to abandon his nuclear ambitions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It has to remain the policy of the United States of America the denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula, plain and simple.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And when Donald Trump is president of the United States, we’re — we’re not going to have the — the kind of posture in the world that has Russia invading Crimea and Ukraine, that has the Chinese building new islands in the South China Sea, that has literally the world, including North Korea, flouting American power.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We’re going to — we’re going to go back to the days of peace through strength.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I have to tell you that — that all this talk about tax returns — and I get it, you know, you want to keep bringing that up.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It must have — must have…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Until he…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … done well in some focus group.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But here — Hillary Clinton and her husband set up a private foundation called the Clinton Foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: While she was secretary of state, the Clinton Foundation accepted tens of millions of dollars from foreign governments and foreign donors.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Now, you all need to know out there, this is basic stuff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Foreign donors, and certainly foreign governments, cannot participate in the American political process.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They cannot make financial contributions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But the Clintons figured out a way to create a foundation where foreign governments and foreign donors could donate millions of dollars.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And then we found, thanks to the good work of the Associated Press, that more than half of her private meetings when she was secretary of state were given to major donors of the Clinton Foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: When you talk about all these — all these baseless rumors about Russia and the rest, Hillary Clinton — you asked the trustworthy question at the very beginning — the reason…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … the reason the American people don’t trust Hillary Clinton is because they are looking at the pay to play politics that she operated with the Clinton Foundation through a private server…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … while she’s secretary of state.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And they’re saying enough is enough.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m going to talk about the foundation, and then I’ll talk about North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So, on the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I am glad to talk about the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation is one of the highest- rated charities in the world.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It provides AIDS drugs to about 11.5 million people.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It helps Americans deal with opioid overdoses.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It gets higher rankings for its charity than the American Red Cross does.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Clinton Foundation does an awful lot of good work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton as secretary of state took no action to benefit the foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The State Department did an investigation, and they concluded that everything Hillary Clinton did as secretary of state was completely in the interest of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So the foundation does good work.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And Hillary Clinton as secretary of state acted in the interests of the United States.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But let’s compare this now with the Trump organization and the Trump Foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The Trump organization is an octopus-like organization with tentacles all over the world whose conflict of interests could only be known if Donald Trump would release his tax returns.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He’s refused to do it.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: His sons have said that the organization has a lot of business dealings in Russia.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And remember, the Trump organization is not a non-profit.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s putting money into Donald Trump’s pockets and into the pockets of his children, whereas the Clinton Foundation is a non-profit and no Clinton family member draws any salary.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Trump Foundation is a non-profit.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: In addition, Donald Trump has a foundation.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The foundation was just fined for illegally contributing foundation dollars to a political campaign of a Florida attorney general.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They made an illegal contribution, and then they tried to hide it by disguising it to somebody else.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And the person they donated to was somebody whose office was charged with investigating Trump University.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is the difference between a foundation that does good work and a secretary of state who acted in accord with American interest and somebody who is conflicted and doing work around the world and won’t share with the American public what he’s doing and what those conflicts are.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, thank you.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The Trump Foundation is a private family foundation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: They give virtually every cent in the Trump Foundation to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Political contributions?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Less than ten cents on the dollar in the Clinton Foundation has gone to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A $20,000 portrait of Donald Trump?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Less than 10 cents on the dollar of the Clinton Foundation has gone to charitable causes.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Ninety percent.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It has been a platform for the Clintons to travel the world, to have staff.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But honestly, Senator, we would know a lot more about it if Hillary Clinton would just turn over the 33,000 e-mails…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … that she refused to turn over in her private server…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … and we’d have a much better picture of what the Clinton Foundation was about.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: If we — look, a president should take action to defend the United States against imminent threat.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You have to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: A president has to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Now exactly what action, you would have to determine what your intelligence was, how certain you were of that intelligence, but you would have to take action.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You asked the question about how do we deal with a North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m on the Foreign Relations Committee.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We just did an extensive sanctions package against North Korea.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And interestingly enough, Elaine, the U.N.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: followed and did this — virtually the same package.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Often China will use their veto in the Security Council to veto a package like that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: They’re starting to get worried about North Korea, too.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So they actually supported the sanctions package, even though many of the sanctions are against Chinese firms, Chinese financial institutions.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So we’re working together with China, and we need to.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: China’s another one of those relationships where it’s competitive, it’s also challenging, and in times like North Korea, we have to be able to cooperate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary understands that very well.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She went once famously to China and stood up at a human rights meeting and looked them in the eye and said, “Women’s rights are human rights.” They didn’t want her to say that, but she did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But she’s also worked on a lot of diplomatic and important diplomatic deals with China.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that’s what it’s going to take.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: The thing I would worry a little bit about is that Donald Trump owes about $650 million to banks, including the Bank of China.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m not sure he could stand up so tough to the people who have loaned him money.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Yeah, that’s an easy one for me, Elaine.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It’s an easy one.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m really fortunate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I grew up in a wonderful household with great Irish Catholic parents.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My mom and dad are sitting right here.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I was educated by Jesuits at Rockhurst High School in Kansas City.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: My 40th reunion is in 10 days.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I worked with Jesuit missionaries in Honduras, now nearly 35 years ago, and they were the heroes of my life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I try to practice my religion in a very devout way and follow the teachings of my church in my own personal life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I don’t believe in this nation, a First Amendment nation, where we don’t raise any religion over the other, and we allow people to worship as they please, that the doctrines of any one religion should be mandated for everyone.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: For me, the hardest struggle in my faith life was the Catholic Church is against the death penalty and so am I.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I was governor of a state, and the state law said that there was a death penalty for crimes if the jury determined them to be heinous.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And so I had to grapple with that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When I was running for governor, I was attacked pretty strongly because of my position on the death penalty.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I looked the voters of Virginia in the eye and said, look, this is my religion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I’m not going to change my religious practice to get one vote, but I know how to take an oath and uphold the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And if you elect me, I will uphold the law.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I was elected, and I did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: It was very, very difficult to allow executions to go forward, but in circumstances where I didn’t feel like there was a case for clemency, I told Virginia voters I would uphold the law, and I did.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That was a real struggle.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But I think it is really, really important that those of us who have deep faith lives don’t feel like we could just substitute our own views for everybody else in society, regardless of their views.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, it’s a wonderful question.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And my Christian faith is at the very heart of who I am.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I was also raised in a wonderful family of faith.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It was a church on Sunday morning and grace before dinner.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But my Christian faith became real for me when I made a personal decision for Christ when I was a freshman in college.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I’ve tried to live that out however imperfectly every day of my life since.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with my wife at my side, we’ve followed a calling into public service, where we’ve — we’ve tried to — we’ve tried to keep faith with the values that we cherish.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And with regard to when I struggle, I appreciate, and — and — and — I have a great deal of respect for Senator Kaine’s sincere faith.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s shared.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But for me, I would tell you that for me the sanctity of life proceeds out of the belief that — that ancient principle that — where God says before you were formed in the womb, I knew you, and so for my first time in public life, I sought to stand with great compassion for the sanctity of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: The state of Indiana has also sought to make sure that we expand alternatives in health care counseling for women, non-abortion alternatives.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m also very pleased at the fact we’re well on our way in Indiana to becoming the most pro-adoption state in America.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I think if you’re going to be pro-life, you should — you should be pro-adoption.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But what I can’t understand is with Hillary Clinton and now Senator Kaine at her side is to support a practice like partial-birth abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I mean, to hold to the view — and I know Senator Kaine, you hold pro-life views personally — but the very idea that a child that is almost born into the world could still have their life taken from them is just anathema to me.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I cannot — I can’t conscience about — about a party that supports that.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Or that — I know you’ve historically opposed taxpayer funding of abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But Hillary Clinton wants to — wants to repeal the longstanding provision in the law where we said we wouldn’t use taxpayer dollars to fund abortion.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: So for me, my faith informs my life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I try and spend a little time on my knees every day.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But it all for me begins with cherishing the dignity, the worth, the value of every human life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Elaine, this is a fundamental question, a fundamental question.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary and I are both people out of religious backgrounds, from Methodist church experience, which was really formative for her as a public servant.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But we really feel like you should live fully and with enthusiasm the commands of your faith.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But it is not the role of the public servant to mandate that for everybody else.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So let’s talk about abortion and choice.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Let’s talk about them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We support Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We support the constitutional right of American women to consult their own conscience, their own supportive partner, their own minister, but then make their own decision about pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s something we trust American women to do that.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we don’t think that women should be punished, as Donald Trump said they should, for making the decision to have an abortion.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor Pence wants to repeal Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: He said he wants to put it on the ash heap of history.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And we have some young people in the audience who weren’t even born when Roe was decided.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is pretty important.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Before Roe v.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Wade, states could pass criminal laws to do just that, to punish women if they made the choice to terminate a pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: I think you should live your moral values.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But the last thing, the very last thing that government should do is have laws that would punish women who make reproductive choices.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And that is the fundamental difference between a Clinton-Kaine ticket and a Trump-Pence ticket that wants to punish women who make that choice.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: No, it’s really not.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Donald Trump and I would never support legislation that punished women who made the heartbreaking choice to end a pregnancy.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Then why did Donald Trump say that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We just never would.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Why did he say that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Well, look, it’s — look, he’s not a polished politician like you and Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, I would admit that’s not a polished…[crosstalk]

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You know, things don’t always come out exactly the way he means them.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Well, can I say…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But I’m telling you what the policy of our administration would be.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Great line from the — great line from the gospel of Matthew.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: From the fullness of the heart, the mouth speaks.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: When Donald Trump says women should be punished or Mexicans are rapists and criminals…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I’m telling you…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … or John McCain is not a hero, he is showing you who he is.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Senator, you’ve whipped out that Mexican thing again.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Can you defend it?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are criminal aliens in this country, Tim, who have come into this country illegally who are perpetrating violence and taking American lives.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: You want to — you want to use a big broad brush against Mexicans on that?

speaker: PENCE
sentence: He also said and many of them are good people.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: You keep leaving that out of your quote.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And if you want me to go there, I’ll go there.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: But here’s — there is a choice, and it is a choice on life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I couldn’t be more proud to be standing with Donald Trump, who’s standing for the right to life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: It’s a principle that — Senator Kaine — and I’m very gentle about this, because I really do respect you — it’s a principle that you embrace.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I have appreciated the fact that you’ve supported the Hyde amendment, which bans the use of taxpayer funding for abortion, in the past, but that’s not Hillary Clinton’s view.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: People need to understand, we can come together as a nation.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can create a culture of life.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: More and more young people today are embracing life because we know we are — we’re better for it.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We can — like Mother Teresa said at that famous national prayer breakfast…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: This is important —

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … bring the — let’s welcome the children into our world.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: There are so many families around the country who can’t have children.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: We could improve adoption…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But, Governor…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: … so that families that can’t have children can adopt more readily those children from crisis pregnancies.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Governor, why don’t you trust women to make this choice for themselves?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: We can encourage people to support life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Of course we can.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But why don’t you trust women?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Why doesn’t Donald Trump trust women to make this choice for themselves?

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s what we ought to be doing in public life.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Living our lives of faith or motivation with enthusiasm and excitement, convincing each other, dialoguing with each other about important moral issues of the day…

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because there are…

speaker: KAINE
sentence: … but on fundamental issues of morality, we should let women make their own decisions.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: Because there is — a society can be judged by how it deals with its most vulnerable, the aged, the infirm, the disabled, and the unborn.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: I believe it with all my heart.

speaker: PENCE
sentence: And I couldn’t be more proud to be standing with a pro-life candidate in Donald Trump.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That’s a really important one.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: That may be the $64,000 question, because it has been a divisive campaign.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And again, Hillary is running a campaign about stronger together, and Donald Trump — and this is — this is not directed at this man, except to the extent that he can’t defend Donald Trump — Donald Trump has run a campaign that’s been about one insult after the next.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: But we do have to bring the country together.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: So here’s what we’ll do.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: Hillary Clinton was first lady, then senator for eight years and secretary of state.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I served in the Senate.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: And I’m really amazed, Elaine, as I talk to Republican senators, how well they regard and respect Hillary Clinton.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was on the Armed Services Committee.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She was on other committees.

speaker: KAINE
sentence: She worked across the aisle when she was first lady to get the CHIP program passed so that 8 million low-income kids have health insurance in this country, including 150,000 in Indiana.

speaker: KAINE

sentence: She’s raising the money from the people she wants to control.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Doesn’t work that way.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: But when I started this campaign, I started it very strongly.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It’s called “Make America Great Again.” We’re going to make America great.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have a depleted military.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: It has to be helped, has to be fixed.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We have the greatest people on Earth in our military.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We don’t take care of our veterans.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We take care of illegal immigrants, people that come into the country illegally, better than we take care of our vets.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: That can’t happen.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our policemen and women are disrespected.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We need law and order, but we need justice, too.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: Our inner cities are a disaster.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: You get shot walking to the store.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They have no education.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: They have no jobs.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: I will do more for African-Americans and Latinos than she can ever do in 10 lifetimes.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: All she’s done is talk to the African-Americans and to the Latinos, but they get the vote, and then they come back, they say, we’ll see you in four years.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We are going to make America strong again, and we are going to make America great again, and it has to start now.

speaker: TRUMP
sentence: We cannot take four more years of Barack Obama, and that’s what you get when you get her.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, thank you very much, Jim, for this opportunity.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to thank Governor Romney and the University of Denver for your hospitality.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are a lot of points I want to make tonight, but the most important one is that 20 years ago I became the luckiest man on Earth because Michelle Obama agreed to marry me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so I just want to wish, Sweetie, you happy anniversary and let you know that a year from now we will not be celebrating it in front of 40 million people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: (LAUGHTER) You know, four years ago we went through the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Millions of jobs were lost, the auto industry was on the brink of collapse.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The financial system had frozen up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And because of the resilience and the determination of the American people, we’ve begun to fight our way back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last 30 months, we’ve seen 5 million jobs in the private sector created.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The auto industry has come roaring back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And housing has begun to rise.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we all know that we’ve still got a lot of work to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question here tonight is not where we’ve been, but where we’re going.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney has a perspective that says if we cut taxes, skewed towards the wealthy, and roll back regulations, that we’ll be better off.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve got a different view.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think we’ve got to invest in education and training.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s important for us to develop new sources of energy here in America, that we change our tax code to make sure that we’re helping small businesses and companies that are investing here in the United States, that we take some of the money that we’re saving as we wind down two wars to rebuild America and that we reduce our deficit in a balanced way that allows us to make these critical investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it ultimately is going to be up to the voters — to you — which path we should take.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Are we going to double on top-down economic policies that helped to get us into this mess or do we embrace a new economic patriotism that says America does best when the middle class does best?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m looking forward to having that debate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jim.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s an honor to be here with you, and I appreciate the chance to be with the president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m pleased to be at the University of Denver, appreciate their welcome, and also the Presidential Commission on these debates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And congratulations to you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, on your anniversary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sure this was the most romantic place you could imagine, here — here with me.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I… (LAUGHTER) Congratulations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is obviously a very tender topic.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve had the occasion over the last couple of years of meeting people across the country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in Dayton, Ohio, and a woman grabbed my arm and she said, “I’ve been out of work since May.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Can you help me?” Ann yesterday was at a rally in Denver and a woman came up to her with a baby in her arms and said, “Ann, my husband has had four jobs in three years, part-time jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’s lost his most recent job and we’ve now just lost our home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Can you help us?” And the answer is, yes, we can help, but it’s going to take a different path.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not the one we’ve been on, not the one the president describes as a top-down, cut taxes for the rich.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not what I’m going to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan has five basic parts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One, get us energy independent, North American energy independent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That creates about 4 million jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, open up more trade, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Crack down on China, if and when they cheat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, make sure our people have the skills they need to succeed and the best schools in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re far away from that now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number four, get to us a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number five, champion small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s small business that creates the jobs in America, and over the last four years, small business people have decided that America may not be the place to open a new business because new business startups are down to a 30-year low.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, I’m concerned that the path that we’re on has just been unsuccessful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president has a view very similar to the view he had when he ran four years, that a bigger government, spending more, taxing more, regulating more — if you will, trickle-down government — would work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right answer for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll restore the vitality that gets America working again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, let me talk specifically about what I think we need to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First, we’ve got to improve our education system and we’ve made enormous progress drawing on ideas both from Democrats and Republicans that are already starting to show gains in some of the toughest to deal with schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got a program called Race to the Top that has prompted reforms in 46 states around the country, raising standards, improving how we train teachers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So now I want to hire another 100,000 new math and science teachers, and create 2 million more slots in our community colleges so that people can get trained for the jobs that are out there right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to make sure that we keep tuition low for our young people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to our tax code, Governor Romney and I both agree that our corporate tax rate is too high, so I want to lower it, particularly for manufacturing, taking it down to 25 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also want to close those loopholes that are giving incentives for companies that are shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to provide tax breaks for companies that are investing here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: On energy, Governor Romney and I, we both agree that we’ve got to boost American energy production, and oil and natural gas production are higher than they’ve been in years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that we’ve got to look at the energy sources of the future, like wind and solar and biofuels, and make those investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So all of this is possible.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, in order for us to do it, we do have to close our deficit, and one of the things I’m sure we’ll be discussing tonight is, how do we deal with our tax code?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And how do we make sure that we are reducing spending in a responsible way, but also, how do we have enough revenue to make those investments?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is where there’s a difference, because Governor Romney’s central economic plan calls for a $5 trillion tax cut — on top of the extension of the Bush tax cuts — that’s another trillion dollars — and $2 trillion in additional military spending that the military hasn’t asked for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s $8 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: How we pay for that, reduce the deficit, and make the investments that we need to make, without dumping those costs onto middle-class Americans, I think is one of the central questions of this campaign.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to clear up the record and go through it piece by piece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, I don’t have a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t have a tax cut of a scale that you’re talking about.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My view is that we ought to provide tax relief to people in the middle class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’m not going to reduce the share of taxes paid by high-income people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: High-income people are doing just fine in this economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ll do fine whether you’re president or I am.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The people who are having the hard time right now are middle- income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Under the president’s policies, middle-income Americans have been buried.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re just being crushed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle- income Americans have seen their income come down by $4,300.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a — this is a tax in and of itself.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll call it the economy tax.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s been crushing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: At the same time, gasoline prices have doubled under the president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Electric rates are up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Food prices are up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Health care costs have gone up by $2,500 a family.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle-income families are being crushed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so the question is how to get them going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ve described it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s energy and trade, the right kind of training programs, balancing our budget and helping small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those are the — the cornerstones of my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
