sentence: But the president mentioned a couple of other ideas I’ll just note.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First, education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I agree: Education is key, particularly the future of our economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But our training programs right now, we’ve got 47 of them, housed in the federal government, reporting to eight different agencies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Overhead is overwhelming.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got to get those dollars back to the states and go to the workers so they can create their own pathways to get in the training they need for jobs that will really help them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The second area, taxation, we agree, we ought to bring the tax rates down.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I do, both for corporations and for individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But in order for us not to lose revenue, have the government run out of money, I also lower deductions and credits and exemptions, so that we keep taking in the same money when you also account for growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The third area, energy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Energy is critical, and the president pointed out correctly that production of oil and gas in the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But not due to his policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In spite of his policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, all of the increase in natural gas and oil has happened on private land, not on government land.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: On government land, your administration has cut the number of permits and licenses in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I’m president, I’ll double them, and also get the — the oil from offshore and Alaska.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ll bring that pipeline in from Canada.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, by the way, I like coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure we can continue to burn clean coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People in the coal industry feel like it’s getting crushed by your policies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get America and North America energy independent so we can create those jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, with regards to that tax cut, look, I’m not looking to cut massive taxes and to reduce the — the revenues going to the government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My — my number-one principal is, there will be no tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to underline that: no tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I do want to reduce the burden being paid by middle-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — and to do that, that also means I cannot reduce the burden paid by high-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So any — any language to the contrary is simply not accurate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think — let’s talk about taxes, because I think it’s instructive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, four years ago, when I stood on this stage, I said that I would cut taxes for middle-class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly what I did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We cut taxes for middle-class families by about $3,600.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason is, because I believe that we do best when the middle class is doing well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And by giving them those tax cuts, they had a little more money in their pocket, and so maybe they can buy a new car.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are certainly in a better position to weather the extraordinary recession that we went through.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They can buy a computer for their kid who’s going off to college, which means they’re spending more money, businesses have more customers, businesses make more profits, and then hire more workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney’s proposal that he has been promoting for 18 months calls for a $5 trillion tax cut, on top of $2 trillion of additional spending for our military.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he is saying that he is going to pay for it by closing loopholes and deductions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The problem is that he’s been asked over 100 times how you would close those deductions and loopholes, and he hasn’t been able to identify them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I’m going to make an important point here, Jim.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When you add up all the loopholes and deductions that upper-income individuals can — are currently taking advantage of, you take those all away, you don’t come close to paying for $5 trillion in tax cuts and $2 trillion in additional military spending.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why independent studies looking at this said the only way to meet Governor Romney’s pledge of not reducing the deficit or — or — or not adding to the deficit is by burdening middle-class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The average middle-class family with children would pay about $2,000 more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, that’s not my analysis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the analysis of economists who have looked at this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and that kind of top — top-down economics, where folks at the top are doing well, so the average person making $3 million is getting a $250,000 tax break, while middle-class families are burdened further, that’s not what I believe is a recipe for economic growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, but — but virtually — virtually everything he just said about my tax plan is inaccurate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So if the tax plan he described were a tax plan I was asked to support, I’d say absolutely not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not looking for a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I’ve said is I won’t put in place a tax cut that adds to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s part one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So there’s no economist that can say Mitt Romney’s tax plan adds $5 trillion if I say I will not add to the deficit with my tax plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, I will not reduce the share paid by high-income individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know that you and your running mate keep saying that and I know it’s a popular thing to say with a lot of people, but it’s just not the case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I’ve got five boys.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m used to people saying something that’s not always true, but just keep on repeating it and ultimately hoping I’ll believe it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But that — that is not the case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not reduce the taxes paid by high-income Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And number three, I will not under any circumstances raise taxes on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will lower taxes on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, you cite a study.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are six other studies that looked at the study you describe and say it’s completely wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I saw a study that came out today that said you’re going to raise taxes by $3,000 to $4,000 on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are all these studies out there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let’s get at the bottom line.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That is, I want to bring down rates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to bring the rates down, at the same time lower deductions and exemptions and credits and so forth, so we keep getting the revenue we need.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you’d think, well, then why lower the rates?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the reason is because small business pays that individual rate; 54 percent of America’s workers work in businesses that are taxed not at the corporate tax rate, but at the individual tax rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we lower that rate, they will be able to hire more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: For me, this is about jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is about getting jobs for the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, for 18 months he’s been running on this tax plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now, five weeks before the election, he’s saying that his big, bold idea is, “Never mind.” And the fact is that if you are lowering the rates the way you described, Governor, then it is not possible to come up with enough deductions and loopholes that only affect high-income individuals to avoid either raising the deficit or burdening the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s — it’s math.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s arithmetic.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney and I do share a deep interest in encouraging small-business growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So at the same time that my tax plan has already lowered taxes for 98 percent of families, I also lowered taxes for small businesses 18 times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I want to do is continue the tax rates — the tax cuts that we put into place for small businesses and families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I have said that for incomes over $250,000 a year, that we should go back to the rates that we had when Bill Clinton was president, when we created 23 million new jobs, went from deficit to surplus, and created a whole lot of millionaires to boot.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason this is important is because by doing that, we cannot only reduce the deficit, we cannot only encourage job growth through small businesses, but we’re also able to make the investments that are necessary in education or in energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we do have a difference, though, when it comes to definitions of small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Under — under my plan, 97 percent of small businesses would not see their income taxes go up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney says, well, those top 3 percent, they’re the job creators, they’d be burdened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But under Governor Romney’s definition, there are a whole bunch of millionaires and billionaires who are small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Donald Trump is a small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I know Donald Trump doesn’t like to think of himself as small anything, but — but that’s how you define small businesses if you’re getting business income.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that kind of approach, I believe, will not grow our economy, because the only way to pay for it without either burdening the middle class or blowing up our deficit is to make drastic cuts in things like education, making sure that we are continuing to invest in basic science and research, all the things that are helping America grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that would be a mistake.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, let me just come back on that — on that point, which is these…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … the small businesses we’re talking about…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s fun, isn’t it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, President, you’re — Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, you’re absolutely right, which is that, with regards to 97 percent of the businesses are not — not taxed at the 35 percent tax rate, they’re taxed at a lower rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But those businesses that are in the last 3 percent of businesses happen to employ half — half of all the people who work in small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those are the businesses that employ one-quarter of all the workers in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And your plan is to take their tax rate from 35 percent to 40 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, and — and I’ve talked to a guy who has a very small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’s in the electronics business in — in St.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He has four employees.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said he and his son calculated how much they pay in taxes, federal income tax, federal payroll tax, state income tax, state sales tax, state property tax, gasoline tax.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It added up to well over 50 percent of what they earned.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And your plan is to take the tax rate on successful small businesses from 35 percent to 40 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The National Federation of Independent Businesses has said that will cost 700,000 jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to cost jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so what I do is I bring down the tax rates, lower deductions and exemptions, the same idea behind Bowles-Simpson, by the way, get the rates down, lower deductions and exemptions, to create more jobs, because there’s nothing better for getting us to a balanced budget than having more people working, earning more money, paying more taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s by far the most effective and efficient way to get this budget balanced.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Jim, I — you may want to move onto another topic, but I — I would just say this to the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you believe that we can cut taxes by $5 trillion and add $2 trillion in additional spending that the military is not asking for, $7 trillion — just to give you a sense, over 10 years, that’s more than our entire defense budget — and you think that by closing loopholes and deductions for the well-to-do, somehow you will not end up picking up the tab, then Governor Romney’s plan may work for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think math, common sense, and our history shows us that’s not a recipe for job growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, we’ve tried this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve tried both approaches.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The approach that Governor Romney’s talking about is the same sales pitch that was made in 2001 and 2003, and we ended up with the slowest job growth in 50 years, we ended up moving from surplus to deficits, and it all culminated in the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bill Clinton tried the approach that I’m talking about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created 23 million new jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We went from deficit to surplus.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And businesses did very well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, in some ways, we’ve got some data on which approach is more likely to create jobs and opportunity for Americans and I believe that the economy works best when middle-class families are getting tax breaks so that they’ve got some money in their pockets, and those of us who have done extraordinarily well because of this magnificent country that we live in, that we can afford to do a little bit more to make sure we’re not blowing up the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, the president began this segment, so I think I get the last word.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, but he gets the first word of that segment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I get the last word (inaudible) I hope.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me just make this comment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think first of all, let me — let me repeat — let me repeat what I said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not in favor of a $5 trillion tax cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is not to put in place any tax cut that will add to the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s point one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So you may keep referring to it as a $5 trillion tax cut, but that’s not my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, let’s look at history.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is not like anything that’s been tried before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan is to bring down rates, but also bring down deductions and exemptions and credits at the same time so the revenue stays in, but that we bring down rates to get more people working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is putting people back to work in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re suffering in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we talk about evidence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look at the evidence of the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely extraordinary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got 23 million people out of work or stopped looking for work in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s just — it’s — we’ve got — when the president took office, 32 million people on food stamps; 47 million on food stamps today; economic growth this year slower than last year, and last year slower than the year before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Going forward with the status quo is not going to cut it for the American people who are struggling today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m glad you raised that, and it’s a — it’s a critical issue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think it’s not just an economic issue, I think it’s a moral issue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think it’s, frankly, not moral for my generation to keep spending massively more than we take in, knowing those burdens are going to be passed on to the next generation and they’re going to be paying the interest and the principal all their lives.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the amount of debt we’re adding, at a trillion a year, is simply not moral.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So how do we deal with it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, mathematically, there are three ways that you can cut a deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One, of course, is to raise taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two is to cut spending.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And number is to grow the economy, because if more people work in a growing economy, they’re paying taxes, and you can get the job done that way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The presidents would — president would prefer raising taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The problem with raising taxes is that it slows down the rate of growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you could never quite get the job done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to lower spending and encourage economic growth at the same time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What things would I cut from spending?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, I will eliminate all programs by this test, if they don’t pass it: Is the program so critical it’s worth borrowing money from China to pay for it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if not, I’ll get rid of it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Obamacare’s on my list.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I apologize, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I use that term with all respect, by the way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I’ll get rid of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sorry, Jim, I’m going to stop the subsidy to PBS.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to stop other things.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I like PBS, I love Big Bird.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Actually like you, too.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’m not going to — I’m not going to keep on spending money on things to borrow money from China to pay for.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, I’ll take programs that are currently good programs but I think could be run more efficiently at the state level and send them to the state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, I’ll make government more efficient and to cut back the number of employees, combine some agencies and departments.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My cutbacks will be done through attrition, by the way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the approach we have to take to get America to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president said he’d cut the deficit in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Unfortunately, he doubled it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Trillion-dollar deficits for the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s put it in place as much public debt — almost as much debt held by the public as al prior presidents combined.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I walked into the Oval Office, I had more than a trillion-dollar deficit greeting me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we know where it came from: two wars that were paid for on a credit card; two tax cuts that were not paid for; and a whole bunch of programs that were not paid for; and then a massive economic crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And despite that, what we’ve said is, yes, we had to take some initial emergency measures to make sure we didn’t slip into a Great Depression, but what we’ve also said is, let’s make sure that we are cutting out those things that are not helping us grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So 77 government programs, everything from aircrafts that the Air Force had ordered but weren’t working very well, 18 government — 18 government programs for education that were well-intentioned, not weren’t helping kids learn, we went after medical fraud in Medicare and Medicaid very aggressively, more aggressively than ever before, and have saved tens of billions of dollars, $50 billion of waste taken out of the system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I worked with Democrats and Republicans to cut a trillion dollars out of our discretionary domestic budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the largest cut in the discretionary domestic budget since Dwight Eisenhower.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, we all know that we’ve got to do more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so I’ve put forward a specific $4 trillion deficit reduction plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s on a website.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can look at all the numbers, what cuts we make and what revenue we raise.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the way we do it is $2.50 for every cut, we ask for $1 of additional revenue, paid for, as I indicated earlier, by asking those of us who have done very well in this country to contribute a little bit more to reduce the deficit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney earlier mentioned the Bowles-Simpson commission.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, that’s how the commission — bipartisan commission that talked about how we should move forward suggested we have to do it, in a balanced way with some revenue and some spending cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is a major difference that Governor Romney and I have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let — let me just finish their point, because you’re looking for contrast.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, when Governor Romney stood on a stage with other Republican candidates for the nomination and he was asked, would you take $10 of spending cuts for just $1 of revenue?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he said no.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, if you take such an unbalanced approach, then that means you are going to be gutting our investments in schools and education.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means that Governor Romney… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … talked about Medicaid and how we could send it back to the states, but effectively this means a 30 percent cut in the primary program we help for seniors who are in nursing homes, for kids who are with disabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and that is not a right strategy for us to move forward.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Simpson-Bowles, the president should have grabbed that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have my own plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not the same as Simpson- Bowles.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But in my view, the president should have grabbed it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you wanted to make some adjustments to it, take it, go to Congress, fight for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what we’ve done, made some adjustments to it, and we’re putting it forward before Congress right now, a $4 trillion plan…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you’ve been — but you’ve been president four years… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ve been president four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said you’d cut the deficit in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s now four years later.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We still have trillion-dollar deficits.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The CBO says we’ll have a trillion-dollar deficit each of the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you’re re-elected, we’ll get to a trillion-dollar debt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean, you have said before you’d cut the deficit in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And this — I love this idea of $4 trillion in cuts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You found $4 trillion of ways to reduce or to get closer to a balanced budget, except we still show trillion-dollar deficits every year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That doesn’t get the job done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me come back and say, why is it that I don’t want to raise taxes?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Why don’t I want to raise taxes on people?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And actually, you said it back in 2010.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said, “Look, I’m going to extend the tax policies that we have now; I’m not going to raise taxes on anyone, because when the economy is growing slow like this, when we’re in recession, you shouldn’t raise taxes on anyone.” Well, the economy is still growing slow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, it’s growing much more slowly now than when you made that statement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so if you believe the same thing, you just don’t want to raise taxes on people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the reality is it’s not just wealthy people — you mentioned Donald Trump.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not just Donald Trump you’re taxing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s all those businesses that employ one-quarter of the workers in America; these small businesses that are taxed as individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You raise taxes and you kill jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why the National Federation of Independent Businesses said your plan will kill 700,000 jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to kill jobs in this environment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll make one more point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, we’ve had this discussion before.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There has to be revenue in addition to cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has ruled out revenue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He’s ruled out revenue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, the revenue I get is by more people working, getting higher pay, paying more taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s how we get growth and how we balance the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the idea of taxing people more, putting more people out of work, you’ll never get there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll never balance the budget by raising taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Spain — Spain spends 42 percent of their total economy on government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re now spending 42 percent of our economy on government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to go down the path to Spain.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to go down the path of growth that puts Americans to work with more money coming in because they’re working.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If — if we’re serious, we’ve got to take a balanced, responsible approach.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And by the way, this is not just when it comes to individual taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s talk about corporate taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I’ve identified areas where we can, right away, make a change that I believe would actually help the economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The oil industry gets $4 billion a year in corporate welfare.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Basically, they get deductions that those small businesses that Governor Romney refers to, they don’t get.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, does anybody think that ExxonMobil needs some extra money, when they’re making money every time you go to the pump?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Why wouldn’t we want to eliminate that?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Why wouldn’t we eliminate tax breaks for corporate jets?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My attitude is, if you got a corporate jet, you can probably afford to pay full freight, not get a special break for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to corporate taxes, Governor Romney has said he wants to, in a revenue neutral way, close loopholes, deductions — he hasn’t identified which ones they are — but that thereby bring down the corporate rate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I want to do the same thing, but I’ve actually identified how we can do that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And part of the way to do it is to not give tax breaks to companies that are shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now, you can actually take a deduction for moving a plant overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think most Americans would say that doesn’t make sense.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And all that raises revenue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so if we take a balanced approach, what that then allows us to do is also to help young people, the way we already have during my administration, make sure that they can afford to go to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means that the teacher that I met in Las Vegas, a wonderful young lady, who describes to me — she’s got 42 kids in her class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The first two weeks she’s got them, some of them sitting on the floor until finally they get reassigned.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’re using text books that are 10 years old.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not a recipe for growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not how America was built.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so budgets reflect choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ultimately, we’re going to have to make some decisions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’re asking for no revenue, then that means that we’ve got to get rid of a whole bunch of stuff.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the magnitude of the tax cuts that you’re talking about, Governor, would end up resulting in severe hardship for people, but more importantly, would not help us grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: As I indicated before, when you talk about shifting Medicaid to states, we’re talking about potentially a 30 — a 30 percent cut in Medicaid over time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, you know, that may not seem like a big deal when it just is, you know, numbers on a sheet of paper, but if we’re talking about a family who’s got an autistic kid and is depending on that Medicaid, that’s a big problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And governors are creative.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s no doubt about it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But they’re not creative enough to make up for 30 percent of revenue on something like Medicaid.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What ends up happening is some people end up not getting help.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, let’s — we’ve gone on a lot of topics there, and so it’s going to take a minute to go from Medicaid to schools…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … to oil, to tax breaks, then companies going overseas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So let’s go through them one by one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, the Department of Energy has said the tax break for oil companies is $2.8 billion a year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s actually an accounting treatment, as you know, that’s been in place for a hundred years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s time to end it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And in one year, you provided $90 billion in breaks to the green energy world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, I like green energy as well, but that’s about 50 years’ worth of what oil and gas receives.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you say Exxon and Mobil.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Actually, this $2.8 billion goes largely to small companies, to drilling operators and so forth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But, you know, if we get that tax rate from 35 percent down to 25 percent, why that $2.8 billion is on the table.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course it’s on the table.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s probably not going to survive you get that rate down to 25 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But don’t forget, you put $90 billion, like 50 years’ worth of breaks, into — into solar and wind, to Solyndra and Fisker and Tester and Ener1.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean, I had a friend who said you don’t just pick the winners and losers, you pick the losers, all right?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So this — this is not — this is not the kind of policy you want to have if you want to get America energy secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The second topic, which is you said you get a deduction for taking a plant overseas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I’ve been in business for 25 years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have no idea what you’re talking about.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I maybe need to get a new accountant.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But — but the idea that you get a break for shipping jobs overseas is simply not the case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What we do have right now is a setting where I’d like to bring money from overseas back to this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, finally, Medicaid to states?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not quite sure where that came in, except this, which is, I would like to take the Medicaid dollars that go to states and say to a state, you’re going to get what you got last year, plus inflation, plus 1 percent, and then you’re going to manage your care for your poor in the way you think best.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I remember, as a governor, when this idea was floated by Tommy Thompson, the governors — Republican and Democrats — said, please let us do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can care for our own poor in so much better and more effective a way than having the federal government tell us how to care for our poor.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So — so let’s state — one of the magnificent things about this country is the whole idea that states are the laboratories of democracy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Don’t have the federal government tell everybody what kind of training programs they have to have and what kind of Medicaid they have to have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let states do this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, by the way, if a state gets in trouble, well, we can step in and see if we can find a way to help them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But — but the right — the right approach is one which relies on the brilliance of our people and states, not the federal government.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, I suspect that, on Social Security, we’ve got a somewhat similar position.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Social Security is structurally sound.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s going to have to be tweaked the way it was by Ronald Reagan and Speaker — Democratic Speaker Tip O’Neill.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it is — the basic structure is sound.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But — but I want to talk about the values behind Social Security and Medicare, and then talk about Medicare, because that’s the big driver of our deficits right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, my grandmother — some of you know — helped to raise me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My grandparents did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My grandfather died a while back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My grandmother died three days before I was elected president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she was fiercely independent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: She worked her way up, only had a high school education, started as a secretary, ended up being the vice president of a local bank.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she ended up living alone by choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason she could be independent was because of Social Security and Medicare.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: She had worked all her life, put in this money, and understood that there was a basic guarantee, a floor under which she could not go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the perspective I bring when I think about what’s called entitlements.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, the name itself implies some sense of dependency on the part of these folks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are folks who’ve worked hard, like my grandmother, and there are millions of people out there who are counting on this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So my approach is to say, how do we strengthen the system over the long term?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And in Medicare, what we did was we said, we are going to have to bring down the costs if we’re going to deal with our long-term deficits, but to do that, let’s look where some of the money’s going.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: $716 billion we were able to save from the Medicare program by no longer overpaying insurance companies by making sure that we weren’t overpaying providers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And using that money, we were actually able to lower prescription drug costs for seniors by an average of $600, and we were also able to make a — make a significant dent in providing them the kind of preventive care that will ultimately save money through the — throughout the system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So the way for us to deal with Medicare in particular is to lower health care costs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to Social Security, as I said, you don’t need a major structural change in order to make sure that Social Security is there for the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, Jim, our seniors depend on these programs, and I know anytime we talk about entitlements, people become concerned that something’s going to happen that’s going to change their life for the worse.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer is neither the president nor I are proposing any changes for any current retirees or near retirees, either to Social Security or Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So if you’re 60 or around 60 or older, you don’t need to listen any further.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But for younger people, we need to talk about what changes are going to be occurring.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Oh, I just thought about one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that is, in fact, I was wrong when I said the president isn’t proposing any changes for current retirees.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In fact he is on Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: On Social Security he’s not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But on Medicare, for current retirees, he’s cutting $716 billion from the program.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, he says by not overpaying hospitals and providers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Actually just going to them and saying, “We’re going to reduce the rates you get paid across the board, everybody’s going to get a lower rate.” That’s not just going after places where there’s abuse.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s saying we’re cutting the rates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Some 15 percent of hospitals and nursing homes say they won’t take anymore Medicare patients under that scenario.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We also have 50 percent of doctors who say they won’t take more Medicare patients.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — we have 4 million people on Medicare Advantage that will lose Medicare Advantage because of those $716 billion in cuts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can’t understand how you can cut Medicare $716 billion for current recipients of Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, you point out, well, we’re putting some back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to give a better prescription program.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s $1 — that’s $1 for every $15 you’ve cut.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re smart enough to know that’s not a good trade.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to take that $716 billion you’ve cut and put it back into Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: By the way, we can include a prescription program if we need to improve it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the idea of cutting $716 billion from Medicare to be able to balance the additional cost of Obamacare is, in my opinion, a mistake.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And with regards to young people coming along, I’ve got proposals to make sure Medicare and Social Security are there for them without any question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, I think it’s important for Governor Romney to present this plan that he says will only affect folks in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the essence of the plan is that you would turn Medicare into a voucher program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s called premium support, but it’s understood to be a voucher program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: His running mate…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And let me explain why.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Again, that’s for future…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I understand.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … people, right, not for current retirees.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For — so if you’re — if you’re 54 or 55, you might want to listen ’cause this — this will affect you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The idea, which was originally presented by Congressman Ryan, your running mate, is that we would give a voucher to seniors and they could go out in the private marketplace and buy their own health insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The problem is that because the voucher wouldn’t necessarily keep up with health care inflation, it was estimated that this would cost the average senior about $6,000 a year.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, in fairness, what Governor Romney has now said is he’ll maintain traditional Medicare alongside it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there’s still a problem, because what happens is, those insurance companies are pretty clever at figuring out who are the younger and healthier seniors.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They recruit them, leaving the older, sicker seniors in Medicare.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And every health care economist that looks at it says, over time, what’ll happen is the traditional Medicare system will collapse.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then what you’ve got is folks like my grandmother at the mercy of the private insurance system precisely at the time when they are most in need of decent health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, I don’t think vouchers are the right way to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is not my own — only my opinion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: AARP thinks that the — the savings that we obtained from Medicare bolster the system, lengthen the Medicare trust fund by eight years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Benefits were not affected at all.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And ironically, if you repeal Obamacare, and I have become fond of this term, “Obamacare,” if you repeal it, what happens is those seniors right away are going to be paying $600 more in prescription care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’re now going to have to be paying copays for basic checkups that can keep them healthier.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the primary beneficiary of that repeal are insurance companies that are estimated to gain billions of dollars back when they aren’t making seniors any healthier.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I don’t think that’s the right approach when it comes to making sure that Medicare is stronger over the long term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I support is no change for current retirees and near-retirees to Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the president supports taking $716 billion out of that program.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So that’s — that’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two is for people coming along that are young, what I do to make sure that we can keep Medicare in place for them is to allow them either to choose the current Medicare program or a private plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Their choice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They get to choose — and they’ll have at least two plans that will be entirely at no cost to them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So they don’t have to pay additional money, no additional $6,000.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not going to happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ll have at least two plans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, if the government can be as efficient as the private sector and offer premiums that are as low as the private sector, people will be happy to get traditional Medicare or they’ll be able to get a private plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know my own view is I’d rather have a private plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d just assume not have the government telling me what kind of health care I get.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d rather be able to have an insurance company.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I don’t like them, I can get rid of them and find a different insurance company.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But people make their own choice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The other thing we have to do to save Medicare?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to have the benefits high for those that are low income, but for higher income people, we’re going to have to lower some of the benefits.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to make sure this program is there for the long term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the plan that I’ve put forward.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, by the way the idea came not even from Paul Ryan or — or Senator Wyden, who’s the co-author of the bill with — with Paul Ryan in the Senate, but also it came from Bill — Bill Clinton’s chief of staff.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is an idea that’s been around a long time, which is saying, hey, let’s see if we can’t get competition into the Medicare world so that people can get the choice of different plans at lower cost, better quality.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe in competition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Jim, if I — if I can just respond very quickly, first of all, every study has shown that Medicare has lower administrative costs than private insurance does, which is why seniors are generally pretty happy with it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And private insurers have to make a profit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nothing wrong with that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what they do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so you’ve got higher administrative costs, plus profit on top of that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if you are going to save any money through what Governor Romney’s proposing, what has to happen is, is that the money has to come from somewhere.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when you move to a voucher system, you are putting seniors at the mercy of those insurance companies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And over time, if traditional Medicare has decayed or fallen apart, then they’re stuck.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is the reason why AARP has said that your plan would weaken Medicare substantially.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why they were supportive of the approach that we took.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One last point I want to make.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We do have to lower the cost of health care, not just in Medicare and Medicaid…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … but — but — but overall.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s — that’s a big topic.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Can we — can we stay on Medicare?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Is that a — is that a separate topic?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s get back to Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s get back to Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president said that the government can provide the service at lower cost and without a profit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If that’s the case, then it will always be the best product that people can purchase.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But my experience — my experience the private sector typically is able to provide a better product at a lower cost.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Regulation is essential.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have a free market work if you don’t have regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a businessperson, I had to have — I need to know the regulations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I needed them there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You couldn’t have people opening up banks in their — in their garage and making loans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean, you have to have regulations so that you can have an economy work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Every free economy has good regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: At the same time, regulation can become excessive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In some places, yes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Other places, no.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, it can become out of date.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what’s happened with some of the legislation that’s been passed during the president’s term, you’ve seen regulation become excessive, and it’s hurt — it’s hurt the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me give you an example.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Dodd-Frank was passed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it includes within it a number of provisions that I think has some unintended consequences that are harmful to the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One is it designates a number of banks as too big to fail, and they’re effectively guaranteed by the federal government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the biggest kiss that’s been given to — to New York banks I’ve ever seen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is an enormous boon for them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There’ve been 122 community and small banks have closed since Dodd- Frank.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So there’s one example.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Here’s another.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In Dodd-Frank…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, I would repeal and replace it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re not going to get rid of all regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You have to have regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there are some parts of Dodd-Frank that make all the sense in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You need transparency, you need to have leverage limits for…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let’s — let’s mention — let me mention the other one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s talk… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think this is a great example.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The reason we have been in such a enormous economic crisis was prompted by reckless behavior across the board.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it wasn’t just on Wall Street.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You had loan officers were — that were giving loans and mortgages that really shouldn’t have been given, because the folks didn’t qualify.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You had people who were borrowing money to buy a house that they couldn’t afford.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You had credit agencies that were stamping these as A1 great investments when they weren’t.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But you also had banks making money hand over fist, churning out products that the bankers themselves didn’t even understand, in order to make big profits, but knowing that it made the entire system vulnerable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what did we do?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We stepped in and had the toughest reforms on Wall Street since the 1930s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We said you’ve got — banks, you’ve got to raise your capital requirements.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can’t engage in some of this risky behavior that is putting Main Street at risk.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve going to make sure that you’ve got to have a living will so — so we can know how you’re going to wind things down if you make a bad bet so we don’t have other taxpayer bailouts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the meantime, by the way, we also made sure that all the help that we provided those banks was paid back every single dime, with interest.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has said he wants to repeal Dodd-Frank.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, I appreciate and it appears we’ve got some agreement that a marketplace to work has to have some regulation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But in the past, Governor Romney has said he just want to repeal Dodd- Frank, roll it back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question is: Does anybody out there think that the big problem we had is that there was too much oversight and regulation of Wall Street?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because if you do, then Governor Romney is your candidate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But that’s not what I believe.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Sorry, but that’s just not — that’s just not the facts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, we have to have regulation on Wall Street.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why I’d have regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I wouldn’t designate five banks as too big to fail and give them a blank check.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s one of the unintended consequences of Dodd-Frank.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It wasn’t thought through properly.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to get rid of that provision because it’s killing regional and small banks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re getting hurt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me mention another regulation in Dodd-Frank.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You say we were giving mortgages to people who weren’t qualified.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s exactly right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s one of the reasons for the great financial calamity we had.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so Dodd-Frank correctly says we need to have qualified mortgages, and if you give a mortgage that’s not qualified, there are big penalties, except they didn’t ever go on and define what a qualified mortgage was.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s been two years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t know what a qualified mortgage is yet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So banks are reluctant to make loans, mortgages.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Try and get a mortgage these days.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s hurt the housing market because Dodd-Frank didn’t anticipate putting in place the kinds of regulations you have to have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not that Dodd-Frank always was wrong with too much regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Sometimes they didn’t come out with a clear regulation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will make sure we don’t hurt the functioning of our — of our marketplace and our business, because I want to bring back housing and get good jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, in part, it comes, again, from my experience.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You know, I was in New Hampshire.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A woman came to me and she said, look, I can’t afford insurance for myself or my son.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I met a couple in Appleton, Wisconsin, and they said, we’re thinking of dropping our insurance, we can’t afford it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the number of small businesses I’ve gone to that are saying they’re dropping insurance because they can’t afford it, the cost of health care is just prohibitive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and we’ve got to deal with cost.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, unfortunately, when — when — when you look at Obamacare, the Congressional Budget Office has said it will cost $2,500 a year more than traditional insurance.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So it’s adding to cost.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And as a matter of fact, when the president ran for office, he said that, by this year, he would have brought down the cost of insurance for each family by $2,500 a family.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Instead, it’s gone up by that amount.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So it’s expensive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Expensive things hurt families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So that’s one reason I don’t want it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Second reason, it cuts $716 billion from Medicare to pay for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to put that money back in Medicare for our seniors.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, it puts in place an unelected board that’s going to tell people ultimately what kind of treatments they can have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t like that idea.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fourth, there was a survey done of small businesses across the country, said, what’s been the effect of Obamacare on your hiring plans?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And three-quarters of them said it makes us less likely to hire people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I just don’t know how the president could have come into office, facing 23 million people out of work, rising unemployment, an economic crisis at the — at the kitchen table, and spend his energy and passion for two years fighting for Obamacare instead of fighting for jobs for the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It has killed jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the best course for health care is to do what we did in my state: craft a plan at the state level that fits the needs of the state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then let’s focus on getting the costs down for people, rather than raising it with the $2,500 additional premium.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, four years ago, when I was running for office, I was traveling around and having those same conversations that Governor Romney talks about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it wasn’t just that small businesses were seeing costs skyrocket and they couldn’t get affordable coverage even if they wanted to provide it to their employees.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It wasn’t just that this was the biggest driver of our federal deficit, our overall health care costs, but it was families who were worried about going bankrupt if they got sick, millions of families, all across the country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If they had a pre-existing condition, they might not be able to get coverage at all.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If they did have coverage, insurance companies might impose an arbitrary limit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so as a consequence, they’re paying their premiums, somebody gets really sick, lo and behold, they don’t have enough money to pay the bills, because the insurance companies say that they’ve hit the limit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we did work on this, alongside working on jobs, because this is part of making sure that middle-class families are secure in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And let me tell you exactly what Obamacare did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, if you’ve got health insurance, it doesn’t mean a government takeover.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You keep your own insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You keep your own doctor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it does say insurance companies can’t jerk you around.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They can’t impose arbitrary lifetime limits.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They have to let you keep your kid on their insurance — your insurance plan until you’re 26 years old.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it also says that you’re going to have to get rebates if insurance companies are spending more on administrative costs and profits than they are on actual care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, if you don’t have health insurance, we’re essentially setting up a group plan that allows you to benefit from group rates that are typically 18 percent lower than if you’re out there trying to get insurance on the individual market.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the last point I’d make before…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I think — I had five seconds before you interrupted me, was … (LAUGHTER) … the irony is that we’ve seen this model work really well in Massachusetts, because Governor Romney did a good thing, working with Democrats in the state to set up what is essentially the identical model and as a consequence people are covered there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It hasn’t destroyed jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, we now have a system in which we have the opportunity to start bringing down costs, as opposed to just leaving millions of people out in the cold.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, I did with my first statement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, I like the way we did it in Massachusetts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I like the fact that in my state, we had Republicans and Democrats come together and work together.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What you did instead was to push through a plan without a single Republican vote.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, when Massachusetts did something quite extraordinary — elected a Republican senator to stop Obamacare, you pushed it through anyway.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So entirely on a partisan basis, instead of bringing America together and having a discussion on this important topic, you pushed through something that you and Nancy Pelosi and Harry Reid thought was the best answer and drove it through.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What we did in a legislature 87 percent Democrat, we worked together; 200 legislators in my legislature, only two voted against the plan by the time we were finished.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What were some differences?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t raise taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ve raised them by $1 trillion under Obamacare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t cut Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course, we don’t have Medicare, but we didn’t cut Medicare by $716 billion.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t put in place a board that can tell people ultimately what treatments they’re going to receive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t also do something that I think a number of people across this country recognize, which is put — put people in a position where they’re going to lose the insurance they had and they wanted.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Right now, the CBO says up to 20 million people will lose their insurance as Obamacare goes into effect next year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And likewise, a study by McKinsey and Company of American businesses said 30 percent of them are anticipating dropping people from coverage.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So for those reasons, for the tax, for Medicare, for this board, and for people losing their insurance, this is why the American people don’t want Medicare — don’t want Obamacare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s why Republicans said, do not do this, and the Republicans had — had the plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They put a plan out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They put out a plan, a bipartisan plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was swept aside.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think something this big, this important has to be done on a bipartisan basis.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we have to have a president who can reach across the aisle and fashion important legislation with the input from both parties.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney said this has to be done on a bipartisan basis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This was a bipartisan idea.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, it was a Republican idea.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney at the beginning of this debate wrote and said what we did in Massachusetts could be a model for the nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I agree that the Democratic legislators in Massachusetts might have given some advice to Republicans in Congress about how to cooperate, but the fact of the matter is, we used the same advisers, and they say it’s the same plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It — when Governor Romney talks about this board, for example, unelected board that we’ve created, what this is, is a group of health care experts, doctors, et cetera, to figure out, how can we reduce the cost of care in the system overall?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because there — there are two ways of dealing with our health care crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One is to simply leave a whole bunch of people uninsured and let them fend for themselves, to let businesses figure out how long they can continue to pay premiums until finally they just give up, and their workers are no longer getting insured, and that’s been the trend line.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Or, alternatively, we can figure out, how do we make the cost of care more effective?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And there are ways of doing it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So at Cleveland Clinic, one of the best health care systems in the world, they actually provide great care cheaper than average.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason they do is because they do some smart things.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They — they say, if a patient’s coming in, let’s get all the doctors together at once, do one test instead of having the patient run around with 10 tests.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s make sure that we’re providing preventive care so we’re catching the onset of something like diabetes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s — let’s pay providers on the basis of performance as opposed to on the basis of how many procedures they’ve — they’ve engaged in.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, so what this board does is basically identifies best practices and says, let’s use the purchasing power of Medicare and Medicaid to help to institutionalize all these good things that we do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact of the matter is that, when Obamacare is fully implemented, we’re going to be in a position to show that costs are going down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And over the last two years, health care premiums have gone up — it’s true — but they’ve gone up slower than any time in the last 50 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’re already beginning to see progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the meantime, folks out there with insurance, you’re already getting a rebate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me make one last point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney says, we should replace it, I’m just going to repeal it, but — but we can replace it with something.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But the problem is, he hasn’t described what exactly we’d replace it with, other than saying we’re going to leave it to the states.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But the fact of the matter is that some of the prescriptions that he’s offered, like letting you buy insurance across state lines, there’s no indication that that somehow is going to help somebody who’s got a pre-existing condition be able to finally buy insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, it’s estimated that by repealing Obamacare, you’re looking at 50 million people losing health insurance…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … at a time when it’s vitally important.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, actually it’s — it’s — it’s a lengthy description.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But, number one, preexisting conditions are covered under my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, young people are able to stay on their family plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s already offered in the private marketplace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You don’t have to have the government mandate that for that to occur.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let’s come back to something the president and I agree on, which is the key task we have in health care is to get the cost down so it’s more affordable for families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then he has as a model for doing that a board of people at the government, an unelected board, appointed board, who are going to decide what kind of treatment you ought to have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In my opinion, the government is not effective in — in bringing down the cost of almost anything.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, free people and free enterprises trying to find ways to do things better are able to be more effective in bringing down the cost than the government will ever be.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Your example of the Cleveland Clinic is my case in point, along with several others I could describe.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the private market.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: These are small — these are enterprises competing with each other, learning how to do better and better jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I used to consult to businesses — excuse me, to hospitals and to health care providers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was astonished at the creativity and innovation that exists in the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In order to bring the cost of health care down, we don’t need to have a board of 15 people telling us what kinds of treatments we should have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We instead need to put insurance plans, providers, hospitals, doctors on target such that they have an incentive, as you say, performance pay, for doing an excellent job, for keeping costs down, and that’s happening.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Innermountain Healthcare does it superbly well, Mayo Clinic is doing it superbly well, Cleveland Clinic, others.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the right answer is not to have the federal government take over health care and start mandating to the providers across America, telling a patient and a doctor what kind of treatment they can have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the wrong way to go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The private market and individual responsibility always work best.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me just point out first of all this board that we’re talking about can’t make decisions about what treatments are given.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s explicitly prohibited in the law.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But let’s go back to what Governor Romney indicated, that under his plan, he would be able to cover people with preexisting conditions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, actually Governor, that isn’t what your plan does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What your plan does is to duplicate what’s already the law, which says if you are out of health insurance for three months, then you can end up getting continuous coverage and an insurance company can’t deny you if you’ve — if it’s been under 90 days.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But that’s already the law and that doesn’t help the millions of people out there with preexisting conditions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s a reason why Governor Romney set up the plan that he did in Massachusetts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It wasn’t a government takeover of health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It was the largest expansion of private insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what it does say is that “insurers, you’ve got to take everybody.” Now, that also means that you’ve got more customers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when — when Governor Romney says that he’ll replace it with something, but can’t detail how it will be in fact replaced and the reason he set up the system he did in Massachusetts was because there isn’t a better way of dealing with the preexisting conditions problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It just reminds me of, you know, he says that he’s going to close deductions and loopholes for his tax plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how it’s going to be paid for, but we don’t know the details.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He says that he’s going to replace Dodd-Frank, Wall Street reform, but we don’t know exactly which ones.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He won’t tell us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He now says he’s going to replace Obamacare and ensure that all the good things that are in it are going to be in there and you don’t have to worry.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And at some point, I think the American people have to ask themselves, is the reason that Governor Romney is keeping all these plans to replace secret because they’re too good?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Is it — is it because that somehow middle-class families are going to benefit too much from them?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The reason is, is because, when we reform Wall Street, when we tackle the problem of pre-existing conditions, then, you know, these are tough problems and we’ve got to make choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the choices we’ve made have been ones that ultimately are benefiting middle-class families all across the country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I have to respond to that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Which is — which is my experience as a governor is if I come in and — and lay down a piece of legislation and say, “It’s my way or the highway,” I don’t get a lot done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I do is the same way that Tip O’Neill and Ronald Reagan worked together some years ago.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When Ronald Reagan ran for office, he laid out the principles that he was going to foster.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said he was going to lower tax rates.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said he was going to broaden the base.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ve said the same thing, you’re going to simplify the tax code, broaden the base.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those are my principles.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to bring down the tax burden on middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’m going to work together with Congress to say, OK, what — what are the various ways we could bring down deductions, for instance?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One way, for instance, would be to have a single number.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Make up a number, $25,000, $50,000.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Anybody can have deductions up to that amount.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then that number disappears for high-income people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s one way one could do it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One could follow Bowles-Simpson as a model and take deduction by deduction and make differences that way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are alternatives to accomplish the objective I have, which is to bring down rates, broaden the base, simplify the code, and create incentives for growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And with regards to health care, you had remarkable details with regards to my pre-existing condition plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You obviously studied up on — on my plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In fact, I do have a plan that deals with people with pre-existing conditions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s part of my health care plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what we did in Massachusetts is a model for the nation state by state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I said that at that time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The federal government taking over health care for the entire nation and whisking aside the 10th Amendment, which gives states the rights for these kinds of things, is not the course for America to have a stronger, more vibrant economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I definitely think there are differences.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The first role of the federal government is to keep the American people safe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s its most basic function.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as commander-in-chief, that is something that I’ve worked on and thought about every single day that I’ve been in the Oval Office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that government has the capacity, the federal government has the capacity to help open up opportunity and create ladders of opportunity and to create frameworks where the American people can succeed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, the genius of America is the free enterprise system and freedom and the fact that people can go out there and start a business, work on an idea, make their own decisions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But as Abraham Lincoln understood, there are also some things we do better together.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, in the middle of the Civil War, Abraham Lincoln said, let’s help to finance the Transcontinental Railroad, let’s start the National Academy of Sciences, let’s start land grant colleges, because we want to give these gateways of opportunity for all Americans, because if all Americans are getting opportunity, we’re all going to be better off.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That doesn’t restrict people’s freedom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That enhances it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what I’ve tried to do as president is to apply those same principles.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to education what I’ve said is we’ve got to reform schools that are not working.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We use something called Race to the Top.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Wasn’t a top-down approach, Governor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we’ve said is to states, we’ll give you more money if you initiate reforms.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, you had 46 states around the country who have made a real difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is let’s hire another 100,000 math and science teachers to make sure we maintain our technological lead and our people are skilled and able to succeed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And hard-pressed states right now can’t all do that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact we’ve seen layoffs of hundreds of thousands of teachers over the last several years, and Governor Romney doesn’t think we need more teachers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I do, because I think that that is the kind of investment where the federal government can help.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It can’t do it all, but it can make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence we’ll have a better trained workforce and that will create jobs because companies want to locate in places where we’ve got a skilled workforce.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first, I love great schools.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Massachusetts, our schools are ranked number one of all 50 states.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the key to great schools, great teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I reject the idea that I don’t believe in great teachers or more teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Every school district, every state should make that decision on their own.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The role of government: Look behind us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The Constitution and the Declaration of Independence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The role of government is to promote and protect the principles of those documents.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First, life and liberty.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have a responsibility to protect the lives and liberties of our people, and that means a military second to none.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I do not believe in cutting our military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe in maintaining the strength of America’s military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Second, in that line that says we are endowed by our creator with our rights, I believe we must maintain our commitment to religious tolerance and freedom in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That statement also says that we are endowed by our creator with the right to pursue happiness as we choose.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I interpret that as, one, making sure that those people who are less fortunate and can’t care for themselves are cared by — by one another.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re a nation that believes that we’re all children of the same god and we care for those that have difficulties, those that are elderly and have problems and challenges, those that are disabled.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We care for them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we — we look for discovery and innovation, all these things desired out of the American heart to provide the pursuit of happiness for our citizens.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we also believe in maintaining for individuals the right to pursue their dreams and not to have the government substitute itself for the rights of free individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what we’re seeing right now is, in my view, a — a trickle-down government approach, which has government thinking it can do a better job than free people pursuing their dreams.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s not working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the proof of that is 23 million people out of work.

speaker: ROMNEY
