sentence: The proof of that is 1 out of 6 people in poverty.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The proof of that is we’ve gone from 32 million on food stamps to 47 million on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The proof of that is that 50 percent of college graduates this year can’t find work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We know that the path we’re taking is not working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s time for a new path.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, the primary responsibility for education is — is, of course, at the state and local level.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the federal government also can play a very important role.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — and I agree with Secretary Arne Duncan, he’s — some ideas he’s put forward on Race to the Top, not all of them, but some of them I agree with and — and congratulate him for pursuing that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The federal government can get local and — and state schools to do a better job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My own view, by the way, is I’ve added to that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I happen to believe, I want the kids that are getting federal dollars from IDEA or Title I — these are disabled kids or — or — or poor kids or — or lower-income kids, rather, I want them to be able to go to the school of their choice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So all federal funds, instead of going to the — to the state or to the school district, I’d have go, if you will, follow the child and let the parent and the child decide where to send their — their — their student.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, as I’ve indicated, I think that it has a significant role to play.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Through our Race to the Top program, we’ve worked with Republican and Democratic governors to initiate major reforms, and they’re having an impact right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, this is where budgets matter, because budgets reflect choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So when Governor Romney indicates that he wants to cut taxes and potentially benefit folks like me and him, and to pay for it we’re having to initiate significant cuts in federal support for education, that makes a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, his — his running mate, Congressman Ryan, put forward a budget that reflects many of the principles that Governor Romney’s talked about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it wasn’t very detailed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This seems to be a trend.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But — but what it did do is to — if you extrapolated how much money we’re talking about, you’d look at cutting the education budget by up to 20 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to community colleges, we are seeing great work done out there all over the country because we have the opportunity to train people for jobs that exist right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one of the things I suspect Governor Romney and I probably agree on is getting businesses to work with community colleges so that they’re setting up their training programs…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me just finish the point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — where they’re partnering so that they’re designing training programs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And people who are going through them know that there’s a job waiting for them if they complete it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That makes a big difference, but that requires some federal support.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me just say one final example.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to making college affordable, whether it’s two-year or four-year, one of the things that I did as president was we were sending $60 billion to banks and lenders as middlemen for the student loan program, even though the loans were guaranteed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So there was no risk for the banks or the lenders, but they were taking billions out of the system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we said, “Why not cut out the middleman?” And as a consequence, what we’ve been able to do is to provide millions more students assistance, lower or keep low interest rates on student loans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is an example of where our priorities make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, I genuinely believe cares about education, but when he tells a student that, you know, “you should borrow money from your parents to go to college,” you know, that indicates the degree to which, you know, there may not be as much of a focus on the fact that folks like myself, folks like Michelle, kids probably who attend University of Denver, just don’t have that option.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And for us to be able to make sure that they’ve got that opportunity and they can walk through that door, that is vitally important not just to those kids.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s how we’re going to grow this economy over the long term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, you’re entitled as the president to your own airplane and to your own house, but not to your own facts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: All right, I’m not going to cut education funding.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t have any plan to cut education funding and — and grants that go to people going to college.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m planning on (inaudible) to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I’m not planning on making changes there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you make a very good point, which is that the place you put your money just makes a pretty clear indication of where your heart is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You put $90 billion into — into green jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — look, I’m all in favor of green energy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: $90 billion, that would have — that would have hired 2 million teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: $90 billion.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And these businesses, many of them have gone out of business, I think about half of them, of the ones have been invested in have gone out of business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A number of them happened to be owned by people who were contributors to your campaigns.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, the right course for America’s government, we were talking about the role of government, is not to become the economic player, picking winners and losers, telling people what kind of health treatment they can receive, taking over the health care system that has existed in this country for a long, long time and has produced the best health records in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The right answer for government is say, How do we make the private sector become more efficient and more effective?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How do we get schools to be more competitive?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s grade them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I propose we grade our schools so parents know which schools are succeeding and failing, so they can take their child to a — to a school that he’s being more successful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to cut our commitment to education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I wanted to make it more effective and efficient.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, I’ve had that experience.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t just talk about it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve been there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Massachusetts schools are ranked number one in the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is not because I didn’t have commitment to education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s because I care about education for all of our kids.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve done a great job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Jim, I had the great experience — it didn’t seem like it at the time — of being elected in a state where my legislature was 87 percent Democrat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that meant I figured out from day one I had to get along and I had to work across the aisle to get anything done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We drove our schools to be number one in the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We cut taxes 19 times.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — as president, I will sit on day one — actually, the day after I get elected — I’ll sit down with leaders — the Democratic leaders, as well as Republican leaders, and continue — as we did in my state — we met every Monday for a couple hours, talked about the issues and the challenges in the — in the — in our state in that case.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to work on a collaborative basis, not because we’re going to compromise our principle, but because there’s common ground.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the challenges America faces right now — look, the reason I’m in this race is there are people that are really hurting today in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we face — this deficit could crush the future generations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What’s happening in the Middle East, there are developments around the world that are of real concern.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And Republicans and Democrats both love America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we need to have leadership — leadership in Washington that will actually bring people together and get the job done and could not care less if — if it’s a Republican or a Democrat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve done it before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll do it again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all, I think Governor Romney’s going to have a busy first day, because he’s also going to repeal Obamacare, which will not be very popular among Democrats as you’re sitting down with them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: (LAUGHTER) But, look, my philosophy has been, I will take ideas from anybody, Democrat or Republican, as long as they’re advancing the cause of making middle-class families stronger and giving ladders of opportunity to the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we cut taxes for middle- class families and small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we cut a trillion dollars of spending that wasn’t advancing that cause.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we signed three trade deals into law that are helping us to double our exports and sell more American products around the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we repealed “don’t ask/don’t tell.” That’s how we ended the war in Iraq, as I promised, and that’s how we’re going to wind down the war in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we went after Al Qaida and bin Laden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’ve — we’ve seen progress even under Republican control of the House of Representatives.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, ultimately, part of being principled, part of being a leader is, A, being able to describe exactly what it is that you intend to do, not just saying, “I’ll sit down,” but you have to have a plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, what’s important is occasionally you’ve got to say no, to — to — to folks both in your own party and in the other party.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, yes, have we had some fights between me and the Republicans when — when they fought back against us reining in the excesses of Wall Street?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Absolutely, because that was a fight that needed to be had.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When — when we were fighting about whether or not we were going to make sure that Americans had more security with their health insurance and they said no, yes, that was a fight that we needed to have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so part of leadership and governing is both saying what it is that you are for, but also being willing to say no to some things.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve got to tell you, Governor Romney, when it comes to his own party during the course of this campaign, has not displayed that willingness to say no to some of the more extreme parts of his party.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Jim, I want to thank you, and I want to thank Governor Romney, because I think was a terrific debate, and I very much appreciate it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to thank the University of Denver.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, four years ago, we were going through a major crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And yet my faith and confidence in the American future is undiminished.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason is because of its people, because of the woman I met in North Carolina who decided at 55 to go back to school because she wanted to inspire her daughter and now has a job from that new training that she’s gotten; because a company in Minnesota who was willing to give up salaries and perks for their executives to make sure that they didn’t lay off workers during a recession.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The auto workers that you meet in Toledo or Detroit take such pride in building the best cars in the world, not just because of a paycheck, but because it gives them that sense of pride, that they’re helping to build America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question now is how do we build on those strengths.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And everything that I’ve tried to do, and everything that I’m now proposing for the next four years in terms of improving our education system or developing American energy or making sure that we’re closing loopholes for companies that are shipping jobs overseas and focusing on small businesses and companies that are creating jobs here in the United States, or closing our deficit in a responsible, balanced way that allows us to invest in our future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All those things are designed to make sure that the American people, their genius, their grit, their determination, is — is channeled and — and they have an opportunity to succeed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And everybody’s getting a fair shot.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And everybody’s getting a fair share — everybody’s doing a fair share, and everybody’s playing by the same rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, four years ago, I said that I’m not a perfect man and I wouldn’t be a perfect president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s probably a promise that Governor Romney thinks I’ve kept.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also promised that I’d fight every single day on behalf of the American people, the middle class, and all those who were striving to get into the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve kept that promise and if you’ll vote for me, then I promise I’ll fight just as hard in a second term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jim, and Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And thank you for tuning in this evening.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a — this is an important election and I’m concerned about America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m concerned about the direction America has been taking over the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I know this is bigger than an election about the two of us as individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s bigger than our respective parties.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s an election about the course of America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What kind of America do you want to have for yourself and for your children.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there really are two very different paths that we began speaking about this evening, and over the course of this month we’re going to have two more presidential debates and a vice presidential debate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re talk about those two paths.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But they lead in very different directions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s not just looking to our words that you have to take in evidence of where they go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can look at the record.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There’s no question in my mind that if the president were to be reelected you’ll continue to see a middle-class squeeze with incomes going down and prices going up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get incomes up again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll see chronic unemployment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve had 43 straight months with unemployment above 8 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I’m president I will create — help create 12 million new jobs in this country with rising incomes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the president’s reelected, Obamacare will be fully installed.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In my view that’s going to mean a whole different way of life for people who counted on the insurance plan they had in the past.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Many will lose it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re going to see health premiums go up by some $2,500 per family.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I’m elected we won’t have Obama.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll put in place the kind of principles that I put in place in my own state and allow each state to craft their own programs to get people insured and we’ll focus on getting the cost of health care down.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the president were to be reelected you’re going to see a $716 billion cut to Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll have 4 million people who will lose Medicare Advantage.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll have hospital and providers that’ll no longer accept Medicare patients.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll restore that $716 billion to Medicare.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s reelected you’ll see dramatic cuts to our military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The secretary of defense has said these would be even devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not cut our commitment to our military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will keep America strong and get America’s middle class working again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jim.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What is was, it was a tragedy, Martha.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It — Chris Stevens was one of our best.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We lost three other brave Americans.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I can make absolutely two commitments to you and all the American people tonight.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: One, we will find and bring to justice the men who did this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And secondly, we will get to the bottom of it, and whatever — wherever the facts lead us, wherever they lead us, we will make clear to the American public, because whatever mistakes were made will not be made again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When you’re looking at a president, Martha, it seems to me that you should take a look at his most important responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s caring for the national security of the country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the best way to do that is take a look at how he’s handled the issues of the day.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: On Iraq, the president said he would end the war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Governor Romney said that was a tragic mistake, we should have left 30,000 — he ended it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Governor Romney said that was a tragic mistake, we should have left 30,000 troops there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With regard to Afghanistan, he said he will end the war in 2014.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Governor Romney said we should not set a date, number one.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And number two, with regard to 2014, it depends.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When it came to Osama bin Laden, the president the first day in office, I was sitting with him in the Oval Office, he called in the CIA and signed an order saying, “My highest priority is to get bin Laden.” Prior to the election, prior to the — him being sworn in, Governor Romney was asked the question about how he would proceed.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He said, “I wouldn’t move heaven and earth to get bin Laden.” He didn’t understand it was more than about taking a murderer off the battlefield.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It was about restoring America’s heart and letting terrorists around the world know, if you do harm to America, we will track you to the gates of hell if need be.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And lastly, the president of the United States has — has led with a steady hand and clear vision.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Governor Romney, the opposite.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The last thing we need now is another war.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We mourn the loss of these four Americans who were murdered.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When you take a look at what has happened just in the last few weeks, they sent the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: ambassador out to say that this was because of a protest and a YouTube video.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It took the president two weeks to acknowledge that this was a terrorist attack.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He went to the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: and in his speech at the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: he said six times — he talked about the YouTube video.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, if we’re hit by terrorists we’re going to call it for what it is, a terrorist attack.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our ambassador in Paris has a Marine detachment guarding him.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Shouldn’t we have a Marine detachment guarding our ambassador in Benghazi, a place where we knew that there was an Al Qaida cell with arms?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is becoming more troubling by the day.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They first blamed the YouTube video.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now they’re trying to blame the Romney-Ryan ticket for making this an issue.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: With respect to Iraq, we had the same position before the withdrawal, which was we agreed with the Obama administration.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let’s have a status of forces agreement to make sure that we secure our gains.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president was put in charge of those negotiations by President Obama and they failed to get the agreement.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t have a status of forces agreement because they failed to get one.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we are talking about.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, when it comes to our veterans, we owe them a great debt of gratitude for what they’ve done for us, including your son Beau.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we also want to make sure that we don’t lose the things we fought so hard to get.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, with respect to Afghanistan, the 2014 deadline, we agree with a 2014 transition.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But what we also want it do is make sure that we’re not projecting weakness abroad, and that’s what’s happening here.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This Benghazi issue would be a tragedy in and of itself, but unfortunately it’s indicative of a broader problem.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And that is what we are watching on our TV screens is the unraveling of the Obama foreign policy, which is making the (inaudible) more chaotic us less safe.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: On that same day, the Obama administration had the exact same position.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let’s recall that they disavowed their own statement that they had put out earlier in the day in Cairo.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: So we had the same position, but we will — it’s never too early to speak out for our values.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We should have spoken out right away when the green revolution was up and starting; when the mullahs in Iran were attacking their people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We should not have called Bashar Assad a reformer when he was turning his Russian-provided guns on his own people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We should always stand up for peace, for democracy, for individual rights.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we should not be imposing these devastating defense cuts, because what that does when we equivocate on our values, when we show that we’re cutting down on defense, it makes us more weak.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It projects weakness.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And when we look weak, our adversaries are much more willing to test us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re more brazen in their attacks, and are allies are less willing to… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With all due respect, that’s a bunch of malarkey.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because not a single thing he said is accurate.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: First of all…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I will be very specific.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Number one, the — this lecture on embassy security — the congressman here cut embassy security in his budget by $300 million below what we asked for, number one.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So much for the embassy security piece.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Number two, Governor Romney, before he knew the facts, before he even knew that our ambassador was killed, he was out making a political statement which was panned by the media around the world.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And this talk about this — this weakness.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — I don’t understand what my friend’s talking about here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We — this is a president who’s gone out and done everything he has said he was going to do.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is a guy who’s repaired our alliances so the rest of the world follows us again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is the guy who brought the entire world, including Russia and China, to bring about the most devastating — most devastating — the most devastating efforts on Iran to make sure that they in fact stop (inaudible).

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, I — I just — I mean, these guys bet against America all the time.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because that was exactly what we were told by the intelligence community.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The intelligence community told us that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: As they learned more facts about exactly what happened, they changed their assessment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why there’s also an investigation headed by Tom Pickering, a leading diplomat from the Reagan years, who is doing an investigation as to whether or not there are any lapses, what the lapses were, so that they will never happen again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, we weren’t told they wanted more security there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We did not know they wanted more security again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And by the way, at the time we were told exactly — we said exactly what the intelligence community told us that they knew.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was the assessment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And as the intelligence community changed their view, we made it clear they changed their view.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why I said we will get to the bottom of this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You know, usually when there’s a crisis, we pull together.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We pull together as a nation.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But as I said, even before we knew what happened to the ambassador, the governor was holding a press conference — was holding a press conference.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s not presidential leadership.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Oh, gosh, yes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Urinating on Taliban corpses?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not apologize for…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What — what we should not be apologizing for are standing up for our values.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not be doing is saying to the Egyptian people, while Mubarak is cracking down on them, that he’s a good guy and, in the next week, say he ought to go.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we should not be doing is rejecting claims for — for calls for more security in our barracks, in our Marine — we need Marines in Benghazi when the commander on the ground says we need more forces for security.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: There were requests for extra security; those requests were not honored.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, this was the anniversary of 9/11.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It was Libya, a country we knew we had Al Qaida cells there, as we know Al Qaida and its affiliates are on the rise in Northern Africa.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we did not give our ambassador in Benghazi a Marine detachment?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of course there’s an investigation, so we can make sure that this never happens again, but when it comes to speaking up for our values, we should not apologize for those.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Here’s the problem.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at all the various issues out there, and it’s unraveling before our eyes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president talks about sanctions on Iran.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They got — we’ve had four…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We cannot allow Iran to gain a nuclear weapons capability.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, let’s take a look at where we’ve gone — come from.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When Barack Obama was elected, they had enough fissile material — nuclear material to make one bomb.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now they have enough for five.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re racing toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re four years closer toward a nuclear weapons capability.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve had four different sanctions, the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: on Iran, three from the Bush administration, one here.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And the only reason we got it is because Russia watered it down and prevented the — the sanctions from hitting the central bank.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney proposed these sanctions in 2007.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In Congress, I’ve been fighting for these sanctions since 2009.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The administration was blocking us every step of the way.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Only because we had strong bipartisan support for these tough sanctions were we able to overrule their objections and put them in spite of the administration.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Imagine what would have happened if we had these sanctions in place earlier.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You think Iran’s not brazen?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at what they’re doing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re stepping up their terrorist attacks.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They tried a terrorist attack in the United States last year when they tried to blow up the Saudi ambassador at a restaurant in Washington, D.C.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And talk about credibility?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When this administration says that all options are on the table, they send out senior administration officials that send all these mixed signals.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so, in order to solve this peacefully — which is everybody’s goal — you have to have the ayatollahs change their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at where they are.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re moving faster toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s because this administration has no credibility on this issue.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s because this administration watered down sanctions, delayed sanctions, tried to stop us for putting the tough sanctions in place.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now we have them in place because of Congress.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They say the military option’s on the table, but it’s not being viewed as credible.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And the key is to do this peacefully, is to make sure that we have credibility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Under a Romney administration, we will have credibility on this issue.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s incredible.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, imagine had we let the Republican Congress work out the sanctions.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You think there’s any possibility the entire world would have joined us, Russia and China, all of our allies?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: These are the most crippling sanctions in the history of sanctions, period.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When Governor Romney’s asked about it, he said, “We gotta keep these sanctions.” When he said, “Well, you’re talking about doing more,” what are you — you’re going to go to war?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Is that what you want to do?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want to prevent war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the interesting thing is, how are they going to prevent war?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: How are they going to prevent war if they say there’s nothing more that we — that they say we should do than what we’ve already done, number one.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And number two, with regard to the ability of the United States to take action militarily, it is — it is not in my purview to talk about classified information.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But we feel quite confident we could deal a serious blow to the Iranians.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But number two, the Iranians are — the Israelis and the United States, our military and intelligence communities are absolutely the same exact place in terms of how close — how close the Iranians are to getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are a good way away.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There is no difference between our view and theirs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When my friend talks about fissile material, they have to take this highly enriched uranium, get it from 20 percent up, then they have to be able to have something to put it in.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There is no weapon that the Iranians have at this point.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Both the Israelis and we know — we’ll know if they start the process of building a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So all this bluster I keep hearing, all this loose talk, what are they talking about?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Are you talking about, to be more credible — what more can the president do, stand before the United Nations, tell the whole world, directly communicate to the ayatollah, we will not let them acquire a nuclear weapon, period, unless he’s talking about going to war.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let’s look at this from the view of the ayatollahs.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What do they see?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see this administration trying to water down sanctions in Congress for over two years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re moving faster toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re spinning the centrifuges faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see us saying when we come into the administration, when they’re sworn in, we need more space with our ally, Israel.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see President Obama in New York City the same day Bibi Netanyahu is and he, instead of meeting with him, goes on a — on a daily talk show.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They see, when we say that these options are on the table, the secretary of defense walked them back.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They are not changing their mind.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we have to do, is change their mind so they stop pursuing nuclear weapons, and they’re going faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can debate a time line.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can debate the time line, whether there’s — it’s that short a time or longer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I agree that it’s probably longer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Number two, it’s all about… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I don’t want to go into classified stuff.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we both agree that to do this peacefully you’ve got to get them to change their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re not changing their minds.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And look at what this administration…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me tell you what the ayatollah sees.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You have to have credibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The ayatollah sees his economy being crippled.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The ayatollah sees that there are 50 percent fewer exports of oil.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He sees the currency going into the tank.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He sees the economy going into freefall.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And he sees the world for the first time totally united in opposition to him getting a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now, with regard to Bibi, who’s been my friend 39 years, the president has met with Bibi a dozen times.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s spoken to Bibi Netanyahu as much as he’s spoken to anybody.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The idea that we’re not — I was in a, just before he went to the U.N., I was in a conference call with the — with the president, with him talking to Bibi for well over an hour, in — in — in stark relief and detail of what was going on.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This is a bunch of stuff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, here’s the deal.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, it means it’s simply inaccurate.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: (LAUGHTER) We Irish call it malarkey.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We Irish call it malarkey.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But last thing.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The secretary of defense has made it absolutely clear, we didn’t walk anything back.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We will not allow the Iranians to get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What Bibi held up there was when they get to the point where they can enrich uranium enough to put into a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They don’t have a weapon to put it into.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let’s all calm down a little bit here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Iran is more isolated today than when we took office.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It was on the ascendancy when we took office.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is totally isolated.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I don’t know what world this guy’s living in.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Thank heavens we had these sanctions in place.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s in spite of their opposition.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’ve given 20 waivers to this sanction.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And all I have to point to are the results.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re four years closer toward a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I think that case speaks for itself.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: By the way, they… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … they are not four years closer to a nuclear weapon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of course they are.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re — they’re closer to being able to get enough fissile material to put in a weapon if they had a weapon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Oh, I didn’t say — no, I’m not saying that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But facts matter, Martha.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You’re a foreign policy expert.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Facts matter.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: All this loose talk about them, “All they have to do is get to enrich uranium in a certain amount and they have a weapon,” not true.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are more — and if we ever have to take action, unlike when we took office, we will have the world behind us, and that matters.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That matters.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He is right.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It could prove catastrophic, if we didn’t do it with precision.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And what it does is it undermines our credibility by backing up the point when we make it that all options are on the table.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the point.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The ayatollahs see these kinds of statements and they think, “I’m going to get a nuclear weapon.” When — when we see the kind of equivocation that took place because this administration wanted a precondition policy, so when the Green Revolution started up, they were silent for nine days.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When they see us putting — when they see us putting daylight between ourselves and our allies in Israel, that gives them encouragement.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When they see Russia watering down any further sanctions, the only reason we got a U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: sanction is because Russia watered it down and prevented these central bank sanctions in the first place.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: So when they see this kind of activity, they are encouraged to continue, and that’s the problem.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Martha, let me tell you what Russia… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I’ll tell you what’s worse.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I’ll tell you what’s worse.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: A nuclear-armed Iran which triggers a nuclear arms race in the Middle East.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is the world’s largest sponsor of — of terrorism.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’ve dedicated themselves… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … to wiping an entire country off the map.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They call us the Great Satan.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And if they get nuclear weapons, other people in the neighborhood will pursue their nuclear weapons, as well.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can’t live with that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: War should always be the absolute last resort.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why these crippling sanctions, which Bibi Netanyahu says we should continue, which — if I’m not mistaken — Governor Romney says we — we should continue.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I may be mistaken.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He changes his mind so often, I could be wrong.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But the fact of the matter is, he says they’re working.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the fact is that they are being crippled by them.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And we’ve made it clear, big nations can’t bluff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This president doesn’t bluff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I don’t know how long it will take.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We can and we will get it under 6 percent.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let’s look at — let’s take a look at the facts.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let’s look at where we were when we came to office.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The economy was in free fall.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We had — the great recession hit; 9 million people lost their job; $1.7 — $1.6 trillion in wealth lost in equity in your homes, in retirement accounts for the middle class.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We knew we had to act for the middle class.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We immediately went out and rescued General Motors.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We went ahead and made sure that we cut taxes for the middle class.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And in addition to that, when that — when that occurred, what did Romney do?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Romney said, “No, let Detroit go bankrupt.” We moved in and helped people refinance their homes.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Governor Romney said, “No, let foreclosures hit the bottom.” But it shouldn’t be surprising for a guy who says 47 percent of the American people are unwilling to take responsibility for their own lives.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My friend recently in a speech in Washington said “30 percent of the American people are takers.” These people are my mom and dad — the people I grew up with, my neighbors.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They pay more effective tax than Governor Romney pays in his federal income tax.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are elderly people who in fact are living off of Social Security.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are veterans and people fighting in Afghanistan right now who are, quote, “not paying any tax.” I’ve had it up to here with this notion that 47 percent — it’s about time they take some responsibility here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And instead of signing pledges to Grover Norquist not to ask the wealthiest among us to contribute to bring back the middle class, they should be signing a pledge saying to the middle class we’re going to level the playing field; we’re going to give you a fair shot again; we are going to not repeat the mistakes we made in the past by having a different set of rules for Wall Street and Main Street, making sure that we continue to hemorrhage these tax cuts for the super wealthy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re pushing the continuation of a tax cut that will give an additional $500 billion in tax cuts to 120,000 families.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And they’re holding hostage the middle class tax cut because they say we won’t pass — we won’t continue the middle class tax cut unless you give the tax cut for the super wealthy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s about time they take some responsibility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Joe and I are from similar towns.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’s from Scranton, Pennsylvania.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I’m from Janesville, Wisconsin.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You know what the unemployment rate in Scranton is today?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s 10 percent.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You know what it was the day you guys came in — 8.5 percent.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s how it’s going all around America.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You don’t read the statistics.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s not how it’s going.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s going down.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, did they come in and inherit a tough situation?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we’re going in the wrong direction.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at where we are.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The economy is barely limping along.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s growing a 1.3 percent.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s slower than it grew last year and last year was slower than the year before.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Job growth in September was slower than it was in August, and August was slower than it was in July.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re heading in the wrong direction; 23 million Americans are struggling for work today; 15 percent of Americans are living in poverty today.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is not what a real recovery looks like.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We need real reforms for real recovery and that’s exactly what Mitt Romney and I are proposing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s a five-point plan.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Get America energy independent in North America by the end of the decade.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Help people who are hurting get the skills they need to get the jobs they want.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Get this deficit and debt under control to prevent a debt crisis.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Make trade work for America so we can make more things in America and sell them overseas, and champion small businesses.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Don’t raise taxes on small businesses because they’re our job creators.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He talks about Detroit.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney’s a car guy.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They keep misquoting him, but let me tell you about the Mitt Romney I know.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is a guy who I was talking to a family in Northborough, Massachusetts the other day, Sheryl and Mark Nixon.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Their kids were hit in a car crash, four of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Two of them, Rob and Reed, were paralyzed.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The Romneys didn’t know them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They went to the same church; they never met before.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt asked if he could come over on Christmas.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He brought his boys, his wife, and gifts.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Later on, he said, “I know you’re struggling, Mark.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Don’t worry about their college.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I’ll pay for it.” When Mark told me this story, because, you know what, Mitt Romney doesn’t tell these stories.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The Nixons told this story.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When he told me this story, he said it wasn’t the help, the cash help.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s that he gave his time, and he has consistently.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is a man who gave 30 percent of his income to charity, more than the two of us combined.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney’s a good man.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He cares about 100 percent of Americans in this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And with respect to that quote, I think the vice president very well knows that sometimes the words don’t come out of your mouth the right way.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But I always say what I mean.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And so does Romney.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want everybody to succeed.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want to get people out of poverty, in the middle class, onto a life of self-sufficiently.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We believe in opportunity and upward mobility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we’re going to push for in a Romney administration.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The idea — if you heard that — that little soliloquy on 47 percent and you think he just made a mistake, then I think you’re — I — I think — I got a bridge to sell you.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, I don’t doubt his personal generosity.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And I understand what it’s like.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: When I was a little younger than the congressman, my wife was in an accident, killed my daughter and my wife, and my two sons survived.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I have sat in the homes of many people who’ve gone through what I get through, because the one thing you can give people solace is to know if they know you’ve been through it, that they can make it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So I don’t doubt his personal commitment to individuals.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But you know what?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I know he had no commitment to the automobile industry.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He just — he said, let it go bankrupt, period.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let it drop out.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: All this talk — we saved a million jobs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Two hundred thousand people are working today.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And I’ve never met two guys who’re more down on America across the board.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’re told everything’s going bad.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There are 5.2 million new jobs, private-sector jobs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We need more, but 5.2 million — if they’d get out of the way, if they’d get out of the way and let us pass the tax cut for the middle class, make it permanent, if they get out of the way and pass the — pass the jobs bill, if they get out of the way and let us allow 14 million people who are struggling to stay in their homes because their mortgages are upside down, but they never missed a mortgage payment, just get out of the way.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Stop talking about how you care about people.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Show me something.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Show me a policy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Show me a policy where you take responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, by the way, they talk about this Great Recession if it fell out of the sky, like, “Oh, my goodness, where did it come from?” It came from this man voting to put two wars on a credit card, to at the same time put a prescription drug benefit on the credit card, a trillion-dollar tax cut for the very wealthy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I was there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I voted against them.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I said, no, we can’t afford that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And now, all of a sudden, these guys are so seized with the concern about the debt that they created.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let’s not forget that they came in with one-party control.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When Barack Obama was elected, his party controlled everything.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They had the ability to do everything of their choosing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And look at where we are right now.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They passed the stimulus.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The idea that we could borrow $831 billion, spend it on all of these special interest groups, and that it would work out just fine, that unemployment would never get to 8 percent — it went up above 8 percent for 43 months.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They said that, right now, if we just passed this stimulus, the economy would grow at 4 percent.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s growing at 1.3.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what our entire premise of our pro-growth plan for a stronger middle class is all about: getting the economy growing at 4 percent, creating 12 million jobs over the next four years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at just the $90 billion in stimulus.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president was in charge of overseeing this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: $90 billion in green pork to campaign contributors and special interest groups.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: There are just at the Department of Energy over 100 criminal investigations that have been launched into just how stimulus… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Martha, look.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: His colleague…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Crony capitalism and corporate welfare.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … runs an investigative committee, spent months and months and months going into this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is the — this is the inspector general.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Months and months.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They found no evidence of cronyism.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And I love my friend here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — I’m not allowed to show letters but go on our website, he sent me two letters saying, “By the way, can you send me some stimulus money for companies here in the state of Wisconsin?” We sent millions of dollars.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You know… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Sure he did.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: On two occasions we — we — we advocated for constituents who were applying for grants.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we do.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We do that for all constituents who are… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I love that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I love that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: This was such a bad program and he writes me a letter saying — writes the Department of Energy a letter saying, “The reason we need this stimulus, it will create growth and jobs.” His words.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And now he’s sitting here looking at me.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And by the way, that program, again, investigated.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What the Congress said was it was a model.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Less than four-tenths of 1 percent waste or fraud in the program.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And all this talk about cronyism.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They investigated and investigated, did not find one single piece of evidence.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I wish he would just tell — be a little more candid.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Was it a good idea to spend taxpayer dollars on electric cars in Finland, or on windmills in China?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Was it a good idea to borrow all this money from countries like China and spend it on all these various different interest groups?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me tell you what was a good idea.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It was a good idea, Moody’s and others said that this was exactly what we needed to stop this from going off the cliff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It set the conditions to be able to grow again.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have, in fact, 4 percent of those green jobs didn’t go under — went under, didn’t work.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s a better batting average than investment bankers have.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They have about a 40 percent…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Where are the 5 million green jobs that were being…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: By the way, any letter you send me, I’ll entertain.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I appreciate that, Joe.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Medicare and Social Security are going bankrupt.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: These are indisputable facts.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, when I look at these programs, we’ve all had tragedies in our lives.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I think about what they’ve done for my own family.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: My mom and I had my grandmother move in with us who was facing Alzheimer’s.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Medicare was there for here, just like it’s there for my mom right now who is a Florida senior.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: After my dad died, my mom and I got Social Security survivors benefits, helped me pay for college, it helped her go back to college in her 50s where she started a small business because of the new skills she got.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: She paid all of her taxes on the promise that these programs would be there for her.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We will honor this promise.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And the best way to do it is reform it for my generation.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You see, if you reform these programs for my generation, people 54 and below, you can guarantee they don’t change for people in or near retirement, which is precisely what Mitt Romney and I are proposing.

speaker: RYAN

sentence: Here’s what we’re saying: give younger people, when they become Medicare eligible, guaranteed coverage options that you can’t be denied, including traditional Medicare.

sentence: We would rather have 50 million future seniors determine how their Medicare is delivered to them instead of 15 bureaucrats deciding what, if, when, where they get it.

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: BIDEN

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You don’t cut defense by a trillion dollars.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we’re talking about.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Who’s cutting it by $1 trillion?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re going to cut 80,000 soldiers, 20,000 Marines, 120 cargo planes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re going to push the Joint Strike Fighter out…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: If these cuts go through, our Navy will be the smallest — the smallest it has been since before World War I.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This invites weakness.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, do we believe in peace through strength?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You bet we do.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And that means you don’t impose these devastating cuts on our military.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: So we’re saying don’t cut the military by a trillion dollars.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not increase it by a trillion, don’t cut it by a trillion dollars.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, we don’t cut it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And I might add, this so-called — I know we don’t want to use the fancy word “sequester,” this automatic cut — that was part of a debt deal that they asked for.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And let me tell you what my friend said at a press conference announcing his support of the deal.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He said, and I’m paraphrase, We’ve been looking for this moment for a long time.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Can I tell you what that meant?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve been looking for bipartisanship for a long time.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And so the bipartisanship is what he voted for, the automatic cuts in defense if they didn’t act.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And beyond that, they asked for another — look, the military says we need a smaller, leaner Army, we need more special forces, we need — we don’t need more M1 tanks, what we need is more UAVs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Not some of the military.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was the decision of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, recommended to us and agreed to by the president.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is a fact.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They made the recommendation first.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Can I get into that for a second?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t want to lose the gains we’ve gotten.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want to make sure that the Taliban does not come back in and give Al Qaida a safe haven.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We agree with the administration on their 2014 transition.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, when I think about Afghanistan, I think about the incredible job that our troops have done.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You’ve been there more than the two of us combined.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: First time I was there in 2002, it was amazing to me what they were facing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When I went to the Ahgandah (ph) Valley in Kandahar before the surge, I sat down with a young private in the 82nd from the Monamanee (ph) Indian reservation who would tell me what he did every day, and I was in awe.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And to see what they had in front of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And then to go back there in December, to go throughout Helmand with the Marines, to see what they had accomplished, it’s nothing short of amazing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we don’t want to do is lose the gains we’ve gotten.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, we’ve disagreed from time to time on a few issues.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We would have more likely taken into accounts the recommendations from our commanders, General Petraeus, Admiral Mullen, on troop levels throughout this year’s fighting season.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve been skeptical about negotiations with the Taliban, especially while they’re shooting at us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we want to see the 2014 transition be successful, and that means we want to make sure our commanders have what they need to make sure that it is successful so that this does not once again become a launching pad for terrorists.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Martha, let’s keep our eye on the ball.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The reason — I’ve been in and out of Afghanistan and Iraq 20 times.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’ve been up in the Konar (ph) Valley.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’ve been throughout that whole country, mostly in a helicopter, and sometimes in a vehicle.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The fact is, we went there for one reason: to get those people who killed Americans, Al Qaida.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’ve decimated Al Qaida central.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have eliminated Osama bin Laden.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was our purpose.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, in fact, in the meantime, what we said we would do, we would help train the Afghan military.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s their responsibility to take over their own security.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s why with 49 of our allies in Afghanistan, we’ve agreed on a gradual drawdown so we’re out of there by the year 20 — in the year 2014.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My friend and the governor say it’s based on conditions, which means it depends.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It does not depend for us.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is the responsibility of the Afghans to take care of their own security.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have trained over 315,000, mostly without incident.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There have been more than two dozen cases of green-on-blue where Americans have been killed.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: If we do not — if the measures the military has taken do not take hold, we will not go on joint patrols.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We will not train in the field.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’ll only train in the — in the Army bases that exist there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But we are leaving.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are leaving in 2014.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And in the process, we’re going to be saving over the next 10 years another $800 billion.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’ve been in this war for over a decade.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The primary objective is almost completed.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now, all we’re doing is putting the Kabul government in a position to be able to maintain their own security.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s their responsibility, not America’s.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t want to stay.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want — look, one of my best friends in Janesville, a reservist, is at a forward-operating base in eastern Afghanistan right now.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our wives are best friends.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our daughters are best friends.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I want — I want him and all of our troops to come home as soon and safely as possible.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We want to make sure that 2014 is successful.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why we want to make sure that we give our commanders what they say they need to make it successful.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t want to extend beyond 2014.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the point we’re making.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You know, if it was just this, I’d feel like we would — we would be able to call this a success, but it’s not.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we are witnessing as we turn on our television screens these days is the absolute unraveling of the Obama foreign policy.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Problems are growing at home, but — problems are growing abroad, but jobs aren’t growing here at home.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Do you know why we say that?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’d like to know… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Because we don’t want to broadcast to our enemies “put a date on your calendar, wait us out, and then come back.” We want to make sure… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We do agree — we do agree with the timeline and the transition, but what we — what any administration will do in 2013 is assess the situation to see how best to complete this timeline.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What we do not want to do…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We will leave in 2014.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … what we don’t want to do is give our allies reason to trust us less and our enemies more — we don’t want to embolden our enemies to hold and wait out for us and then take over… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Martha, that’s a bizarre statement.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why we want to make sure — no, that’s why we want to make sure that… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Forty-nine of our allies — hear me — 49 of our allies signed on to this position.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we’re reading that they want to… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Forty-nine — 49 of our allies said “out in 2014.” It’s the responsibility of the Afghans.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have other responsibilities… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Which is — which is… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look, the Taliban — what we’ve found out, and we — you saw it in Iraq, Martha, unless you set a timeline, Baghdad, in the case of Iraq, and — and Kabul, in the case of Afghanistan will not step up.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re happy to let us continue to do the job; international security forces to do the job.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The only way they step up is to say, “Fellas, we’re leaving; we’ve trained you; step up, step up.”

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s the only way it works.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The military reason…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … was bringing — by the way, when the president announced the surge, you’ll remember, Martha, he said the surge will be out by the end of the summer.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The military said the surge will be out.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Nothing political about this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Before the surge occurred — so you be a little straight with me here, too — before the surge occurred, we said they’ll be out by the end of the summer.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s what the military said.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The reason for that is…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There are people that are concerned, but not the Joint Chiefs.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was their recommendation in the Oval Office to the president of the United States of America.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I sat there.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’m sure you’ll find someone who disagrees with the Pentagon.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’m positive you’ll find that within the military.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But that’s not the case here.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, secondly, the reason why the military said that is, you cannot wait and have a cliff.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It takes — you know — months and months and months to draw down forces.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let me… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Let me try and illustrate the issue here, because I think this — it can get a little confusing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve all met with General Allen and General Scaparrotti in Afghanistan to talk about fighting seasons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Here’s the way it works.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The mountain passes fill in with snow.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The Taliban and the terrorists and the Haqqani and the Quetta Shura come over from Pakistan to fight our men and women.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: When it fills in with snow, they can’t do it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what we call fighting seasons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In the warm months, fighting gets really high.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: In the winter, it goes down.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so when Admiral Mullen and General Petraeus came to Congress and said, if you pull these people out before the fighting season is end, it puts people more at risk.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the problem.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Yes, we drew 22,000 troops down last month, but the remaining troops that are there, who still have the same mission to prosecute counterinsurgency, are doing it with fewer people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That makes them less safe.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Fighting season…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re sending fewer people out in all of these hotspots to do the same job that they were supposed to do a month ago.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Because we turned it over…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But we took 22,000 people out…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: … we turned it over to the Afghan troops we trained.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: No one got pulled out that didn’t get filled in by trained Afghan personnel.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And he’s — he’s conflating two issues.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The fighting season that Petraeus was talking about and former — and Admiral Mullen was the fighting season this spring.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s what he was talking about.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We did not — we did not pull them out.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The calendar works the same every year.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It does work the same every year.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But we’re not staying there…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Spring, summer, fall.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s warm, or it’s not.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still fighting us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still coming over the passes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re still coming into Zabul, to Kunar, to all of these areas, but we are sending fewer people to the front to fight them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And that’s… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s right, because that’s the Afghan responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’ve trained them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not in the east.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Not in the east?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: East is the most dangerous place in the world.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s right.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why we don’t want to send fewer people to the…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s — that’s why we should send Americans in to do the job, instead of the — you’d rather Americans be going in doing the job instead of the trainees?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We are already sending Americans to do the job, but fewer of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s the whole problem.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s right.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’re sending in more Afghans to do the job, Afghans to do the job.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Different country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s a different country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is five times as large geographically, it has one-fifth the population, that is Libya, one-fifth the population, five times as large geographically.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s in a part of the world where they’re not going to see whatever would come from that war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It seep into a regional war.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You’re in a country that is heavily populated in the midst of the most dangerous area in the world.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, in fact, if in fact it blows up and the wrong people gain control, it’s going to have impact on the entire region causing potentially regional wars.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are working hand and glove with the Turks, with the Jordanians, with the Saudis, and with all the people in the region attempting to identify the people who deserve the help so that when Assad goes — and he will go — there will be a legitimate government that follows on, not an Al Qaida-sponsored government that follows on.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And all this loose talk of my friend, Governor Romney, and the congressman, about how we’re going to do, we could do so much more in there, what more would they do other than put American boots on the ground?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The last thing America needs is to get in another ground war in the Middle East, requiring tens of thousands, if not well over 100,000 American forces.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That — they are the facts.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They are the facts.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now, every time the governor is asked about this, he doesn’t say anything.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He — he goes up with a whole lot of verbiage, but when he gets pressed he says, no, he would not do anything different than we are doing now.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Are they proposing putting American troops on the ground?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Putting American aircraft in the airspace?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Is that what they’re proposing?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: If they do, they should speak up and say so, but that’s not what they’re saying.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are doing it exactly like we need to do to identify those forces who, in fact, will provide for a stable government and not cause a regional Sunni-Shia war when Bassad (sic) — when Bashar Assad falls.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Nobody is proposing to send troops to Syria.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: American troops.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, let me say it this way.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: How would we do things differently?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We wouldn’t refer to Bashar Assad as a reformer when he’s killing his own civilians with his Russian-provided weapons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We wouldn’t be outsourcing our foreign policy to the United Nations giving Vladimir Putin veto power over our efforts to try and deal with this issue.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’s vetoed three of them.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Hillary Clinton went to Russia to try and convince them not to do so.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They thwarted her efforts.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: She said they were on the wrong side of history.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: She was right about that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is just one more example of how the Russia reset’s not working.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And so where are we?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: After international pressure mounted, the President Obama said Bashar Assad should go.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s been over a year.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The man has slaughtered tens of thousands of his own people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And more foreign fighters are spilling into this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: So the longer this has gone on, the more people, groups like Al Qaida are going in.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We could have more easily identified the free Syrian army, the freedom fighters, working with our allies, the Turks, the Qataris, the Saudis, had we had a better plan in place to begin with working through our allies.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But, no, we waited for Kofi Annan to try and come up with an agreement through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That bought Bashar Assad time.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We gave Russia veto power over our efforts through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And meanwhile about 30,000 Syrians are dead.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: What would my friend do differently?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: If you notice, he never answers the question.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: No, I would — I — we would not be going through the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: in all of these things.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me — you don’t go through the U.N.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We are in the process now — and have been for months — in making sure that help, humanitarian aid, as well as other aid and training is getting to those forces that we believe, the Turks believe, the Jordanians believe, the Saudis believe are the free forces inside of Syria.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is underway.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Our allies were all on the same page, NATO, as well as our Arab allies, in terms of trying to get a settlement.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That was their idea.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We’re the ones that said, “Enough.” With regard to the reset not working, the fact of the matter is that Russia has a different interest in Syria than we do, and that’s not in our interest.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Then Iran keeps their greatest ally in the region.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’s a sponsor of terrorism.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He’ll probably continue slaughtering his people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We and the world community will lose our credibility on this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, he mentioned the reset…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Well, we agree with the same red line, actually, they do on chemical weapons, but not putting American troops in, other than to secure those chemical weapons.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re right about that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But what we should have done earlier is work with those freedom fighters, those dissidents in Syria.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We should not have called Bashar Assad a reformer.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … we should not have — we should not have waited to Russia…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … should not have waited for Russia to give us the green light at the U.N.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: to do something about it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re — they’re still arming the man.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Iran is flying flights over Iraq…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the opposition is being armed.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … to help Bashar Assad.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And, by the way, if we had the status-of-forces agreement that the vice president said he would bet his vice presidency on in Iraq, we probably would have been able to prevent that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But he failed to achieve that, as well, again.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What is in the national interests of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What is in the national security of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s got to be in the strategic national interests of our country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Each situation will — will come up with its own set of circumstances, but putting American troops on the ground?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s got to be within the national security interests of the American people.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That means like embargoes and sanctions and overflights, those are things that don’t put American troops on the ground.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But if you’re talking about putting American troops on the ground, only in our national security interests.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I don’t see how a person can separate their public life from their private life or from their faith.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our faith informs us in everything we do.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: My faith informs me about how to take care of the vulnerable, of how to make sure that people have a chance in life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, you want to ask basically why I’m pro-life?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s not simply because of my Catholic faith.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s a factor, of course.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But it’s also because of reason and science.

speaker: RYAN
