sentence: You know, I think about 10 1/2 years ago, my wife Janna and I went to Mercy Hospital in Janesville where I was born, for our seven week ultrasound for our firstborn child, and we saw that heartbeat.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: A little baby was in the shape of a bean.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And to this day, we have nicknamed our firstborn child Liza, “Bean.” Now I believe that life begins at conception.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s why — those are the reasons why I’m pro-life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now I understand this is a difficult issue, and I respect people who don’t agree with me on this, but the policy of a Romney administration will be to oppose abortions with the exceptions for rape, incest and life of the mother.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What troubles me more is how this administration has handled all of these issues.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at what they’re doing through Obamacare with respect to assaulting the religious liberties of this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’re infringing upon our first freedom, the freedom of religion, by infringing on Catholic charities, Catholic churches, Catholic hospitals.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Our church should not have to sue our federal government to maintain their religious liberties.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And with respect to abortion, the Democratic Party used to say they wanted it to be safe, legal and rare.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now they support it without restriction and with taxpayer funding.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Taxpayer funding in Obamacare, taxpayer funding with foreign aid.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The vice president himself went to China and said that he sympathized and wouldn’t second guess their one child policy of forced abortions and sterilizations.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That to me is pretty extreme.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My religion defines who I am, and I’ve been a practicing Catholic my whole life.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And has particularly informed my social doctrine.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The Catholic social doctrine talks about taking care of those who — who can’t take care of themselves, people who need help.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With regard to — with regard to abortion, I accept my church’s position on abortion as a — what we call a (inaudible) doctrine.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Life begins at conception in the church’s judgment.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I accept it in my personal life.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But I refuse to impose it on equally devout Christians and Muslims and Jews, and I just refuse to impose that on others, unlike my friend here, the — the congressman.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — I do not believe that we have a right to tell other people that — women they can’t control their body.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s a decision between them and their doctor.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: In my view and the Supreme Court, I’m not going to interfere with that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: With regard to the assault on the Catholic church, let me make it absolutely clear, no religious institution, Catholic or otherwise, including Catholic Social Services, Georgetown Hospital, Mercy Hospital, any hospital, none has to either refer contraception, none has to pay for contraception, none has to be a vehicle to get contraception in any insurance policy they provide.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is a fact.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That is a fact.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Now with regard to the way in which the — we differ, my friend says that he — well I guess he accepts Governor Romney’s position now, because in the past he has argued that there was — there’s rape and forcible rape.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s argued that in the case of rape or incest, it was still — it would be a crime to engage in having an abortion.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I just fundamentally disagree with my friend.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: All I’m saying is, if you believe that life begins at conception, that, therefore, doesn’t change the definition of life.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s a principle.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The policy of a Romney administration is to oppose abortion with exceptions for rape, incest and life of the mother.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Now, I’ve got to take issue with the Catholic church and religious liberty.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: You have on the issue… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … why would they keep — why would they keep suing you?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s a distinction without a difference.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We don’t think that unelected judges should make this decision; that people through their elected representatives in reaching a consensus in society through the democratic process should make this determination.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The court — the next president will get one or two Supreme Court nominees.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s how close Roe v.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Just ask yourself, with Robert Bork being the chief adviser on the court for — for Mr.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Romney, who do you think he’s likely to appoint?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Do you think he’s likely to appoint someone like Scalia or someone else on the court far right that would outlaw (inaudible) — outlaw abortion?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I suspect that would happen.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I guarantee you, that will not happen.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We picked two people.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We pick people who are open-minded.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’ve been good justices.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: So keep an eye on the Supreme Court…

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Was there a litmus test on them?

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: There was no litmus test.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We picked people who had an open mind; did not come with an agenda.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would say to him the same thing I say to my son who did serve a year in Iraq, that we only have one truly sacred obligation as a government.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s to equip those we send into harm’s way and care for those who come home.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s the only sacred obligation we have.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Everything else falls behind that.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would also tell him that the fact that he, this decorated soldier you talked about, fought for his country, that that should be honored.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He should not be thrown into a category of a 47 percent who don’t pay their taxes while he was out there fighting and not having to pay taxes, and somehow not taking responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I would also tell him that there are things that have occurred in this campaign and occur in every campaign that I’m sure both of us regret anyone having said, particularly in these — these special new groups that can go out there, raise all the money they want, not have to identify themselves, who say the most scurrilous things about the other candidate.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s — it’s an abomination.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But the bottom line here is I’d ask that hero you referenced to take a look at whether or not Governor Romney or President Obama has the conviction to help lift up the middle class, restore them to where they were before this great recession hit and they got wiped out.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Or whether or not he’s going to continue to focus on taking care of only the very wealthy, not asking them to make — pay any part of the deal to bring — bring back the middle class and the economy of this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I’d ask him to take a look at whether the president of the United States has acted wisely in the use of force and whether or not the slipshod comments being made by my — my — or by Governor Romney serve — serve our interests very well.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: But there are things that have been said in campaigns that I — I find not very appealing.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: First of all, I’d thank him to his service to our country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Second of all, I’d say we are not going to impose these devastating cuts on our military which compromises their mission and their safety.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And then I would say, you have a president who ran for president four years ago promising hope and change, who has now turned his campaign into attack, blame and defame.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You see, if you don’t have a good record to run on, then you paint your opponent as someone to run from.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That was what President Obama said in 2008.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s what he’s doing right now.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look at all the string of broken promises.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: If you like your health care plan, you can keep it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Try telling that to the 20 million people who are projected to lose their health insurance if Obamacare goes through or the 7-point million — 7.4 million seniors who are going to lose it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said this: I guarantee if you make less than $250,000, your taxes won’t go up.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Of the 21 tax increases in Obamacare, 12 of them hit the middle class.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said health insurance premiums will go down $2,500 per family, per year?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: They’ve gone up $3,000, and they’re expected to go up another $2,400.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Or remember when he said, “I promise by the end of my first term I’ll cut the deficit in half in four years”?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve had four budgets, four trillion-dollar deficits.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: A debt crisis is coming.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can’t keep spending and borrowing like this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can’t keep spending money we don’t have.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Leaders run to problem to fix problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: President Obama has not even put a credible plan on the table in any of his four years to deal with this debt crisis.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I passed two budgets to deal with this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney’s put ideas on the table.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’ve got to tackle this debt crisis before it tackles us.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The president likes to say he has a plan.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He gave a speech.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We asked his budget office, “Can we see the plan?” They sent us to the press secretary.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He gave us a copy of the speech.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We asked the Congressional Budget Office, “Tell us what President Obama’s plan is to prevent a debt crisis.” They said, “It’s a speech, we can’t estimate speeches.” You see, that’s what we get in this administration — speeches — but we’re not getting leadership.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney is uniquely qualified to fix these problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: His lifetime of experience, his proven track record of bipartisanship.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And what do we have from the president?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He broke his big promise to bring people together to solve the country’s biggest problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And what I would tell him is we don’t have to settle for this.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We can do better than this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I hope I’ll get equal time.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The two budgets the congressman introduced have eviscerated all the things that the middle class cares about.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It is (inaudible) he will knock 19 million people off of Medicare.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It will kick 200,000 children off of early education.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It will eliminate the tax credit people have to be able to send their children to college.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It cuts education by $450 billion.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It does — it does virtually nothing except continue to increase the tax cuts for the very wealthy.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And, you know, we’ve had enough of this.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The idea that he’s so concerned about these deficits, I’ve pointed out he voted to put two wars on a credit card.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not raising taxes is not cutting taxes.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And by the way, our budget…

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We have not raised… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: RYAN
sentence: … by 3 percent a year instead of 4.5 percent like they propose.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Not spending more money as much as they say is not a spending cut.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Honesty, no one else could?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: There are plenty of fine people who could lead this country.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: But what you need are people who, when they say they’re going to do something, they go do it.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What you need are, when people see problems, they offer solutions to fix those problems.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re not getting that.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Look, we can grow this economy faster.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That’s what our five- point plan for a stronger middle class is all about.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s about getting 12 million jobs, higher take-home pay, getting people out of poverty into the middle class.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: That means going with proven, pro- growth policies that we know works to get people back to work.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Putting ideas on the table, working with Democrats — that actually works sometimes — and then… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, he gets 40, I get 15, that’s OK.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: That’s all right.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Let me tell you.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I — my — my record stands for itself.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: I never say anything I don’t mean.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Everybody knows, whatever I say, I do.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And my whole life has been devoted to leveling the playing field for middle-class people, giving them an even break, treating Main Street and Wall Street the same, hold them to the same responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Look at my record.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s been all about the middle class.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’re the people who grow this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We think you grow this country from the middle out, not from the top down.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Well, let — let me say at the outset that I want to thank you, Martha, for doing this, and Centre College.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: The fact is that we’re in a situation where we inherited a god-awful circumstance.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: People are in real trouble.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: We acted to move to bring relief to the people who need the most help now.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And — and in the process, we — in case you haven’t noticed, we have strong disagreements, but I — you probably detected my frustration with their attitude about the American people.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: My friend says that 30 percent of the American people are takers.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Romney points out 47 percent of the people won’t take responsibility.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s talking about my mother and father.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: He’s talking about the places I grew up in, my neighbors in Scranton and Claymont, and he’s talking about — he’s talking about the people that have built this country.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: All they’re looking for, Martha, all they’re looking for is an even shot.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Whenever you give them the shot, they’ve done it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: They’ve done it.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: Whenever you’ve leveled the playing field, they’ve been able to move.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And they want a little bit of peace of mind.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: And the president and I are not going to rest until that playing field is leveled, they, in fact, have a clear shot, and they have peace of mind, until they can turn to their kid and say with a degree of confidence, “Honey, it’s going to be OK.

speaker: BIDEN
sentence: It’s going to be OK.” That’s what this is all about.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: I want to thank you, as well, Martha, Danville, Kentucky, Centre College, and I want to thank you, Joe.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s been an honor to engage in this critical debate.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We face a very big choice.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What kind of country are we going to be?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: What kind of country are we going to give our kids?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: President Obama, he had his chance.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: He made his choices.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: His economic agenda, more spending, more borrowing, higher taxes, a government takeover of health care.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s not working.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: It’s failed to create the jobs we need.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Twenty-three million Americans are struggling for work today.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Fifteen percent of Americans are in poverty.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: This is not what a real recovery looks like.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: You deserve better.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney and I want to earn your support.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We’re offering real reforms for a real recovery for every American.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney — his experience, his ideas, his solutions — is uniquely qualified to get this job done.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: At a time when we have a jobs crisis in America, wouldn’t it be nice to have a job-creator in the White House?

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The choice is clear: a stagnant economy that promotes more government dependency or a dynamic, growing economy that promotes opportunity and jobs.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: Mitt Romney and I will not duck the tough issues, and we will not blame others for the next four years.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We will take responsibility.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we will not try to replace our founding principles.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: We will reapply our founding principles.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: The choice is clear, and the choice rests with you.

speaker: RYAN
sentence: And we ask you for your vote.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Jeremy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate your — your question, and thank you for being here this evening and to all of those from Nassau County that have come, thank you for your time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you to Hofstra University and to Candy Crowley for organizing and leading this — this event.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, also for being part of this — this debate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Your question — your question is one that’s being asked by college kids all over this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in Pennsylvania with someone who had just graduated — this was in Philadelphia — and she said, “I’ve got my degree.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can’t find a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve got three part- time jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re just barely enough to pay for my food and pay for an apartment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can’t begin to pay back my student loans.” So what we have to do is two things.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to make sure that we make it easier for kids to afford college.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And also make sure that when they get out of college, there’s a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When I was governor of Massachusetts, to get a high school degree, you had to pass an exam.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you graduated in the top quarter of your airlines, we gave you a John and Abigail Adams scholarship, four years tuition free in the college of your choice in Massachusetts, it’s a public institution.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure we keep our Pell grant program growing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re also going to have our loan program, so that people are able to afford school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the key thing is to make sure you can get a job when you get out of school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what’s happened over the last four years has been very, very hard for America’s young people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want you to be able to get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to get this economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With half of college kids graduating this year without a college — excuse me, without a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And without a college level job, that’s just unacceptable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And likewise you’ve got more and more debt on your back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So more debt and less jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to change that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to create good jobs again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to make sure that you have the kind of opportunity you deserve.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And kids across this country are going to recognize, we’re bringing back an economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not going to be like the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The middle-class has been crushed over the last four years, and jobs have been too scarce.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to bring them back, and I’m going to do that, and make sure that when you graduate — when do you graduate?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you come out in 2014, I presume I’m going to be president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure you get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thanks Jeremy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Yeah, you bet.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Jeremy, first of all, your future is bright.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact that you’re making an investment in higher education is critical.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not just to you, but to the entire nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the most important thing we can do is to make sure that we are creating jobs in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But not just jobs, good paying jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ones that can support a family.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I want to do, is build on the five million jobs that we’ve created over the last 30 months in the private sector alone.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And there are a bunch of things we can do to make sure your future is bright.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, I want to build manufacturing jobs in this country again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now when Governor Romney said we should let Detroit go bankrupt.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said we’re going to bet on American workers and the American auto industry and it’s come surging back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to do that in industries, not just in Detroit, but all across the country and that means we change our tax code so we’re giving incentives to companies that are investing here in the United States and creating jobs here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It also means we’re helping them and small businesses to export all around the world to new markets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, we’ve got to make sure that we have the best education system in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact that you’re going to college is great, but I want everybody to get a great education and we’ve worked hard to make sure that student loans are available for folks like you, but I also want to make sure that community colleges are offering slots for workers to get retrained for the jobs that are out there right now and the jobs of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number three, we’ve got to control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, not only oil and natural gas, which we’ve been investing in; but also, we’ve got to make sure we’re building the energy source of the future, not just thinking about next year, but ten years from now, 20 years from now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we’ve invested in solar and wind and biofuels, energy efficient cars.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to reduce our deficit, but we’ve got to do it in a balanced way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Asking the wealthy to pay a little bit more along with cuts so that we can invest in education like yours.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And let’s take the money that we’ve been spending on war over the last decade to rebuild America, roads, bridges schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We do those things, not only is your future going to be bright but America’s future is going to bright as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well what you’re seeing in this country is 23 million people struggling to find a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And a lot of them, as you say, Candy, have been out of work for a long, long, long time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s policies have been exercised over the last four years and they haven’t put Americans back to work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have fewer people working today than we had when the president took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the — the unemployment rate was 7.8 percent when he took office, it’s 7.8 percent now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But if you calculated that unemployment rate, taking back the people who dropped out of the workforce, it would be 10.7 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have not made the progress we need to make to put people back to work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why I put out a five-point plan that gets America 12 million new jobs in four years and rising take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help Jeremy get a job when he comes out of school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help people across the country that are unemployed right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And one thing that the president said, which I want to make sure that we understand, he said that I said we should take Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan was to have the company go through bankruptcy like 7-Eleven did and Macy’s and Condell (ph) Airlines and come out stronger.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I know he keeps saying, you want to take Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, the president took Detroit bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You took General Motors bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You took Chrysler bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So when you say that I wanted to take the auto industry bankrupt, you actually did.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think it’s important to know that that was a process that was necessary to get those companies back on their feet, so they could start hiring more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was precisely what I recommended and ultimately what happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, what Governor Romney said just isn’t true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He wanted to take them into bankruptcy without providing them any way to stay open.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we would have lost a million jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that — don’t take my word for it, take the executives at GM and Chrysler, some of whom are Republicans, may even support Governor Romney.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But they’ll tell you his prescription wasn’t going to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney’s says he’s got a five-point plan?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney doesn’t have a five-point plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He has a one-point plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that plan is to make sure that folks at the top play by a different set of rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s been his philosophy in the private sector, that’s been his philosophy as governor, that’s been his philosophy as a presidential candidate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can make a lot of money and pay lower tax rates than somebody who makes a lot less.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can ship jobs overseas and get tax breaks for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can invest in a company, bankrupt it, lay off the workers, strip away their pensions, and you still make money.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly the philosophy that we’ve seen in place for the last decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what’s been squeezing middle class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have fought back for four years to get out of that mess.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The last thing we need to do is to go back to the very same policies that got us there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That — that Detroit — that Detroit answer…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … that Detroit answer…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … and the rest of the answer, way off the mark.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The most important thing we can do is to make sure we control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what I’ve done since I’ve been president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have increased oil production to the highest levels in 16 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Natural gas production is the highest it’s been in decades.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have seen increases in coal production and coal employment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is we can’t just produce traditional source of energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve also got to look to the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we doubled fuel efficiency standards on cars.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That means that in the middle of the next decade, any car you buy, you’re going to end up going twice as far on a gallon of gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we doubled clean — clean energy production like wind and solar and biofuels.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And all these things have contributed to us lowering our oil imports to the lowest levels in 16 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I want to build on that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means, yes, we still continue to open up new areas for drilling.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We continue to make it a priority for us to go after natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got potentially 600,000 jobs and 100 years worth of energy right beneath our feet with natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we can do it in an environmentally sound way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’ve also got to continue to figure out how we have efficiency energy, because ultimately that’s how we’re going to reduce demand and that’s what’s going to keep gas prices lower.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney will say he’s got an all-of-the-above plan, but basically his plan is to let the oil companies write the energy policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So he’s got the oil and gas part, but he doesn’t have the clean energy part.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we are only thinking about tomorrow or the next day and not thinking about 10 years from now, we’re not going to control our own economic future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because China, Germany, they’re making these investments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m not going to cede those jobs of the future to those countries.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I expect those new energy sources to be built right here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s going to help Jeremy get a job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s also going to make sure that you’re not paying as much for gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s look at the president’s policies, all right, as opposed to the rhetoric, because we’ve had four years of policies being played out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the president’s right in terms of the additional oil production, but none of it came on federal land.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, oil production is down 14 percent this year on federal land, and gas production was down 9 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because the president cut in half the number of licenses and permits for drilling on federal lands, and in federal waters.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So where’d the increase come from?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well a lot of it came from the Bakken Range in North Dakota.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What was his participation there?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The administration brought a criminal action against the people drilling up there for oil, this massive new resource we have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what was the cost?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: 20 or 25 birds were killed and brought out a migratory bird act to go after them on a criminal basis.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I want to make sure we use our oil, our coal, our gas, our nuclear, our renewables.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe very much in our renewable capabilities; ethanol, wind, solar will be an important part of our energy mix.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But what we don’t need is to have the president keeping us from taking advantage of oil, coal and gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This has not been Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Talk to the people that are working in those industries.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in coal country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People grabbed my arms and said, “Please save my job.” The head of the EPA said, “You can’t build a coal plant.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll virtually — it’s virtually impossible given our regulations.” When the president ran for office, he said if you build a coal plant, you can go ahead, but you’ll go bankrupt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right course for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s take advantage of the energy resources we have, as well as the energy sources for the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we do that, if we do what I’m planning on doing, which is getting us energy independent, North America energy independence within eight years, you’re going to see manufacturing jobs come back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because our energy is low cost, that are already beginning to come back because of our abundant energy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get America and North America energy independent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll do it by more drilling, more permits and licenses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to bring that pipeline in from Canada.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How in the world the president said no to that pipeline?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will never know.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is about bringing good jobs back for the middle class of America, and that’s what I’m going to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, there’s no doubt that world demand’s gone up, but our production is going up, and we’re using oil more efficiently.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And very little of what Governor Romney just said is true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve opened up public lands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re actually drilling more on public lands than in the previous administration and my — the previous president was an oil man.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And natural gas isn’t just appearing magically.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re encouraging it and working with the industry.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when I hear Governor Romney say he’s a big coal guy, I mean, keep in mind, when — Governor, when you were governor of Massachusetts, you stood in front of a coal plant and pointed at it and said, “This plant kills,” and took great pride in shutting it down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now suddenly you’re a big champion of coal.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I’ve tried to do is be consistent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: With respect to something like coal, we made the largest investment in clean coal technology, to make sure that even as we’re producing more coal, we’re producing it cleaner and smarter.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Same thing with oil, same thing with natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the proof is our oil imports are down to the lowest levels in 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Oil production is up, natural gas production is up, and, most importantly, we’re also starting to build cars that are more efficient.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s creating jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That means those cars can be exported, ’cause that’s the demand around the world, and it also means that it’ll save money in your pocketbook.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the strategy you need, an all-of-the-above strategy, and that’s what we’re going to do in the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But that’s not what you’ve done in the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the problem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the last four years, you cut permits and licenses on federal land and federal waters in half.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not true, Governor Romney.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So how much did you cut (inaudible)?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut them by, then?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, we have actually produced more oil —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut licenses and permits on federal land and federal waters?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, here’s what we did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There were a whole bunch of oil companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, no, I had a question and the question was how much did you cut them by?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You want me to answer a question —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How much did you cut them by?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m happy to answer the question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Here’s what happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You had a whole bunch of oil companies who had leases on public lands that they weren’t using.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what we said was you can’t just sit on this for 10, 20, 30 years, decide when you want to drill, when you want to produce, when it’s most profitable for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are public lands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if you want to drill on public lands, you use it or you lose it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: OK, (inaudible) —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what we did was take away those leases.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are now reletting them so that we can actually make a profit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And production on private — on government land —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Production is up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, it isn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Production on government land of oil is down 14 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And production on gas — (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s just not true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, there’s no question but the people recognize that we have not produced more (inaudible) on federal lands and in federal waters.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And coal, coal production is not up; coal jobs are not up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was just at a coal facility, where some 1,200 people lost their jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The right course for America is to have a true all-of-the-above policy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think anyone really believes that you’re a person who’s going to be pushing for oil and gas and coal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ll get your chance in a moment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m still speaking.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer is I don’t believe people think that’s the case —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: — (inaudible).

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That wasn’t the question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was a statement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think the American people believe that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will fight for oil, coal and natural gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the proof, the proof of whether a strategy is working or not is what the price is that you’re paying at the pump.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you’re paying less than you paid a year or two ago, why, then, the strategy is working.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you’re paying more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When the president took office, the price of gasoline here in Nassau County was about $1.86 a gallon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, it’s $4.00 a gallon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The price of electricity is up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the president’s energy policies are working, you’re going to see the cost of energy come down.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will fight to create more energy in this country, to get America energy secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And part of that is bringing in a pipeline of oil from Canada, taking advantage of the oil and coal we have here, drilling offshore in Alaska, drilling offshore in Virginia where the people want it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those things will get us the energy we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, think about what the governor — think about what the governor just said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said when I took office, the price of gasoline was $1.80, $1.86.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Why is that?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because the economy was on the verge of collapse, because we were about to go through the worst recession since the Great Depression, as a consequence of some of the same policies that Governor Romney’s now promoting.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, it’s conceivable that Governor Romney could bring down gas prices because with his policies, we might be back in that same mess.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I want to do is to create an economy that is strong, and at the same time produce energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And with respect to this pipeline that Governor Romney keeps on talking about, we’ve — we’ve built enough pipeline to wrap around the entire earth once.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, I’m all for pipelines.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m all for oil production.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I’m not for is us ignoring the other half of the equation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, for example, on wind energy, when Governor Romney says “these are imaginary jobs.” When you’ve got thousands of people right now in Iowa, right now in Colorado, who are working, creating wind power with good-paying manufacturing jobs, and the Republican senator in that — in Iowa is all for it, providing tax breaks (ph) to help this work and Governor Romney says I’m opposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’d get rid of it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not an energy strategy for the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we need to win that future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I intend to win it as President of the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He gets the first —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He actually got —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He actually got the first question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I get the last question — last answer —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Candy, Candy —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Candy, I don’t have a policy of stopping wind jobs in Iowa and that — they’re not phantom jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re real jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate wind jobs in Iowa and across our country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I appreciate the jobs in coal and oil and gas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: — we’re taking advantage of our energy resources.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll bring back manufacturing to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to get through a very aggressive energy policy, 31/2 million more jobs in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s critical to our future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, it’s not going to —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Used to being interrupted.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re doing great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you very much.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And let me tell you, you’re absolutely right about part of that, which is I want to bring the rates down, I want to simplify the tax code, and I want to get middle- income taxpayers to have lower taxes.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the reason I want middle-income taxpayers to have lower taxes is because middle-income taxpayers have been buried over the past four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’ve seen, as middle-income people in this country, incomes go down $4,300 a family, even as gasoline prices have gone up $2,000.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Health insurance premiums, up $2,500.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Food prices up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Utility prices up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The middle-income families in America have been crushed over the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I want to get some relief to middle-income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s part — that’s part one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, how about deductions?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: ‘Cause I’m going to bring rates down across the board for everybody, but I’m going to limit deductions and exemptions and credits, particularly for people at the high end, because I am not going to have people at the high end pay less than they’re paying now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The top 5 percent of taxpayers will continue to pay 60 percent of the income tax the nation collects.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So that’ll stay the same.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Middle-income people are going to get a tax break.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so, in terms of bringing down deductions, one way of doing that would be say everybody gets — I’ll pick a number — $25,000 of deductions and credits, and you can decide which ones to use.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Your home mortgage interest deduction, charity, child tax credit, and so forth, you can use those as part of filling that bucket, if you will, of deductions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But your rate comes down and the burden also comes down on you for one more reason, and that is every middle-income taxpayer no longer will pay any tax on interest, dividends or capital gains.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No tax on your savings.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That makes life a lot easier.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you’re getting interest from a bank, if you’re getting a statement from a mutual fund or any other kind of investment you have, you don’t have to worry about filing taxes on that, because there’ll be no taxes for anybody making $200,000.00 per year and less, on your interest, dividends and capital gains.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Why am I lowering taxes on the middle-class?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because under the last four years, they’ve been buried.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I want to help people in the middle-class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not — I will not under any circumstances, reduce the share that’s being paid by the highest income taxpayers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not, under any circumstances increase taxes on the middle-class.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s spending, the president’s borrowing will cost this nation to have to raise taxes on the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not just at the high end.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A recent study has shown the people in the middle-class will see $4,000.00 per year in higher taxes as a result of the spending and borrowing of this administration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not let that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get us on track to a balanced budget, and I’m going to reduce the tax burden on middle income families.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what’s that going to do?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s going to help those families, and it’s going to create incentives to start growing jobs again in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My philosophy on taxes has been simple.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is, I want to give middle-class families and folks who are striving to get into the middle-class some relief.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because they have been hit hard over the last decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last 15, over the last 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So four years ago I stood on a stage just like this one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Actually it was a town hall, and I said I would cut taxes for middle- class families, and that’s what I’ve done, by $3,600.00.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said I would cut taxes for small businesses, who are the drivers and engines of growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve cut them 18 times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to continue those tax cuts for middle-class families, and for small business.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is, if we’re serious about reducing the deficit, if this is genuinely a moral obligation to the next generation, then in addition to some tough spending cuts, we’ve also got to make sure that the wealthy do a little bit more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I’ve said is, your first $250,000.00 worth of income, no change.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means 98 percent of American families, 97 percent of small businesses, they will not see a tax increase.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m ready to sign that bill right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The only reason it’s not happening is because Governor Romney’s allies in Congress have held the 98 percent hostage because they want tax breaks for the top 2 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also says is for above $250,000, we can go back to the tax rates we had when Bill Clinton was president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created 23 million new jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of what took us from deficits to surplus.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It will be good for our economy and it will be good for job creation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has a different philosophy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He was on 60 Minutes just two weeks ago and he was asked: Is it fair for somebody like you, making $20 million a year, to pay a lower tax rate than a nurse or a bus driver, somebody making $50,000 year?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he said, “Yes, I think that’s fair.” Not only that, he said, “I think that’s what grows the economy.” Well, I fundamentally disagree with that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think what grows the economy is when you get that tax credit that we put in place for your kids going to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that grows the economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think what grows the economy is when we make sure small businesses are getting a tax credit for hiring veterans who fought for our country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That grows our economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we just have a different theory.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when Governor Romney stands here, after a year of campaigning, when during a Republican primary he stood on stage and said “I’m going to give tax cuts” — he didn’t say tax rate cuts, he said “tax cuts to everybody,” including the top 1 percent, you should believe him because that’s been his history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly the kind of top-down economics that is not going to work if we want a strong middle class and an economy that’s striving for everybody.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re absolutely right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You heard what I said about my tax plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The top 5 percent will continue to pay 60 percent, as they do today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not looking to cut taxes for wealthy people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I am looking to cut taxes for middle-income people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And why do I want to bring rates down, and at the same time lower exemptions and deductions, particularly for people at the high end?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because if you bring rates down, it makes it easier for small business to keep more of their capital and hire people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And for me, this is about jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to get America’s economy going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fifty-four percent of America’s workers work in businesses that are taxed as individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So when you bring those rates down, those small businesses are able to keep more money and hire more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: For me, I look at what’s happened in the last four years and say this has been a disappointment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can do better than this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for, how many months, 43 months with unemployment above 8 percent, 23 million Americans struggling to find a good job right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are 3.5 million more women living in poverty today than when the president took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to live like this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can get this economy going again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My five-point plan does it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Energy independence for North America in five years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Opening up more trade, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Cracking down on China when they cheat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Getting us to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fixing our training programs for our workers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, championing small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make small businesses grow and thrive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know how to make that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I spent my life in the private sector.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know why jobs come and why they go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they’re going now because of the policies of this administration.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, it’s not settled.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, the cost of lowering rates for everybody across the board, 20 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Along with what he also wants to do in terms of eliminating the estate tax, along what he wants to do in terms of corporates, changes in the tax code, it costs about $5 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney then also wants to spend $2 trillion on additional military programs even though the military’s not asking for them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s $7 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He also wants to continue the Bush tax cuts for the wealthiest Americans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s another trillion dollars — that’s $8 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, what he says is he’s going to make sure that this doesn’t add to the deficit and he’s going to cut middleclass taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when he’s asked, how are you going to do it, which deductions, which loopholes are you going to close?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He can’t tell you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — the fact that he only has to pay 14 percent on his taxes when a lot of you are paying much higher.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He’s already taken that off the board, capital gains are going to continue to be at a low rate so we — we’re not going to get money that way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We haven’t heard from the governor any specifics beyond Big Bird and eliminating funding for Planned Parenthood in terms of how he pays for that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney was a very successful investor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If somebody came to you, Governor, with a plan that said, here, I want to spend $7 or $8 trillion, and then we’re going to pay for it, but we can’t tell you until maybe after the election how we’re going to do it, you wouldn’t take such a sketchy deal and neither should you, the American people, because the math doesn’t add up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and what’s at stake here is one of two things, either Candy — this blows up the deficit because keep in mind, this is just to pay for the additional spending that he’s talking about, $7 trillion – $8 trillion before we even get to the deficit we already have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Or, alternatively, it’s got to be paid for, not only by closing deductions for wealthy individuals, that — that will pay for about 4 percent reduction in tax rates.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re going to be paying for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re going to lose some deductions, and you can’t buy the sales pitch.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nobody who’s looked at it that’s serious, actually believes it adds up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well of course they add up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I was — I was someone who ran businesses for 25 years, and balanced the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I ran the Olympics and balanced the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I ran the — the state of Massachusetts as a governor, to the extent any governor does, and balanced the budget all four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When we’re talking about math that doesn’t add up, how about $4 trillion of deficits over the last four years, $5 trillion?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s math that doesn’t add up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have — we have a president talking about someone’s plan in a way that’s completely foreign to what my real plan is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then we have his own record, which is we have four consecutive years where he said when he was running for office, he would cut the deficit in half.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Instead he’s doubled it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve gone from $10 trillion of national debt, to $16 trillion of national debt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If the president were reelected, we’d go to almost $20 trillion of national debt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This puts us on a road to Greece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to balance budgets.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve done it my entire life.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So for instance when he says, “Yours is a $5 trillion cut.” Well, no it’s not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because I’m offsetting some of the reductions with holding down some of the deductions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I just described — I just described to you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President — I just described to you precisely how I’d do it which is with a single number that people can put — and they can put they’re — they’re deductions and credits… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Looking forward to it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s Katherine Fenton, who has a question for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Katherine, that’s a great question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, I was raised by a single mom who had to put herself through school while looking after two kids.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she worked hard every day and made a lot of sacrifices to make sure we got everything we needed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My grandmother, she started off as a secretary in a bank.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: She never got a college education, even though she was smart as a whip.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she worked her way up to become a vice president of a local bank, but she hit the glass ceiling.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: She trained people who would end up becoming her bosses during the course of her career.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: She didn’t complain.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not what you did in that generation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is one of the reasons why one of the first — the first bill I signed was something called the Lily Ledbetter bill.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s named after this amazing woman who had been doing the same job as a man for years, found out that she was getting paid less, and the Supreme Court said that she couldn’t bring suit because she should have found about it earlier, whereas she had no way of finding out about it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we fixed that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s an example of the kind of advocacy that we need, because women are increasingly the breadwinners in the family.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is not just a women’s issue, this is a family issue, this is a middle-class issue, and that’s why we’ve got to fight for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It also means that we’ve got to make sure that young people like yourself are able to afford a college education.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Earlier, Governor Romney talked about he wants to make Pell Grants and other education accessible for young people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, the truth of the matter is, is that that’s exactly what we’ve done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve expanded Pell Grants for millions of people, including millions of young women, all across the country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We did it by taking $60 billion that was going to banks and lenders as middlemen for the student loan program, and we said, let’s just cut out the middleman.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s give the money directly to students.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, we’ve seen millions of young people be able to afford college, and that’s going to make sure that young women are going to be able to compete in that marketplace.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’ve got to enforce the laws, which is what we are doing, and we’ve also got to make sure that in every walk of life we do not tolerate discrimination.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s been one of the hallmarks of my administration.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m going to continue to push on this issue for the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And important topic, and one which I learned a great deal about, particularly as I was serving as governor of my state, because I had the chance to pull together a cabinet and all the applicants seemed to be men.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — and I went to my staff, and I said, “How come all the people for these jobs are — are all men.” They said, “Well, these are the people that have the qualifications.” And I said, “Well, gosh, can’t we — can’t we find some — some women that are also qualified?”

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and so we — we took a concerted effort to go out and find women who had backgrounds that could be qualified to become members of our cabinet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I went to a number of women’s groups and said, “Can you help us find folks,” and they brought us whole binders full of women.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was proud of the fact that after I staffed my Cabinet and my senior staff, that the University of New York in Albany did a survey of all 50 states, and concluded that mine had more women in senior leadership positions than any other state in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now one of the reasons I was able to get so many good women to be part of that team was because of our recruiting effort.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But number two, because I recognized that if you’re going to have women in the workforce that sometimes you need to be more flexible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My chief of staff, for instance, had two kids that were still in school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: She said, I can’t be here until 7 or 8 o’clock at night.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I need to be able to get home at 5 o’clock so I can be there for making dinner for my kids and being with them when they get home from school.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So we said fine.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s have a flexible schedule so you can have hours that work for you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to have to have employers in the new economy, in the economy I’m going to bring to play, that are going to be so anxious to get good workers they’re going to be anxious to hire women.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the — in the last women have lost 580,000 jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the net of what’s happened in the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re still down 580,000 jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mentioned 31/2 million women, more now in poverty than four years ago.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What we can do to help young women and women of all ages is to have a strong economy, so strong that employers that are looking to find good employees and bringing them into their workforce and adapting to a flexible work schedule that gives women opportunities that they would otherwise not be able to afford.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is what I have done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s what I look forward to doing and I know what it takes to make an economy work, and I know what a working economy looks like.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And an economy with 7.8 percent unemployment is not a real strong economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: An economy that has 23 million people looking for work is not a strong economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: An economy with 50 percent of kids graduating from college that can’t finds a job, or a college level job, that’s not what we have to have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to help women in America get good work by getting a stronger economy and by supporting women in the workforce.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Katherine, I just want to point out that when Governor Romney’s campaign was asked about the Lilly Ledbetter bill, whether he supported it?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said, “I’ll get back to you.” And that’s not the kind of advocacy that women need in any economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, there are some other issues that have a bearing on how women succeed in the workplace.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For example, their healthcare.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know a major difference in this campaign is that Governor Romney feels comfortable having politicians in Washington decide the health care choices that women are making.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that’s a mistake.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In my health care bill, I said insurance companies need to provide contraceptive coverage to everybody who is insured.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because this is not just a — a health issue, it’s an economic issue for women.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It makes a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is money out of that family’s pocket.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney not only opposed it, he suggested that in fact employers should be able to make the decision as to whether or not a woman gets contraception through her insurance coverage.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not the kind of advocacy that women need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When Governor Romney says that we should eliminate funding for Planned Parenthood, there are millions of women all across the country, who rely on Planned Parenthood for, not just contraceptive care, they rely on it for mammograms, for cervical cancer screenings.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s a pocketbook issue for women and families all across the country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it makes a difference in terms of how well and effectively women are able to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When we talk about child care, and the credits that we’re providing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That makes a difference in whether they can go out there and — and earn a living for their family.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are not just women’s issues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are family issues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are economic issues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one of the things that makes us grow as an economy is when everybody participates and women are getting the same fair deal as men are.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve got two daughters and I want to make sure that they have the same opportunities that anybody’s sons have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of what I’m fighting for as president of the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I appreciate that question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I just want to make sure that, I think I was supposed to get that last answer, but I want to point out that that I don’t believe…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I don’t think so, Candy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … I don’t believe…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to make sure our timekeepers are working here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The time — the time…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d just note that I don’t believe that bureaucrats in Washington should tell someone whether they can use contraceptives or not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I don’t believe employers should tell someone whether they could have contraceptive care of not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Every woman in America should have access to contraceptives.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and the — and the president’s statement of my policy is completely and totally wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me come back and — and answer your question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President Bush and I are — are different people and these are different times and that’s why my five point plan is so different than what he would have done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean for instance, we can now, by virtue of new technology actually get all the energy we need in North America without having to go to the — the Arabs or the Venezuelans or anyone else.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That wasn’t true in his time, that’s why my policy starts with a very robust policy to get all that energy in North America — become energy secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, trade — I’ll crack down on China, President Bush didn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m also going to dramatically expand trade in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s been growing about 12 percent per year over a long period of time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to add more free trade agreements so we’ll have more trade.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, I’m going to get us to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President Bush didn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President Obama was right, he said that that was outrageous to have deficits as high as half a trillion dollars under the Bush years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He was right, but then he put in place deficits twice that size for every one of his four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And his forecast for the next four years is more deficits, almost that large.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So that’s the next area I’m different than President Bush.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then let’s take the last one, championing small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our party has been focused too long.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I came through small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand how hard it is to start a small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why everything I’ll do is designed to help small businesses grow and add jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to keep their taxes down on small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want regulators to see their job as encouraging small enterprise, not crushing it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the thing I find the most troubling about Obama Care, well it’s a long list, but one of the things I find most troubling is that when you go out and talk to small businesses and ask them what they think about it, they tell you it keeps them from hiring more people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know how to make that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And President Bush has a very different path for a very different time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My path is designed in getting small businesses to grow and hire people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all, I think it’s important to tell you that we did come in during some tough times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We were losing 800,000 jobs a month when I started.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we had been digging our way out of policies that were misplaced and focused on the top doing very well and middle class folks not doing well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, we’ve seen 30 consecutive — 31 consecutive months of job growth; 5.2 million new jobs created.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the plans that I talked about will create even more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when Governor Romney says that he has a very different economic plan, the centerpiece of his economic plan are tax cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what took us from surplus to deficit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When he talks about getting tough on China, keep in mind that Governor Romney invested in companies that were pioneers of outsourcing to China, and is currently investing in countries — in companies that are building surveillance equipment for China to spy on its own folks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s — Governor, you’re the last person who’s going to get tough on China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we’ve done when it comes to trade is not only sign three trade deals to open up new markets, but we’ve also set up a task force for trade that goes after anybody who is taking advantage of American workers or businesses and not creating a level playing field.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve brought twice as many cases against unfair trading practices than the previous administration and we’ve won every single one that’s been decided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I said that we had to make sure that China was not flooding our domestic market with cheap tires, Governor Romney said I was being protectionist; that it wouldn’t be helpful to American workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, in fact we saved 1,000 jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the kind of tough trade actions that are required.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But the last point I want to make is this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, there are some things where Governor Romney is different from George Bush.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: George Bush didn’t propose turning Medicare into a voucher.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: George Bush embraced comprehensive immigration reform.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He didn’t call for self-deportation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: George Bush never suggested that we eliminate funding for Planned Parenthood, so there are differences between Governor Romney and George Bush, but they’re not on economic policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In some ways, he’s gone to a more extreme place when it comes to social policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that’s a mistake.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not how we’re going to move our economy forward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, we’ve gone through a tough four years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s no doubt about it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But four years ago, I told the American people and I told you I would cut taxes for middle class families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I told you I’d cut taxes for small businesses, and I have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said that I’d end the war in Iraq, and I did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said we’d refocus attention on those who actually attacked us on 9/11, and we have gone after Al Qaeda’s leadership like never before and Osama bin Laden is dead.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said that we would put in place health care reform to make sure that insurance companies can’t jerk you around and if you don’t have health insurance, that you’d have a chance to get affordable insurance, and I have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I committed that I would rein in the excesses of Wall Street, and we passed the toughest Wall Street reforms since the 1930s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve created five million jobs, and gone from 800 jobs a month being lost, and we are making progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We saved an auto industry that was on the brink of collapse.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, does that mean you’re not struggling?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Absolutely not.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: A lot of us are.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why the plan that I’ve put forward for manufacturing and education, and reducing our deficit in a sensible way, using the savings from ending wars, to rebuild America and putting people back to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that we are controlling our own energy, but not only the energy of today, but also the energy of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All of those things will make a difference, so the point is the commitments I’ve made, I’ve kept.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And those that I haven’t been able to keep, it’s not for lack of trying and we’re going to get it done in a second term.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, you should pay attention to this campaign, because Governor Romney has made some commitments as well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I suspect he’ll keep those too.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know when members of the Republican Congress say, “We’re going to sign a no tax pledge, so that we don’t ask a dime for millionaires and billionaires to reduce our deficit so we can still invest in education, and helping kids go to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said, “Me too.” When they said, “We’re going to cut Planned Parenthood funding.” He said, “Me too.” When he said, “We’re going to repeal Obamacare.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First thing I’m going to do,” despite the fact that it’s the same health care plan that he passed in Massachusetts and is working well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said, “Me too.” That is not the kind of leadership that you need, but you should expect that those are promises he’s going to keep.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …the choice in this election is going to be whose promises are going to be more likely to help you in your life?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Make sure your kids can go to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Make sure that you are getting a good paying job, making sure that Medicare and Social Security… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …will be there for you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think you know better.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think you know that these last four years haven’t been so good as the president just described and that you don’t feel like your confident that the next four years are going to be much better either.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can tell you that if you were to elect President Obama, you know what you’re going to get.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re going to get a repeat of the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We just can’t afford four more years like the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said that by now we’d have unemployment at 5.4 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The difference between where it is and 5.4 percent is 9 million Americans without work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I wasn’t the one that said 5.4 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This was the president’s plan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Didn’t get there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said he would have by now put forward a plan to reform Medicare and Social Security, because he pointed out they’re on the road to bankruptcy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He would reform them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’d get that done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He hasn’t even made a proposal on either one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said in his first year he’d put out an immigration plan that would deal with our immigration challenges.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Didn’t even file it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a president who has not been able to do what he said he’d do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said that he’d cut in half the deficit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He hasn’t done that either.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In fact, he doubled it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said that by now middle-income families would have a reduction in their health insurance premiums by $2,500 a year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s gone up by $2,500 a year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if Obamacare is passed, or implemented — it’s already been passed — if it’s implemented fully, it’ll be another $2,500 on top.

speaker: ROMNEY
