sentence: The middle class is getting crushed under the policies of a president who has not understood what it takes to get the economy working again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He keeps saying, “Look, I’ve created 5 million jobs.” That’s after losing 5 million jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The entire record is such that the unemployment has not been reduced in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The unemployment, the number of people who are still looking for work, is still 23 million Americans.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are more people in poverty, one out of six people in poverty.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How about food stamps?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When he took office, 32 million people were on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Today, 47 million people are on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How about the growth of the economy?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s growing more slowly this year than last year, and more slowly last year than the year before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president wants to do well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the policies he’s put in place from Obamacare to Dodd-Frank to his tax policies to his regulatory policies, these policies combined have not let this economy take off and grow like it could have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You might say, “Well, you got an example of one that worked better?” Yeah, in the Reagan recession where unemployment hit 10.8 percent, between that period — the end of that recession and the equivalent of time to today, Ronald Reagan’s recovery created twice as many jobs as this president’s recovery.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Five million jobs doesn’t even keep up with our population growth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the only reason the unemployment rate seems a little lower today is because of all the people that have dropped out of the workforce.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president has tried, but his policies haven’t worked.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He’s great as a — as a — as a speaker and describing his plans and his vision.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s wonderful, except we have a record to look at.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that record shows he just hasn’t been able to cut the deficit, to put in place reforms for Medicare and Social Security to preserve them, to get us the rising incomes we need.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Median income is down $4,300 a family and 23 million Americans out of work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s what this election is about.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s about who can get the middle class in this country a bright and prosperous future and assure our kids the kind of hope and optimism they deserve.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is for Governor Romney?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is it Loraina?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Good, thanks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Did I get that right?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you for your question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And let me step back and tell you what I would like to do with our immigration policy broadly and include an answer to your question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But first of all, this is a nation of immigrants.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We welcome people coming to this country as immigrants.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My dad was born in Mexico of American parents; Ann’s dad was born in Wales and is a first-generation American.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We welcome legal immigrants into this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want our legal system to work better.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want it to be streamlined.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want it to be clearer.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think you have to — shouldn’t have to hire a lawyer to figure out how to get into this country legally.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I also think that we should give visas to people — green cards, rather, to people who graduate with skills that we need.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People around the world with accredited degrees in science and math get a green card stapled to their diploma, come to the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should make sure our legal system works.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, we’re going to have to stop illegal immigration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are 4 million people who are waiting in line to get here legally.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those who’ve come here illegally take their place.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I will not grant amnesty to those who have come here illegally.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I will do is I’ll put in place an employment verification system and make sure that employers that hire people who have come here illegally are sanctioned for doing so.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I won’t put in place magnets for people coming here illegally.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So for instance, I would not give driver’s licenses to those that have come here illegally as the president would.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The kids of those that came here illegally, those kids, I think, should have a pathway to become a permanent resident of the United States and military service, for instance, is one way they would have that kind of pathway to become a permanent resident.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now when the president ran for office, he said that he’d put in place, in his first year, a piece of legislation — he’d file a bill in his first year that would reform our — our immigration system, protect legal immigration, stop illegal immigration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He didn’t do it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He had a Democrat House, a Democrat Senate, super majority in both Houses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Why did he fail to even promote legislation that would have provided an answer for those that want to come legally and for those that are here illegally today?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What’s a question I think the — the president will have a chance to answer right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Good, I look forward to it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Was — Lorranna — Lorraine — we are a nation of immigrants.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean we’re just a few miles away from Ellis Island.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We all understand what this country has become because talent from all around the world wants to come here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People are willing to take risks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People who want to build on their dreams and make sure their kids have an even bigger dreams than they have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’re also a nation of laws.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I’ve said is we need to fix a broken immigration system and I’ve done everything that I can on my own and sought cooperation from Congress to make sure that we fix the system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The first thing we did was to streamline the legal immigration system, to reduce the backlog, make it easier, simpler and cheaper for people who are waiting in line, obeying the law to make sure that they can come here and contribute to our country and that’s good for our economic growth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’ll start new businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’ll make things happen to create jobs here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, we do have to deal with our border so we put more border patrol on the — any time in history and the flow of undocumented works across the border is actually lower than it’s been in 40 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I’ve also said is if we’re going to go after folks who are here illegally, we should do it smartly and go after folks who are criminals, gang bangers, people who are hurting the community, not after students, not after folks who are here just because they’re trying to figure out how to feed their families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what we’ve done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I’ve also said is for young people who come here, brought here often times by their parents.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Had gone to school here, pledged allegiance to the flag.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Think of this as their country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Understand themselves as Americans in every way except having papers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we should make sure that we give them a pathway to citizenship.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what I’ve done administratively.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney just said, you know he wants to help those young people too, but during the Republican primary, he said, “I will veto the DREAM Act”, that would allow these young people to have access.” His main strategy during the Republican primary was to say, “We’re going to encourage self-deportation.” Making life so miserable on folks that they’ll leave.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He called the Arizona law a model for the nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of the Arizona law said that law enforcement officers could stop folks because they suspected maybe they looked like they might be undocumented workers and check their papers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know what?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If my daughter or yours looks to somebody like they’re not a citizen, I don’t want — I don’t want to empower somebody like that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, we can fix this system in a comprehensive way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when Governor Romney says, the challenge is, “Well Obama didn’t try.” That’s not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I have sat down with Democrats and Republicans at the beginning of my term.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I said, let’s fix this system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Including Senators previously who had supported it on the Republican side.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it’s very hard for Republican’s in Congress to support comprehensive immigration reform, if their standard bearer has said that, this is not something I’m interested in supporting.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, let — let — let me go back and speak to the points that the president made and — and — and let’s get them correct.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I did not say that the Arizona law was a model for the nation in that aspect.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I said that the E-Verify portion of the Arizona law, which is — which is the portion of the law which says that employers could be able to determine whether someone is here illegally or not illegally, that that was a model for the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, I asked the president a question I think Hispanics and immigrants all over the nation have asked.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He was asked this on Univision the other day.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Why, when you said you’d filed legislation in your first year didn’t you do it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And he didn’t answer.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He — he doesn’t answer that question.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He said the standard bearer wasn’t for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m glad you thought I was a standard bearer four years ago, but I wasn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Four years ago you said in your first year you would file legislation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In his first year, I was just getting — licking my wounds from having been beaten by John McCain, all right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was not the standard bearer.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My — my view is that this president should have honored his promise to do as he said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, let me mention one other thing, and that is self-deportation says let people make their own choice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I was saying is, we’re not going to round up 12 million people, undocumented illegals, and take them out of the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Instead let people make their own choice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if they — if they find that — that they can’t get the benefits here that they want and they can’t — and they can’t find the job they want, then they’ll make a decision to go a place where — where they have better opportunities.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’m not in favor of rounding up people and — and — and taking them out of this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I am in favor, as the president has said, and I agree with him, which is that if people have committed crimes we got to get them out of this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me mention something else the president said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was a moment ago and I didn’t get a chance to, when he was describing Chinese investments and so forth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Hold on a second.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, I’m still speaking.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, let me finish.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve gotta continue.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Just going to make a point.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Any investments I have over the last eight years have been managed by a blind trust.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I understand they do include investments outside the United States, including in — in Chinese companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, have you looked at your pension?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Have you looked at your pension?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve got to say…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, have you looked at your pension?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, I — I don’t look at my pension.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not as big as yours so it doesn’t take as long.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let me give you some advice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I don’t check it that often.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me give you some advice.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look at your pension.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You also have investments in Chinese companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You also have investments outside the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You also have investments through a Cayman’s trust.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I thought we were talking about immigration.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I do want to make sure that…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I do want to make sure that — I do want to make sure that we just understand something.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney says he wasn’t referring to Arizona as a model for the nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: His top adviser on immigration is the guy who designed the Arizona law, the entirety of it; not E-Verify, the whole thing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s his policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s a bad policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it won’t help us grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, when we think about immigration, we have to understand there are folks all around the world who still see America as the land of promise.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they provide us energy and they provide us innovation and they start companies like Intel and Google.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we want to encourage that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, we’ve got to make sure that we do it in a smart way and a comprehensive way, and we make the legal system better.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when we make this into a divisive political issue, and when we don’t have bipartisan support — I can deliver, Governor, a whole bunch of Democrats to get comprehensive immigration reform done, and we can’t…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get it done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get it done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … we can’t — we have not seen Republicans serious about this issue at all.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s time for them to get serious on it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This used to be a bipartisan issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What’s your name?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Great to see you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, let me first of all talk about our diplomats, because they serve all around the world and do an incredible job in a very dangerous situation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And these aren’t just representatives of the United States, they are my representatives.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I send them there, oftentimes into harm’s way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I know these folks and I know their families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So nobody is more concerned about their safety and security than I am.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So as soon as we found out that the Benghazi consulate was being overrun, I was on the phone with my national security team and I gave them three instructions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, beef up our security and procedures, not just in Libya, but at every embassy and consulate in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, investigate exactly what happened, regardless of where the facts lead us, to make sure folks are held accountable and it doesn’t happen again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And number three, we are going to find out who did this and we’re going to hunt them down, because one of the things that I’ve said throughout my presidency is when folks mess with Americans, we go after them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now Governor Romney had a very different response.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: While we were still dealing with our diplomats being threatened, Governor Romney put out a press release, trying to make political points, and that’s not how a commander in chief operates.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You don’t turn national security into a political issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Certainly not right when it’s happening.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And people — not everybody agrees with some of the decisions I’ve made.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it comes to our national security, I mean what I say.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said I’d end the war in Libya — in — in Iraq, and I did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said that we’d go after al-Qaeda and bin Laden, we have.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I said we’d transition out of Afghanistan, and start making sure that Afghans are responsible for their own security, that’s what I’m doing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to this issue, when I say that we are going to find out exactly what happened, everybody will be held accountable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I am ultimately responsible for what’s taking place there because these are my folks, and I’m the one who has to greet those coffins when they come home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know that I mean what I say.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you Kerry for your question, it’s an important one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and I — I think the president just said correctly that the buck does stop at his desk and — and he takes responsibility for — for that — for the failure in providing those security resources, and — and those terrible things may well happen from time to time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I’m — I feel very deeply sympathetic for the families of those who lost loved ones.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And today there’s a memorial service for one of those that was lost in this tragedy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we think of their families and care for them deeply.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There were other issues associated with this — with this tragedy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There were many days that passed before we knew whether this was a spontaneous demonstration, or actually whether it was a terrorist attack.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there was no demonstration involved.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was a terrorist attack and it took a long time for that to be told to the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Whether there was some misleading, or instead whether we just didn’t know what happened, you have to ask yourself why didn’t we know five days later when the ambassador to the United Nations went on TV to say that this was a demonstration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How could we have not known?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I find more troubling than this, that on — on the day following the assassination of the United States ambassador, the first time that’s happened since 1979, when — when we have four Americans killed there, when apparently we didn’t know what happened, that the president, the day after that happened, flies to Las Vegas for a political fund-raiser, then the next day to Colorado for another event, other political event.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think these — these actions taken by a president and a leader have symbolic significance and perhaps even material significance in that you’d hope that during that time we could call in the people who were actually eyewitnesses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve read their accounts now about what happened.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was very clear this was not a demonstration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This was an attack by terrorists.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And this calls into question the president’s whole policy in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look what’s happening in Syria, in Egypt, now in Libya.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Consider the distance between ourselves and — and Israel, the president said that — that he was going to put daylight between us and Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have Iran four years closer to a nuclear bomb.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Syria — Syria’s not just a tragedy of 30,000 civilians being killed by a military, but also a strategic — strategically significant player for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s policies throughout the Middle East began with an apology tour and — and — and pursue a strategy of leading from behind, and this strategy is unraveling before our very eyes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Secretary Clinton has done an extraordinary job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But she works for me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m the president and I’m always responsible, and that’s why nobody’s more interested in finding out exactly what happened than I do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The day after the attack, governor, I stood in the Rose Garden and I told the American people in the world that we are going to find out exactly what happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That this was an act of terror and I also said that we’re going to hunt down those who committed this crime.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then a few days later, I was there greeting the caskets coming into Andrews Air Force Base and grieving with the families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the suggestion that anybody in my team, whether the Secretary of State, our U.N.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ambassador, anybody on my team would play politics or mislead when we’ve lost four of our own, governor, is offensive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not what we do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not what I do as president, that’s not what I do as Commander in Chief.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I think interesting the president just said something which — which is that on the day after the attack he went into the Rose Garden and said that this was an act of terror.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what I said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said in the Rose Garden the day after the attack, it was an act of terror.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was not a spontaneous demonstration, is that what you’re saying?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Please proceed governor.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure we get that for the record because it took the president 14 days before he called the attack in Benghazi an act of terror.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Get the transcript.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Can you say that a little louder, Candy?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — the administration — the administration indicated this was a reaction to a video and was a spontaneous reaction.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It took them a long time to say this was a terrorist act by a terrorist group.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And to suggest — am I incorrect in that regard, on Sunday, the — your secretary —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The ambassador of the United Nations went on the Sunday television shows and spoke about how —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, I’m —

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: — this was a spontaneous —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m happy to have a longer conversation —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: — about foreign policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m happy to do that, too.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I just want to make sure that —

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: — all of these wonderful folks are going to have a chance to get some of their questions answered.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re a nation that believes in the Second Amendment, and I believe in the Second Amendment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got a long tradition of hunting and sportsmen and people who want to make sure they can protect themselves.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there have been too many instances during the course of my presidency, where I’ve had to comfort families who have lost somebody.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Most recently out in Aurora.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, just a couple of weeks ago, actually, probably about a month, I saw a mother, who I had met at the bedside of her son, who had been shot in that theater.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And her son had been shot through the head.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we spent some time, and we said a prayer and, remarkably, about two months later, this young man and his mom showed up, and he looked unbelievable, good as new.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there were a lot of families who didn’t have that good fortune and whose sons or daughters or husbands didn’t survive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So my belief is that, (A), we have to enforce the laws we’ve already got, make sure that we’re keeping guns out of the hands of criminals, those who are mentally ill.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve done a much better job in terms of background checks, but we’ve got more to do when it comes to enforcement.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also share your belief that weapons that were designed for soldiers in war theaters don’t belong on our streets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what I’m trying to do is to get a broader conversation about how do we reduce the violence generally.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of it is seeing if we can get an assault weapons ban reintroduced.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But part of it is also looking at other sources of the violence.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because frankly, in my home town of Chicago, there’s an awful lot of violence and they’re not using AK-47s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’re using cheap hand guns.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what can we do to intervene, to make sure that young people have opportunity; that our schools are working; that if there’s violence on the streets, that working with faith groups and law enforcement, we can catch it before it gets out of control.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what I want is a — is a comprehensive strategy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of it is seeing if we can get automatic weapons that kill folks in amazing numbers out of the hands of criminals and the mentally ill.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But part of it is also going deeper and seeing if we can get into these communities and making sure we catch violent impulses before they occur.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Yeah, I’m not in favor of new pieces of legislation on — on guns and taking guns away or making certain guns illegal.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We, of course, don’t want to have automatic weapons, and that’s already illegal in this country to have automatic weapons.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I believe is we have to do, as the president mentioned towards the end of his remarks there, which is to make enormous efforts to enforce the gun laws that we have, and to change the culture of violence that we have.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And you ask how — how are we going to do that?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there are a number of things.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: He mentioned good schools.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I totally agree.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We were able to drive our schools to be number one in the nation in my state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I believe if we do a better job in education, we’ll — we’ll give people the — the hope and opportunity they deserve and perhaps less violence from that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me mention another thing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that is parents.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need moms and dads, helping to raise kids.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Wherever possible the — the benefit of having two parents in the home, and that’s not always possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A lot of great single moms, single dads.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But gosh to tell our kids that before they have babies, they ought to think about getting married to someone, that’s a great idea.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because if there’s a two parent family, the prospect of living in poverty goes down dramatically.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The opportunities that the child will — will be able to achieve increase dramatically.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So we can make changes in the way our culture works to help bring people away from violence and give them opportunity, and bring them in the American system.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The — the greatest failure we’ve had with regards to — to gun violence in some respects is what — what is known as Fast and Furious.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Which was a program under this administration, and how it worked exactly I think we don’t know precisely, where thousands of automatic, and AK-47 type weapons were — were given to people that ultimately gave them to — to drug lords.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They used those weapons against — against their own citizens and killed Americans with them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And this was a — this was a program of the government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: For what purpose it was put in place, I can’t imagine.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s one of the great tragedies related to violence in our society which has occurred during this administration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Which I think the American people would like to understand fully, it’s been investigated to a degree, but — but the administration has carried out executive privilege to prevent all of the information from coming out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to understand who it was that did this, what the idea was behind it, why it led to the violence, thousands of guns going to Mexican drug lords.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, Candy, actually, in my state, the pro-gun folks and the anti-gun folks came together and put together a piece of legislation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s referred to as an assault weapon ban, but it had, at the signing of the bill, both the pro-gun and the anti-gun people came together, because it provided opportunities for both that both wanted.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There were hunting opportunities, for instance, that haven’t previously been available and so forth, so it was a mutually agreed- upon piece of legislation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s what we need more of, Candy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What we have right now in Washington is a place that’s gridlocked.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: — we haven’t had the leadership in Washington to work on a bipartisan basis.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was able to do that in my state and bring these two together.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — first of all, I think Governor Romney was for an assault weapons ban before he was against it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he said that the reason he changed his mind was, in part, because he was seeking the endorsement of the National Rifle Association.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So that’s on the record.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think that one area we agree on is the important of parents and the importance of schools, because I do believe that if our young people have opportunity, then they are less likely to engage in these kinds of violent acts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re not going to eliminate everybody who is mentally disturbed and we have got to make sure they don’t get weapons.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we can make a difference in terms ensuring that every young person in America, regardless of where they come from, what they look like, have a chance to succeed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, Candy, we haven’t had a chance to talk about education much, but I think it is very important to understand that the reforms we’ve put in place, working with 46 governors around the country, are seeing schools that are some of the ones that are the toughest for kids starting to succeed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re starting to see gains in math and science.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to community colleges, we are setting up programs, including with Nassau Community College, to retrain workers, including young people who may have dropped out of school but now are getting another chance, training them for the jobs that exist right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And in fact, employers are looking for skilled workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we’re matching them up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Giving them access to higher education.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: As I said, we have made sure that millions of young people are able to get an education that they weren’t able to get before.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But — but it’ll — it’ll — it’ll be… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … just one second.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because — because this is important.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is part of the choice in this election.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When Governor Romney was asked whether teachers, hiring more teachers was important to growing our economy, Governor Romney said that doesn’t grow our economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When — when he was asked would class size… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I understand.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But this will make a difference in terms of whether or not we can move this economy forward for these young people…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … and reduce our violence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Boy, great question and important question, because you’re absolutely right.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The place where we’ve seen manufacturing go has been China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China is now the largest manufacturer in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It used to be the United States of America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A lot of good people have lost jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A half a million manufacturing jobs have been lost in the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s total over the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One of the reasons for that is that people think it’s more attractive in some cases to go offshore than to stay here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have made it less attractive for enterprises to stay here than to go offshore from time to time.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: What I will do as president is make sure it’s more attractive to come to America again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the way we’re going to create jobs in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not by trickle-down government, saying we’re going to take more money from people and hire more government workers, raise more taxes, put in place more regulations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Trickle-down government has never worked here, has never worked anywhere.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make America the most attractive place in the world for entrepreneurs, for small business, for big business, to invest and grow in America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, we’re going to have to make sure that as we trade with other nations that they play by the rules.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And China hasn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One of the reasons — or one of the ways they don’t play by the rules is artificially holding down the value of their currency.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because if they put their currency down low, that means their prices on their goods are low.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that makes them advantageous in the marketplace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We lose sales.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And manufacturers here in the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: making the same products can’t compete.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China has been a currency manipulator for years and years and years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the president has a regular opportunity to label them as a currency manipulator, but refuses to do so.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: On day one, I will label China a currency manipulator, which will allow me as president to be able to put in place, if necessary, tariffs where I believe that they are taking unfair advantage of our manufacturers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So we’re going to make sure that people we trade with around the world play by the rules.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me — let me not just stop there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Don’t forget, what’s key to bringing back jobs here is not just finding someone else to punish, and I’m going to be strict with people who we trade with to make sure they — they follow the law and play by the rules, but it’s also to make America the most attractive place in the world for businesses of all kinds.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why I want to down the tax rates on small employers, big employers, so they want to be here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Canada’s tax rate on companies is now 15 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Ours is 35 percent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So if you’re starting a business, where would you rather start it?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to be competitive if we’re going to create more jobs here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Regulations have quadrupled.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The rate of regulations quadrupled under this president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I talk to small businesses across the country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They say, “We feel like we’re under attack from our own government.” I want to make sure that regulators see their job as encouraging small business, not crushing it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there’s no question but that Obamacare has been an extraordinary deterrent to enterprises of all kinds hiring people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My priority is making sure that we get more people hired.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If we have more people hired, if we get back manufacturing jobs, if we get back all kinds of jobs into this country, then you’re going to see rising incomes again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The reason incomes are down is because unemployment is so high.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to get this to happen, and my plan will do that, and one part of it is to make sure that we keep China playing by the rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to create jobs here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And both Governor Romney and I agree actually that we should lower our corporate tax rate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s too high.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there’s a difference in terms of how we would do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to close loopholes that allow companies to deduct expenses when they move to China; that allow them to profit offshore and not have to get taxed, so they have tax advantages offshore.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All those changes in our tax code would make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney actually wants to expand those tax breaks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One of his big ideas when it comes to corporate tax reform would be to say, if you invest overseas, you make profits overseas, you don’t have to pay U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, of course, if you’re a small business or a mom-and-pop business or a big business starting up here, you’ve got to pay even the reduced rate that Governor Romney’s talking about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s estimated that that will create 800,000 new jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The problem is they’ll be in china.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not the way we’re going to create jobs here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The way we’re going to create jobs here is not just to change our tax code, but also to double our exports.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are on pace to double our exports, one of the commitments I made when I was president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s creating tens of thousands of jobs all across the country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we’ve kept on pushing trade deals, but trade deals that make sure that American workers and American businesses are getting a good deal.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney talked about China, as I already indicated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the private sector, Governor Romney’s company invested in what were called pioneers of outsourcing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not my phrase.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what reporters called it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as far as currency manipulation, the currency has actually gone up 11 percent since I’ve been president because we have pushed them hard.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve put unprecedented trade pressure on China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why exports have significantly increased under my presidency.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s going to help to create jobs here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The answer is very straightforward.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can compete with anyone in the world as long as the playing field is level.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China’s been cheating over the years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One by holding down the value of their currency.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, by stealing our intellectual property; our designs, our patents, our technology.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There’s even an Apple store in China that’s a counterfeit Apple store, selling counterfeit goods.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They hack into our computers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We will have to have people play on a fair basis, that’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, we have to make America the most attractive place for entrepreneurs, for people who want to expand their business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s what brings jobs in.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s characterization of my tax plan… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …is completely…is completely… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …is completely false.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me tell you…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Candy, there are some jobs that are not going to come back.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because they are low wage, low skill jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want high wage, high skill jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we have to emphasize manufacturing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we have to invest in advanced manufacturing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we’ve got to make sure that we’ve got the best science and research in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when we talk about deficits, if we’re adding to our deficit for tax cuts for folks who don’t need them, and we’re cutting investments in research and science that will create the next Apple, create the next new innovation that will sell products around the world, we will lose that race.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we’re not training engineers to make sure that they are equipped here in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Then companies won’t come here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those investments are what’s going to help to make sure that we continue to lead this world economy, not just next year, but 10 years from now, 50 years from now, 100 years from now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Government does not create jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Government does not create jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Where is Barry?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, and that’s an opportunity for me, and I appreciate it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the nature of a campaign, it seems that some campaigns are focused on attacking a person rather than prescribing their own future and the things they’d like to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the course of that, I think the president’s campaign has tried to characterize me as — as someone who’s very different than who I am.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I care about 100 percent of the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want 100 percent of the American people to have a bright and prosperous future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I care about our kids.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand what it takes to make a bright and prosperous future for America again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I spent my life in the private sector, not in government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m a guy who wants to help with the experience I have, the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My — my passion probably flows from the fact that I believe in God.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I believe we’re all children of the same God.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe we have a responsibility to care for one another.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I served as a missionary for my church.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I served as a pastor in my congregation for about 10 years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve sat across the table from people who were out of work and worked with them to try and find new work or to help them through tough times.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I went to the Olympics when they were in trouble to try and get them on track.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And as governor of my state, I was able to get 100 percent of my people insured, all my kids, about 98 percent of the adults.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was able also to get our schools ranked number one in the nation, so 100 percent of our kids would have a bright opportunity for a future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I understand that I can get this country on track again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for what we’re going through.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for gasoline at four bucks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for unemployment at a chronically high level.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for 47 million people on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for 50 percent of kids coming out of college not able to get work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to settle for 23 million people struggling to find a good job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If I become president, I’ll get America working again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will get us on track to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president hasn’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll make sure we can reform Medicare and Social Security to preserve them for coming — coming generations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president said he would.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get our incomes up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, I’ve done these things.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I served as governor and showed I could get them done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Barry, I think a lot of this campaign, maybe over the last four years, has been devoted to this nation that I think government creates jobs, that that somehow is the answer.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not what I believe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe that the free enterprise system is the greatest engine of prosperity the world’s ever known.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe in self-reliance and individual initiative and risk takers being rewarded.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that everybody should have a fair shot and everybody should do their fair share and everybody should play by the same rules, because that’s how our economy’s grown.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we built the world’s greatest middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and that is part of what’s at stake in this election.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s a fundamentally different vision about how we move our country forward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe Governor Romney is a good man.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Loves his family, cares about his faith.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that when he said behind closed doors that 47 percent of the country considered themselves victims who refuse personal responsibility, think about who he was talking about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Folks on Social Security who’ve worked all their lives.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Veterans who’ve sacrificed for this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Students who are out there trying to hopefully advance their own dreams, but also this country’s dreams.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Soldiers who are overseas fighting for us right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People who are working hard every day, paying payroll tax, gas taxes, but don’t make enough income.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to fight for them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what I’ve been doing for the last four years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because if they succeed, I believe the country succeeds.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When my grandfather fought in World War II and he came back and he got a G.I.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bill and that allowed him to go to college, that wasn’t a handout.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That was something that advanced the entire country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to make sure that the next generation has those same opportunities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why I’m asking for your vote and that’s why I’m asking for another four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you, Bob.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And thank you for agreeing to moderate this debate this evening.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you to Lynn University for welcoming us here.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, it’s good to be with you again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We were together at a humorous event a little earlier, and it’s nice to maybe funny this time, not on purpose.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll see what happens.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is obviously an area of great concern to the entire world, and to America in particular, which is to see a — a complete change in the — the structure and the — the environment in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With the Arab Spring, came a great deal of hope that there would be a change towards more moderation, and opportunity for greater participation on the part of women in public life, and in economic life in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But instead, we’ve seen in nation after nation, a number of disturbing events.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course we see in Syria, 30,000 civilians having been killed by the military there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We see in — in Libya, an attack apparently by, I think we know now, by terrorists of some kind against — against our people there, four people dead.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our hearts and — and minds go out to them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Mali has been taken over, the northern part of Mali by al-Qaeda type individuals.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have in — in Egypt, a Muslim Brotherhood president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so what we’re seeing is a pretty dramatic reversal in the kind of hopes we had for that region.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course the greatest threat of all is Iran, four years closer to a nuclear weapon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and we’re going to have to recognize that we have to do as the president has done.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I congratulate him on — on taking out Osama bin Laden and going after the leadership in al-Qaeda.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we can’t kill our way out of this mess.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to have to put in place a very comprehensive and robust strategy to help the — the world of Islam and other parts of the world, reject this radical violent extremism, which is — it’s certainly not on the run.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s certainly not hiding.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a group that is now involved in 10 or 12 countries, and it presents an enormous threat to our friends, to the world, to America, long term, and we must have a comprehensive strategy to help reject this kind of extremism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, my first job as commander in chief, Bob, is to keep the American people safe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what we’ve done over the last four years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We ended the war in Iraq, refocused our attention on those who actually killed us on 9/11.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, Al Qaeda’s core leadership has been decimated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In addition, we’re now able to transition out of Afghanistan in a responsible way, making sure that Afghans take responsibility for their own security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that allows us also to rebuild alliances and make friends around the world to combat future threats.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now with respect to Libya, as I indicated in the last debate, when we received that phone call, I immediately made sure that, number one, that we did everything we could to secure those Americans who were still in harm’s way; number two, that we would investigate exactly what happened, and number three, most importantly, that we would go after those who killed Americans and we would bring them to justice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s exactly what we’re going to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think it’s important to step back and think about what happened in Libya.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Keep in mind that I and Americans took leadership in organizing an international coalition that made sure that we were able to, without putting troops on the ground at the cost of less than what we spent in two weeks in Iraq, liberate a country that had been under the yoke of dictatorship for 40 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Got rid of a despot who had killed Americans and as a consequence, despite this tragedy, you had tens of thousands of Libyans after the events in Benghazi marching and saying America is our friend.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We stand with them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now that represents the opportunity we have to take advantage of.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, Governor Romney, I’m glad that you agree that we have been successful in going after Al Qaida, but I have to tell you that, you know, your strategy previously has been one that has been all over the map and is not designed to keep Americans safe or to build on the opportunities that exist in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, my strategy is pretty straightforward, which is to go after the bad guys, to make sure we do our very best to interrupt them, to — to kill them, to take them out of the picture.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But my strategy is broader than that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s — that’s important, of course.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the key that we’re going to have to pursue is a — is a pathway to get the Muslim world to be able to reject extremism on its own.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t want another Iraq, we don’t want another Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s not the right course for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The right course for us is to make sure that we go after the — the people who are leaders of these various anti-American groups and these — these jihadists, but also help the Muslim world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And how do we do that?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A group of Arab scholars came together, organized by the U.N., to look at how we can help the — the world reject these — these terrorists.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer they came up with was this: One, more economic development.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should key our foreign aid, our direct foreign investment, and that of our friends, we should coordinate it to make sure that we — we push back and give them more economic development.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, better education.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, gender equality.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number four, the rule of law.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to help these nations create civil societies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But what’s been happening over the last couple of years is, as we’ve watched this tumult in the Middle East, this rising tide of chaos occur, you see Al Qaida rushing in, you see other jihadist groups rushing in.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and they’re throughout many nations in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s wonderful that Libya seems to be making some progress, despite this terrible tragedy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But next door, of course, we have Egypt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Libya’s 6 million population; Egypt, 80 million population.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want — we want to make sure that we’re seeing progress throughout the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: With Mali now having North Mali taken over by Al Qaida; with Syria having Assad continuing to — to kill, to murder his own people, this is a region in tumult.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, of course, Iran on the path to a nuclear weapon, we’ve got real (inaudible).

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, I’m glad that you recognize that Al Qaida is a threat, because a few months ago when you were asked what’s the biggest geopolitical threat facing America, you said Russia, not Al Qaida; you said Russia, in the 1980s, they’re now calling to ask for their foreign policy back because, you know, the Cold War’s been over for 20 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But Governor, when it comes to our foreign policy, you seem to want to import the foreign policies of the 1980s, just like the social policies of the 1950s and the economic policies of the 1920s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You say that you’re not interested in duplicating what happened in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But just a few weeks ago, you said you think we should have more troops in Iraq right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the — the challenge we have — I know you haven’t been in a position to actually execute foreign policy — but every time you’ve offered an opinion, you’ve been wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said we should have gone into Iraq, despite that fact that there were no weapons of mass destruction.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that we should still have troops in Iraq to this day.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You indicated that we shouldn’t be passing nuclear treaties with Russia despite the fact that 71 senators, Democrats and Republicans, voted for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that, first, we should not have a timeline in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Then you said we should.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now you say maybe or it depends, which means not only were you wrong, but you were also confusing in sending mixed messages both to our troops and our allies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, what — what we need to do with respect to the Middle East is strong, steady leadership, not wrong and reckless leadership that is all over the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And unfortunately, that’s the kind of opinions that you’ve offered throughout this campaign, and it is not a recipe for American strength, or keeping America safe over the long haul.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, of course I don’t concur with what the president said about my own record and the things that I’ve said.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t happen to be accurate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But — but I can say this, that we’re talking about the Middle East and how to help the Middle East reject the kind of terrorism we’re seeing, and the rising tide of tumult and — and confusion.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and attacking me is not an agenda.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Attacking me is not talking about how we’re going to deal with the challenges that exist in the Middle East, and take advantage of the opportunity there, and stem the tide of this violence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I’ll respond to a couple of things that you mentioned.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, Russia I indicated is a geopolitical foe.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a geopolitical foe, and I said in the same — in the same paragraph I said, and Iran is the greatest national security threat we face.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Russia does continue to battle us in the U.N.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: time and time again.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have clear eyes on this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m not going to wear rose-colored glasses when it comes to Russia, or Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’m certainly not going to say to him, I’ll give you more flexibility after the election.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: After the election, he’ll get more backbone.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, with regards to Iraq, you and I agreed I believe that there should be a status of forces agreement.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Oh you didn’t?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You didn’t want a status of…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I would not have had done was left 10,000 troops in Iraq that would tie us down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that certainly would not help us in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m sorry, you actually — there was a — there was an effort on the part of the president to have a status of forces agreement, and I concurred in that, and said that we should have some number of troops that stayed on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was something I concurred with… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …that your posture.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was my posture as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You thought it should have been 5,000 troops… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … I thought there should have been more troops, but you know what?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The answer was we got… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … no troops through whatsoever.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This was just a few weeks ago that you indicated that we should still have troops in Iraq.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, I… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I’m sorry that’s a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …that’s a — I indicated… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …major speech.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I indicated that you failed to put in place a status… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …of forces agreement at the end of the conflict that existed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor — here — here’s — here’s one thing… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …here’s one thing I’ve learned as commander in chief.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve got to be clear, both to our allies and our enemies, about where you stand and what you mean.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You just gave a speech a few weeks ago in which you said we should still have troops in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not a recipe for making sure that we are taking advantage of the opportunities and meeting the challenges of the Middle East.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it is absolutely true that we cannot just meet these challenges militarily.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so what I’ve done throughout my presidency and will continue to do is, number one, make sure that these countries are supporting our counterterrorism efforts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, make sure that they are standing by our interests in Israel’s security, because it is a true friend and our greatest ally in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number three, we do have to make sure that we’re protecting religious minorities and women because these countries can’t develop unless all the population, not just half of it, is developing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number four, we do have to develop their economic — their economic capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But number five, the other thing that we have to do is recognize that we can’t continue to do nation building in these regions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of American leadership is making sure that we’re doing nation building here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will help us maintain the kind of American leadership that we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we’ve done is organize the international community, saying Assad has to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve mobilized sanctions against that government.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have made sure that they are isolated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have provided humanitarian assistance and we are helping the opposition organize, and we’re particularly interested in making sure that we’re mobilizing the moderate forces inside of Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But ultimately, Syrians are going to have to determine their own future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so everything we’re doing, we’re doing in consultation with our partners in the region, including Israel which obviously has a huge interest in seeing what happens in Syria; coordinating with Turkey and other countries in the region that have a great interest in this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This — what we’re seeing taking place in Syria is heartbreaking, and that’s why we are going to do everything we can to make sure that we are helping the opposition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we also have to recognize that, you know, for us to get more entangled militarily in Syria is a serious step, and we have to do so making absolutely certain that we know who we are helping; that we’re not putting arms in the hands of folks who eventually could turn them against us or allies in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I am confident that Assad’s days are numbered.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what we can’t do is to simply suggest that, as Governor Romney at times has suggested, that giving heavy weapons, for example, to the Syrian opposition is a simple proposition that would lead us to be safer over the long term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s step back and talk about what’s happening in Syria and how important it is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, 30,000 people being killed by their government is a humanitarian disaster.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Secondly, Syria is an opportunity for us because Syria plays an important role in the Middle East, particularly right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Syria is Iran’s only ally in the Arab world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s their route to the sea.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s the route for them to arm Hezbollah in Lebanon, which threatens, of course, our ally, Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so seeing Syria remove Assad is a very high priority for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, seeing a — a replacement government being responsible people is critical for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, we don’t want to have military involvement there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t want to get drawn into a military conflict.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so the right course for us, is working through our partners and with our own resources, to identify responsible parties within Syria, organize them, bring them together in a — in a form of — if not government, a form of — of — of council that can take the lead in Syria.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then make sure they have the arms necessary to defend themselves.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We do need to make sure that they don’t have arms that get into the — the wrong hands.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Those arms could be used to hurt us down the road.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to make sure as well that we coordinate this effort with our allies, and particularly with — with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the Saudi’s and the Qatari, and — and the Turks are all very concerned about this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re willing to work with us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have a very effective leadership effort in Syria, making sure that the — the insurgent there are armed and that the insurgents that become armed, are people who will be the responsible parties.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Recognize — I believe that Assad must go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe he will go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I believe — we want to make sure that we have the relationships of friendship with the people that take his place, steps that in the years to come we see Syria as a — as a friend, and Syria as a responsible party in the Middle East.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — this is a critical opportunity for America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what I’m afraid of is we’ve watched over the past year or so, first the president saying, well we’ll let the U.N.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: deal with it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And Assad — excuse me, Kofi Annan came in and said we’re going to try to have a ceasefire.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That didn’t work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then it went to the Russians and said, let’s see if you can do something.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should be playing the leadership role there, not on the ground with military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …by the leadership role.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are playing the leadership role.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We organized the Friends of Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are mobilizing humanitarian support, and support for the opposition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are making sure that those we help are those who will be friends of ours in the long term and friends of our allies in the region over the long term.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But going back to Libya — because this is an example of how we make choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When we went in to Libya, and we were able to immediately stop the massacre there, because of the unique circumstances and the coalition that we had helped to organize.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We also had to make sure that Moammar Gadhafi didn’t stay there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And to the governor’s credit, you supported us going into Libya and the coalition that we organized.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it came time to making sure that Gadhafi did not stay in power, that he was captured, Governor, your suggestion was that this was mission creep, that this was mission muddle.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Imagine if we had pulled out at that point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, Moammar Gadhafi had more American blood on his hands than any individual other than Osama bin Laden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we were going to make sure that we finished the job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of the reason why the Libyans stand with us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we did so in a careful, thoughtful way, making certain that we knew who we were dealing with, that those forces of moderation on the ground were ones that we could work with, and we have to take the same kind of steady, thoughtful leadership when it comes to Syria.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what we’re doing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t want to have our military involved in Syria.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t think there is a necessity to put our military in Syria at this stage.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I don’t anticipate that in the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As I indicated, our objectives are to replace Assad and to have in place a new government which is friendly to us, a responsible government, if possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I want to make sure they get armed and they have the arms necessary to defend themselves, but also to remove — to remove Assad.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I do not want to see a military involvement on the part of our — of our troops.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And this isn’t — this isn’t going to be necessary.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we have, with our partners in the region, we have sufficient resources to support those groups.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But look, this has been going on for a year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a time — this should have been a time for American leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We should have taken a leading role, not militarily, but a leading role organizationally, governmentally to bring together the parties; to find responsible parties.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As you hear from intelligence sources even today, the — the insurgents are highly disparate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They haven’t come together.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They haven’t formed a unity group, a council of some kind.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That needs to happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America can help that happen.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And we need to make sure they have the arms they need to carry out the very important role which is getting rid of Assad.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I’ll — I’ll be very quick.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What you just heard Governor Romney said is he doesn’t have different ideas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s because we’re doing exactly what we should be doing to try to promote a moderate Syrian leadership and a — an effective transition so that we get Assad out.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ve shown.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ll continue to show.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I don’t, because I think that America has to stand with democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The notion that we would have tanks run over those young people who were in Tahrir Square, that is not the kind of American leadership that John F.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Kennedy talked about 50 years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I’ve also said is that now that you have a democratically elected government in Egypt, that they have to make sure that they take responsibility for protecting religious minorities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have put significant pressure on them to make sure they’re doing that; to recognize the rights of women, which is critical throughout the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These countries can’t develop if young women are not given the kind of education that they need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They have to abide by their treaty with Israel.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is a red line for us, because not only is Israel’s security at stake, but our security is at stake if that unravels.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They have to make sure that they’re cooperating with us when it comes to counterterrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we will help them with respect to developing their own economy, because ultimately what’s going to make the Egyptian revolution successful for the people of Egypt, but also for the world, is if those young people who gathered there are seeing opportunities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their aspirations are similar to young people’s here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They want jobs, they want to be able to make sure their kids are going to a good school.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They want to make sure that they have a roof over their heads and that they have the prospects of a better life in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so one of the things that we’ve been doing is, is, for example, organizing entrepreneurship conferences with these Egyptians to give them a sense of how they can start rebuilding their economy in a way that’s noncorrupt, that’s transparent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what is also important for us to understand is, is that for America to be successful in this region there’s some things that we’re going to have to do here at home as well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, one of the challenges over the last decade is we’ve done experiments in nation building in places like Iraq and Afghanistan and we’ve neglected, for example, developing our own economy, our own energy sectors, our own education system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s very hard for us to project leadership around the world when we’re not doing what we need to do…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I believe, as the president indicated, and said at the time that I supported his — his action there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I felt that — I wish we’d have had a better vision of the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I wish that, looking back at the beginning of the president’s term and even further back than that, that we’d have recognized that there was a growing energy and passion for freedom in that part of the world, and that we would have worked more aggressively with our friend and with other friends in the region to have them make the transition towards a more representative form of government, such that it didn’t explode in the way that it did.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But once it exploded, I felt the same as the president did, which is these freedom voices and the streets of Egypt, where the people who were speaking of our principles and the President Mubarak had done things which were unimaginable and the idea of him crushing his people was not something that we could possibly support.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me step back and talk about what I think our mission has to be in the Middle East and even more broadly, because our purpose is to make sure the world is more — is peaceful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want a peaceful planet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want people to be able to enjoy their lives and know they’re going to have a bright and prosperous future, not be at war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s our purpose.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the mantle of leadership for the — promoting the principles of peace has fallen to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We didn’t ask for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s an honor that we have it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But for us to be able to promote those principles of peace requires us to be strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that begins with a strong economy here at home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Unfortunately, the economy is not stronger.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When the — when the president of Iraq — excuse me, of Iran, Ahmadinejad, says that our debt makes us not a great country, that’s a frightening thing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Former chief of the — Joint Chiefs of Staff said that — Admiral Mullen said that our debt is the biggest national security threat we face.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This — we have weakened our economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need a strong economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have as well a strong military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our military is second to none in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re blessed with terrific soldiers, and extraordinary technology and intelligence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the idea of a trillion dollar in cuts through sequestration and budget cuts to the military would change that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to have strong allies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our association and connection with our allies is essential to America’s strength.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re the great nation that has allies, 42 allies and friends around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And, finally, we have to stand by our principles.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if we’re strong in each of those things, American influence will grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
