sentence: But unfortunately, in nowhere in the world is America’s influence will grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But unfortunately, in — nowhere in the world is America’s influence greater today than it was four years ago.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s because we’ve become weaker in each of those four… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well I — I absolutely believe that America has a — a responsibility, and the privilege of helping defend freedom and promote the principles that — that make the world more peaceful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And those principles include human rights, human dignity, free enterprise, freedom of expression, elections.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because when there are elections, people tend to vote for peace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t vote for war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So we want to promote those principles around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We recognize that there are places of conflict in the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want to end those conflicts to the extent humanly possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But in order to be able to fulfill our role in the world, America must be strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America must lead.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And for that to happen, we have to strengthen our economy here at home.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have 23 million people struggling to get a job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have an economy that over the last three years keeps slowing down its growth rate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t have kids coming out of college, half of them can’t find a job today, or a job that’s commensurate with their college degree.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to get our economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And our military, we’ve got to strengthen our military long-term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t know what the world is going to throw at us down the road.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we make decisions today in the military that — that will confront challenges we can’t imagine.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In the 2000 debates, there was no mention of terrorism, for instance.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And a year later, 9/11 happened.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So, we have to make decisions based upon uncertainty, and that means a strong military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will not cut our military budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to also stand by our allies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I think the tension that existed between Israel and the United States was very unfortunate.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think also that pulling our missile defense program out of Poland in the way we did was also unfortunate in terms of, if you will, disrupting the relationship in some ways that existed between us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then, of course, with regards to standing for our principles, when — when the students took to the streets in Tehran and the people there protested, the Green Revolution occurred, for the president to be silent I thought was an enormous mistake.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to stand for our principles, stand for our allies, stand for a strong military and stand for a stronger economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: America remains the one indispensable nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the world needs a strong America, and it is stronger now than when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because we ended the war in Iraq, we were able to refocus our attention on not only the terrorist threat, but also beginning a transition process in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It also allowed us to refocus on alliances and relationships that had been neglected for a decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney, our alliances have never been stronger, in Asia, in Europe, in Africa, with Israel, where we have unprecedented military and intelligence cooperation, including dealing with the Iranian threat.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what we also have been able to do is position ourselves so we can start rebuilding America, and that’s what my plan does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that we’re bringing manufacturing back to our shores so that we’re creating jobs here, as we’ve done with the auto industry, not rewarding companies that are shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that we’ve got the best education system in the world, including retraining our workers for the jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Doing everything we can to control our own energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve cut our oil imports to the lowest level in two decades because we’ve developed oil and natural gas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we also have to develop clean energy technologies that will allow us to cut our exports in half by 2020.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership that we need to show.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got to make sure that we reduce our deficit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Unfortunately, Governor Romney’s plan doesn’t do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to do it in a responsible way by cutting out spending we don’t need, but also asking the wealthiest to pay a little bit more.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That way we can invest in the research and technology that’s always kept us at the cutting edge.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney has taken a different approach throughout this campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Both at home and abroad, he has proposed wrong and reckless policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He’s praised George Bush as a good economic steward and Dick Cheney as somebody who’s — who shows great wisdom and judgment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And taking us back to those kinds of strategies that got us into this mess are not the way that we are going to maintain leadership in the 21st century.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve got a policy for the future and agenda for the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And when it comes to our economy here at home, I know what it takes to create 12 million new jobs and rising take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And what we’ve seen over the last four years is something I don’t want to see over the next four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president said by now we’d be a 5.4 percent unemployment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re 9 million jobs short of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I will get America working again and see rising take-home pay again, and I’ll do it with five simple steps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number one, we are going to have North American energy independence.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to do it by taking full advantage of oil, coal, gas, nuclear and our renewables.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, we’re going to increase our trade.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Trade grows about 12 percent year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It doubles about every — every five or so years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can do better than that, particularly in Latin America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The opportunities for us in Latin America we have just not taken advantage of fully.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: As a matter of fact, Latin America’s economy is almost as big as the economy of China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re all focused on China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Latin America is a huge opportunity for us — time zone, language opportunities.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number three, we’re going to have to have training programs that work for our workers and schools that finally put the parents and the teachers and the kids first, and the teachers’ unions going to have to go behind.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then we’re going to have to get to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can’t expect entrepreneurs and businesses large and small to take their life savings or their company’s money and invest in America if they think we’re headed to the road to Greece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s where we’re going right now unless we finally get off this spending and borrowing binge.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I’ll get us on track to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And finally, number five, we’ve got to champion small business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Small business is where jobs come from.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Two-thirds of our jobs come from small businesses.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: New business formation is down to the lowest level in 30 years under this administration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to bring it back and get back good jobs and rising take-home pay.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, let’s talk about what we need to compete.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, Governor Romney talks about small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, Governor, when you were in Massachusetts, small businesses development ranked about 48th, I think out of 50 states in Massachusetts, because the policies that you are promoting actually don’t help small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the way you define small businesses includes folks at the very top.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they include you and me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not the kind of small business promotion we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But let’s take an example that we know is going to make a difference in the 21st century and that’s our education policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We didn’t have a lot of chance to talk about this in the last debate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, under my leadership, what we’ve done is reformed education, working with governors, 46 states.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve seen progress and gains in schools that were having a terrible time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they’re starting to finally make progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I now want to do is to hire more teachers, especially in math and science, because we know that we’ve fallen behind when it comes to math and science.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And those teachers can make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Governor Romney, when you were asked by teachers whether or not this would help the economy grow, you said this isn’t going to help the economy grow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When you were asked about reduced class sizes, you said class sizes don’t make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I tell you, if you talk to teachers, they will tell you it does make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’ve got math teachers who are able to provide the kind of support that they need for our kids, that’s what’s going to determine whether or not the new businesses are created here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Companies are going to locate here depending on whether we’ve got the most highly skilled workforce.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the kinds of budget proposals that you’ve put forward, when we don’t ask either you or me to pay a dime more in terms of reducing the deficit, but instead we slash support for education, that’s undermining our long-term competitiveness.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not good for America’s position in the world, and the world notices.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well — well, I need to speak a moment…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … if you’ll let me, Bob, just about education…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … because I’m — I’m so proud of the state that I had the chance to be governor of.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have every two years tests that look at how well our kids are doing.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Fourth graders and eighth graders are tested in English and math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: While I was governor, I was proud that our fourth graders came out number one of all 50 states in English, and then also in math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And our eighth graders number one in English and also in math.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First time one state had been number one in all four measures.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How did we do that?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, Republicans and Democrats came together on a bipartisan basis to put in place education principles that focused on having great teachers in the classroom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ten years earlier…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that was — that was — that was what allowed us to become the number one state in the nation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But that was 10 years before you took office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then you cut education spending when you came into office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The first — the first — the first — and we kept our schools number one in the nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re still number one today.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the principles that we put in place, we also gave kids not just a graduation exam that determined whether they were up to the skills needed to — to be able compete, but also if they graduated the quarter of their class, they got a four-year tuition- free ride at any Massachusetts public institution of higher learning.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That happened before you came into office.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was actually mine, actually, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You got that fact wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, let’s come back and talk about the military, but all the way — all the way through.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: First of all, I’m going through from the very beginning — we’re going to cut about 5 percent of the discretionary budget, excluding military.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He should have answered the first question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, Governor Romney’s called for $5 trillion of tax cuts that he says he’s going to pay for by closing deductions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the math doesn’t work, but he continues to claim that he’s going to do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He then wants to spend another $2 trillion on military spending that our military is not asking for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, keep in mind that our military spending has gone up every single year that I’ve been in office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We spend more on our military than the next 10 countries combined; China, Russia, France, the United Kingdom, you name it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The next 10.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I did was work with our joint chiefs of staff to think about, what are we going to need in the future to make sure that we are safe?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the budget that we’ve put forward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, what you can’t do is spend $2 trillion in additional military spending that the military is not asking for, $5 trillion on tax cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You say that you’re going to pay for it by closing loopholes and deductions, without naming what those loopholes and deductions are.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then somehow you’re also going to deal with the deficit that we’ve already got.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The math simply doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it comes to our military, what we have to think about is not, you know just budgets, we’ve got to think about capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to be thinking about cyber security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to be talking about space.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what our budget does, but it’s driven by strategy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not driven by politics.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not driven by members of Congress, and what they would like to see.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s driven by, what are we going to need to keep the American people safe?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s exactly what our budget does, and it also then allows us to reduce our deficit, which is a significant national security concern.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because we’ve got to make sure that our economy is strong at home so that we can project military power overseas.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m pleased that I’ve balanced budgets.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was on the world of business for 25 years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: If you didn’t balance your budget, you went out of business.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I went into the Olympics that was out of balance, and we got it on balance, and made a success there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I had the chance to be governor of a state.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Four years in a row, Democrats and Republicans came together to balance the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We cut taxes 19 times and balanced our budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president hasn’t balanced a budget yet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I expect to have the opportunity to do so myself.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to be able to balance the budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s talk about military spending, and that’s this.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our Navy is old — excuse me, our Navy is smaller now than at any time since 1917.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The Navy said they needed 313 ships to carry out their mission.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re now at under 285.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re headed down to the low 200s if we go through a sequestration.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s unacceptable to me.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure that we have the ships that are required by our Navy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our Air Force is older and smaller than at any time since it was founded in 1947.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve changed for the first time since FDR — since FDR we had the — we’ve always had the strategy of saying we could fight in two conflicts at once.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now we’re changing to one conflict.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, this, in my view, is the highest responsibility of the President of the United States, which is to maintain the safety of the American people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I will not cut our military budget by a trillion dollars, which is a combination of the budget cuts the president has, as well as the sequestration cuts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That, in my view, is making — is making our future less certain and less secure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, I just need to comment on this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, the sequester is not something that I’ve proposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is something that Congress has proposed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It will not happen.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The budget that we are talking about is not reducing our military spending.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is maintaining it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think Governor Romney maybe hasn’t spent enough time looking at how our military works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You mentioned the Navy, for example, and that we have fewer ships than we did in 1916.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Governor, we also have fewer horses and bayonets, because the nature of our military’s changed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have these things called aircraft carriers, where planes land on them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have these ships that go underwater, nuclear submarines.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so the question is not a game of Battleship, where we’re counting slips.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s what are our capabilities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so when I sit down with the Secretary of the Navy and the Joint Chiefs of Staff, we determine how are we going to be best able to meet all of our defense needs in a way that also keeps faith with our troops, that also makes sure that our veterans have the kind of support that they need when they come home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is not reflected in the kind of budget that you’re putting forward because it just doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, we visited the website quite a bit and it still doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, Israel is a true friend.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is our greatest ally in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if Israel is attacked, America will stand with Israel.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ve made that clear throughout my presidency.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will stand with Israel if they are attacked.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is the reason why, working with Israel, we have created the strongest military and intelligence cooperation between our two countries in history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, this week we’ll be carrying out the largest military exercise with Israel in history, this very week.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But to the issue of Iran, as long as I’m president of the United States Iran will not get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I made that clear when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We then organized the strongest coalition and the strongest sanctions against Iran in history, and it is crippling their economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their currency has dropped 80 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their oil production has plunged to the lowest level since they were fighting a war with Iraq 20 years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So their economy is in a shambles.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason we did this is because a nuclear Iran is a threat to our national security, and it is a threat to Israel’s national security.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We cannot afford to have a nuclear arms race in the most volatile region of the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran is a state sponsor of terrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And for them to be able to provide nuclear technology to non-state actors, that’s unacceptable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they have said that they want to see Israel wiped off the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So the work that we’ve done with respect to sanctions now offers Iran a choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They can take the diplomatic route and end their nuclear program or they will have to face a united world and a United States president, me, who said we’re not going to take any options off the table.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The disagreement I have with Governor Romney is that, during the course of this campaign, he’s often talked as if we should take premature military action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that would be a mistake, because when I’ve sent young men and women into harm’s way, I always understand that that is the last resort, not the first resort.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, I want to underscore the same point the president mad,e which is that if I’m President of the United States, when I’m President of the United States, we will stand with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And if Israel is attacked, we have their back, not just diplomatically, not just culturally, but militarily.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, with regards to Iran and the threat of Iran, there’s no question but that a nuclear Iran, a nuclear-capable Iran is unacceptable to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It presents a threat not only to our friends but ultimately a threat to us to have Iran have nuclear material, nuclear weapons that could be used against us or used to be threatening to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It is also essential for us to understand what our mission is in Iran, and that is to dissuade Iran from having a nuclear weapon through peaceful and diplomatic means.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And crippling sanctions are something I called for five years ago, when I was in Israel, speaking at the Herzliya Conference.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I laid out seven steps, crippling sanctions were number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they do work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re seeing it right now in the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s absolutely the right thing to do, to have crippling sanctions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would have put them in place earlier.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s good that we have them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, something I would add today is I would tighten those sanctions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would say that ships that carry Iranian oil, can’t come into our ports.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I imagine the E.U.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: would agree with us as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Not only ships couldn’t, but I’d say companies that are moving their oil can’t, people who are trading in their oil can’t.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would tighten those sanctions further.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Secondly, I’d take on diplomatic isolation efforts.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d make sure that Ahmadinejad is indicted under the Genocide Convention.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: His words amount to genocide incitation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would indict him for it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I would also make sure that their diplomats are treated like the pariah they are around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The same way we treated the apartheid diplomats of South Africa.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to increase pressure time, and time again on Iran because anything other than a — a — a solution to this, which says — which stops this — this nuclear folly of theirs, is unacceptable to America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And of course, a military action is the last resort.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It is something one would only – only consider if all of the other avenues had been — had been tried to their full extent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all those are reports in the newspaper.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But our goal is to get Iran to recognize it needs to give up its nuclear program and abide by the U.N.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: resolutions that have been in place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because they have the opportunity to reenter the community of nations, and we would welcome that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There — there are people in Iran who have the same aspirations as people all around the world for a better life.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we hope that their leadership takes the right decision, but the deal we’ll accept is they end their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s very straightforward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m glad that Governor Romney agrees with the steps that we’re taking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, there have been times, Governor, frankly, during the course of this campaign, where it sounded like you thought that you’d do the same things we did, but you’d say them louder and somehow that — that would make a difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it turns out that the work involved in setting up these crippling sanctions is painstaking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s meticulous.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We started from the day we got into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason is was so important — and this is a testament to how we’ve restored American credibility and strength around the world — is we had to make sure that all the countries participated, even countries like Russia and China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because if it’s just us that are imposing sanctions — we’ve had sanctions in place a long time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s because we got everybody to agree that Iran is seeing so much pressure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got to maintain that pressure.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There is a deal to be had, and that is that they abide by the rules that have already been established.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They convince the international community they are not pursuing a nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are inspections that are very intrusive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But over time, what they can do is regain credibility.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the meantime, though, we’re not going to let up the pressure until we have clear evidence that that takes place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one last thing — just — just to make this point.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The clock is ticking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re not going to allow Iran to perpetually engage in negotiations that lead nowhere.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve been very clear to them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, because of the intelligence coordination that we do with a range of countries, including Israel, we have a sense of when they would get breakout capacity, which means that we would not be able to intervene in time to stop their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that clock is ticking.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re going to make sure that if they do not meet the demands of the international community, then we are going to take all options necessary to make sure they don’t have a nuclear weapon.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think from the very beginning, one of the challenges we’ve had with Iran is that they have looked at this administration, and felt that the administration was not as strong as it needed to be.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they saw weakness where they had expected to find American strength.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I say that because from the very beginning, the president in his campaign four years ago, said he would meet with all the world’s worst actors in his first year, he’d sit down with Chavez and Kim Jong-il, with Castro and President Ahmadinejad of Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think they looked and thought, well, that’s an unusual honor to receive from the President of the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then the president began what I have called an apology tour, of going to various nations in the Middle East and criticizing America.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they looked at that and saw weakness.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then when there were dissidents in the streets of Tehran, a Green Revolution, holding signs saying, is America with us, the president was silent.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I think they noticed that as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I think that when the president said he was going to create daylight between ourselves and Israel, that they noticed that as well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: All of these things suggested, I think, to the Iranian mullahs that, hey, you know, we can keep on pushing along here, we can keep talks going on, we’re just going to keep on spinning centrifuges.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now there are some 10,000 centrifuges spinning uranium, preparing to create a nuclear threat to the United States and to the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s unacceptable for us, and it’s essential for a president to show strength from the very beginning, to make it very clear what is acceptable and not acceptable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And an Iranian nuclear program is not acceptable to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They must not develop nuclear capability.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the way to make sure they understand that is by having, from the very beginning, the tightest sanctions possible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They need to be tightened.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our diplomatic isolation needs to be tougher.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to indict Ahmadinejad.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need to put the pressure on them as hard as we possibly can, because if we do that, we won’t have to take the military action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, let me just respond.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nothing Governor Romney just said is true, starting with this notion of me apologizing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This has been probably the biggest whopper that’s been told during the course of this campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And every fact checker and every reporter who’s looked at it, Governor, has said this is not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to tightening sanctions, look, as I said before, we’ve put in the toughest, most crippling sanctions ever.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the fact is, while we were coordinating an international coalition to make sure these sanctions were effective, you were still invested in a Chinese state oil company that was doing business with the Iranian oil sector.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So I’ll let the American people decide, judge, who’s going to be more effective and more credible when it comes to imposing crippling sanctions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And with respect to our attitude about the Iranian revolution, I was very clear about the murderous activities that had taken place and that was contrary to international law and everything that civilized people stand for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And — and so the strength that we have shown in Iran is shown by the fact that we’ve been able to mobilize the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I came into office, the world was divided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran was resurgent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran is at its weakest point, economically, strategically, militarily, then since — then in many years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are going to continue to keep the pressure on to make sure that they do not get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s in America’s national interest and that will be the case so long as I’m president.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re four years closer to a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re four years closer to a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and — we should not have wasted these four years to the extent they — they continue to be able to spin these centrifuges and get that much closer.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s number one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Number two, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, the reason I call it an apology tour is because you went to the Middle East and you flew to Egypt and to Saudi Arabia and to Turkey and Iraq.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, you skipped Israel, our closest friend in the region, but you went to the other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And by the way, they noticed that you skipped Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then in those nations, and on Arabic TV, you said that America had been dismissive and derisive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said that on occasion America had dictated to other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, America has not dictated to other nations.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have freed other nations from dictators.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, let me — let me respond.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we’re going to talk about trips that we’ve taken — when I was a candidate for office, first trip I took was to visit our troops.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when I went to Israel as a candidate, I didn’t take donors.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I didn’t attend fundraisers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I went to Yad Beshef (ph), the Holocaust museum there, to remind myself the nature of evil and why our bond with Israel will be unbreakable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then I went down to the border towns of Storok (ph), which had experienced missiles raining dowm from Hamas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I saw families there who showed me there where missiles had come down near their children’s bedrooms.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I was reminded of what that would mean if those were my kids.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Which is why as president, we funded an Iron Dome program to stop those missiles.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So that’s how I’ve used my travels, when I travel to Israel and when I travel to the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the — the central question at this point is going to be: Who is going to be credible to all parties involved?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they can look at my track record, whether it’s Iran sanctions, whether it’s dealing with counterterrorism, whether it’s supporting democracy, whether it’s supporting women’s rights, whether it’s supporting religious minorities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they can say that the President of the United States and the United States of America has stood on the right side of history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that kind of credibility is precisely why we’ve been able to show leadership on a wide range of issues facing the world right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Bob, let’s not go into hypotheticals of that nature.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Our relationship with Israel, my relationship with the prime minister of Israel is such that we would not get a call saying our bombers are on the way, or their fighters are on the way.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is the kind of thing that would have been discussed and thoroughly evaluated well before that kind of — (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me — let me come back — we can come back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s come back to what the president was speaking about, which is what’s happening in the world and the president’s statement that things are going so well.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I look at what’s happening around the world, and i see Iran four years closer to a bomb.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see the Middle East with a rising tide of violence, chaos, tumult.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see jihadists continuing to spread, whether they’re rising or just about the same level, hard to precisely measure, but it’s clear they’re there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re very strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see Syria with 30,000 civilians dead, Assad still in power.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see our trade deficit with China, larger than it’s — growing larger every year, as a matter of fact.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I look around the world and I don’t feel that you see North Korea, continuing to export their nuclear technology, Russia said they’re not going to follow Nunn-Lugar any more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re back away from a nuclear proliferation treaty that we had with them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I look around the world, I don’t see our influence growing around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I see our influence receding, in part because of the failure of the president to deal with our economic challenges at home; in part because of our withdrawal from our commitment to our military in the way I think it ought to be; in part because of the — the — the turmoil with Israel.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I mean, the president received a letter from 38 Democrat senators saying the tensions with Israel were a real problem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They asked him, please repair the tension — Democrat senators — please repair the tension…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: … the damage in his — in his own party.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, the problem is, is that on a whole range of issues, whether it’s the Middle East, whether it’s Afghanistan, whether it’s Iraq, whether it’s now Iran, you’ve been all over the map.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, I’m — I’m pleased that you now are endorsing our policy of applying diplomatic pressure and potentially having bilateral discussions with the Iranians to end their nuclear program.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But just a few years ago you said that’s something you’d never do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the same way that you initially opposed a timetable in Afghanistan, now you’re for it, although it depends.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the same way that you say you would have ended the war in Iraq, but recently gave a speech saying that we should have 20,000 more folks in there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The same way that you said that it was mission creep to go after Gadhafi.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When it comes to going after Osama bin Laden, you said, well, any president would make that call.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when you were a candidate in 2008, as I was, and I said if I got bin Laden in our sights I would take that shot, you said we shouldn’t move heaven and earth to get one man.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you said we should ask Pakistan for permission.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we had asked Pakistan permission, we would not have gotten him.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it was worth moving heaven and earth to get him.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, after we killed bin Laden I was at ground zero for a memorial and talked to a young women who was four years old when 9/11 happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the last conversation she had with her father was him calling from the twin towers, saying “Peyton (ph), I love you and I will always watch over you.” And for the next decade, she was haunted by that conversation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And she said to me, “You know, by finally getting bin Laden, that brought some closure to me.” And when we do things like that — when we bring those who have harmed us to justice, that sends a message to the world and it tells Peyton (ph) that we did not forget her father.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I make that point because that’s the kind of clarity of leadership, and those decisions are not always popular.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those decisions generally — generally are not poll-tested.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And even some in my own party, including my current vice president, had the same critique as you did.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what the American people understand is that I look at what we need to get done to keep the American people safe and to move our interests forward, and I make those decisions.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t — but you can’t have the president just lay out a whole series of items without giving me a chance to respond.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, that’s probably true.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, we’re going to be finished by 2014, and when I’m president, we’ll make sure we bring our troops out by the end of 2014.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The commanders and the generals there are on track to do so.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve seen progress over the past several years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The surge has been successful and the training program is proceeding apace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: There are now a large number of Afghan Security Forces, 350,000 that are ready to step in to provide security and we’re going to be able to make that transition by the end of 2014.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So our troops will come home at that point.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I can tell you at the same time, that we will make sure that we look at what’s happening in Pakistan, and recognize that what’s happening in Pakistan is going to have a major impact on the success in Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I say that because I know a lot of people that feel like we should just brush our hands and walk away.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I don’t mean you, Mr.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, but some people in the — in our nation feel that Pakistan is being nice to us, and that we should walk away fro mthem.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But Pakistan is important to the region, to the world and to us, because Pakistan has 100 nuclear warheads and they’re rushing to build a lot more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ll have more than Great Britain sometime in the — in the relatively near future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They also have the Haqqani Network and the Taliban existent within their country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so a Pakistan that falls apart, becomes a failed state, would be of extraordinary danger to Afghanistan and to us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we’re going to have to remain helpful in encouraging Pakistan to move towards a more stable government and rebuild the relationship with us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that means that our aid that we provide to Pakistan is going to have to be conditioned upon certain benchmarks being met.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So for me, I look at this as both a need to help move Pakistan in the right direction, and also to get Afghanistan to be ready, and they will be ready by the end of 2014.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I came into office, we were still bogged down in Iraq and Afghanistan had been drifting for a decade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We ended the war in Iraq, refocused our attention on Afghanistan, and we did deliver a surge of troops.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That was facilitated in part because we had ended the war in Iraq.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are now in a position where we have met many of the objectives that got us there in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of what had happened is we’d forgotten why we had gone.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We went because there were people who were responsible for 3,000 American deaths.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we decimated Al Qaida’s core leadership in the border regions between Afghanistan and Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We then started to build up Afghan forces.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re now in a position where we can transition out, because there’s no reason why Americans should die when Afghans are perfectly capable of defending their own country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, that transition has to take place in a responsible fashion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve been there a long time, and we’ve got to make sure that we and our coalition partners are pulling out responsibly and giving Afghans the capabilities that they need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what I think the American people recognize is after a decade of war it’s time to do some nation building here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we can now do is free up some resources, to, for example, put Americans back to work, especially our veterans, rebuilding our roads, our bridges, our schools, making sure that, you know, our veterans are getting the care that they need when it comes to post-traumatic stress disorder and traumatic brain injury, making sure that the certifications that they need for good jobs of the future are in place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, I was having lunch with some — a veteran in Minnesota who had been a medic dealing with the most extreme circumstances.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When he came home and he wanted to become a nurse, he had to start from scratch.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we’ve said is let’s change those certifications.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The first lady has done great work with an organization called Joining Forces putting our veterans back to work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, veterans’ unemployment is actually now lower than general population.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It was higher when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So those are the kinds of things that we can now do because we’re making that transition in Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, it’s not time to divorce a nation on Earth that has 100 nuclear weapons and is on the way to double that at some point, a nation that has serious threats from terrorist groups within its nation, as I indicated before, the Taliban, Haqqani Network.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a nation that’s not like — like others and it does not have a civilian leadership that is calling the shots there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You have the ISI, their intelligence organization, is probably the most powerful of the — of three branches there.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Then you have the military and then you have the civilian government.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a nation, which, if it falls apart, if it — if it becomes a failed state, there are nuclear weapons there and you’ve got — you’ve got terrorists there who could grab their — their hands onto those nuclear weapons.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is — this is an important part of the world for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Pakistan is — is technically an ally, and they’re not acting very much like an ally right now.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we have some work to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I — I don’t blame the administration for the fact that the relationship with Pakistan is strained.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We — we had to go into Pakistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We had to go in there to get Osama bin Laden.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That was the right thing to do.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And — and that upset them, but obviously there was a great deal of anger even before that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we’re going to have to work with the — with the people in Pakistan to try and help them move to a more responsible course than the one that they’re on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s important for them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s important for the nuclear weapons.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s important for the success of Afghanistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Because inside Pakistan, you have a — a large group of Pashtun that are — that are Taliban.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re going to come rushing back in to Afghanistan when we go.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s one of the reasons the Afghan Security Forces have so much work to do to be able to fight against that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But it’s important for us to recognize that we can’t just walk away from Pakistan.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But we do need to make sure that as we — as we send support for them, that this is tied to them making progress on — on matters that would lead them to becoming a civil society.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well I believe we should use any and all means necessary to take out people who pose a threat to us and our friends around the world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And it’s widely reported that drones are being used in drone strikes, and I support that and entirely, and feel the president was right to up the usage of that technology, and believe that we should continue to use it, to continue to go after the people that represent a threat to this nation and to our friends.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But let me also note that as I said earlier, we’re going to have to do more than just going after leaders and — and killing bad guys, important as that is.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re also going to have to have a farm more effective and comprehensive strategy to help move the world away from terror and Islamic extremism.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We haven’t done that yet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We talk a lot about these things, but you look at the — the record, you look at the record.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You look at the record of the last four years and say is Iran closer to a bomb?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is the Middle East in tumult?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is — is al-Qaida on the run, on its heels?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is — are Israel and the Palestinians closer to reaching a peace agreement?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: No, they haven’t had talks in two years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have not seen the progress we need to have, and I’m convinced that with strong leadership and an effort to build a strategy based upon helping these nations reject extremism, we can see the kind of peace and prosperity the world demands.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, keep in mind our strategy wasn’t just going after bin Laden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We created partnerships throughout the region to deal with extremism in Somalia, in Yemen, in Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what we’ve also done is engaged these governments in the kind of reforms that are actually going to make a difference in people’s lives day to day, to make sure that their governments aren’t corrupt, to make sure that they’re treating women with the kind of respect and dignity that every nation that succeeds has shown and to make sure that they’ve got a free market system that works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So across the board, we are engaging them in building capacity in these countries.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we have stood on the side of democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One thing I think Americans should be proud of, when Tunisians began to protest, this nation — me, my administration — stood with them earlier than just about any country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In Egypt we stood on the side of democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In Libya we stood on the side of the people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And as a consequence, there’s no doubt that attitudes about Americans have changed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there are always going to be elements in these countries that potentially threaten the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we want to shrink those groups and those networks and we can do that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’re always also going to have to maintain vigilance when it comes to terrorist activities.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The truth, though, is that Al Qaeda is much weaker than it was when I came into office.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they don’t have the same capacities to attack the U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: homeland and our allies as they did four years ago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it will continue to be terrorist networks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We have to remain vigilant, as I just said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But with respect to China, China is both an adversary, but also a potential partner in the international community if it’s following the rules.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So my attitude coming into office was that we are going to insist that China plays by the same rules as everybody else.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I know Americans had seen jobs being shipped overseas; businesses and workers not getting a level playing field when it came to trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the reason why I set up a trade task force to go after cheaters when it came to international trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the reason why we have brought more cases against China for violating trade rules than the other — the previous administration had done in two terms.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve won just about every case that we’ve filed, that has been decided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, just recently steelworkers in Ohio and throughout the Midwest — Pennsylvania — are in a position now to sell steel to China because we won that case.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We had a tire case in which they were flooding us with cheap domestic tires — or — or cheap Chinese tires.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we put a stop to it and as a consequence saved jobs throughout America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I have to say that Governor Romney criticized me for being too tough in that tire case; said this wouldn’t be good for American workers and that it would be protectionist.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I tell you, those workers don’t feel that way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They feel as if they had finally an administration who was going to take this issue seriously.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the long term, in order for us to compete with China, we’ve also got to make sure, though, that we’re taking — taking care of business here at home.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we don’t have the best education system in the world, if we don’t continue to put money into research and technology that will allow us to create great businesses here in the United States, that’s how we lose the competition.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, unfortunately, Governor Romney’s budget and his proposals would not allow us to make those investments.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, first of all, it’s not government that makes business successful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not government investments that makes businesses grow and hire people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let me also note that the greatest threat that the world faces, the greatest national security threat is a nuclear Iran.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let’s talk about China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China has an interest that’s very much like ours in one respect, and that is they want a stable world.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want to see protectionism.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They don’t want to see the world break out into — into various forms of chaos, because they have to — they have to manufacture goods and put people to work and they have about 20,000 — 20 million, rather, people coming out of the farms every year coming into the cities, needing jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So they want the economy to work and the world to be free and open.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we can be a partner with China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We don’t have to be an adversary in any way, shape or form.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We can work with them, we can collaborate with them, if they’re willing to be responsible.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, they look at us and say, Is it a good idea to be with America?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How strong are we going to be?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: How strong is our economy?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at the fact that we owe ’em a trillion dollars and owe other people $16 trillion in total, including that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at our — our decision to — to cut back on our military capabilities.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: A trillion dollars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The secretary of defense called these trillion dollars of cuts to our military devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not my term, it’s the president’s own secretary of defense called these trillion dollars of cuts to our military devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s not my term, it’s the president’s own Secretary of Defense, called them devastating.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They look at America’s commitments around the world and they see what’s happening, and they say, well, OK.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Is America going to be strong?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the answer is, yes, if I’m president, America will be very strong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ll also make sure that we have trade relations with China that work for us.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve watched year in and year out as companies have shut down and people have lost their jobs because China has not played by the same rules, in part by holding down artificially the value of their currency.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It holds down the prices of their goods.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It means our goods aren’t as competitive and we lose jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s got to end.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re making some progress; they need to make more.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s why on day one, i will label them a currency manipulator, which allows us to apply tariffs where they’re taking jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’re stealing our intellectual property, our patents, our designs, our technology, hacking into our computers, counterfeiting our goods.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They have to understand we want to trade with them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We want a world that’s stable.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We like free enterprise, but you got to play by the rules.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Well, they sell us about this much stuff every year, and we sell them about this much stuff every year.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So it’s pretty clear who doesn’t want a trade war.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there’s one going on right now, which we don’t know about it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s a silent one.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And they’re winning.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have enormous trade imbalance with China, and it’s worse this year than last year, and it’s worse last year than the year before.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And so we have to understand that we can’t just surrender and lose jobs year in and year out.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have to say to our friend in China, look, you guys are playing aggressively.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We understand it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But this can’t keep on going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can’t keep on holding down the value of your currency, stealing our intellectual property, counterfeiting our products, selling them around the world, even to the United States.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was with one company that makes valves and — and process industries and they said, look, we were — we were having some valves coming in that — that were broken and we had to repair them under warranty and we looked them and — and they had our serial number on them.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And then we noticed that there was more than one with that same serial number.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They were counterfeit products being made overseas with the same serial number as a U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: company, the same packaging, these were being sold into our market and around the world as if they were made by the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This can’t go on.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want a great relationship with China.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: China can be our partner, but — but that doesn’t mean they can just roll all over us and steal our jobs on an unfair basis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Governor Romney’s right, you are familiar with jobs being shipped overseas because you invested in companies that were shipping jobs overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, you know, that’s — you’re right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean that’s how our free market works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I’ve made a different bet on American workers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we had taken your advice Governor Romney about our auto industry, we’d be buying cars from China instead of selling cars to China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we take your advice with respect to how we change our tax codes so that companies that earn profits overseas don’t pay U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: taxes compared to companies here that are paying taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now that’s estimated to create 800,000 jobs, the problem is they won’t be here, they’ll be in places like China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’re not making investments in education and basic research, which is not something that the private sector is doing at a sufficient pace right now and has never done, then we will lose the (inaudible) in things like clean energy technology.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now with respect to what we’ve done with China already, U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: exports have doubled since I came into office, to China and actually currencies are at their most advantageous point for U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: exporters since 1993.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We absolutely have to make more progress and that’s why we’re going to keep on pressing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to our military and Chinese security, part of the reason that we were able to pivot to the Asia-Pacific region after having ended the war in Iraq and transitioning out of Afghanistan, is precisely because this is going to be a massive growth area in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we believe China can be a partner, but we’re also sending a very clear signal that America is a Pacific power; that we are going to have a presence there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are working with countries in the region to make sure, for example, that ships can pass through; that commerce continues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re organizing trade relations with countries other than China so that China starts feeling more pressure about meeting basic international standards.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership we’ve shown in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the kind of leadership that we’ll continue to show.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I just want to take one of those points, again, attacking me as not talking about an agenda for — for getting more trade and opening up more jobs in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But the president mentioned the auto industry and that somehow I would be in favor of jobs being elsewhere.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Nothing could be further from the truth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m a son of Detroit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was born in Detroit.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My dad was head of a car company.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I like American cars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And I would do nothing to hurt the U.S.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: auto industry.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: My plan to get the industry on its feet when it was in real trouble was not to start writing checks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It was President Bush that wrote the first checks.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I disagree with that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I said they need — these companies need to go through a managed bankruptcy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And in that process, they can get government help and government guarantees, but they need to go through bankruptcy to get rid of excess cost and the debt burden that they’d — they’d built up.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And fortunately… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, that’s not what you said… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, you did not…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You can take a look at the op-ed… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You did not say that you would provide government help.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I said that we would provide guarantees, and — and that was what was able to allow these companies to go through bankruptcy, to come out of bankruptcy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Under no circumstances would I do anything other than to help this industry get on its feet.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And the idea that has been suggested that I would liquidate the industry, of course not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Of course not.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s check the record.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the height of silliness… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let — let — let’s… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I have never said I would liquidate… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …I would liquidate the industry.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor, the people in Detroit don’t forget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …and — and that’s why I have the kind of commitment to ensure that our industries in this country can compete and be successful.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We in this country can — can compete successfully with anyone in the world, and we’re going to.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re going to have to have a president, however, that doesn’t think that somehow the government investing in — in car companies like Tesla and — and Fisker, making electric battery cars.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is not research, Mr President, these are the government investing in companies.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Investing in Solyndra.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This is a company, this isn’t basic research.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I — I want to invest in research.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Research is great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Providing funding to universities and think tanks is great.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But investing in companies?

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Absolutely not.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: That’s the wrong way to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The fact of the matter is… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m still speaking.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: So I want to make sure that we make — we make America more competitive.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that we do those things that make America the most attractive place in the world for entrepreneurs, innovators, businesses to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But you’re investing in companies doesn’t do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: In fact it makes it less likely for them to come here…

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …because the private sector’s not going to invest in a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m — I’m — I’m happy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …company… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …to respond to you… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …if — if you’re… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …you’ve had the floor for a while.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …get someone else’s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — look, I think anybody out there can check the record.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Governor Romney, you keep on trying to, you know airbrush history here.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You were very clear that you would not provide, government assistance to the U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: auto companies, even if they went through bankruptcy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You said that they could get it in the private marketplace.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That wasn’t true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They would have gone through a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You’re wrong… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: …they would have gone through a… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: …you’re wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I am not wrong.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I am not wrong.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: People can look it up, you’re right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People will look it up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But more importantly it is true that in order for us to be competitive, we’re going to have to make some smart choices right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Cutting our education budget, that’s not a smart choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will not help us compete with China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Cutting our investments in research and technology, that’s not a smart choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will not help us compete with China.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bringing down our deficit by adding $7 trillion of tax cuts and military spending that our military is not asking for, before we even get to the debt that we currently have, that is not going to make us more competitive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those are the kinds of choices that the American people face right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Having a tax code that rewards companies that are shipping jobs overseas instead of companies that are investing here in the United States, that will not make us more competitive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the one thing that I’m absolutely clear about is that after a decade in which we saw drift, jobs being shipped overseas, nobody championing American workers and American businesses, we’ve now begun to make some real progress.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we can’t do is go back to the same policies that got us into such difficulty in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we have to move forward and not go back.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I couldn’t agree more about going forward, but I certainly don’t want to go back to the policies of the last four years.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The policies of the last four years have seen incomes in America decline every year for middle income families, now down $4,300 during your term.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Twenty-three million Americans still struggling to find a good job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you came to office 32 million people on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Today, 47 million people on food stamps.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: When you came to office, just over $10 trillion in debt, now $16 trillion in debt.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It hasn’t worked.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: You said by now we’d be at 5.4 percent unemployment.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’re 9 million jobs short of that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve met some of those people.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve met them in Appleton, Wisconsin.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I met a young woman in — in Philadelphia who’s coming out of — out of college, can’t find work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ve been — Ann was with someone just the other day that was just weeping about not being able to get work.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s just a tragedy in a nation so prosperous as ours, that the last four years have been so hard.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s why it’s so critical, that we make America once again the most attractive place in the world to start businesses, to build jobs, to grow the economy.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And that’s not going to happen by just hiring teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Look, I love to — I love teachers, and I’m happy to have states and communities that want to hire teachers do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: By the way, I don’t like to have the federal government start pushing its weight deeper and deeper into our schools.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Let the states and localities do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was a governor.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The federal government didn’t hire our teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: UNIDENTIFIED

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I love teachers.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: But I want to get our private sector growing and I know how to do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, thank you very much, Bob, Governor Romney, and to Lynn University.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve now heard three debates, months of campaigning and way too many TV commercials.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now you’ve got a choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last four years we’ve made real progress digging our way out of policies that gave us two prolonged wars, record deficits and the worst economic crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Governor Romney wants to take us back to those policies, a foreign policy that’s wrong and reckless, economic policies that won’t create jobs, won’t reduce our deficit, but will make sure that folks at the very top don’t have to play by the same rules that you do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve got a different vision for America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to build on our strengths.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ve put forward a plan to make sure that we’re bringing manufacturing jobs back to our shores by rewarding companies and small businesses that are investing here, not overseas.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to make sure we’ve got the best education system in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re retaining our workers for the jobs of tomorrow.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to control our own energy by developing oil and natural gas but also the energy sources of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Yes, I want to reduce our deficit by cutting spending that we don’t need but also by asking the wealthy to do a little bit more so that we can invest in things like research and technology that are the key to a 21st century economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: As Commander in Chief, I will maintain the strongest military in the world, keep faith with our troops and go after those who would do us harm.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: but after a decade of war, I think we all recognize we’ve got to do some nation building here at home, rebuilding our roads, our bridges and especially caring for our Veterans who sacrificed so much for our freedom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve been through tough times but we always bounce back because of our character, because we pull together and if I have the privilege of being your president for another four years, I promise you I will always listen to your voices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will fight for your families and I will work every single day to make sure that America continues to be the greatest nation on earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: President, folks at Lynn University, good to be with you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m optimistic about the future.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m excited about our prospects as a nation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to see peace.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to see growing peace in this country.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: It’s our objective.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We have an opportunity to have real leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America’s going to have that kind of leadership and continue to promote principles of peace to make a world a safer place and make people in this country more confident that their future is secure.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I also want to make sure that we get this economy going.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: And there are two very different paths the country can take.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: One is a path represented by the president, which at the end of four years would mean we’d have $20 trillion in debt heading towards Greece.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get us on track to a balanced budget.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path will mean continuing declining in take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure our take-home pay turns around and starts to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path will mean continuing declining in take-home pay.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I want to make sure take-home pay turns around and starts to grow.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: The president’s path means 20 million people out of work struggling for a good job.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll get people back to work with 12 million new jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m going to make sure that we get people off of food stamps, not by cutting the program, but by getting them good jobs.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: America’s going to come back, and for that to happen, we’re going to have to have a president who can work across the aisle.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I was in a state where my legislature was 87 percent Democrat.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I learned how to get along on the other side of the aisle.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve got to do that in Washington.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Washington is broken.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I know what it takes to get this country back, and will work with good Democrats and good Republicans to do that.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: This nation is the hope of the earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We’ve been blessed by having a nation that’s free and prosperous thanks to the contributions of the greatest generation.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: They’ve held a torch for the world to see — the torch of freedom and hope and opportunity.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Now, it’s our turn to take that torch.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’m convinced we’ll do it.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: We need strong leadership.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to be that leader with your support.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll work with you.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’ll lead you in an open and honest way, and I ask for your vote.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: I’d like to be the next president of the United States to support and help this great nation and to make sure that we all together remain America as the hope of the earth.

speaker: ROMNEY
sentence: Thank you so much.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Alan (ph), thank you very much for the question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to first, obviously, thank Belmont University, Tom, thank you, and to all of you who are participating tonight and those of you who sent e-mail questions in.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think everybody knows now we are in the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And a lot of you I think are worried about your jobs, your pensions, your retirement accounts, your ability to send your child or your grandchild to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I believe this is a final verdict on the failed economic policies of the last eight years, strongly promoted by President Bush and supported by Senator McCain, that essentially said that we should strip away regulations, consumer protections, let the market run wild, and prosperity would rain down on all of us.
