speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It hasn’t worked out that way.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so now we’ve got to take some decisive action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, step one was a rescue package that was passed last week.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to make sure that works properly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means strong oversight, making sure that investors, taxpayers are getting their money back and treated as investors.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means that we are cracking down on CEOs and making sure that they’re not getting bonuses or golden parachutes as a consequence of this package.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, in fact, we just found out that AIG, a company that got a bailout, just a week after they got help went on a $400,000 junket.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’ll tell you what, the Treasury should demand that money back and those executives should be fired.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But that’s only step one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The middle-class need a rescue package.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that means tax cuts for the middle-class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means help for homeowners so that they can stay in their homes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means that we are helping state and local governments set up road projects and bridge projects that keep people in their jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And then long-term we’ve got to fix our health care system, we’ve got to fix our energy system that is putting such an enormous burden on families.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You need somebody working for you and you’ve got to have somebody in Washington who is thinking about the middle class and not just those who can afford to hire lobbyists.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thank you, Belmont University.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And Senator Obama, it’s good to be with you at a town hall meeting.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, Alan (ph), thank you for your question.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You go to the heart of America’s worries tonight.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Americans are angry, they’re upset, and they’re a little fearful.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s our job to fix the problem.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, I have a plan to fix this problem and it has got to do with energy independence.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to stop sending $700 billion a year to countries that don’t want us very — like us very much.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to keep Americans’ taxes low.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: All Americans’ taxes low.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s not raise taxes on anybody today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We obviously have to stop this spending spree that’s going on in Washington.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Do you know that we’ve laid a $10 trillion debt on these young Americans who are here with us tonight, $500 billion of it we owe to China?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to have a package of reforms and it has got to lead to reform prosperity and peace in the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I think that this problem has become so severe, as you know, that we’re going to have to do something about home values.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know that home values of retirees continues to decline and people are no longer able to afford their mortgage payments.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: As president of the United States, Alan, I would order the secretary of the treasury to immediately buy up the bad home loan mortgages in America and renegotiate at the new value of those homes — at the diminished value of those homes and let people be able to make those — be able to make those payments and stay in their homes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Is it expensive?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But we all know, my friends, until we stabilize home values in America, we’re never going to start turning around and creating jobs and fixing our economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ve got to give some trust and confidence back to America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how the do that, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s my proposal, it’s not Senator Obama’s proposal, it’s not President Bush’s proposal.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I know how to get America working again, restore our economy and take care of working Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Not you, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, that’s a tough question and there’s a lot of qualified Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I think the first criteria, Tom, would have to be somebody who immediately Americans identify with, immediately say, we can trust that individual.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: A supporter of Senator Obama’s is Warren Buffett.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He has already weighed in and helped stabilize some of the difficulties in the markets and with companies and corporations, institutions today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I like Meg Whitman, she knows what it’s like to be out there in the marketplace.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She knows how to create jobs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Meg Whitman was CEO of a company that started with 12 people and is now 1.3 million people in America make their living off eBay.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Maybe somebody here has done a little business with them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the point is it’s going to have to be somebody who inspires trust and confidence.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because the problem in America today to a large extent, Tom, is that we don’t have trust and confidence in our institutions because of the corruption on Wall Street and the greed and excess and the cronyism in Washington, D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Warren would be a pretty good choice — Warren Buffett, and I’m pleased to have his support.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there are other folks out there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The key is making sure that the next treasury secretary understands that it’s not enough just to help those at the top.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Prosperity is not just going to trickle down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to help the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve — you know, Senator McCain and I have some fundamental disagreements on the economy, starting with Senator McCain’s statement earlier that he thought the fundamentals of the economy were sound.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Part of the problem here is that for many of you, wages and incomes have flat-lined.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For many of you, it is getting harder and harder to save, harder and harder to retire.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why, for example, on tax policy, what I want to do is provide a middle class tax cut to 95 percent of working Americans, those who are working two jobs, people who are not spending enough time with their kids, because they are struggling to make ends meet.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain is right that we’ve got to stabilize housing prices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But underlying that is loss of jobs and loss of income.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s something that the next treasury secretary is going to have to work on.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you, Oliver, and that’s an excellent question, because as you just described it, bailout, when I believe that it’s rescue, because — because of the greed and excess in Washington and Wall Street, Main Street was paying a very heavy price, and we know that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I left my campaign and suspended it to go back to Washington to make sure that there were additional protections for the taxpayer in the form of good oversight, in the form of taxpayers being the first to be paid back when our economy recovers — and it will recover — and a number of other measures.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But you know, one of the real catalysts, really the match that lit this fire was Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ll bet you, you may never even have heard of them before this crisis.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But you know, they’re the ones that, with the encouragement of Senator Obama and his cronies and his friends in Washington, that went out and made all these risky loans, gave them to people that could never afford to pay back.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And you know, there were some of us that stood up two years ago and said we’ve got to enact legislation to fix this.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to stop this greed and excess.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Meanwhile, the Democrats in the Senate and some — and some members of Congress defended what Fannie and Freddie were doing.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They resisted any change.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Meanwhile, they were getting all kinds of money in campaign contributions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama was the second highest recipient of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac money in history — in history.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So this rescue package means that we will stabilize markets, we will shore up these institutions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But it’s not enough.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s why we’re going to have to go out into the housing market and we’re going to have to buy up these bad loans and we’re going to have to stabilize home values, and that way, Americans, like Alan, can realize the American dream and stay in their home.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But Fannie and Freddie were the catalysts, the match that started this forest fire.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There were some of us — there were some of us that stood up against it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There were others who took a hike.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Oliver, first, let me tell you what’s in the rescue package for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now, the credit markets are frozen up and what that means, as a practical matter, is that small businesses and some large businesses just can’t get loans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If they can’t get a loan, that means that they can’t make payroll.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If they can’t make payroll, then they may end up having to shut their doors and lay people off.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if you imagine just one company trying to deal with that, now imagine a million companies all across the country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So it could end up having an adverse effect on everybody, and that’s why we had to take action.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we shouldn’t have been there in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I’ve got to correct a little bit of Senator McCain’s history, not surprisingly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s, first of all, understand that the biggest problem in this whole process was the deregulation of the financial system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain, as recently as March, bragged about the fact that he is a deregulator.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: On the other hand, two years ago, I said that we’ve got a sub-prime lending crisis that has to be dealt with.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I wrote to Secretary Paulson, I wrote to Federal Reserve Chairman Bernanke, and told them this is something we have to deal with, and nobody did anything about it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: A year ago, I went to Wall Street and said we’ve got to reregulate, and nothing happened.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Senator McCain during that period said that we should keep on deregulating because that’s how the free enterprise system works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, with respect to Fannie Mae, what Senator McCain didn’t mention is the fact that this bill that he talked about wasn’t his own bill.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He jumped on it a year after it had been introduced and it never got passed.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I never promoted Fannie Mae.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, Senator McCain’s campaign chairman’s firm was a lobbyist on behalf of Fannie Mae, not me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So — but, look, you’re not interested in hearing politicians pointing fingers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What you’re interested in is trying to figure out, how is this going to impact you?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is not the end of the process; this is the beginning of the process.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why it’s going to be so important for us to work with homeowners to make sure that they can stay in their homes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The secretary already has the power to do that in the rescue package, but it hasn’t been exercised yet.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the next president has to make sure that the next Treasury secretary is thinking about how to strengthen you as a home buyer, you as a homeowner, and not simply think about bailing out banks on Wall Street.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: No, I am confident about the American economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we are going to have to have some leadership from Washington that not only sets out much better regulations for the financial system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The problem is we still have a archaic, 20th-century regulatory system for 21st-century financial markets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have to coordinate with other countries to make sure that whatever actions we take work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But most importantly, we’re going to have to help ordinary families be able to stay in their homes, make sure that they can pay their bills, deal with critical issues like health care and energy, and we’re going to have to change the culture in Washington so that lobbyists and special interests aren’t driving the process and your voices aren’t being drowned out.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think it depends on what we do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think if we act effectively, if we stabilize the housing market — which I believe we can, if we go out and buy up these bad loans, so that people can have a new mortgage at the new value of their home — I think if we get rid of the cronyism and special interest influence in Washington so we can act more effectively.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friend, I’d like you to see the letter that a group of senators and I wrote warning exactly of this crisis.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama’s name was not on that letter.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The point is — the point is that we can fix our economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Americans’ workers are the best in the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re the fundamental aspect of America’s economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re the most innovative.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re the best — they’re most — have best — we’re the best exporters.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re the best importers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re most effective.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They are the best workers in the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ve got to give them a chance.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’ve got — we’ve got to give them a chance to do their best again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they are the innocent bystanders here in what is the biggest financial crisis and challenge of our time.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, look, I understand your frustration and your cynicism, because while you’ve been carrying out your responsibilities — most of the people here, you’ve got a family budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If less money is coming in, you end up making cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Maybe you don’t go out to dinner as much.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Maybe you put off buying a new car.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s not what happens in Washington.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you’re right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There is a lot of blame to go around.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think it’s important just to remember a little bit of history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When George Bush came into office, we had surpluses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now we have half-a-trillion-dollar deficit annually.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When George Bush came into office, our debt — national debt was around $5 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s now over $10 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve almost doubled it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so while it’s true that nobody’s completely innocent here, we have had over the last eight years the biggest increases in deficit spending and national debt in our history.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Senator McCain voted for four out of five of those George Bush budgets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what I would do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m going to spend some money on the key issues that we’ve got to work on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, you may have seen your health care premiums go up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to reform health care to help you and your budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are going to have to deal with energy because we can’t keep on borrowing from the Chinese and sending money to Saudi Arabia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are mortgaging our children’s future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to have a different energy plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to invest in college affordability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’re going to have to make some investments, but we’ve also got to make spending cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I’ve proposed, you’ll hear Senator McCain say, well, he’s proposing a whole bunch of new spending, but actually I’m cutting more than I’m spending so that it will be a net spending cut.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The key is whether or not we’ve got priorities that are working for you as opposed to those who have been dictating the policy in Washington lately, and that’s mostly lobbyists and special interests.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to put an end to that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, Theresa (ph), thank you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I can see why you feel that cynicism and mistrust, because the system in Washington is broken.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I have been a consistent reformer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have advocated and taken on the special interests, whether they be the big money people by reaching across the aisle and working with Senator Feingold on campaign finance reform, whether it being a variety of other issues, working with Senator Lieberman on trying to address climate change.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have a clear record of bipartisanship.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The situation today cries out for bipartisanship.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has never taken on his leaders of his party on a single issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we need to reform.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And so let’s look at our records as well as our rhetoric.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s really part of your mistrust here.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And now I suggest that maybe you go to some of these organizations that are the watchdogs of what we do, like the Citizens Against Government Waste or the National Taxpayers Union or these other organizations that watch us all the time.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t expect you to watch every vote.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And you know what you’ll find?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This is the most liberal big-spending record in the United States Senate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought against excessive spending and outrages.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought to reduce the earmarks and eliminate them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Do you know that Senator Obama has voted for — is proposing $860 billion of new spending now?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: New spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Do you know that he voted for every increase in spending that I saw come across the floor of the United States Senate while we were working to eliminate these pork barrel earmarks?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted for nearly a billion dollars in pork barrel earmark projects, including, by the way, $3 million for an overhead projector at a planetarium in Chicago, Illinois.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, do we need to spend that kind of money?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think you have to look at my record and you have to look at his.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Then you have to look at our proposals for our economy, not $860 billion in new spending, but for the kinds of reforms that keep people in their jobs, get middle-income Americans working again, and getting our economy moving again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You’re going to be examining our proposals tonight and in the future, and energy independence is a way to do that, is one of them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And drilling offshore and nuclear power are two vital elements of that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve been supporting those and I know how to fix this economy, and eliminate our dependence on foreign oil, and stop sending $700 billion a year overseas.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The three priorities were health…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think you can work on all three at once, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think it’s very important that we reform our entitlement programs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, we are not going to be able to provide the same benefit for present-day workers that we are going — that present-day retirees have today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re going to have to sit down across the table, Republican and Democrat, as we did in 1983 between Ronald Reagan and Tip O’Neill.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how to do that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have a clear record of reaching across the aisle, whether it be Joe Lieberman or Russ Feingold or Ted Kennedy or others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s my clear record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can work on nuclear power plants.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Build a whole bunch of them, create millions of new jobs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to have all of the above, alternative fuels, wind, tide, solar, natural gas, clean coal technology.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: All of these things we can do as Americans and we can take on this mission and we can overcome it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, some of this $700 billion ends up in the hands of terrorist organizations.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: As far as health care is concerned, obviously, everyone is struggling to make sure that they can afford their premiums and that they can have affordable and available health care.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s the next issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But we can do them all at once.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There’s no — and we have to do them all at once.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: All three you mentioned are compelling national security requirements.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have to prioritize, just like a family has to prioritize.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, I’ve listed the things that I think have to be at the top of the list.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Energy we have to deal with today, because you’re paying $3.80 here in Nashville for gasoline, and it could go up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it’s a strain on your family budget, but it’s also bad for our national security, because countries like Russia and Venezuela and, you know, in some cases, countries like Iran, are benefiting from higher oil prices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’ve got to deal with that right away.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why I’ve called for an investment of $15 billion a year over 10 years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Our goal should be, in 10 year’s time, we are free of dependence on Middle Eastern oil.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we can do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, when JFK said we’re going to the Moon in 10 years, nobody was sure how to do it, but we understood that, if the American people make a decision to do something, it gets done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So that would be priority number one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Health care is priority number two, because that broken health care system is bad not only for families, but it’s making our businesses less competitive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, number three, we’ve got to deal with education so that our young people are competitive in a global economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But just one point I want to make, Tom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain mentioned looking at our records.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We do need to look at our records.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain likes to talk about earmarks a lot.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s important.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to go line by line through every item in the federal budget and eliminate programs that don’t work and make sure that those that do work, work better and cheaper.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But understand this: We also have to look at where some of our tax revenues are going.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So when Senator McCain proposes a $300 billion tax cut, a continuation not only of the Bush tax cuts, but an additional $200 billion that he’s going to give to big corporations, including big oil companies, $4 billion worth, that’s money out of the system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so we’ve got to prioritize both our spending side and our tax policies to make sure that they’re working for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what I’m going to do as president of the United States.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, Fiora (ph), I’m going to ask the American people to understand that there are some programs that we may have to eliminate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I first proposed a long time ago that we would have to examine every agency and every bureaucracy of government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’re going to have to eliminate those that aren’t working.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know a lot of them that aren’t working.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: One of them is in defense spending, because I’ve taken on some of the defense contractors.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I saved the taxpayers $6.8 billion in a deal for an Air Force tanker that was done in a corrupt fashion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I believe that we have to eliminate the earmarks.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And sometimes those projects, not — not the overhead projector that Senator Obama asked for, but some of them that are really good projects, will have — will have to be eliminated, as well.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they’ll have to undergo the same scrutiny that all projects should in competition with others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So we’re going to have to tell the American people that spending is going to have to be cut in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I recommend a spending freeze that — except for defense, Veterans Affairs, and some other vital programs, we’ll just have to have across-the-board freeze.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And some of those programs may not grow as much as we would like for them to, but we can establish priorities with full transparency, with full knowledge of the American people, and full consultation, not done behind closed doors and shoving earmarks in the middle of the night into programs that we don’t even — sometimes we don’t even know about until months later.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, by the way, I want to go back a second.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, we can attack health care and energy at the same time.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re not — we’re not — we’re not rifle shots here.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We are Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can, with the participation of all Americans, work together and solve these problems together.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Frankly, I’m not going to tell that person without health insurance that, “I’m sorry, you’ll have to wait.” I’m going to tell you Americans we’ll get to work right away and we’ll get to work together, and we can get them all done, because that’s what America has been doing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, a lot of you remember the tragedy of 9/11 and where you were on that day and, you know, how all of the country was ready to come together and make enormous changes to make us not only safer, but to make us a better country and a more unified country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And President Bush did some smart things at the outset, but one of the opportunities that was missed was, when he spoke to the American people, he said, “Go out and shop.” That wasn’t the kind of call to service that I think the American people were looking for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so it’s important to understand that the — I think the American people are hungry for the kind of leadership that is going to tackle these problems not just in government, but outside of government.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And let’s take the example of energy, which we already spoke about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There is going to be the need for each and every one of us to start thinking about how we use energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe in the need for increased oil production.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have to explore new ways to get more oil, and that includes offshore drilling.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It includes telling the oil companies, that currently have 68 million acres that they’re not using, that either you use them or you lose them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have to develop clean coal technology and safe ways to store nuclear energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But each and every one of us can start thinking about how can we save energy in our homes, in our buildings.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one of the things I want to do is make sure that we’re providing incentives so that you can buy a fuel efficient car that’s made right here in the United States of America, not in Japan or South Korea, making sure that you are able to weatherize your home or make your business more fuel efficient.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s going to require effort from each and every one of us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the last point I just want to make.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think the young people of America are especially interested in how they can serve, and that’s one of the reasons why I’m interested in doubling the Peace Corps, making sure that we are creating a volunteer corps all across this country that can be involved in their community, involved in military service, so that military families and our troops are not the only ones bearing the burden of renewing America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s something that all of us have to be involved with and that requires some leadership from Washington.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it starts with Washington.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to show that we’ve got good habits, because if we’re running up trillion dollar debts that we’re passing on to the next generation, then a lot of people are going to think, “Well, you know what?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s easy money out there.” It means — and I have to, again, repeat this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It means looking (ph) at the spending side, but also at the revenue side.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, Senator McCain has been talking tough about earmarks, and that’s good, but earmarks account for about $18 billion of our budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, when Senator McCain is proposing tax cuts that would give the average Fortune 500 CEO an additional $700,000 in tax cuts, that’s not sharing a burden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so part of the problem, I think, for a lot of people who are listening here tonight is they don’t feel as if they are sharing the burden with other folks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, you know, it’s tough to ask a teacher who’s making $30,000 or $35,000 a year to tighten her belt when people who are making much more than her are living pretty high on the hog.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I think it’s important for the president to set a tone that says all of us are going to contribute, all of us are going to make sacrifices, and it means that, yes, we may have to cut some spending, although I disagree with Senator McCain about an across-the- board freeze.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s an example of an unfair burden sharing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s using a hatchet to cut the federal budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to use a scalpel so that people who need help are getting help and those of us, like myself and Senator McCain, who don’t need help, aren’t getting it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how we make sure that everybody is willing to make a few sacrifices.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, you know, nailing down Senator Obama’s various tax proposals is like nailing Jell-O to the wall.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There has been five or six of them and if you wait long enough, there will probably be another one.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But he wants to raise taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, the last president to raise taxes during tough economic times was Herbert Hoover, and he practiced protectionism as well, which I’m sure we’ll get to at some point.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, last year up to this time, we’ve lost 700,000 jobs in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The only bright spot is that over 300,000 jobs have been created by small businesses.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama’s secret that you don’t know is that his tax increases will increase taxes on 50 percent of small business revenue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Small businesses across America will have to cut jobs and will have their taxes increase and won’t be able to hire because of Senator Obama’s tax policies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, he said some time ago, he said he would forgo his tax increases if the economy was bad.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve got some news, Senator Obama, the news is bad.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So let’s not raise anybody’s taxes, my friends, and make it be very clear to you I am not in favor of tax cuts for the wealthy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I am in favor of leaving the tax rates alone and reducing the tax burden on middle-income Americans by doubling your tax exemption for every child from $3,500 to $7,000.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: To giving every American a $5,000 refundable tax credit and go out and get the health insurance you want rather than mandates and fines for small businesses, as Senator Obama’s plan calls for.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And let’s create jobs and let’s get our economy going again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And let’s not raise anybody’s taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Tom, can I respond to this briefly?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The tax issue, because I think it’s very important.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Tom, we’re going to have to take on entitlements and I think we’ve got to do it quickly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have a lot of work to do, so I can’t guarantee that we’re going to do it in the next two years, but I’d like to do in the my first term as president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think it’s important to understand, we’re not going to solve Social Security and Medicare unless we understand the rest of our tax policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you know, Senator McCain, I think the “Straight Talk Express” lost a wheel on that one.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So let’s be clear about my tax plan and Senator McCain’s, because we’re not going to be able to deal with entitlements unless we understand the revenues coming in.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to provide a tax cut for 95 percent of Americans, 95 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you make less than a quarter of a million dollars a year, you will not see a single dime of your taxes go up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you make $200,000 a year or less, your taxes will go down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain talks about small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Only a few percent of small businesses make more than $250,000 a year.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So the vast majority of small businesses would get a tax cut under my plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we provide a 50 percent tax credit so that they can buy health insurance for their workers, because there are an awful lot of small businesses that I meet across America that want to do right by their workers but they just can’t afford it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Some small business owners, a lot of them, can’t even afford health insurance for themselves.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, in contrast, Senator McCain wants to give a $300 billion tax cut, $200 billion of it to the largest corporations and a hundred thousand of it — a hundred billion of it going to people like CEOs on Wall Street.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He wants to give average Fortune 500 CEO an additional $700,000 in tax cuts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not fair.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it doesn’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, if we get our tax policies right so that they’re good for the middle class, if we reverse the policies of the last eight years that got us into this fix in the first place and that Senator McCain supported, then we are going to be in a position to deal with Social Security and deal with Medicare, because we will have a health care plan that actually works for you, reduces spending and costs over the long term, and Social Security that is stable and solvent for all Americans and not just some.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Hey, I’ll answer the question.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look — look, it’s not that hard to fix Social Security, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: … tough decisions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to get to Medicare in a second.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Social Security is not that tough.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We know what the problems are, my friends, and we know what the fixes are.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to sit down together across the table.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s been done before.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I saw it done with our — our wonderful Ronald Reagan, a conservative from California, and the liberal Democrat Tip O’Neill from Massachusetts.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what we need more of, and that’s what I’ve done in Washington.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has never taken on his party leaders on a single major issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve taken them on.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m not too popular sometimes with my own party, much less his.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So Medicare, it’s going to be a little tougher.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s going to be a little tougher because we’re talking about very complex and difficult issues.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, what we have to do with Medicare is have a commission, have the smartest people in America come together, come up with recommendations, and then, like the base-closing commission idea we had, then we should have Congress vote up or down.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s not let them fool with it anymore.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There’s too much special interests and too many lobbyists working there.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So let’s have — and let’s have the American people say, “Fix it for us.” Now, just back on this — on this tax, you know, again, it’s back to our first question here about rhetoric and record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has voted 94 times to either increase your taxes or against tax cuts.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s his record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: When he ran for the United States Senate from Illinois, he said he would have a middle-income tax cut.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know he came to the Senate and never once proposed legislation to do that?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So let’s look at our record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve fought higher taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought excess spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought to reform government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s look at our records, my friends, and then listen to my vision for the future of America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ll get our economy going again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And our best days are ahead of us.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, we are in tough economic times; we all know that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And let’s keep — never forget the struggle that Americans are in today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But when we can — when we have an issue that we may hand our children and our grandchildren a damaged planet, I have disagreed strongly with the Bush administration on this issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I traveled all over the world looking at the effects of greenhouse gas emissions, Joe Lieberman and I.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I introduced the first legislation, and we forced votes on it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s the good news, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The bad news is we lost.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But we kept the debate going, and we kept this issue to — to posing to Americans the danger that climate change opposes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, how — what’s — what’s the best way of fixing it?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Nuclear power.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama says that it has to be safe or disposable or something like that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, I — I was on Navy ships that had nuclear power plants.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Nuclear power is safe, and it’s clean, and it creates hundreds of thousands of jobs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And — and I know that we can reprocess the spent nuclear fuel.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The Japanese, the British, the French do it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we can do it, too.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has opposed that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can move forward, and clean up our climate, and develop green technologies, and alternate — alternative energies for — for hybrid, for hydrogen, for battery-powered cars, so that we can clean up our environment and at the same time get our economy going by creating millions of jobs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can do that, we as Americans, because we’re the best innovators, we’re the best producers, and 95 percent of the people who are our market live outside of the United States of America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is one of the biggest challenges of our times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it is absolutely critical that we understand this is not just a challenge, it’s an opportunity, because if we create a new energy economy, we can create five million new jobs, easily, here in the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It can be an engine that drives us into the future the same way the computer was the engine for economic growth over the last couple of decades.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we can do it, but we’re going to have to make an investment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The same way the computer was originally invented by a bunch of government scientists who were trying to figure out, for defense purposes, how to communicate, we’ve got to understand that this is a national security issue, as well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why we’ve got to make some investments and I’ve called for investments in solar, wind, geothermal.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Contrary to what Senator McCain keeps on saying, I favor nuclear power as one component of our overall energy mix.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But this is another example where I think it is important to look at the record.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain and I actually agree on something.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He said a while back that the big problem with energy is that for 30 years, politicians in Washington haven’t done anything.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What Senator McCain doesn’t mention is he’s been there 26 of them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And during that time, he voted 23 times against alternative fuels, 23 times.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So it’s easy to talk about this stuff during a campaign, but it’s important for us to understand that it requires a sustained effort from the next president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: One last point I want to make on energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain talks a lot about drilling, and that’s important, but we have three percent of the world’s oil reserves and we use 25 percent of the world’s oil.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what that means is that we can’t simply drill our way out of the problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re not going to be able to deal with the climate crisis if our only solution is to use more fossil fuels that create global warming.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to have to come up with alternatives, and that means that the United States government is working with the private sector to fund the kind of innovation that we can then export to countries like China that also need energy and are setting up one coal power plant a week.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to make sure that we’re giving them the energy that they need or helping them to create the energy that they need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m just trying to keep up with John.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Tom, wave like that and I’ll look at you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think pure research and development investment on the part of the United States government is certainly appropriate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think once it gets into productive stages, that we ought to, obviously, turn it over to the private sector.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: By the way, my friends, I know you grow a little weary with this back-and-forth.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It was an energy bill on the floor of the Senate loaded down with goodies, billions for the oil companies, and it was sponsored by Bush and Cheney.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know who voted for it?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You might never know.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know who voted against it?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought time after time against these pork barrel — these bills that come to the floor and they have all kinds of goodies and all kinds of things in them for everybody and they buy off the votes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I vote against them, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I vote against them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the point is, also, on oil drilling, oil drilling offshore now is vital so that we can bridge the gap.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can bridge the gap between imported oil, which is a national security issue, as well as any other, and it will reduce the price of a barrel of oil, because when people know there’s a greater supply, then the cost of that will go down.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s fundamental economics.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to drill offshore, my friends, and we’ve got to do it now, and we can do it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And as far as nuclear power is concerned, again, look at the record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has approved storage and reprocessing of spent nuclear fuel.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ll stop, Tom, and you didn’t even wave.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, you know, as I travel around the country, this is one of the single most frequently asked issues that I get, is the issue of health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is breaking family budgets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I can’t tell you how many people I meet who don’t have health insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’ve got health insurance, most of you have seen your premiums double over the last eight years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And your co-payments and deductibles have gone up 30 percent just in the last year alone.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’re a small business, it’s a crushing burden.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So one of the things that I have said from the start of this campaign is that we have a moral commitment as well as an economic imperative to do something about the health care crisis that so many families are facing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what I would do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’ve got health care already, and probably the majority of you do, then you can keep your plan if you are satisfied with it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You can keep your choice of doctor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to work with your employer to lower the cost of your premiums by up to $2,500 a year.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re going to do it by investing in prevention.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to do it by making sure that we use information technology so that medical records are actually on computers instead of you filling forms out in triplicate when you go to the hospital.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will reduce medical errors and reduce costs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you don’t have health insurance, you’re going to be able to buy the same kind of insurance that Senator McCain and I enjoy as federal employees.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because there’s a huge pool, we can drop the costs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And nobody will be excluded for pre-existing conditions, which is a huge problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain has a different kind of approach.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He says that he’s going to give you a $5,000 tax credit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What he doesn’t tell you is that he is going to tax your employer-based health care benefits for the first time ever.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what one hand giveth, the other hand taketh away.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He would also strip away the ability of states to provide some of the regulations on insurance companies to make sure you’re not excluded for pre-existing conditions or your mammograms are covered or your maternity is covered.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is fundamentally the wrong way to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, just today business organizations like the United States Chamber of Commerce, which generally are pretty supportive of Republicans, said that this would lead to the unraveling of the employer-based health care system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That, I don’t think, is the kind of change that we need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to have somebody who is fighting for patients and making sure that you get decent, affordable health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s something that I’m committed to doing as president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you for the question.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You really identified one of the really major challenges that America faces.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Co-payments go up, costs go up, skyrocketing costs, which make people less and less able to afford health insurance in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we need to do all of the things that are necessary to make it more efficient.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s put health records online, that will reduce medical errors, as they call them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s have community health centers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s have walk-in clinics.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s do a lot of things to impose efficiencies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But what is at stake here in this health care issue is the fundamental difference between myself and Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: As you notice, he starts talking about government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He starts saying, government will do this and government will do that, and then government will, and he’ll impose mandates.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you’re a small business person and you don’t insure your employees, Senator Obama will fine you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Will fine you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s remarkable.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you’re a parent and you’re struggling to get health insurance for your children, Senator Obama will fine you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to give every American a $5,000 refundable tax credit.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They can take it anywhere, across state lines.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Don’t we go across state lines when we purchase other things in America?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course it’s OK to go across state lines because in Arizona they may offer a better plan that suits you best than it does here in Tennessee.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And if you do the math, those people who have employer-based health benefits, if you put the tax on it and you have what’s left over and you add $5,000 that you’re going to get as a refundable tax credit, do the math, 95 percent of the American people will have increased funds to go out and buy the insurance of their choice and to shop around and to get — all of those people will be covered except for those who have these gold-plated Cadillac kinds of policies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, like hair transplants, I might need one of those myself.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the point is that we have got to give people choice in America and not mandate things on them and give them the ability.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Every parent I know would acquire health insurance for their children if they could.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obviously small business people want to give their employees health insurance.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course they all want to do that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to give them the wherewithal to do it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can do it by giving them, as a start, a $5,000 refundable tax credit to go around and get the health insurance policy of their choice.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think it’s a responsibility, in this respect, in that we should have available and affordable health care to every American citizen, to every family member.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And with the plan that — that I have, that will do that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But government mandates I — I’m always a little nervous about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But it is certainly my responsibility.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It is certainly small-business people and others, and they understand that responsibility.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: American citizens understand that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Employers understand that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But they certainly are a little nervous when Senator Obama says, if you don’t get the health care policy that I think you should have, then you’re going to get fined.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, by the way, Senator Obama has never mentioned how much that fine might be.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Perhaps we might find that out tonight.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, why don’t — why don’t — let’s talk about this, Tom, because there was just a lot of stuff out there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it should be a right for every American.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In a country as wealthy as ours, for us to have people who are going bankrupt because they can’t pay their medical bills — for my mother to die of cancer at the age of 53 and have to spend the last months of her life in the hospital room arguing with insurance companies because they’re saying that this may be a pre-existing condition and they don’t have to pay her treatment, there’s something fundamentally wrong about that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So let me — let me just talk about this fundamental difference.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, Tom, I know that we’re under time constraints, but Senator McCain through a lot of stuff out there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, let me just repeat, if you’ve got a health care plan that you like, you can keep it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All I’m going to do is help you to lower the premiums on it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ll still have choice of doctor.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s no mandate involved.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Small businesses are not going to have a mandate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we’re going to give you is a 50 percent tax credit to help provide health care for those that you need.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it’s true that I say that you are going to have to make sure that your child has health care, because children are relatively cheap to insure and we don’t want them going to the emergency room for treatable illnesses like asthma.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when Senator McCain says that he wants to provide children health care, what he doesn’t mention is he voted against the expansion of the Children’s Health Insurance Program that is responsible for making sure that so many children who didn’t have previously health insurance have it now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the final point I’ll make on this whole issue of government intrusion and mandates — it is absolutely true that I think it is important for government to crack down on insurance companies that are cheating their customers, that don’t give you the fine print, so you end up thinking that you’re paying for something and, when you finally get sick and you need it, you’re not getting it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason that it’s a problem to go shopping state by state, you know what insurance companies will do?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They will find a state — maybe Arizona, maybe another state — where there are no requirements for you to get cancer screenings, where there are no requirements for you to have to get pre-existing conditions, and they will all set up shop there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s how in banking it works.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Everybody goes to Delaware, because they’ve got very — pretty loose laws when it comes to things like credit cards.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And in that situation, what happens is, is that the protections you have, the consumer protections that you need, you’re not going to have available to you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is a fundamental difference that I have with Senator McCain.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He believes in deregulation in every circumstance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what we’ve been going through for the last eight years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It hasn’t worked, and we need fundamental change.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t believe that — did we hear the size of the fine?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, I thank you for that question, because there’s no doubt that history shows us that nations that are strong militarily over time have to have a strong economy, as well.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that is one of the challenges that America faces.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But having said that, America — and we’ll hear a lot of criticism.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve heard a lot of criticism about America, and our national security policy, and all that, and much of that criticism is justified.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the fact is, America is the greatest force for good in the history of the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, we have gone to all four corners of the Earth and shed American blood in defense, usually, of somebody else’s freedom and our own.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So we are peacemakers and we’re peacekeepers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the challenge is to know when the United States of American can beneficially effect the outcome of a crisis, when to go in and when not, when American military power is worth the expenditure of our most precious treasure.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that question can only be answered by someone with the knowledge and experience and the judgment, the judgment to know when our national security is not only at risk, but where the United States of America can make a difference in preventing genocide, in preventing the spread of terrorism, in doing the things that the United States has done, not always well, but we’ve done because we’re a nation of good.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I am convinced that my record, going back to my opposition from sending the Marines to Lebanon, to supporting our efforts in Kosovo and Bosnia and the first Gulf War, and my judgment, I think, is something that I’m — a record that I’m willing to stand on.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama was wrong about Iraq and the surge.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He was wrong about Russia when they committed aggression against Georgia.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And in his short career, he does not understand our national security challenges.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We don’t have time for on-the-job training, my friends.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, you know, Senator McCain, in the last debate and today, again, suggested that I don’t understand.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are some things I don’t understand.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I don’t understand how we ended up invading a country that had nothing to do with 9/11, while Osama Bin Laden and Al Qaeda are setting up base camps and safe havens to train terrorists to attack us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That was Senator McCain’s judgment and it was the wrong judgment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When Senator McCain was cheerleading the president to go into Iraq, he suggested it was going to be quick and easy, we’d be greeted as liberators.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That was the wrong judgment, and it’s been costly to us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So one of the difficulties with Iraq is that it has put an enormous strain, first of all, on our troops, obviously, and they have performed heroically and honorably and we owe them an extraordinary debt of gratitude.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it’s also put an enormous strain on our budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve spent, so far, close to $700 billion and if we continue on the path that we’re on, as Senator McCain is suggesting, it’s going to go well over $1 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re spending $10 billion a month in Iraq at a time when the Iraqis have a $79 billion surplus, $79 billion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we need that $10 billion a month here in the United States to put people back to work, to do all these wonderful things that Senator McCain suggested we should be doing, but has not yet explained how he would pay for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain and I do agree, this is the greatest nation on earth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are a force of good in the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there has never been a nation in the history of the world that saw its economy decline and maintained its military superiority.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the strains that have been placed on our alliances around the world and the respect that’s been diminished over the last eight years has constrained us being able to act on something like the genocide in Darfur, because we don’t have the resources or the allies to do everything that we should be doing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s going to change when I’m president, but we can’t change it unless we fundamentally change Senator McCain’s and George Bush’s foreign policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It has not worked for America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, we may not always have national security issues at stake, but we have moral issues at stake.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we could have intervened effectively in the Holocaust, who among us would say that we had a moral obligation not to go in?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we could’ve stopped Rwanda, surely, if we had the ability, that would be something that we would have to strongly consider and act.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So when genocide is happening, when ethnic cleansing is happening somewhere around the world and we stand idly by, that diminishes us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so I do believe that we have to consider it as part of our interests, our national interests, in intervening where possible.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But understand that there’s a lot of cruelty around the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re not going to be able to be everywhere all the time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why it’s so important for us to be able to work in concert with our allies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let’s take the example of Darfur just for a moment.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now there’s a peacekeeping force that has been set up and we have African Union troops in Darfur to stop a genocide that has killed hundreds of thousands of people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We could be providing logistical support, setting up a no-fly zone at relatively little cost to us, but we can only do it if we can help mobilize the international community and lead.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what I intend to do when I’m president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, let me just follow up, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If we had done what Senator Obama wanted done in Iraq, and that was set a date for withdrawal, which General Petraeus, our chief — chairman of our Joint Chiefs of Staff said would be a very dangerous course to take for America, then we would have had a wider war, we would have been back, Iranian influence would have increased, al Qaeda would have re- established a base.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There was a lot at stake there, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I can tell you right now that Senator Obama would have brought our troops home in defeat.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ll bring them home with victory and with honor and that is a fundamental difference.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The United States of America, Tom, is the greatest force for good, as I said.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we must do whatever we can to prevent genocide, whatever we can to prevent these terrible calamities that we have said never again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But it also has to be tempered with our ability to beneficially affect the situation.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That requires a cool hand at the tiller.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This requires a person who understands what our — the limits of our capability are.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We went in to Somalia as a peacemaking organization, we ended up trying to be — excuse me, as a peacekeeping organization, we ended up trying to be peacemakers and we ended up having to withdraw in humiliation.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: In Lebanon, I stood up to President Reagan, my hero, and said, if we send Marines in there, how can we possibly beneficially affect this situation?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And said we shouldn’t.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Unfortunately, almost 300 brave young Marines were killed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So you have to temper your decisions with the ability to beneficially affect the situation and realize you’re sending America’s most precious asset, American blood, into harm’s way.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, again, I know those situations.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve been in them all my life.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I can tell you right now the security of your young men and women who are serving in the military are my first priority right after our nation’s security.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I may have to make those tough decisions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I won’t take them lightly.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I understand that we have to say never again to a Holocaust and never again to Rwanda.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But we had also better be darn sure we don’t leave and make the situation worse, thereby exacerbating our reputation and our ability to address crises in other parts of the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Katie, it’s a terrific question and we have a difficult situation in Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe that part of the reason we have a difficult situation is because we made a bad judgment going into Iraq in the first place when we hadn’t finished the job of hunting down bin Laden and crushing al Qaeda.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what happened was we got distracted, we diverted resources, and ultimately bin Laden escaped, set up base camps in the mountains of Pakistan in the northwest provinces there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are now raiding our troops in Afghanistan, destabilizing the situation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’re stronger now than at any time since 2001.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I think it’s so important for us to reverse course, because that’s the central front on terrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They are plotting to kill Americans right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: As Secretary Gates, the defense secretary, said, the war against terrorism began in that region and that’s where it will end.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So part of the reason I think it’s so important for us to end the war in Iraq is to be able to get more troops into Afghanistan, put more pressure on the Afghan government to do what it needs to do, eliminate some of the drug trafficking that’s funding terrorism.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I do believe that we have to change our policies with Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can’t coddle, as we did, a dictator, give him billions of dollars and then he’s making peace treaties with the Taliban and militants.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I’ve said is we’re going to encourage democracy in Pakistan, expand our nonmilitary aid to Pakistan so that they have more of a stake in working with us, but insisting that they go after these militants.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we have Osama bin Laden in our sights and the Pakistani government is unable or unwilling to take them out, then I think that we have to act and we will take them out.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We will kill bin Laden; we will crush Al Qaida.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That has to be our biggest national security priority.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, Katie (ph), thank you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, my hero is a guy named Teddy Roosevelt.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Teddy Roosevelt used to say walk softly — talk softly, but carry a big stick.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama likes to talk loudly.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: In fact, he said he wants to announce that he’s going to attack Pakistan.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, if you are a country and you’re trying to gain the support of another country, then you want to do everything you can that they would act in a cooperative fashion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: When you announce that you’re going to launch an attack into another country, it’s pretty obvious that you have the effect that it had in Pakistan: It turns public opinion against us.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, let me just go back with you very briefly.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We drove the Russians out with — the Afghan freedom fighters drove the Russians out of Afghanistan, and then we made a most serious mistake.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We washed our hands of Afghanistan.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The Taliban came back in, Al Qaida, we then had the situation that required us to conduct the Afghan war.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, our relations with Pakistan are critical, because the border areas are being used as safe havens by the Taliban and Al Qaida and other extremist organizations, and we have to get their support.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, General Petraeus had a strategy, the same strategy — very, very different, because of the conditions and the situation — but the same fundamental strategy that succeeded in Iraq.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that is to get the support of the people.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to help the Pakistani government go into Waziristan, where I visited, a very rough country, and — and get the support of the people, and get them to work with us and turn against the cruel Taliban and others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And by working and coordinating our efforts together, not threatening to attack them, but working with them, and where necessary use force, but talk softly, but carry a big stick.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Tom, just a…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … just a quick follow-up on this.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If we’re going to have follow-ups, then I will want follow-ups, as well.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’d be fine with me.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’d be fine with me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Then let’s have one.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’d be fine with me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Just — just — just a quick follow-up, because I think — I think this is important.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’re doing a great job, Tom.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, I — I want to be very clear about what I said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Nobody called for the invasion of Pakistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain continues to repeat this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I said was the same thing that the audience here today heard me say, which is, if Pakistan is unable or unwilling to hunt down bin Laden and take him out, then we should.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, that I think has to be our policy, because they are threatening to kill more Americans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain suggests that somehow, you know, I’m green behind the ears and, you know, I’m just spouting off, and he’s somber and responsible.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thank you very much.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain, this is the guy who sang, “Bomb, bomb, bomb Iran,” who called for the annihilation of North Korea.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That I don’t think is an example of “speaking softly.” This is the person who, after we had — we hadn’t even finished Afghanistan, where he said, “Next up, Baghdad.” So I agree that we have to speak responsibly and we have to act responsibly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the reason Pakistan — the popular opinion of America had diminished in Pakistan was because we were supporting a dictator, Musharraf, had given him $10 billion over seven years, and he had suspended civil liberties.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We were not promoting democracy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is the kind of policies that ultimately end up undermining our ability to fight the war on terrorism, and it will change when I’m president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, Tom, if — if we’re going to go back and forth, I then — I’d like to have equal time to go — to respond to…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: … to — to — to…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have, obviously, supported those efforts that the United States had to go in militarily and I have opposed that I didn’t think so.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I understand what it’s like to send young American’s in harm’s way.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I say — I was joking with a veteran — I hate to even go into this.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I was joking with an old veteran friend, who joked with me, about Iran.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the point is that I know how to handle these crises.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And Senator Obama, by saying that he would attack Pakistan, look at the context of his words.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ll get Osama bin Laden, my friends.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ll get him.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how to get him.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ll get him no matter what and I know how to do it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I’m not going to telegraph my punches, which is what Senator Obama did.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’m going to act responsibly, as I have acted responsibly throughout my military career and throughout my career in the United States Senate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we have fundamental disagreements about the use of military power and how you do it, and you just saw it in response to previous questions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ll be very brief.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are going to have to make the Iraqi government start taking more responsibility, withdraw our troops in a responsible way over time, because we’re going to have to put some additional troops in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: General McKiernan, the commander in Afghanistan right now, is desperate for more help, because our bases and outposts are now targets for more aggressive Afghan — Taliban offenses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re also going to have to work with the Karzai government, and when I met with President Karzai, I was very clear that, “You are going to have to do better by your people in order for us to gain the popular support that’s necessary.” I don’t think he has to be a dictator.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we want a democracy in Afghanistan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we have to have a government that is responsive to the Afghan people, and, frankly, it’s just not responsive right now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: General Petraeus has just taken over a position of responsibility, where he has the command and will really set the tone for the strategy and tactics that are used.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve had conversations with him.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It is the same overall strategy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we have to do some things tactically, some of which Senator Obama is correct on.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to double the size of the Afghan army.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to have a streamlined NATO command structure.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to do a lot of things.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to work much more closely with the Pakistanis.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But most importantly, we have to have the same strategy, which Senator Obama said wouldn’t work, couldn’t work, still fails to admit that he was wrong about Iraq.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He still will not admit that he was wrong about the strategy of the surge in Iraq, and that’s the same kind of strategy of go out and secure and hold and allow people to live normal lives.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And once they feel secure, then they lead normal, social, economic, political lives, the same thing that’s happening in Iraq today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I have confidence that General Petraeus, working with the Pakistanis, working with the Afghans, doing the same job that he did in Iraq, will again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We will succeed and we will bring our troops home with honor and victory and not in defeat.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: First of all, as I say, I don’t think that — we’re not going to have another Cold War with Russia.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But have no doubt that Russia’s behavior is certainly outside the norms of behavior that we would expect for nations which are very wealthy, as Russia has become, because of their petro dollars.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, long ago, I warned about Vladimir Putin.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I said I looked into his eyes and saw three letters, a K, a G and a B.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He has surrounded himself with former KGB apparatchiks.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He has gradually repressed most of the liberties that we would expect for nations to observe, and he has exhibited most aggressive behavior, obviously, in Georgia.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I said before, watch Ukraine.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Ukraine, right now, is in the sights of Vladimir Putin, those that want to reassemble the old Soviet Union.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to show moral support for Georgia.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to show moral support for Ukraine.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to advocate for their membership in NATO.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to make the Russians understand that there are penalties for these this kind of behavior, this kind of naked aggression into Georgia, a tiny country and a tiny democracy.

speaker: MCCAIN
