sentence: And so, of course we want to bring international pressures to bear on Russia in hopes that that will modify and eventually change their behavior.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, the G-8 is one of those, but there are many others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the Russians must understand that these kinds of actions and activities are not acceptable and hopefully we will use the leverage, economic, diplomatic and others united with our allies, with our allies and friends in Europe who are equally disturbed as we are about their recent behaviors.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It will not be a re-ignition of the Cold War, but Russia is a challenge.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, the resurgence of Russia is one of the central issues that we’re going to have to deal with in the next presidency.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And for the most part I agree with Senator McCain on many of the steps that have to be taken.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we can’t just provide moral support.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to provide moral support to the Poles and Estonia and Latvia and all of the nations that were former Soviet satellites.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’ve also got to provide them with financial and concrete assistance to help rebuild their economies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Georgia in particular is now on the brink of enormous economic challenges.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And some say that that’s what Putin intended in the first place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The other thing we have to do, though, is we’ve got to see around the corners.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to anticipate some of these problems ahead of time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, back in April, I put out a statement saying that the situation in Georgia was unsustainable because you had Russian peacekeepers in these territories that were under dispute.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you knew that if the Russians themselves were trying to obtain some of these territories or push back against Georgia, that that was not a stable situation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So part of the job of the next commander-in-chief, in keeping all of you safe, is making sure that we can see some of the 21st Century challenges and anticipate them before they happen.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We haven’t been doing enough of that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We tend to be reactive.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what we’ve been doing over the last eight years and that has actually made us more safe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s part of what happened in Afghanistan, where we rushed into Iraq and Senator McCain and President Bush suggested that it wasn’t that important to catch bin Laden right now and that we could muddle through, and that has cost us dearly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to be much more strategic if we’re going to be able to deal with all of the challenges that we face out there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And one last point I want to make about Russia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Energy is going to be key in dealing with Russia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we can reduce our energy consumption, that reduces the amount of petro dollars that they have to make mischief around the world.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That will strengthen us and weaken them when it comes to issues like Georgia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think they’ve engaged in an evil behavior and I think that it is important that we understand they’re not the old Soviet Union but they still have nationalist impulses that I think are very dangerous.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Depends on how we respond to Russia and it depends on a lot of things.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If I say yes, then that means that we’re reigniting the old Cold War.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If I say no, it ignores their behavior.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obviously energy is going to be a big, big factor.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And Georgia and Ukraine are both major gateways of energy into Europe.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s one of the reasons why it’s in our interest.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the Russians, I think we can deal with them but they’ve got to understand that they’re facing a very firm and determined United States of America that will defend our interests and that of other countries in the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you, Terry (ph).

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And thank you for your service to the country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to say, everything I ever learned about leadership I learned from a chief petty officer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I thank you, and I thank you, my friend.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thanks for serving.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let — let — let me say that we obviously would not wait for the United Nations Security Council.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think the realities are that both Russia and China would probably pose significant obstacles.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And our challenge right now is the Iranians continue on the path to acquiring nuclear weapons, and it’s a great threat.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s not just a threat — threat to the state of Israel.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s a threat to the stability of the entire Middle East.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If Iran acquires nuclear weapons, all the other countries will acquire them, too.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The tensions will be ratcheted up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: What would you do if you were the Israelis and the president of a country says that they are — they are determined to wipe you off the map, calls your country a stinking corpse?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, Senator Obama without precondition wants to sit down and negotiate with them, without preconditions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what he stated, again, a matter of record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to make sure that the Iranians are put enough — that we put enough pressure on the Iranians by joining with our allies, imposing significant, tough sanctions to modify their behavior.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I think we can do that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think, joining with our allies and friends in a league of democracies, that we can effectively abridge their behavior, and hopefully they would abandon this quest that they are on for nuclear weapons.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But, at the end of the day, my friend, I have to tell you again, and you know what it’s like to serve, and you know what it’s like to sacrifice, but we can never allow a second Holocaust to take place.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Terry, first of all, we honor your service, and we’re grateful for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We cannot allow Iran to get a nuclear weapon.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It would be a game-changer in the region.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not only would it threaten Israel, our strongest ally in the region and one of our strongest allies in the world, but it would also create a possibility of nuclear weapons falling into the hands of terrorists.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And so it’s unacceptable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I will do everything that’s required to prevent it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we will never take military options off the table.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it is important that we don’t provide veto power to the United Nations or anyone else in acting in our interests.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is important, though, for us to use all the tools at our disposal to prevent the scenario where we’ve got to make those kinds of choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I have consistently said that, if we can work more effectively with other countries diplomatically to tighten sanctions on Iran, if we can reduce our energy consumption through alternative energy, so that Iran has less money, if we can impose the kinds of sanctions that, say, for example, Iran right now imports gasoline, even though it’s an oil-producer, because its oil infrastructure has broken down, if we can prevent them from importing the gasoline that they need and the refined petroleum products, that starts changing their cost-benefit analysis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That starts putting the squeeze on them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it is true, though, that I believe that we should have direct talks — not just with our friends, but also with our enemies — to deliver a tough, direct message to Iran that, if you don’t change your behavior, then there will be dire consequences.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you do change your behavior, then it is possible for you to re-join the community of nations.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it may not work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But one of the things we’ve learned is, is that when we take that approach, whether it’s in North Korea or in Iran, then we have a better chance at better outcomes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When President Bush decided we’re not going to talk to Iran, we’re not going to talk to North Korea, you know what happened?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Iran went from zero centrifuges to develop nuclear weapons to 4,000.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: North Korea quadrupled its nuclear capability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to try to have talks, understanding that we’re not taking military options off the table.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: My wife, Michelle, is there and she could give you a much longer list than I do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And most of the time, I learn it by asking her.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But, look, the nature of the challenges that we’re going to face are immense and one of the things that we know about the presidency is that it’s never the challenges that you expect.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s the challenges that you don’t that end up consuming most of your time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But here’s what I do know.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I know that I wouldn’t be standing here if it weren’t for the fact that this country gave me opportunity.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I came from very modest means.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I had a single mom and my grandparents raised me and it was because of the help of scholarships and my grandmother scrimping on things that she might have wanted to purchase and my mom, at one point, getting food stamps in order for us to put food on the table.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Despite all that, I was able to go to the best schools on earth and I was able to succeed in a way that I could not have succeeded anywhere else in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The same is true for Michelle and I’m sure the same is true for a lot of you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the question in this election is: are we going to pass on that same American dream to the next generation?

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Over the last eight years, we’ve seen that dream diminish.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Wages and incomes have gone down.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: People have lost their health care or are going bankrupt because they get sick.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got young people who have got the grades and the will and the drive to go to college, but they just don’t have the money.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we can’t expect that if we do the same things that we’ve been doing over the last eight years, that somehow we are going to have a different outcome.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need fundamental change.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what’s at stake in this election.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the reason I decided to run for president, and I’m hopeful that all of you are prepared to continue this extraordinary journey that we call America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’re going to have to have the courage and the sacrifice, the nerve to move in a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I think what I don’t know is what all of us don’t know, and that’s what’s going to happen both here at home and abroad.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The challenges that we face are unprecedented.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Americans are hurting tonight in a way they have not in our generation.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There are challenges around the world that are new and different and there will be different — we will be talking about countries sometime in the future that we hardly know where they are on the map, some Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So what I don’t know is what the unexpected will be.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I have spent my whole life serving this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I grew up in a family where my father was gone most of the time because he was at sea and doing our country’s business.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My mother basically raised our family.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know what it’s like in dark times.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know what it’s like to have to fight to keep one’s hope going through difficult times.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know what it’s like to rely on others for support and courage and love in tough times.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know what it’s like to have your comrades reach out to you and your neighbors and your fellow citizens and pick you up and put you back in the fight.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what America’s all about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I believe in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I believe in its future.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I believe in its greatness.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s been my great honor to serve it for many, many years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’m asking the American people to give me another opportunity and I’ll rest on my record, but I’ll also tell you, when times are tough, we need a steady hand at the tiller and the great honor of my life was to always put my country first.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thank you, Tom.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, let — let me say, Bob, thank you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And thanks to Hofstra.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, by the way, our beloved Nancy Reagan is in the hospital tonight, so our thoughts and prayers are going with you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s good to see you again, Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Americans are hurting right now, and they’re angry.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re hurting, and they’re angry.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re innocent victims of greed and excess on Wall Street and as well as Washington, D.C.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they’re angry, and they have every reason to be angry.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they want this country to go in a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And there are elements of my proposal that you just outlined which I won’t repeat.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But we also have to have a short-term fix, in my view, and long- term fixes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let me just talk to you about one of the short-term fixes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The catalyst for this housing crisis was the Fannie and Freddie Mae that caused subprime lending situation that now caused the housing market in America to collapse.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I am convinced that, until we reverse this continued decline in home ownership and put a floor under it, and so that people have not only the hope and belief they can stay in their homes and realize the American dream, but that value will come up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, we have allocated $750 billion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s take 300 of that billion and go in and buy those home loan mortgages and negotiate with those people in their homes, 11 million homes or more, so that they can afford to pay the mortgage, stay in their home.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, I know the criticism of this.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, what about the citizen that stayed in their homes?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That paid their mortgage payments?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It doesn’t help that person in their home if the next door neighbor’s house is abandoned.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And so we’ve got to reverse this.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We ought to put the homeowners first.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I am disappointed that Secretary Paulson and others have not made that their first priority.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all, I want to thank Hofstra University and the people of New York for hosting us tonight and it’s wonderful to join Senator McCain again, and thank you, Bob.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think everybody understands at this point that we are experiencing the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the financial rescue plan that Senator McCain and I supported is an important first step.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I pushed for some core principles: making sure that taxpayer can get their money back if they’re putting money up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Making sure that CEOs are not enriching themselves through this process.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that it’s going to take some time to work itself out.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what we haven’t yet seen is a rescue package for the middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because the fundamentals of the economy were weak even before this latest crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So I’ve proposed four specific things that I think can help.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, let’s focus on jobs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I want to end the tax breaks for companies that are shipping jobs overseas and provide a tax credit for every company that’s creating a job right here in America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number two, let’s help families right away by providing them a tax cut — a middle-class tax cut for people making less than $200,000, and let’s allow them to access their IRA accounts without penalty if they’re experiencing a crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now Senator McCain and I agree with your idea that we’ve got to help homeowners.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why we included in the financial package a proposal to get homeowners in a position where they can renegotiate their mortgages.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I disagree with Senator McCain in how to do it, because the way Senator McCain has designed his plan, it could be a giveaway to banks if we’re buying full price for mortgages that now are worth a lot less.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we don’t want to waste taxpayer money.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got to get the financial package working much quicker than it has been working.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Last point I want to make, though.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got some long-term challenges in this economy that have to be dealt with.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to fix our energy policy that’s giving our wealth away.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to fix our health care system and we’ve got to invest in our education system for every young person to be able to learn.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would like to mention that a couple days ago Senator Obama was out in Ohio and he had an encounter with a guy who’s a plumber, his name is Joe Wurzelbacher.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Joe wants to buy the business that he has been in for all of these years, worked 10, 12 hours a day.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And he wanted to buy the business but he looked at your tax plan and he saw that he was going to pay much higher taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You were going to put him in a higher tax bracket which was going to increase his taxes, which was going to cause him not to be able to employ people, which Joe was trying to realize the American dream.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now Senator Obama talks about the very, very rich.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Joe, I want to tell you, I’ll not only help you buy that business that you worked your whole life for and be able — and I’ll keep your taxes low and I’ll provide available and affordable health care for you and your employees.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I will not have — I will not stand for a tax increase on small business income.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Fifty percent of small business income taxes are paid by small businesses.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s 16 million jobs in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And what you want to do to Joe the plumber and millions more like him is have their taxes increased and not be able to realize the American dream of owning their own business.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what Joe believes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He has been watching ads of Senator McCain’s.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me tell you what I’m actually going to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think tax policy is a major difference between Senator McCain and myself.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we both want to cut taxes, the difference is who we want to cut taxes for.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain, the centerpiece of his economic proposal is to provide $200 billion in additional tax breaks to some of the wealthiest corporations in America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Exxon Mobil, and other oil companies, for example, would get an additional $4 billion in tax breaks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I’ve said is I want to provide a tax cut for 95 percent of working Americans, 95 percent.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you make more — if you make less than a quarter million dollars a year, then you will not see your income tax go up, your capital gains tax go up, your payroll tax.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not one dime.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And 95 percent of working families, 95 percent of you out there, will get a tax cut.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, independent studies have looked at our respective plans and have concluded that I provide three times the amount of tax relief to middle-class families than Senator McCain does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the conversation I had with Joe the plumber, what I essentially said to him was, “Five years ago, when you were in a position to buy your business, you needed a tax cut then.” And what I want to do is to make sure that the plumber, the nurse, the firefighter, the teacher, the young entrepreneur who doesn’t yet have money, I want to give them a tax break now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that requires us to make some important choices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The last point I’ll make about small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Not only do 98 percent of small businesses make less than $250,000, but I also want to give them additional tax breaks, because they are the drivers of the economy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They produce the most jobs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, when Senator Obama ended up his conversation with Joe the plumber — we need to spread the wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: In other words, we’re going to take Joe’s money, give it to Senator Obama, and let him spread the wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want Joe the plumber to spread that wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You told him you wanted to spread the wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The whole premise behind Senator Obama’s plans are class warfare, let’s spread the wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want small businesses — and by the way, the small businesses that we’re talking about would receive an increase in their taxes right now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Who — why would you want to increase anybody’s taxes right now?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Why would you want to do that, anyone, anyone in America, when we have such a tough time, when these small business people, like Joe the plumber, are going to create jobs, unless you take that money from him and spread the wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m not going to…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re not going to do that in my administration.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If I can answer the question.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, I want to cut taxes for 95 percent of Americans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, it is true that my friend and supporter, Warren Buffett, for example, could afford to pay a little more in taxes in order…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re talking about Joe the plumber.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … in order to give — in order to give additional tax cuts to Joe the plumber before he was at the point where he could make $250,000.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Then Exxon Mobil, which made $12 billion, record profits, over the last several quarters, they can afford to pay a little more so that ordinary families who are hurting out there — they’re trying to figure out how they’re going to afford food, how they’re going to save for their kids’ college education, they need a break.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, look, nobody likes taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I would prefer that none of us had to pay taxes, including myself.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But ultimately, we’ve got to pay for the core investments that make this economy strong and somebody’s got to do it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Nobody likes taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s not raise anybody’s taxes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I don’t mind paying a little more.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The fact is that businesses in America today are paying the second highest tax rate of anywhere in the world.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Our tax rate for business in America is 35 percent.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Ireland, it’s 11 percent.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Where are companies going to go where they can create jobs and where they can do best in business?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to cut the business tax rate in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to encourage business.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, of all times in America, we need to cut people’s taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to encourage business, create jobs, not spread the wealth around.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, first of all, I think it’s important for the American public to understand that the $750 billion rescue package, if it’s structured properly, and, as president, I will make sure it’s structured properly, means that ultimately taxpayers get their money back, and that’s important to understand.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there is no doubt that we’ve been living beyond our means and we’re going to have to make some adjustments.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, what I’ve done throughout this campaign is to propose a net spending cut.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I haven’t made a promise about…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So let me get to that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I want to emphasize, though, is that I have been a strong proponent of pay-as- you-go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Every dollar that I’ve proposed, I’ve proposed an additional cut so that it matches.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And some of the cuts, just to give you an example, we spend $15 billion a year on subsidies to insurance companies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It doesn’t — under the Medicare plan — it doesn’t help seniors get any better.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not improving our health care system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s just a giveaway.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to eliminate a whole host of programs that don’t work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to go through the federal budget line by line, page by page, programs that don’t work, we should cut.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Programs that we need, we should make them work better.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, what is true is that Senator McCain and I have a difference in terms of the need to invest in America and the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mentioned health care earlier.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we make investments now so that people have coverage, that we are preventing diseases, that will save on Medicare and Medicaid in the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we invest in a serious energy policy, that will save in the amount of money we’re borrowing from China to send to Saudi Arabia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we invest now in our young people and their ability to go to college, that will allow them to drive this economy into the 21st century.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what is absolutely true is that, once we get through this economic crisis and some of the specific proposals to get us out of this slump, that we’re not going to be able to go back to our profligate ways.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’re going to have to embrace a culture and an ethic of responsibility, all of us, corporations, the federal government, and individuals out there who may be living beyond their means.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you, Bob.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I just want to get back to this home ownership.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: During the Depression era, we had a thing called the home ownership loan corporation.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they went out and bought up these mortgages.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And people were able to stay in their homes, and eventually the values of those homes went up, and they actually made money.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, by the way, this was a proposal made by Senator Clinton not too long ago.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So, obviously, if we can start increasing home values, then there will be creation of wealth.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Energy — well, first — second of all, energy independence.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to have nuclear power.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to stop sending $700 billion a year to countries that don’t like us very much.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s wind, tide, solar, natural gas, nuclear, off-shore drilling, which Senator Obama has opposed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the point is that we become energy independent and we will create millions of jobs — millions of jobs in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: OK, what — what would I cut?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would have, first of all, across-the-board spending freeze, OK?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Some people say that’s a hatchet.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s a hatchet, and then I would get out a scalpel, OK?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because we’ve got — we have presided over the largest increase — we’ve got to have a new direction for this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have presided over the largest increase in government since the Great Society.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Government spending has gone completely out of control; $10 trillion dollar debt we’re giving to our kids, a half-a-trillion dollars we owe China.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how to save billions of dollars in defense spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how to eliminate programs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought against — well, one of them would be the marketing assistance program.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Another one would be a number of subsidies for ethanol.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I oppose subsidies for ethanol because I thought it distorted the market and created inflation; Senator Obama supported those subsidies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would eliminate the tariff on imported sugarcane-based ethanol from Brazil.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I know how to save billions.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I saved the taxpayer $6.8 billion by fighting a deal for a couple of years, as you might recall, that was a sweetheart deal between an aircraft manufacturer, DOD, and people ended up in jail.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I would fight for a line-item veto, and I would certainly veto every earmark pork-barrel bill.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama has asked for nearly $1 billion in pork-barrel earmark projects…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: … including $3 million for an overhead projector in a planetarium in his hometown.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s not the way we cut — we’ll cut out all the pork.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, look, I think that we do have a disagreement about an across-the-board spending freeze.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It sounds good.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s proposed periodically.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It doesn’t happen.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, in fact, an across-the-board spending freeze is a hatchet, and we do need a scalpel, because there are some programs that don’t work at all.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There are some programs that are underfunded.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I want to make sure that we are focused on those programs that work.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, Senator McCain talks a lot about earmarks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s one of the centerpieces of his campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Earmarks account for 0.5 percent of the total federal budget.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There’s no doubt that the system needs reform and there are a lot of screwy things that we end up spending money on, and they need to be eliminated.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it’s not going to solve the problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the last thing I think we have to focus on is a little bit of history, just so that we understand what we’re doing going forward.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When President Bush came into office, we had a budget surplus and the national debt was a little over $5 trillion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It has doubled over the last eight years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are now looking at a deficit of well over half a trillion dollars.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So one of the things that I think we have to recognize is pursuing the same kinds of policies that we pursued over the last eight years is not going to bring down the deficit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, frankly, Senator McCain voted for four out of five of President Bush’s budgets.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to take this in a new direction, that’s what I propose as president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And let me tell you…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama, I am not President Bush.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you wanted to run against President Bush, you should have run four years ago.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m going to give a new direction to this economy in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama talks about voting for budgets.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted twice for a budget resolution that increases the taxes on individuals making $42,000 a year.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we can take a hatchet and a scalpel to this budget.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s completely out of control.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The mayor of New York, Mayor Bloomberg, just imposed an across- the-board spending freeze on New York City.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re doing it all over America because they have to.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because they have to balance their budgets.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I will balance our budgets and I will get them and I will…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: … reduce this — I can — we can do it with this kind of job creation of energy independence.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, look, Americans are hurting tonight and they’re angry and I understand that, and they want a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I can bring them in that direction by eliminating spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama talks about the budgets I voted for.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted for the last two budgets that had that $24 billion more in spending than the budget that the Bush administration proposed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted for the energy bill that was full of goodies for the oil companies that I opposed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So the fact is, let’s look at our records, Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s look at it as graded by the National Taxpayers Union and the Citizens Against Government Waste and the other watchdog organizations.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought against spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought against special interests.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have fought for reform.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You have to tell me one time when you have stood up with the leaders of your party on one single major issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, there’s a lot of stuff that was put out there, so let me try to address it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: First of all, in terms of standing up to the leaders of my party, the first major bill that I voted on in the Senate was in support of tort reform, which wasn’t very popular with trial lawyers, a major constituency in the Democratic Party.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: An overwhelming vote.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I support charter schools and pay for performance for teachers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Doesn’t make me popular with the teachers union.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I support clean coal technology.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Doesn’t make me popular with environmentalists.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So I’ve got a history of reaching across the aisle.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now with respect to a couple of things Senator McCain said, the notion that I voted for a tax increase for people making $42,000 a year has been disputed by everybody who has looked at this claim that Senator McCain keeps on making.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Even FOX News disputes it, and that doesn’t happen very often when it comes to accusations about me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So the fact of the matter is that if I occasionally have mistaken your policies for George Bush’s policies, it’s because on the core economic issues that matter to the American people, on tax policy, on energy policy, on spending priorities, you have been a vigorous supporter of President Bush.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, you’ve shown independence — commendable independence, on some key issues like torture, for example, and I give you enormous credit for that.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when it comes to economic policies, essentially what you’re proposing is eight more years of the same thing.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it hasn’t worked.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think the American people understand it hasn’t worked.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need to move in a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let me just say, Bob.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But it’s very clear that I have disagreed with the Bush administration.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have disagreed with leaders of my own party.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve got the scars to prove it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whether it be bringing climate change to the floor of the Senate for the first time.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whether it be opposition to spending and earmarks, whether it be the issue of torture, whether it be the conduct of the war in Iraq, which I vigorously opposed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whether it be on fighting the pharmaceutical companies on Medicare prescription drugs, importation.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whether it be fighting for an HMO patient’s bill of rights.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whether it be the establishment of the 9/11 Commission.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have a long record of reform and fighting through on the floor of the United States Senate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama, your argument for standing up to the leadership of your party isn’t very convincing.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, this has been a tough campaign.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s been a very tough campaign.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I know from my experience in many campaigns that, if Senator Obama had asked — responded to my urgent request to sit down, and do town hall meetings, and come before the American people, we could have done at least 10 of them by now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: When Senator Obama was first asked, he said, “Any place, any time,” the way Barry Goldwater and Jack Kennedy agreed to do, before the intervention of the tragedy at Dallas.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I think the tone of this campaign could have been very different.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the fact is, it’s gotten pretty tough.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I regret some of the negative aspects of both campaigns.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the fact is that it has taken many turns which I think are unacceptable.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: One of them happened just the other day, when a man I admire and respect — I’ve written about him — Congressman John Lewis, an American hero, made allegations that Sarah Palin and I were somehow associated with the worst chapter in American history, segregation, deaths of children in church bombings, George Wallace.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That, to me, was so hurtful.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, Senator Obama, you didn’t repudiate those remarks.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Every time there’s been an out-of-bounds remark made by a Republican, no matter where they are, I have repudiated them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I hope that Senator Obama will repudiate those remarks that were made by Congressman John Lewis, very unfair and totally inappropriate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I want to tell you, we will run a truthful campaign.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This is a tough campaign.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s a matter of fact that Senator Obama has spent more money on negative ads than any political campaign in history.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I can prove it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, Senator Obama, when he said — and he signed a piece of paper that said he would take public financing for his campaign if I did — that was back when he was a long-shot candidate — you didn’t keep your word.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And when you looked into the camera in a debate with Senator Clinton and said, “I will sit down and negotiate with John McCain about public financing before I make a decision,” you didn’t tell the American people the truth because you didn’t.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s — that’s — that’s an unfortunate part.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now we have the highest spending by Senator Obama’s campaign than any time since Watergate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, look, you know, I think that we expect presidential campaigns to be tough.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that, if you look at the record and the impressions of the American people — Bob, your network just did a poll, showing that two-thirds of the American people think that Senator McCain is running a negative campaign versus one-third of mine.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And 100 percent, John, of your ads — 100 percent of them have been negative.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It absolutely is true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, now, I think the American people are less interested in our hurt feelings during the course of the campaign than addressing the issues that matter to them so deeply.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And there is nothing wrong with us having a vigorous debate like we’re having tonight about health care, about energy policy, about tax policy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the stuff that campaigns should be made of.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The notion, though, that because we’re not doing town hall meetings that justifies some of the ads that have been going up, not just from your own campaign directly, John, but 527s and other organizations that make some pretty tough accusations, well, I don’t mind being attacked for the next three weeks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What the American people can’t afford, though, is four more years of failed economic policies.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what they deserve over the next four weeks is that we talk about what’s most pressing to them: the economic crisis.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain’s own campaign said publicly last week that, if we keep on talking about the economic crisis, we lose, so we need to change the subject.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I would love to see the next three weeks devoted to talking about the economy, devoted to talking about health care, devoted to talking about energy, and figuring out how the American people can send their kids to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that is something that I would welcome.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it requires, I think, a recognition that politics as usual, as been practiced over the last several years, is not solving the big problems here in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, if you’ll turn on the television, as I — I watched the Arizona Cardinals defeat the Dallas Cowboys on Sunday.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Congratulations.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Every other ad — ever other ad was an attack ad on my health care plan.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And any objective observer has said it’s not true.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You’re running ads right now that say that I oppose federal funding for stem cell research.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You’re running ads that misportray completely my position on immigration.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So the fact is that Senator Obama is spending unprecedented — unprecedented in the history of American politics, going back to the beginning, amounts of money in negative attack ads on me.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And of course, I’ve been talking about the economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, I’ve talked to people like Joe the plumber and tell him that I’m not going to spread his wealth around.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m going to let him keep his wealth.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And of course, we’re talking about positive plan of action to restore this economy and restore jobs in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what my campaign is all about and that’s what it’ll continue to be all about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But again, I did not hear a repudiation of Congressman…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, look, if we want to talk about Congressman Lewis, who is an American hero, he, unprompted by my campaign, without my campaign’s awareness, made a statement that he was troubled with what he was hearing at some of the rallies that your running mate was holding, in which all the Republican reports indicated were shouting, when my name came up, things like “terrorist” and “kill him,” and that you’re running mate didn’t mention, didn’t stop, didn’t say “Hold on a second, that’s kind of out of line.” And I think Congressman Lewis’ point was that we have to be careful about how we deal with our supporters.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You’ve got to read what he said… (CROSSTALK)

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let — let — let…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You’ve got to read what he said.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me — let me complete…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … my response.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I do think that he inappropriately drew a comparison between what was happening there and what had happened during the civil rights movement, and we immediately put out a statement saying that we don’t think that comparison is appropriate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, in fact, afterwards, Congressman Lewis put out a similar statement, saying that he had probably gone over the line.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The important point here is, though, the American people have become so cynical about our politics, because all they see is a tit- for-tat and back-and-forth.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what they want is the ability to just focus on some really big challenges that we face right now, and that’s what I have been trying to focus on this entire campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can have serious differences about our health care policy, for example, John, because we do have a difference on health care policy, but we…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We do and I hope…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … talking about it this evening.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But when people suggest that I pal around with terrorists, then we’re not talking about issues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we’re talking about…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, let me just say I would…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let me just say categorically I’m proud of the people that come to our rallies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Whenever you get a large rally of 10,000, 15,000, 20,000 people, you’re going to have some fringe peoples.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve — and we’ve always said that that’s not appropriate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But to somehow say that group of young women who said “Military wives for McCain” are somehow saying anything derogatory about you, but anything — and those veterans that wear those hats that say “World War II, Vietnam, Korea, Iraq,” I’m not going to stand for people saying that the people that come to my rallies are anything but the most dedicated, patriotic men and women that are in this nation and they’re great citizens.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’m not going to stand for somebody saying that because someone yelled something at a rally — there’s a lot of things that have been yelled at your rallies, Senator Obama, that I’m not happy about either.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: In fact, some T-shirts that are very…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: … unacceptable.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So the point is — the point is that I have repudiated every time someone’s been out of line, whether they’ve been part of my campaign or not, and I will continue to do that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But the fact is that we need to absolutely not stand for the kind of things that have been going on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, look, Bob, as I said…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, here’s what I would say.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I mean, we can have a debate back and forth about the merits of each other’s campaigns.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I suspect we won’t agree here tonight.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What I think is most important is that we recognize that to solve the key problems that we’re facing, if we’re going to solve two wars, the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression, if we can — if we’re going to focus on lifting wages that have declined over the last eight years and create jobs here in America, then Democrats, independents and Republicans, we’re going to have to be able to work together.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what is important is making sure that we disagree without being disagreeable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it means that we can have tough, vigorous debates around issues.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: What we can’t do, I think, is try to characterize each other as bad people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that has been a culture in Washington that has been taking place for too long.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, Bob, you asked me a direct question.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Yes, real quick.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Ayers, I don’t care about an old washed-up terrorist.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But as Senator Clinton said in her debates with you, we need to know the full extent of that relationship.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to know the full extent of Senator Obama’s relationship with ACORN, who is now on the verge of maybe perpetrating one of the greatest frauds in voter history in this country, maybe destroying the fabric of democracy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The same front outfit organization that your campaign gave $832,000 for “lighting and site selection.” So all of these things need to be examined, of course.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bob, I think it’s going to be important to just — I’ll respond to these two particular allegations that Senator McCain has made and that have gotten a lot of attention.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, Mr.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ayers has become the centerpiece of Senator McCain’s campaign over the last two or three weeks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This has been their primary focus.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So let’s get the record straight.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Bill Ayers is a professor of education in Chicago.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Forty years ago, when I was 8 years old, he engaged in despicable acts with a radical domestic group.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I have roundly condemned those acts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ten years ago he served and I served on a school reform board that was funded by one of Ronald Reagan’s former ambassadors and close friends, Mr.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Other members on that board were the presidents of the University of Illinois, the president of Northwestern University, who happens to be a Republican, the president of The Chicago Tribune, a Republican- leaning newspaper.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Ayers is not involved in my campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He has never been involved in this campaign.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And he will not advise me in the White House.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So that’s Mr.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, with respect to ACORN, ACORN is a community organization.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Apparently what they’ve done is they were paying people to go out and register folks, and apparently some of the people who were out there didn’t really register people, they just filled out a bunch of names.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It had nothing to do with us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We were not involved.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The only involvement I’ve had with ACORN was I represented them alongside the U.S.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Justice Department in making Illinois implement a motor voter law that helped people get registered at DMVs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, the reason I think that it’s important to just get these facts out is because the allegation that Senator McCain has continually made is that somehow my associations are troubling.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me tell you who I associate with.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: On economic policy, I associate with Warren Buffett and former Fed Chairman Paul Volcker.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If I’m interested in figuring out my foreign policy, I associate myself with my running mate, Joe Biden or with Dick Lugar, the Republican ranking member on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, or General Jim Jones, the former supreme allied commander of NATO.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Those are the people, Democrats and Republicans, who have shaped my ideas and who will be surrounding me in the White House.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think the fact that this has become such an important part of your campaign, Senator McCain, says more about your campaign than it says about me.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, again, while you were on the board of the Woods Foundation, you and Mr.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Ayers, together, you sent $230,000 to ACORN.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So — and you launched your political campaign in Mr.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Ayers’ living room.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s absolutely not true.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the facts are facts and records are records.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s not the facts.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s not the fact — it’s not the fact that Senator Obama chooses to associate with a guy who in 2001 said that he wished he had have bombed more, and he had a long association with him.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s the fact that all the — all of the details need to be known about Senator Obama’s relationship with them and with ACORN and the American people will make a judgment.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And my campaign is about getting this economy back on track, about creating jobs, about a brighter future for America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s what my campaign is about and I’m not going to raise taxes the way Senator Obama wants to raise taxes in a tough economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s really what this campaign is going to be about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, Joe Biden, I think, is one of the finest public servants that has served in this country.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not just that he has some of the best foreign policy credentials of anybody.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Democrats and Republicans alike, I think, acknowledge his expertise there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it’s also that his entire life he has never forgotten where he came from, coming from Scranton, fighting on behalf of working families, remembering what it’s like to see his father lose his job and go through a downward spiral economically.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And, as a consequence, his consistent pattern throughout his career is to fight for the little guy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what he’s done when it comes to economic policies that will help working families get a leg up.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s what he’s done when it comes to, for example, passing the landmark 1994 crime bill, the Violence Against Women’s Act.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Joe has always made sure that he is fighting on behalf of working families, and I think he shares my core values and my sense of where the country needs to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Because after eight years of failed policies, he and I both agree that what we’re going to have to do is to reprioritize, make sure that we’re investing in the American people, give tax cuts not to the wealthiest corporations, but give them to small businesses and give them to individuals who are struggling right now, make sure that we finally get serious about energy independence, something that has been languishing in Washington for 30 years, and make sure that our kids get a great education and can afford to go to college.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So, on the key issues that are of importance to American families, Joe Biden’s always been on the right side, and I think he will make an outstanding president if, heaven forbid, something happened to me.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, Americans have gotten to know Sarah Palin.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They know that she’s a role model to women and other — and reformers all over America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She’s a reformer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She is — she took on a governor who was a member of her own party when she ran for governor.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: When she was the head of their energy and natural resources board, she saw corruption, she resigned and said, “This can’t go on.” She’s given money back to the taxpayers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She’s cut the size of government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She negotiated with the oil companies and faced them down, a $40 billion pipeline of natural gas that’s going to relieve the energy needs of the United — of what they call the lower 48.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She’s a reformer through and through.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s time we had that bresh of freth air (sic) — breath of fresh air coming into our nation’s capital and sweep out the old-boy network and the cronyism that’s been so much a part of it that I’ve fought against for all these years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She’ll be my partner.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She understands reform.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And, by the way, she also understands special-needs families.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She understands that autism is on the rise, that we’ve got to find out what’s causing it, and we’ve got to reach out to these families, and help them, and give them the help they need as they raise these very special needs children.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She understands that better than almost any American that I know.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m proud of her.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And she has ignited our party and people all over America that have never been involved in the political process.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I can’t tell how proud I am of her and her family.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Her husband’s a pretty tough guy, by the way, too.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, I think it’s — that’s going to be up to the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that, obviously, she’s a capable politician who has, I think, excited the — a base in the Republican Party.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think it’s very commendable the work she’s done on behalf of special needs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I agree with that, John.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I do want to just point out that autism, for example, or other special needs will require some additional funding, if we’re going to get serious in terms of research.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is something that every family that advocates on behalf of disabled children talk about.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we have an across-the-board spending freeze, we’re not going to be able to do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s an example of, I think, the kind of use of the scalpel that we want to make sure that we’re funding some of those programs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think that Joe Biden is qualified in many respects.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I do point out that he’s been wrong on many foreign policy and national security issues, which is supposed to be his strength.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted against the first Gulf War.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted against it and, obviously, we had to take Saddam Hussein out of Kuwait or it would’ve threatened the Middle Eastern world supply.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: In Iraq, he had this cockamamie idea about dividing Iraq into three countries.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re seeing Iraq united as Iraqis, tough, hard, but we’re seeing them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re now about to have an agreement for status of forces in Iraq coming up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There are several issues in which, frankly, Joe Biden and I openly and honestly disagreed on national security policy, and he’s been wrong on a number of the major ones.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But again, I want to come back to, notice every time Senator Obama says, “We need to spend more, we need to spend more, that’s the answer” — why do we always have to spend more?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Why can’t we have transparency, accountability, reform of these agencies of government?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Maybe that’s why he’s asked for 860 — sought and proposed $860 billion worth of new spending and wants to raise people’s taxes in a time of incredible challenge and difficulty and heartache for the American families.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Climate change.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I believe we can, for all intents and purposes, eliminate our dependence on Middle Eastern oil and Venezuelan oil.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Canadian oil is fine.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: By the way, when Senator Obama said he would unilaterally renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement, the Canadians said, “Yes, and we’ll sell our oil to China.” You don’t tell countries you’re going to unilaterally renegotiate agreements with them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can eliminate our dependence on foreign oil by building 45 new nuclear plants, power plants, right away.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can store and we can reprocess.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama will tell you, in the — as the extreme environmentalists do, it has to be safe.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, we’ve sailed Navy ships around the world for 60 years with nuclear power plants on them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can store and reprocess spent nuclear fuel, Senator Obama, no problem.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So the point is with nuclear power, with wind, tide, solar, natural gas, with development of flex fuel, hybrid, clean coal technology, clean coal technology is key in the heartland of America that’s hurting rather badly.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I think we can easily, within seven, eight, ten years, if we put our minds to it, we can eliminate our dependence on the places in the world that harm our national security if we don’t achieve our independence.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that in ten years, we can reduce our dependence so that we no longer have to import oil from the Middle East or Venezuela.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that’s about a realistic timeframe.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is the most important issue that our future economy is going to face.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Obviously, we’ve got an immediate crisis right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But nothing is more important than us no longer borrowing $700 billion or more from China and sending it to Saudi Arabia.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s mortgaging our children’s future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, from the start of this campaign, I’ve identified this as one of my top priorities and here is what I think we have to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Number one, we do need to expand domestic production and that means, for example, telling the oil companies the 68 million acres that they currently have leased that they’re not drilling, use them or lose them.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that we should look at offshore drilling and implement it in a way that allows us to get some additional oil.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But understand, we only have three to four percent of the world’s oil reserves and we use 25 percent of the world’s oil, which means that we can’t drill our way out of the problem.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why I’ve focused on putting resources into solar, wind, biodiesel, geothermal.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These have been priorities of mine since I got to the Senate, and it is absolutely critical that we develop a high fuel efficient car that’s built not in Japan and not in South Korea, but built here in the United States of America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We invented the auto industry and the fact that we have fallen so far behind is something that we have to work on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now I just want to make one last point because Senator McCain mentioned NAFTA and the issue of trade and that actually bears on this issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I believe in free trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I also believe that for far too long, certainly during the course of the Bush administration with the support of Senator McCain, the attitude has been that any trade agreement is a good trade agreement.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And NAFTA doesn’t have — did not have enforceable labor agreements and environmental agreements.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I said was we should include those and make them enforceable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In the same way that we should enforce rules against China manipulating its currency to make our exports more expensive and their exports to us cheaper.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when it comes to South Korea, we’ve got a trade agreement up right now, they are sending hundreds of thousands of South Korean cars into the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s all good.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can only get 4,000 to 5,000 into South Korea.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That is not free trade.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to have a president who is going to be advocating on behalf of American businesses and American workers and I make no apology for that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, you know, I admire so much Senator Obama’s eloquence.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And you really have to pay attention to words.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He said, we will look at offshore drilling.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Did you get that?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can offshore drill now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ve got to do it now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We will reduce the cost of a barrel of oil because we show the world that we have a supply of our own.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s doable.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The technology is there and we have to drill now.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, on the subject of free trade agreements.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I am a free trader.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I need — we need to have education and training programs for displaced workers that work, going to our community colleges.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But let me give you another example of a free trade agreement that Senator Obama opposes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Right now, because of previous agreements, some made by President Clinton, the goods and products that we send to Colombia, which is our largest agricultural importer of our products, is — there’s a billion dollars that we — our businesses have paid so far in order to get our goods in there.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because of previous agreements, their goods and products come into our country for free.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So Senator Obama, who has never traveled south of our border, opposes the Colombia Free Trade Agreement.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The same country that’s helping us try to stop the flow of drugs into our country that’s killing young Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And also the country that just freed three Americans that will help us create jobs in America because they will be a market for our goods and products without having to pay — without us having to pay the billions of dollars — the billion dollars and more that we’ve already paid.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Free trade with Colombia is something that’s a no-brainer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But maybe you ought to travel down there and visit them and maybe you could understand it a lot better.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Let me respond.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Actually, I understand it pretty well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The history in Colombia right now is that labor leaders have been targeted for assassination on a fairly consistent basis and there have not been prosecutions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And what I have said, because the free trade — the trade agreement itself does have labor and environmental protections, but we have to stand for human rights and we have to make sure that violence isn’t being perpetrated against workers who are just trying to organize for their rights, which is why, for example, I supported the Peruvian Free Trade Agreement which was a well-structured agreement.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I think that the important point is we’ve got to have a president who understands the benefits of free trade but also is going to enforce unfair trade agreements and is going to stand up to other countries.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the last point I’ll make, because we started on energy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: When I talked about the automakers, they are obviously getting hammered right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They were already having a tough time because of high gas prices.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And now with the financial crisis, car dealerships are closing and people can’t get car loans.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s why I think it’s important for us to get loan guarantees to the automakers, but we do have to hold them responsible as well to start producing the highly fuel-efficient cars of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And Detroit had dragged its feet too long in terms of getting that done.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s going to be one of my highest priorities because transportation accounts for about 30 percent of our total energy consumption.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we can get that right, then we can move in a direction not only of energy independence, but we can create 5 million new jobs all across America, including in the heartland where we can retool some of these plants to make these highly fuel-efficient cars and also to make wind turbines and solar panels, the kinds of clean energy approaches that should be the driver of our economy for the next century.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, let me just said that that this is — he — Senator Obama doesn’t want a free trade agreement with our best ally in the region but wants to sit down across the table without precondition to — with Hugo Chavez, the guy who has been helping FARC, the terrorist organization.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Free trade between ourselves and Colombia, I just recited to you the benefits of concluding that agreement, a billion dollars of American dollars that could have gone to creating jobs and businesses in the United States, opening up those markets.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I don’t — I don’t think there’s any doubt that Senator Obama wants to restrict trade and he wants to raise taxes.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the last president of the United States that tried that was Herbert Hoover, and we went from a deep recession into a depression.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’re not going to follow that path while I’m — when I’m president of the United States.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to do both, and that’s exactly what my plan does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Look, as I travel around the country, this is the issue that will break your heart over and over again.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Just yesterday, I was in Toledo shaking some hands in a line.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Two women, both of them probably in their mid- to late-50s, had just been laid off of their plant.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Neither of them have health insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And they were desperate for some way of getting coverage, because, understandably, they’re worried that, if they get sick, they could go bankrupt.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what my plan does.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you have health insurance, then you don’t have to do anything.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’ve got health insurance through your employer, you can keep your health insurance, keep your choice of doctor, keep your plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The only thing we’re going to try to do is lower costs so that those cost savings are passed onto you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we estimate we can cut the average family’s premium by about $2,500 per year.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you don’t have health insurance, then what we’re going to do is to provide you the option of buying into the same kind of federal pool that both Senator McCain and I enjoy as federal employees, which will give you high-quality care, choice of doctors, at lower costs, because so many people are part of this insured group.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’re going to make sure that insurance companies can’t discriminate on the basis of pre-existing conditions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ll negotiate with the drug companies for the cheapest available price on drugs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We are going to invest in information technology to eliminate bureaucracy and make the system more efficient.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we are going to make sure that we manage chronic illnesses, like diabetes and heart disease, that cost a huge amount, but could be prevented.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We’ve got to put more money into preventive care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This will cost some money on the front end, but over the long term this is the only way that not only are we going to make families healthy, but it’s also how we’re going to save the federal budget, because we can’t afford these escalating costs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, it is a terribly painful situation for Americans.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They’re seeing their premiums, their co-pays go up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Forty-seven million Americans are without health insurance in America today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it really is the cost, the escalating costs of health care that are inflicting such pain on working families and people across this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I am convinced we need to do a lot of things.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to put health care records online.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That will — that will reduce costs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have more community health centers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have walk-in clinics.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The rise of obesity amongst young Americans is one of the most alarming statistics that there is.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We should have physical fitness programs and nutrition programs in schools.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Every parent should know what’s going on there.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We — we need to have — we need to have employers reward employees who join health clubs and practice wellness and fitness.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I want to give every American family a $5,000 refundable tax credit.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Take it and get anywhere in America the health care that you wish.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, my old buddy, Joe, Joe the plumber, is out there.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, Joe, Senator Obama’s plan, if you’re a small business and you are able — and your — the guy that sells to you will not have his capital gains tax increase, which Senator Obama wants, if you’re out there, my friend, and you’ve got employees, and you’ve got kids, if you don’t get — adopt the health care plan that Senator Obama mandates, he’s going to fine you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, Senator Obama, I’d like — still like to know what that fine is going to be, and I don’t think that Joe right now wants to pay a fine when he is seeing such difficult times in America’s economy.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama wants to set up health care bureaucracies, take over the health care of America through — as he said, his object is a single payer system.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you like that, you’ll love Canada and England.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So the point is…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is not and I just described it…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: No, you stated it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I just described what my plan is.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I’m happy to talk to you, Joe, too, if you’re out there.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Here’s your fine — zero.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You won’t pay a fine, because…

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Zero, because as I said in our last debate and I’ll repeat, John, I exempt small businesses from the requirement for large businesses that can afford to provide health care to their employees, but are not doing it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I exempt small businesses from having to pay into a kitty.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But large businesses that can afford it, we’ve got a choice.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Either they provide health insurance to their employees or somebody has to.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now, what happens is those employees get dumped into either the Medicaid system, which taxpayers pick up, or they’re going to the emergency room for uncompensated care, which everybody picks up in their premiums.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The average family is paying an additional $900 a year in higher premiums because of the uninsured.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So here’s what we do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We exempt small businesses.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In fact, what, Joe, if you want to do the right thing with your employees and you want to provide them health insurance, we’ll give you a 50 percent credit so that you will actually be able to afford it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you don’t have health insurance or you want to buy into a group plan, you will be able to buy into the plan that I just described.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, what we haven’t talked about is Senator McCain’s plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He says he’s going to give you all a $5,000 tax credit.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That sounds pretty good.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you can go out and buy your own insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Here’s the problem — that for about 20 million people, you may find yourselves no longer having employer-based health insurance.

speaker: OBAMA
