sentence: This is because younger people might be able to get health insurance for $5,000, young and healthy folks.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Older folks, less healthy folks, what’s going to end up happening is that you’re going to be the only ones left in your employer-based system, your employers won’t be able to afford it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And once you’re out on your own with this $5,000 credit, Senator McCain, for the first time, is going to be taxing the health care benefits that you have from your employer.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is your plan, John.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For the first time in history, you will be taxing people’s health care benefits.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: By the way, the average policy costs about $12,000.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if you’ve got $5,000 and it’s going to cost you $12,000, that’s a loss for you.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Last point about Senator McCain’s plan is that insurers right now, the main restrictions on what they do is primarily state law and, under Senator McCain’s plan, those rules would be stripped away and you would start seeing a lot more insurance companies cherry-picking and excluding people from coverage.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That, I think, is a mistake and I think that this is a fundamental difference in our campaign and how we would approach health care.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Hey, Joe, you’re rich, congratulations, because what Joe wanted to do was buy the business that he’s been working for 10-12 hours a day, seven days a week, and you said that you wanted to spread the wealth, but — in other words, take Joe’s money and then you decide what to do with it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, Joe, you’re rich, congratulations, and you will then fall into the category where you’ll have to pay a fine if you don’t provide health insurance that Senator Obama mandates, not the kind that you think is best for your family, your children, your employees, but the kind that he mandates for you.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s big government at its best.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, 95 percent of the people in America will receive more money under my plan because they will receive not only their present benefits, which may be taxed, which will be taxed, but then you add $5,000 onto it, except for those people who have the gold-plated Cadillac insurance policies that have to do with cosmetic surgery and transplants and all of those kinds of things.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And the good thing about this is they’ll be able to go across America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: The average cost of a health care insurance plan in America today is $5,800.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m going to give them $5,000 to take with them wherever they want to go, and this will give them affordability.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This will give them availability.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This will give them a chance to choose their own futures, not have Senator Obama and government decide that for them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This really gets down to the fundamental difference in our philosophies.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: If you notice that in all of this proposal, Senator — government wants — Senator Obama wants government to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama wants government to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want, Joe, you to do the job.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want to leave money in your pocket.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I want you to be able to choose the health care for you and your family.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s what I’m all about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ve got too much government and too much spending and the government is — the size of government has grown by 40 percent in the last eight years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We can’t afford that in the next eight years and Senator Obama, with the Democrats in charge of Congress, things have gotten worse.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Have you noticed, they’ve been in charge the last two years.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Very briefly.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You all just heard my plan.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If you’ve got an employer-based health care plan, you keep it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, under Senator McCain’s plan there is a strong risk that people would lose their employer-based health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That’s the choice you’ll have is having your employer no longer provide you health care.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And don’t take my word for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Chamber of Commerce, which generally doesn’t support a lot of Democrats, said that this plan could lead to the unraveling of the employer-based health care system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: All I want to do, if you’ve already got health care, is lower your costs.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That includes you, Joe.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would never and have never in all the years I’ve been there imposed a litmus test on any nominee to the court.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s not appropriate to do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I thought it was a bad decision.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think there were a lot of decisions that were bad.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think that decisions should rest in the hands of the states.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m a federalist.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I believe strongly that we should have nominees to the United States Supreme Court based on their qualifications rather than any litmus test.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, let me say that there was a time a few years ago when the United States Senate was about to blow up.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Republicans wanted to have just a majority vote to confirm a judge and the Democrats were blocking in an unprecedented fashion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We got together seven Republicans, seven Democrats.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You were offered a chance to join.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You chose not to because you were afraid of the appointment of, quote, “conservative judges.” I voted for Justice Breyer and Justice Ginsburg.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Not because I agreed with their ideology, but because I thought they were qualified and that elections have consequences when presidents are nominated.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: This is a very important issue we’re talking about.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama voted against Justice Breyer and Justice Roberts on the grounds that they didn’t meet his ideological standards.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s not the way we should judge these nominees.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Elections have consequences.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They should be judged on their qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And so that’s what I will do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I will find the best people in the world — in the United States of America who have a history of strict adherence to the Constitution.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And not legislating from the bench.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I would consider anyone in their qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I do not believe that someone who has supported Roe v.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Wade that would be part of those qualifications.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I certainly would not impose any litmus test.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I think it’s true that we shouldn’t apply a strict litmus test and the most important thing in any judge is their capacity to provide fairness and justice to the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And it is true that this is going to be, I think, one of the most consequential decisions of the next president.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is very likely that one of us will be making at least one and probably more than one appointments and Roe versus Wade probably hangs in the balance.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now I would not provide a litmus test.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I am somebody who believes that Roe versus Wade was rightly decided.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that abortion is a very difficult issue and it is a moral issue and one that I think good people on both sides can disagree on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But what ultimately I believe is that women in consultation with their families, their doctors, their religious advisers, are in the best position to make this decision.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think that the Constitution has a right to privacy in it that shouldn’t be subject to state referendum, any more than our First Amendment rights are subject to state referendum, any more than many of the other rights that we have should be subject to popular vote.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So this is going to be an important issue.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I will look for those judges who have an outstanding judicial record, who have the intellect, and who hopefully have a sense of what real-world folks are going through.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ll just give you one quick example.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain and I disagreed recently when the Supreme Court made it more difficult for a woman named Lilly Ledbetter to press her claim for pay discrimination.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: For years, she had been getting paid less than a man had been paid for doing the exact same job.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And when she brought a suit, saying equal pay for equal work, the judges said, well, you know, it’s taken you too long to bring this lawsuit, even though she didn’t know about it until fairly recently.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We tried to overturn it in the Senate.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I supported that effort to provide better guidance to the courts; John McCain opposed it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think that it’s important for judges to understand that if a woman is out there trying to raise a family, trying to support her family, and is being treated unfairly, then the court has to stand up, if nobody else will.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s the kind of judge that I want.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obviously, that law waived the statute of limitations, which you could have gone back 20 or 30 years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It was a trial lawyer’s dream.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let me talk to you about an important aspect of this issue.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to change the culture of America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Those of us who are proudly pro-life understand that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s got to be courage and compassion that we show to a young woman who’s facing this terribly difficult decision.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Senator Obama, as a member of the Illinois State Senate, voted in the Judiciary Committee against a law that would provide immediate medical attention to a child born of a failed abortion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He voted against that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And then, on the floor of the State Senate, as he did 130 times as a state senator, he voted present.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Then there was another bill before the Senate Judiciary Committee in the state of Illinois not that long ago, where he voted against a ban on partial-birth abortion, one of the late-term abortion, a really — one of the bad procedures, a terrible.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And then, on the floor of the Illinois State Senate, he voted present.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t know how you vote “present” on some of that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I don’t know how you align yourself with the extreme aspect of the pro- abortion movement in America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that’s his record, and that’s a matter of his record.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And he’ll say it has something to do with Roe v.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Wade, about the Illinois State Senate.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It was clear-cut votes that Senator Obama voted, I think, in direct contradiction to the feelings and views of mainstream America.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Yes, let me respond to this.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If it sounds incredible that I would vote to withhold lifesaving treatment from an infant, that’s because it’s not true.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The — here are the facts.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: There was a bill that was put forward before the Illinois Senate that said you have to provide lifesaving treatment and that would have helped to undermine Roe v.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The fact is that there was already a law on the books in Illinois that required providing lifesaving treatment, which is why not only myself but pro-choice Republicans and Democrats voted against it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And the Illinois Medical Society, the organization of doctors in Illinois, voted against it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Their Hippocratic Oath would have required them to provide care, and there was already a law in the books.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: With respect to partial-birth abortion, I am completely supportive of a ban on late-term abortions, partial-birth or otherwise, as long as there’s an exception for the mother’s health and life, and this did not contain that exception.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I attempted, as many have in the past, of including that so that it is constitutional.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that was rejected, and that’s why I voted present, because I’m willing to support a ban on late-term abortions as long as we have that exception.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The last point I want to make on the issue of abortion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This is an issue that — look, it divides us.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And in some ways, it may be difficult to — to reconcile the two views.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there surely is some common ground when both those who believe in choice and those who are opposed to abortion can come together and say, “We should try to prevent unintended pregnancies by providing appropriate education to our youth, communicating that sexuality is sacred and that they should not be engaged in cavalier activity, and providing options for adoption, and helping single mothers if they want to choose to keep the baby.” Those are all things that we put in the Democratic platform for the first time this year, and I think that’s where we can find some common ground, because nobody’s pro-abortion.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s always a tragic situation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We should try to reduce these circumstances.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Just again, the example of the eloquence of Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: He’s health for the mother.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You know, that’s been stretched by the pro-abortion movement in America to mean almost anything.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s the extreme pro-abortion position, quote, “health.” But, look, Cindy and I are adoptive parents.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We know what a treasure and joy it is to have an adopted child in our lives.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We’ll do everything we can to improve adoption in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But that does not mean that we will cease to protect the rights of the unborn.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we have to come together.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Of course, we have to work together, and, of course, it’s vital that we do so and help these young women who are facing such a difficult decision, with a compassion, that we’ll help them with the adoptive services, with the courage to bring that child into this world and we’ll help take care of it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: This probably has more to do with our economic future than anything and that means it also has a national security implication, because there’s never been a nation on earth that saw its economy decline and continued to maintain its primacy as a military power.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So we’ve got to get our education system right.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now, typically, what’s happened is that there’s been a debate between more money or reform, and I think we need both.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In some cases, we are going to have to invest.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Early childhood education, which closes the achievement gap, so that every child is prepared for school, every dollar we invest in that, we end up getting huge benefits with improved reading scores, reduced dropout rates, reduced delinquency rates.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s going to be critically important for us to recruit a generation of new teachers, an army of new teachers, especially in math and science, give them higher pay, give them more professional development and support in exchange for higher standards and accountability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I think it’s important for us to make college affordable.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Right now, I meet young people all across the country who either have decided not to go to college or if they’re going to college, they are taking on $20,000, $30,000, $50,000, $60,000 worth of debt, and it’s very difficult for them to go into some fields, like basic research in science, for example, thinking to themselves that they’re going to have a mortgage before they even buy a house.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I’ve proposed a $4,000 tuition credit, every student, every year, in exchange for some form of community service, whether it’s military service, whether it’s Peace Corps, whether it’s working in a community.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: If we do those things, then I believe that we can create a better school system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But there’s one last ingredient that I just want to mention, and that’s parents.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can’t do it just in the schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Parents are going to have to show more responsibility.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: They’ve got to turn off the TV set, put away the video games, and, finally, start instilling that thirst for knowledge that our students need.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, it’s the civil rights issue of the 21st century.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: There’s no doubt that we have achieved equal access to schools in America after a long and difficult and terrible struggle.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But what is the advantage in a low income area of sending a child to a failed school and that being your only choice?

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So choice and competition amongst schools is one of the key elements that’s already been proven in places in like New Orleans and New York City and other places, where we have charter schools, where we take good teachers and we reward them and promote them.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we find bad teachers another line of work.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we have to be able to give parents the same choice, frankly, that Senator Obama and Mrs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Obama had and Cindy and I had to send our kids to the school — their kids to the school of their choice.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Charter schools aren’t the only answer, but they’re providing competition.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: They are providing the kind of competitions that have upgraded both schools — types of schools.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now, throwing money at the problem is not the answer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: You will find that some of the worst school systems in America get the most money per student.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: So I believe that we need to reward these good teachers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to encourage programs such as Teach for America and Troops to Teachers where people, after having served in the military, can go right to teaching and not have to take these examinations which — or have the certification that some are required in some states.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Look, we must improve education in this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: As far as college education is concerned, we need to make those student loans available.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to give them a repayment schedule that they can meet.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have full student loan program for in-state tuition.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we certainly need to adjust the certain loan eligibility to inflation.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, we have a tradition of local control of the schools and that’s a tradition that has served us well.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But I do think that it is important for the federal government to step up and help local school districts do some of the things they need to do.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Now we tried to do this under President Bush.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: He put forward No Child Left Behind.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Unfortunately, they left the money behind for No Child Left Behind.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And local school districts end up having more of a burden, a bunch of unfunded mandates, the same kind of thing that happened with special education where we did the right thing by saying every school should provide education to kids with special needs, but we never followed through on the promise of funding, and that left local school districts very cash-strapped.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So what I want to do is focus on early childhood education, providing teachers higher salaries in exchange for more support.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain and I actually agree on two things that he just mentioned.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Charter schools, I doubled the number of charter schools in Illinois despite some reservations from teachers unions.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think it’s important to foster competition inside the public schools.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we also agree on the need for making sure that if we have bad teachers that they are swiftly — after given an opportunity to prove themselves, if they can’t hack it, then we need to move on because our kids have to have their best future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Where we disagree is on the idea that we can somehow give out vouchers — give vouchers as a way of securing the problems in our education system.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And I also have to disagree on Senator McCain’s record when it comes to college accessibility and affordability.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Recently his key economic adviser was asked about why he didn’t seem to have some specific programs to help young people go to college and the response was, well, you know, we can’t give money to every interest group that comes along.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I don’t think America’s youth are interest groups, I think they’re our future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And this is an example of where we are going to have to prioritize.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We can’t say we’re going to do things and then not explain in concrete terms how we’re going to pay for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And if we’re going to do some of the things you mentioned, like lowering loan rates or what have you, somebody has got to pay for it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not going to happen on its own.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’m sure you’re aware, Senator Obama, of the program in the Washington, D.C., school system where vouchers are provided and there’s a certain number, I think it’s a thousand and some and some 9,000 parents asked to be eligible for that.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because they wanted to have the same choice that you and I and Cindy and your wife have had.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And that is because they wanted to choose the school that they thought was best for their children.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we all know the state of the Washington, D.C., school system.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was vouchers.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was vouchers, Senator Obama.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’m frankly surprised you didn’t pay more attention to that example.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Now as far as the No Child Left Behind is concerned, it was a great first beginning in my view.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It had its flaws, it had its problems, the first time we had looked at the issue of education in America from a nationwide perspective.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we need to fix a lot of the problems.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to sit down and reauthorize it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But, again, spending more money isn’t always the answer.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think the Head Start program is a great program.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: A lot of people, including me, said, look, it’s not doing what it should do.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: By the third grade many times children who were in the Head Start program aren’t any better off than the others.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s reform it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Let’s reform it and fund it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That was, of course, out-of-bounds by the Democrats.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to reform these programs.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have transparency.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We need to have rewards.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: It’s a system that cries out for accountability and transparency and the adequate funding.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I just said to you earlier, town hall meeting after town hall meeting, parents come with kids, children — precious children who have autism.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Sarah Palin knows about that better than most.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ll find and we’ll spend the money, research, to find the cause of autism.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And we’ll care for these young children.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And all Americans will open their wallets and their hearts to do so.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But to have a situation, as you mentioned in our earlier comments, that the most expensive education in the world is in the United States of America also means that it cries out for reform, as well.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I will support those reforms, and I will fund the ones that are reformed.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: But I’m not going to continue to throw money at a problem.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve got to tell you that vouchers, where they are requested and where they are agreed to, are a good and workable system.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s been proven.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’ll just make a quick comment about vouchers in D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Senator McCain’s absolutely right: The D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: school system is in terrible shape, and it has been for a very long time.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And we’ve got a wonderful new superintendent there who’s working very hard with the young mayor there to try…

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Who supports vouchers.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: … who initiated — actually, supports charters.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: She supports vouchers, also.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But the — but here’s the thing, is that, even if Senator McCain were to say that vouchers were the way to go — I disagree with him on this, because the data doesn’t show that it actually solves the problem — the centerpiece of Senator McCain’s education policy is to increase the voucher program in D.C.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: by 2,000 slots.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: That leaves all of you who live in the other 50 states without an education reform policy from Senator McCain.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: So if we are going to be serious about this issue, we’ve got to have a president who is going to tackle it head-on.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s what I intend to do as president.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Because there’s not enough vouchers; therefore, we shouldn’t do it, even though it’s working.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Well, thank you again, Bob.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thanks to Hofstra.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And it’s great to be with you again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I think we’ve had a very healthy discussion.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: My friends, as I said in my opening remarks, these are very difficult times and challenges for America.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And they were graphically demonstrated again today.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: America needs a new direction.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We cannot be satisfied with what we’ve been doing for the last eight years.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I have a record of reform, and taking on my party, the other party, the special interests, whether it be an HMO Patients’ Bill of Rights, or trying to clean up the campaign finance system in — in this country, or whether it be establishment of a 9/11 Commission, I have a long record of it.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I’ve been a careful steward of your tax dollars.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to make health care affordable and available.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to make quality education there for all of our citizens, not just the privileged few.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to stop the spending.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: We have to stop the spending, which has mortgaged your children’s futures.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: All of these things and all the promises and commitments that Senator Obama and I made (inaudible) made to you tonight will base — will be based on whether you can trust us or not to be careful stewards of your tax dollar, to make sure America is safe and secure and prosperous, to make sure we reform the institutions of government.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: That’s why I’ve asked you not only to examine my record, but my proposals for the future of this country.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’ve spent my entire life in the service of this nation and putting my country first.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: Thereâ€™s a long line of McCains that have served our country for a long time in war and in peace, it’s been the great honor of my life, and I’ve been proud to serve.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: And I hope you’ll give me an opportunity to serve again.

speaker: MCCAIN
sentence: I’d be honored and humbled.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Well, I want to thank Senator McCain and Bob for moderating.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I think we all know America is going through tough times right now.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: The policies of the last eight years and — and Washington’s unwillingness to tackle the tough problems for decades has left us in the worst economic crisis since the Great Depression.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why the biggest risk we could take right now is to adopt the same failed policies and the same failed politics that we’ve seen over the last eight years and somehow expect a different result.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: We need fundamental change in this country, and that’s what I’d like to bring.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You know, over the last 20 months, you’ve invited me into your homes.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: You’ve shared your stories with me.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And you’ve confirmed once again the fundamental decency and generosity of the American people.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And that’s why I’m sure that our brighter days are still ahead.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But we’re going to have to invest in the American people again, in tax cuts for the middle class, in health care for all Americans, and college for every young person who wants to go.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In businesses that can create the new energy economy of the future.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: In policies that will lift wages and will grow our middle class.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: These are the policies I have fought for my entire career.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: And these are the policies I want to bring to the White House.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: But it’s not going to be easy.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It’s not going to be quick.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: It is going to be requiring all of us — Democrats, Republicans, independents — to come together and to renew a spirit of sacrifice and service and responsibility.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I’m absolutely convinced we can do it.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: I would ask for your vote, and I promise you that if you give me the extraordinary honor of serving as your president, I will work every single day, tirelessly, on your behalf and on the behalf of the future of our children.

speaker: OBAMA
sentence: Thank you very much.
